title: Generating in the Limit with Infinitely Many Hallucinations
summary: The classic paradigm of language identification in the limit models learning as a game between an adversary, who reveals strings from an unknown target language, and a learner tasked with identifying that language. The recently introduced framework of language generation in the limit shifted the objective to better reflect modern language modeling, requiring the learner to produce valid, unseen strings from the target language. Related work highlighted a fundamental tension: a broad coverage of the target often comes at the cost of validity. We introduce a new notion of precision and recast this problem as the classic recall-precision trade-off. We analyze generation in the limit under varying constraints on enumeration, novelty, and validity, aimed at reflecting settings closer to those encountered by large language models. A key contribution is our analysis of learners that are not eventually valid: we allow infinitely many mistakes, provided their frequency tends to zero so that precision remains one. We show that this relaxation can strictly increase recall when the adversary permanently withholds a large portion of the target language. We also study a continuous relaxation of the novelty constraint that requires only a fixed fraction of outputs to be novel. Taken together, our results move toward a more realistic model of language generation where occasional errors and repetitions are unavoidable, but their rates are controlled.

AI.tldr: They generalize classic language identification theory to a more realistic setting of language generation, analyzing trade-offs between validity, coverage, and novelty, and show that allowing rare errors or repetitions can strictly improve coverage under adversarial data exposure.
AI.motivation: Classic language identification in the limit assumes a learner only needs to eventually name the target language given positive examples. Modern large language models instead must generate new valid strings, facing trade-offs between covering the full language and avoiding invalid outputs or repetitions. Prior work found a tension between broad coverage and validity but lacked a precise way to frame it using familiar performance notions and did not fully explore learners that may keep making rare mistakes or repeat outputs. This paper aims to better align the theoretical model with how contemporary language models behave in practice, where occasional errors and repetitions are unavoidable but rates can be controlled.
AI.method: They extend the formal framework of language generation in the limit by introducing a new definition of precision, allowing analysis of the recall-precision trade-off for generative learners. They study learners under different constraints: (1) enumeration constraints on how outputs are produced; (2) novelty constraints, including both strict novelty and a relaxed version where only a fixed fraction of outputs must be novel; and (3) validity constraints, especially relaxing “eventual validity” to allow infinitely many mistakes as long as their asymptotic frequency tends to zero so precision remains 1. Under adversarial data presentation, they use formal learnability and identifiability arguments to compare what languages are generable under these different constraint regimes.
AI.result: They show that relaxing the requirement of eventual validity to allow an infinite number of mistakes whose frequency tends to zero can strictly increase recall (coverage of the target language) when the adversary withholds a large portion of the language permanently. This demonstrates that insisting on zero future errors can be too restrictive in adversarial settings. They also provide results on how relaxing strict novelty to only require that a fixed fraction of outputs be novel affects generability, giving characterizations of what can be generated under this fractional-novelty constraint. Overall, they delineate conditions under which higher recall can be achieved without sacrificing asymptotic precision, at the cost of tolerating occasional errors and repetitions.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that classic language-identification-in-the-limit models are too rigid to capture realistic language generation behavior. By reframing generation as a recall-precision trade-off and allowing controlled rates of errors and repetitions, one can obtain more powerful learners in adversarial environments, especially when the adversary reveals only part of the language. Their framework, including the new precision notion and relaxed novelty constraints, provides a more faithful theoretical abstraction of large language models, where non-zero error rates and repeated outputs are inevitable but can be bounded, and it clarifies how these relaxations expand what languages can be effectively generated.

title: Extracting Knowledge from an Arabic-English Machine-Readable Dictionary Using Information Extraction
comment: 9 pages, 7 figures, 4 tables, Conference version,CITALA 2014: 5th International Conference on Arabic Language Processing,Oujda, Morocco, 26-27 November 2014. Paper listed in archived accepted papers: https://web.archive.org/web/20150205160611/http://citala.org/acceptedpapers.php Original conference site defunct: http://citala.org No proceedings PDF is publicly available
summary: Natural language processing (NLP) applications need large and rich amount of linguistic knowledge. Furthermore, electronic language sources such as dictionaries, encyclopedia, and corpora became available. So, automatic methods are emerged to extract lexical information from those sources to overcome the knowledge acquisition bottleneck. We presented a method to automatically extract lexical information from a machine-readable version of the Arabic-English Al-Mawrid dictionary. We used n-gram analysis and key-word-in-context (KWIC) analysis to discover lexical patterns that manifest morphologic, syntactic, or semantic information. Then, we used hand-crafted rule-based information extraction to extract that information. Furthermore, we used punctuation marks and some heuristics to extract a set of synonyms in a subentry. This study registered high precision for all types of information, high recall for synonyms, and low recall for the other information. The study also showed that the Al-Mawrid has significant amount of derivations (morphologic information) and synonyms, domain labels, and hyponym/hypernym relations (semantic information).

AI.tldr: Automatic extraction of morphological, syntactic, and semantic lexical information (especially synonyms) from the Arabic-English Al-Mawrid dictionary using n-grams, KWIC, and rule-based patterns, with high precision but mixed recall.
AI.motivation: NLP systems require large, high-quality lexical resources, but manually building them is expensive and slow (the knowledge acquisition bottleneck). At the same time, many rich electronic language resources such as machine-readable dictionaries have become available and are underexploited. The paper is motivated by the need to automatically mine these existing resources—specifically the Al-Mawrid Arabic-English dictionary—to obtain structured lexical, morphological, and semantic information for Arabic, a relatively under-resourced language.
AI.method: The authors process a machine-readable version of the Arabic-English Al-Mawrid dictionary. They apply n-gram analysis and key-word-in-context (KWIC) analysis to identify recurring lexical patterns that indicate morphological, syntactic, or semantic information. Based on these observed patterns, they design hand-crafted, rule-based information extraction rules that capture and structure the relevant information. They also rely on punctuation and simple heuristics to detect and group synonyms within a subentry.
AI.result: The system successfully extracts different types of lexical information (morphological derivations, synonyms, domain labels, and hyponym/hypernym relations). Evaluation shows high precision across all categories, meaning extracted information is mostly correct. Recall is high for synonym extraction but low for other types of information, indicating many non-synonym facts are missed by the current rules.
AI.conclusion: Machine-readable dictionaries like Al-Mawrid are valuable sources of Arabic lexical knowledge and can be mined automatically. The proposed combination of n-gram and KWIC-based pattern discovery with hand-crafted extraction rules can reliably (high precision) obtain morphological and semantic information, particularly synonyms, from such resources. However, the low recall for non-synonym information suggests that more comprehensive or more sophisticated extraction methods are needed to capture the full richness of the dictionary.

title: Developmental Trajectories of Situation Modeling and Mentalizing in Transformer Language Models
comment: Non-archival submission to the First Workshop on Computational Developmental Linguistics
summary: Recent work suggests that Large Language Models (LLMs) are sensitive to the belief states of agents described by text, as measured by the false belief task (FBT), yet persistent concerns of construct validity remain. We adopt a **developmental perspective**, tracing the pattern of mental state reasoning behavior -- and likely **preconditions** for this behavior -- across multiple training stages in the Olmo2 and Pythia language model suites. We find that above-chance FBT performance depends both on model size and sufficient training volume, emerges relatively late in pretraining, and is most improved by post-training interventions (SFT, DPO) in the condition most diagnostic of mentalizing (False Belief, Implicit). However, FBT performance is fragile: consistent with past work, the use of non-factive verbs (e.g., thinks) increases false belief attributions even in the True Belief condition. To contextualize these findings, we track the emergence of **situation modeling**: the ability to report on basic factual properties of a described scene. Situation modeling accuracy generally precedes and exceeds FBT accuracy, yet situational representations also prove surprisingly incoherent in certain respects: when asked about the knowledge states of the Antagonist agent -- who always knows the item's true location -- Olmo2 13b is consistently influenced both by the Target agent's knowledge state and the presence of non-factive verbs. Together, these results suggest that larger, sufficiently trained models build partially coherent situation models in a developmentally appropriate sequence, yet display surprising fragility -- highlighting the value of developmental and stress-testing approaches for evaluating LLM capabilities.

AI.tldr: The paper examines when and how large language models (LLMs) develop the ability to reason about others’ beliefs (especially false beliefs) during training, and shows that this ability appears late, depends on scale and data, and remains fragile and inconsistent.
AI.motivation: Prior work claims LLMs can pass false belief tasks, suggesting they might ‘understand’ other agents’ mental states, but there are doubts about what these tests really measure and how such abilities develop. The authors want to study the developmental trajectory and prerequisites of mental state reasoning in LLMs, to see whether and when genuine belief-sensitive behavior emerges and how robust it is.
AI.method: They take a developmental-style approach, evaluating multiple checkpoints across the pretraining and post-training (SFT, DPO) process in two model families (Olmo2 and Pythia). They test models on several variants of the false belief task (including the most diagnostic False Belief, Implicit condition) and also on ‘situation modeling’ tasks that require reporting simple facts about the described scenario. They analyze how performance changes with model size, training volume, and post-training, and probe for fragility by manipulating linguistic cues such as non-factive verbs like “thinks.”
AI.result: Above-chance performance on false belief tasks appears only in larger models that have undergone sufficient training and typically emerges relatively late in pretraining. Post-training methods (SFT, DPO) substantially boost performance, especially on the most diagnostic false belief condition. However, performance is fragile: wording changes like using non-factive verbs lead models to incorrectly attribute false beliefs even when the character actually has true beliefs. Situation modeling (tracking basic facts about the scene) appears earlier and is generally more accurate than false belief reasoning, yet still shows incoherencies: for instance, Olmo2 13B’s judgments about an always-informed Antagonist’s knowledge are influenced by another agent’s belief state and by verbal framing.
AI.conclusion: LLMs develop a partially ordered, “developmental” progression in which basic situation modeling emerges before more sophisticated belief reasoning, and larger, better-trained models show stronger mental state sensitivity. Nonetheless, their internal situation and belief representations remain fragile and sometimes inconsistent, especially under linguistic perturbations. This supports using developmental lenses and stress tests to more precisely characterize what mental state reasoning abilities LLMs do and do not genuinely possess.

title: A French OSCE Dialogue Dataset and Controllable Virtual Patient System for Clinical Training
summary: The clinical and communication skills of medical students are commonly assessed through Objective Structured Clinical Examinations (OSCEs), which consist of brief scenario-driven simulations of doctor-patient interactions. However, training is often limited by the low availability of human standardized patients, motivating the development of realistic virtual patients (VPs). To address this gap, we introduce a French OSCE dialogue dataset comprising 240 student-patient training interactions. We build upon it a controllable LLM-based pipeline to generate synthetic OSCE dialogues. The pipeline integrates modular components, such as retrieval-based grounding and a reflection loop, to ensure patient fidelity, coherence, and realism. Additionally, we propose a multi-level evaluation framework assessing patient simulation quality, student performance, and linguistic quality, using an LLM-as-a-Judge approach. Experiments suggest that controllability modules generally improve patient fidelity and student evaluation consistency. Finally, we implement an interactive prototype in which students can practice with a VP and receive automatic feedback.

AI.tldr: Paper creates a French OSCE dialogue dataset and a controllable LLM-based system to generate and evaluate realistic virtual patient simulations for medical student training.
AI.motivation: OSCEs require standardized patients for assessing medical students’ clinical and communication skills, but human standardized patients are scarce and costly, limiting training opportunities. There is a need for realistic, controllable virtual patients and robust evaluation methods to scale training, especially in non-English languages such as French.
AI.method: They compile a dataset of 240 French student-patient OSCE training dialogues. On top of this, they design a controllable LLM-based generation pipeline with modular components, including retrieval-based grounding to inject scenario-specific knowledge and a reflection loop to revise outputs, aiming to maintain patient fidelity, coherence, and realism. They also design a multi-level evaluation framework, using LLM-as-a-Judge to rate (1) patient simulation quality, (2) student performance, and (3) linguistic quality, and conduct experiments to assess the impact of controllability modules. Finally, they build an interactive prototype where students converse with a virtual patient and receive automatic feedback.
AI.result: The synthetic dialogue generation pipeline successfully produces OSCE-like French dialogues. Experiments indicate that adding controllability modules (retrieval, reflection, etc.) generally increases patient fidelity and leads to more consistent evaluations of student performance. The LLM-as-a-Judge framework can quantify multiple aspects of dialogue quality. The prototype VP system functions interactively and can provide automatic feedback to students.
AI.conclusion: A French OSCE dialogue dataset plus a controllable LLM-based generation and evaluation pipeline can produce realistic virtual patient simulations and reliable automatic assessments, partially alleviating standardized patient scarcity. The proposed modular architecture and LLM-as-a-Judge evaluation appear effective and can support interactive training tools for medical students, though further validation and scaling are likely needed.

title: Recursive Self-Evolving Agents via Held-Out Selection
summary:
LLM agents are increasingly improved without weight updates by evolving a natural-language artifact, such as reflections, workflows, playbooks, cheatsheets, or optimized prompts, that conditions a frozen policy. Such methods are typically reported as wins on the single benchmark where they help. We study them apples-to-apples and surface a sharper picture. We introduce RSEA, a Recursive Self-Evolving Agent that carries a compact three-layer natural-language state: an imperative strategy, reusable skills, and a procedural playbook. Across generations, RSEA rewrites all three layers from its own trajectories and commits a candidate only if it does not regress on a disjoint held-out split, using a strict keep-better gate.
  Across four diverse benchmarks, ALFWorld, GAIA, (τ)-bench, and WebShop, and six faithful baselines, ReAct, Reflexion, GEPA, AWM, ACE, and Dynamic Cheatsheet, all evaluated on one shared local backbone, we find three main results. First, no artifact universally wins. RSEA is the strongest single-pass method on ALFWorld, reaching 69.3% compared with 64.6% for ReAct (McNemar (p=0.015)), and reaches 79.4% with retry, the best overall result. However, concrete-workflow induction, represented by AWM, is best on the strong-backbone tool-use tasks. Second, unguarded context evolution is high-variance and unsafe. Dynamic Cheatsheet, which curates context online without a held-out gate, is near-best on ALFWorld at 70.7%, yet collapses on WebShop, with a score of 0.14 compared with 0.43 for ReAct. Third, RSEA's strict held-out selection is what makes recursive self-evolution monotone-safe: it never significantly underperforms the base agent on any benchmark and falls back to vanilla ReAct when evolved context would hurt.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes RSEA, a recursive self-evolving LLM agent that improves itself by editing a structured natural-language state (strategy, skills, playbook) while keeping model weights frozen, and evaluates it systematically against other context-evolution methods across multiple benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Many recent methods improve frozen LLM agents by evolving natural-language artifacts (prompts, workflows, reflections, cheatsheets), but each is usually shown to work only on its chosen benchmark, making it unclear which ideas are truly robust, when they help, and how to make such evolution safe and reliable. The authors want a unified comparison and a method that can self-improve without risking large regressions.
AI.method: They introduce RSEA, a Recursive Self-Evolving Agent that maintains a compact, three-layer natural-language state: (1) an imperative high-level strategy, (2) a set of reusable skills, and (3) a procedural playbook. From its own trajectories, RSEA proposes edits to all three layers and evaluates each candidate on a disjoint held-out split. A strict “keep-better” gate only accepts updates that demonstrably do not regress performance; otherwise it keeps or reverts to the previous configuration (or vanilla ReAct). They compare RSEA against six baseline methods (ReAct, Reflexion, GEPA, AWM, ACE, Dynamic Cheatsheet) across four benchmarks, all using the same local backbone model, to isolate the effect of context evolution.
AI.result: 1) No single artifact or method wins on all tasks. RSEA is the strongest single-pass method on ALFWorld (69.3% vs 64.6% for ReAct, statistically significant) and reaches 79.4% with retries, which is the best reported result there, but AWM (workflow induction) performs best on strong tool-use benchmarks. 2) Methods that evolve context without safeguards are unstable: Dynamic Cheatsheet performs near-best on ALFWorld (70.7%) but catastrophically fails on WebShop (0.14 vs 0.43 for ReAct). 3) RSEA’s strict held-out gating makes recursive self-evolution “monotone-safe”: across all tested benchmarks it never significantly underperforms the base agent and reverts to vanilla ReAct when an evolved context would degrade performance.
AI.conclusion: Structured, recursively updated natural-language state can improve frozen LLM agents, but there is no universally best artifact; different benchmarks favor different evolution schemes. Unconstrained or ungated context evolution is risky and can lead to severe performance collapse on some tasks. A strict held-out evaluation gate, as in RSEA, provides a practical way to make self-evolving agents monotone-safe, achieving gains where possible while avoiding regressions by reverting to a strong base agent when needed.

title: Legal Domain Adaptation of Modern BERT Models
comment: To appear in Proceedings of the 21st International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Law (ICAIL 2026), June 9-12, 2026, Singapore
summary: We investigate domain adaptation of modern BERT models in the legal domain. We further pre-train ModernBERT on all US court opinions using the masked language modeling objective. Although ModernBERT has been trained on roughly 500x more data than original BERT, we still find that this model benefits from further pre-training and domain adaptation in the legal domain: we report significant improvements compared to vanilla ModernBERT on all datasets connected to US court opinions. We find gains similar to those reported in early work on domain adaptation of BERT-like models. However, from scratch pre-training does not match the performance of further pre-training an existing ModernBERT checkpoint in our experiments. The resulting models are capable of processing sequences up to 8,192 tokens, and can be used to compute meaningful embeddings of legal passages, or could quickly rerank hundreds of legal passages for a given search query. We release all model checkpoints publicly.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how additional pre-training and domain adaptation of ModernBERT on US court opinions improves legal NLP performance, compares it to training from scratch, and releases long-context legal models.
AI.motivation: General BERT/ModernBERT models, though trained on massive generic corpora, may not optimally capture the specialized vocabulary, style, and reasoning patterns of legal texts such as US court opinions. Prior work showed domain adaptation helps smaller BERT-like models, but it is unclear whether modern, heavily pre-trained models still benefit, and whether starting from scratch on domain data can rival further pre-training. The authors aim to answer these questions for the legal domain and produce practically useful legal-language models.
AI.method: They take an existing ModernBERT model that was pre-trained on approximately 500 times more data than original BERT, and further pre-train it on a large corpus of all US court opinions using a masked language modeling objective for domain adaptation. They also run from-scratch pre-training on the same corpus to compare with continued pre-training from a ModernBERT checkpoint. They evaluate on multiple datasets tied to US court opinions and analyze performance, sequence length capabilities, and embedding / reranking utility.
AI.result: Further pre-training ModernBERT on US court opinions yields significant performance gains across all evaluated datasets involving US court opinions compared with the unadapted ModernBERT. The magnitude of gains is comparable to those reported in earlier work on domain adaptation of BERT-like models. In contrast, models trained from scratch on legal data underperform those obtained by continued pre-training of ModernBERT. The adapted models also support long sequences up to 8,192 tokens and produce useful embeddings for legal passages and efficient reranking for legal search.
AI.conclusion: Even highly pre-trained, modern BERT variants still benefit substantially from domain-specific continued pre-training in specialized areas such as law. For US court opinions, domain adaptation of ModernBERT outperforms from-scratch pre-training and yields strong models for long-context legal understanding, passage embedding, and search reranking. The authors provide these domain-adapted ModernBERT checkpoints as public resources for legal NLP applications.

title: Data and Evaluation Closed-Loop for Model Capability Enhancement
summary: Model capability is the central variable in LLM pre-training, yet is never observed directly: data shapes it prospectively, while evaluation reveals it only retrospectively, compressing samples, prompts, decoding, and scoring rules into one noisy score. Practical optimization runs this backward: a failure is observed first, and the engineer must infer the corpus fix. The two sides speak incompatible vocabularies -- benchmark names and per-sample correctness versus data sources, domains, and quality labels -- so this inference is usually intuition, not method. We close this gap with the \emph{capability slice}: a group of evaluation samples sharing background condition, task type, solving operation, and output constraint -- precise enough to localize a single weakness yet stable enough to survive aggregation, unlike a benchmark name, too coarse, or a single sample, too noisy. Built around this unit, an evaluation taxonomy, a non-instruction data taxonomy, and mapping rules form a closed loop turning a benchmark-level failure into a targeted, testable data intervention. We test this loop on two case studies pulling in opposite directions. First, the loop rules the data out: continued pre-training drives BBH down by $-46.82\%$, but diagnosis traces this to a single masked \texttt{\textless EOS\textgreater} loss rather than weakened reasoning; restoring it recovers BBH to $66.44$, above the original checkpoint, without changing the data. Second, the loop rules the data in: a persistent math-reasoning weakness is decomposed by solving operation into specific failing combinations, and a weakness-targeted sampling procedure built from it lifts AIME2025/AIME2026 Pass@128 from $6.67$/$0.00$ to $26.67$ each. The same unmodified loop reaches opposite, correct verdicts in both cases, showing the evaluation-to-data inference can be routine, auditable, and experimentally validated rather than intuitive.

AI.tldr: They introduce "capability slices" to more precisely link LLM evaluation failures to specific data issues, enabling systematic, testable data interventions instead of intuition-driven debugging.
AI.motivation: LLM capability is shaped by pre-training data but only observed indirectly through evaluations, which bundle many factors into noisy scores. When a benchmark degrades, engineers must guess which data changes caused it, because evaluation concepts (benchmarks, per-sample scores) and data concepts (sources, domains, quality labels) don’t align, making the process ad hoc and non-systematic.
AI.method: They define a "capability slice" as a tightly specified group of evaluation samples sharing background condition, task type, solving operation, and output constraint. Around this unit they build: (1) an evaluation taxonomy, (2) a taxonomy of non-instruction pre-training data, and (3) mapping rules between capability slices and data segments. This creates a closed loop: benchmark failure → diagnose which capability slice regressed → map that slice to relevant data regions → propose a targeted data intervention → re-evaluate on the same slices. They validate this loop through controlled case studies.
AI.result: Case study 1: A continued pre-training run appears to hurt BBH performance by −46.82%. Their diagnostic loop localizes the issue to a single masked <EOS> loss rather than genuine reasoning degradation. Fixing this restores BBH to 66.44, better than the original, without altering the data. Case study 2: For a persistent math reasoning weakness, they decompose it by solving operation to find specific failing combinations. Using this analysis, they design a weakness-targeted sampling strategy for additional data, which improves AIME2025/AIME2026 Pass@128 from 6.67/0.00 to 26.67/26.67. In both cases, the same pipeline yields correct but opposite prescriptions: ignore data vs. add targeted data.
AI.conclusion: Capability slices, backed by a taxonomy and mapping to data, enable a repeatable and auditable loop from evaluation failures to data-level actions. This replaces intuition-driven debugging with a structured procedure that can distinguish model bugs, data bugs, and capability gaps, and can be validated experimentally. The framework works across qualitatively different problems, suggesting it can generalize as a principled tool for pre-training data optimization.

title: Turn-Averaged SAEs for Feature Discovery and Long-Context Attribution
summary: Sparse autoencoders (SAEs) have become a useful tool for extracting interpretable features in language models. However, standard SAE architectures operate on individual token activations, meaning that the number of active features scales linearly with context length, and studying long model transcripts becomes difficult. We introduce turn-averaged SAEs, which represent a single Human or Assistant turn with a fixed number of features by learning to reconstruct the average model activation across the turn. We find that turn-averaged features describe a single turn's high-level characteristics more completely than per-token features when judged by an LLM. We also demonstrate that turn-averaged SAEs greatly simplify common downstream uses of SAEs like attribution graphs. Broadly, turn-averaged SAEs make interpretability techniques practical at long context lengths.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces turn-averaged sparse autoencoders that compress an entire conversational turn into a fixed set of interpretable features, making SAE-based interpretability practical for long contexts.
AI.motivation: Standard sparse autoencoders work on individual token activations, so the number of active features grows with sequence length. This makes it hard to analyze long transcripts and to use SAE features in downstream interpretability tools, because there are too many token-level activations to inspect or summarize for long contexts.
AI.method: They modify the SAE setup so that, instead of reconstructing each token’s activation separately, the autoencoder reconstructs the average activation across all tokens within a single Human or Assistant turn. Each turn is thus encoded into a fixed-size sparse feature vector. They then compare these turn-averaged features with standard per-token features, including using an LLM to judge which better captures a turn’s high-level characteristics, and they evaluate how this affects common downstream interpretability workflows such as attribution graphs.
AI.result: Turn-averaged SAEs produce features that LLM evaluators judge as more complete descriptions of a single turn’s high-level characteristics than the features from per-token SAEs. Because each turn is represented by a single sparse vector rather than many token-level activations, constructing and reading downstream analyses like attribution graphs becomes significantly simpler and more scalable for long conversations.
AI.conclusion: Turn-averaged SAEs offer a practical way to apply sparse-autoencoder-based interpretability at long context lengths by compressing each conversational turn into a small set of interpretable features. This improves both the faithfulness of high-level turn descriptions and the usability of common interpretability tools that rely on SAE features.

title: GPTNT: Benchmarking Real-Time Collaboration Between Multimodal Agents on Keep Talking And Nobody Explodes
comment: Project website and code at https://gptnt.github.io
summary: Multimodal models are increasingly deployed to solve tasks collaboratively with humans or other artificial agents. Existing benchmarks show that these models possess many of the required component capabilities, but the conditions that coincide in collaboration, including time pressure, information asymmetry, and imperfect communication, are usually studied in isolation. We introduce GPTNT, a benchmark built on the cooperative video game Keep Talking and Nobody Explodes, in which two agents must coordinate to defuse procedurally generated bomb puzzles against a live countdown. One agent can see and manipulate the bomb but does not have the defusal instructions; the other has the instructions but cannot see or manipulate the bomb. Neither agent can succeed alone: success requires effective and efficient communication. Unlike turn-based proxies, GPTNT requires agents to act asynchronously and communicate in real time. GPTNT is designed to separate collaboration from reliance on memorized solutions: the instruction manual, the partner, or both can be withheld to isolate what a model derives in the moment from what it already knows. We show that GPTNT poses a substantial challenge for state-of-the-art systems: none of the closed- or open-source models we test defuses a single bomb in real time, a bar that human players clear. Through controlled experiments, we identify critical weaknesses in state tracking, efficient action under time pressure, ambiguity handling, and error recovery. We release GPTNT as a benchmark for collaborative performance that current evaluations leave unmeasured. Because it runs on the real game, GPTNT benefits from procedural generation and inherits a living modding community, allowing the benchmark to evolve as models improve rather than being solved once and retired.

AI.tldr: They introduce GPTNT, a real‑time benchmark based on the cooperative game “Keep Talking and Nobody Explodes” to evaluate multimodal models’ collaborative abilities under realistic conditions like time pressure and partial information.
AI.motivation: Existing multimodal benchmarks test isolated capabilities (perception, language understanding, etc.) but not the combined demands of real collaborative settings: time pressure, asymmetric information, imperfect and asynchronous communication. The authors want a benchmark that specifically stresses coordination and communication between agents, separate from memorized solutions, to see how well current models actually collaborate with partners (human or AI) in realistic tasks.
AI.method: They build GPTNT on top of the real-time cooperative video game “Keep Talking and Nobody Explodes.” Two agents play distinct roles: a ‘Defuser’ agent that can see and interact with the bomb but lacks the manual, and an ‘Expert’ agent that has the manual but cannot see the bomb. Bombs are procedurally generated. The game enforces time pressure via a countdown and requires asynchronous, real-time communication. The benchmark can systematically vary what’s available: withholding the manual, the partner, or both, to decouple on-the-fly reasoning from prior memorized knowledge. They run controlled experiments with multiple state-of-the-art open- and closed-source models in different roles and configurations, and analyze their behavior in terms of state tracking, speed and efficiency, ambiguity resolution, and error recovery.
AI.result: No tested model, whether open- or closed-source, managed to defuse even a single bomb in real time under the benchmark conditions, whereas human players can. The experiments reveal systematic failures: models struggle to maintain an accurate, updated representation of the bomb state; they are slow or inefficient under time constraints; they mishandle ambiguous instructions or descriptions; and they have poor strategies for recognizing and recovering from errors during collaboration.
AI.conclusion: GPTNT exposes a gap between current multimodal model capabilities and the demands of real-time collaborative tasks. It shows that models that perform well on existing benchmarks can still fail dramatically when faced with time pressure, partial observability, and the need for efficient, bi-directional communication. GPTNT, by using a procedurally generated, mod-friendly real game environment, offers an evolving benchmark that can track progress in collaborative AI over time instead of becoming obsolete once solved.

title: Depth-Staggered Fibonacci Spacing for Sparse Attention: Static Schedules Beat Learned Dilation and Extrapolate Where Dense Attention Fails
summary: We study sparse self-attention in which each query attends to a dense local window plus a set of Fibonacci-spaced offsets, with a per-layer scalar alpha that compresses or expands the spacing. Across 21 language models trained under one matched recipe (60M parameters, 512 hidden, 16 layers, 426M tokens), we compare four ways of setting alpha across depth: fixed, per-layer learned, a static linear stagger, and a coprime (anti-gridding) reassignment of that stagger, together with a reach-matched power-of-2 control. Three results stand out. First, a static per-layer stagger improves perplexity over both fixed and learned alpha, and the gain is base-agnostic: applying the same stagger to a power-of-2 base lifts it above fixed Fibonacci and to parity with learned Fibonacci attention. Second, learning per layer is inert: it does not beat the static schedule and costs roughly five times the inference latency. Third, and most consequential, all sparse variants extrapolate to four times their training length with little or no degradation, whereas a recipe-matched dense baseline collapses (perplexity rises by 201% at 4x length); we attribute this to fixed-offset attention only ever querying relative positions seen during training. We also report two honest negatives: at training length the best sparse model has about 26% higher perplexity than the dense baseline, and the staggering gain is uniform across context positions rather than concentrated at long range.

AI.tldr: Paper analyzes sparse self-attention with Fibonacci-spaced offsets and different per-layer spacing schedules, showing static staggering outperforms learned spacing and sparsity enables strong length extrapolation but worse in-distribution perplexity than dense attention.
AI.motivation: Transformers with dense attention have quadratic complexity and often fail to extrapolate to much longer sequences than seen during training. Sparse attention patterns can reduce compute and potentially improve length generalization, but it is unclear how to best design and schedule the sparsity pattern across depth, and how these choices trade off perplexity vs extrapolation compared to dense baselines.
AI.method: Train 21 language models (60M parameters, 512 hidden size, 16 layers) on 426M tokens using the same recipe while varying only the attention pattern. Use a sparse self-attention where each query attends to a dense local window plus a set of Fibonacci-spaced offsets, scaled by a per-layer scalar alpha that contracts or expands the spacing. Compare four strategies for alpha across layers: (1) fixed, (2) per-layer learned, (3) a static linear stagger across depth, and (4) a coprime reassignment of the linear stagger to avoid gridding artifacts. Also include a power-of-2 offset pattern control with matched maximum reach. Evaluate perplexity at training length and at up to 4x sequence length, as well as inference latency costs.
AI.result: 1) A simple static per-layer stagger schedule for alpha yields lower perplexity than both fixed and learned alpha, and this improvement holds across different offset bases (Fibonacci and power-of-2); with a power-of-2 base, the staggered schedule surpasses fixed Fibonacci and matches learned Fibonacci attention. 2) Allowing alpha to be learned per layer does not improve over the static schedule but increases inference latency by about 5x. 3) All sparse attention variants generalize well to sequences up to 4x the training context length with minimal perplexity degradation, while a matched dense-attention baseline fails catastrophically, exhibiting a 201% perplexity increase at 4x length. The authors argue this is because fixed-offset sparse attention only queries relative positions that were observed during training. Two negative findings are also reported: at training length, the best sparse model has roughly 26% higher perplexity than the dense baseline, and the benefits of staggering are spread uniformly across positions rather than being specifically larger at long-range positions.
AI.conclusion: Sparse attention with fixed offset patterns plus a simple static per-layer staggering of spacing can provide strong length extrapolation and competitive perplexity without expensive per-layer learning of the sparsity pattern. However, at training context lengths, dense attention still gives substantially better perplexity, and the gains from staggering are generic rather than targeted to long-range dependencies. Designing sparse patterns that close this in-distribution performance gap while retaining extrapolation remains an open challenge.

title: IMCBench: A benchmark for multimodal LLMs in Image-grounded Medical Conversations
comment: Accepted at ECML PKDD 2026. 22 pages, 2 figures
summary: Recent advances in large language models and vision-language models have enabled reasoning over multimodal data, offering opportunities for clinical applications such as decision support and triaging. However, existing medical AI benchmarks are fragmented: some support multi-turn dialogues but lack images, while others provide multimodal inputs but focus on single-turn QA tasks. To address this gap, we introduce IMCBench, an image-grounded, multi-turn medical conversation benchmark that pairs real, publicly available clinical images with synthetic patient profiles to simulate realistic patient-clinician interactions. Each conversation is evaluated across three clinical dimensions: safety, accuracy, and appropriate use of uncertainty in diagnosis. We benchmark eight multimodal frontier models across four model families (Claude, GPT, Nova, and Llama), scoring each on a 1-5 scale using LLM-as-Jury scoring calibrated against expert clinician annotations. Our results show that Claude Opus 4.6 achieves the highest overall score (3.61), followed by Claude Sonnet 4.6 (3.30) and GPT-5.2 (3.29), though no model dominates all dimensions and safety degrades for both malignant and rare conditions ($Δ$ = -0.27 each). Ablation studies further reveal that both visual input and EHR context contribute to safe guidance (safety drops of 0.18 and 0.23 on average when each is removed), with stronger models leveraging visual features more effectively. Together, these findings demonstrate that accurate clinical description does not guarantee safe patient guidance, motivating the need for multi-dimensional evaluation frameworks in medical AI.

AI.tldr: IMCBench is a new benchmark for evaluating multimodal medical conversation models using real clinical images and synthetic patient profiles, scored on safety, accuracy, and uncertainty handling.
AI.motivation: Existing medical AI benchmarks are fragmented: dialog benchmarks usually lack images, and multimodal datasets are mostly single-turn QA. This makes it hard to evaluate how models behave in realistic, image-grounded, multi-turn clinical interactions, especially regarding patient safety and uncertainty management.
AI.method: The authors construct IMCBench, pairing real, publicly available clinical images with synthetic but realistic patient profiles to create multi-turn patient-clinician style conversations. They define three evaluation dimensions—safety, diagnostic/clinical accuracy, and appropriate use of uncertainty—and use an LLM-as-Jury approach calibrated with expert clinician annotations to rate model responses on a 1–5 scale. Eight frontier multimodal models from four families (Claude, GPT, Nova, Llama) are benchmarked, with ablation studies removing visual input or EHR context.
AI.result: Claude Opus 4.6 scores highest overall (3.61), followed by Claude Sonnet 4.6 (3.30) and GPT-5.2 (3.29). No single model leads on all dimensions. Safety performance worsens particularly for malignant and rare conditions (average drop of 0.27). Removing visual input or EHR context reduces safety scores by 0.18 and 0.23 on average, respectively, and stronger models exploit visual features more effectively.
AI.conclusion: IMCBench shows that strong clinical description or diagnostic ability does not automatically translate into safe patient guidance. Safety is sensitive to case type (malignant/rare) and to the presence of visual and EHR context. This underscores the need for multidimensional, image-grounded, conversation-based evaluation frameworks for medical AI systems, rather than relying on single-turn or unimodal accuracy metrics alone.

title: SEAD: Competence-Aware On-Policy Distillation via Entropy-Guided Supervision
summary: On-policy distillation (OPD) has a property absent in offline distillation and RL: teacher supervision quality depends on student competence. Incoherent rollouts yield noisy gradients; already-mastered tokens yield redundant ones. This creates waste at three scales (tokens, training phases, and prompts) yet existing methods supervise uniformly. We introduce SEAD, which uses entropy as a unified probe of this competence-dependent degradation at three scales: (1) joint teacher-student entropy partitions tokens into zones receiving tailored divergences or zero gradient (approx. 50% skipped); (2) a cosine schedule anneals from forward to reverse KL as competence grows; (3) a competence-gated curriculum introduces prompts easy-to-hard. These components are symbiotically necessary: token selection requires coherent rollouts (curriculum), annealing requires monotonic improvement (also curriculum). On OLMo-3 (7B to 32B), SEAD achieves +4.8 avg accuracy over vanilla OPD across six math benchmarks, with ablations confirming super-additive interactions.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes SEAD, an entropy-based framework to make on-policy distillation more efficient by adapting supervision to the student model’s competence at three scales (tokens, training phases, prompts), achieving significant math benchmark gains over vanilla OPD.
AI.motivation: In on-policy distillation, the quality and usefulness of teacher supervision depend heavily on the student’s current competence: low-quality, incoherent student rollouts induce noisy gradients, and tokens the student already masters provide little learning signal. Existing methods treat all supervision uniformly, causing inefficiency and wasted compute at token, phase, and prompt levels. The authors aim to systematically reduce this waste and better exploit the competence-dependent structure of OPD.
AI.method: They introduce SEAD, which uses entropy as a unified measure of student competence and supervision quality. (1) At the token level, joint teacher–student entropy partitions tokens into regions that receive different divergence objectives or no gradient at all, skipping roughly half the tokens. (2) Across training phases, they anneal the divergence objective from forward KL to reverse KL with a cosine schedule as the student becomes more competent. (3) At the prompt level, they design a competence-gated curriculum that feeds prompts from easy to hard. The three mechanisms are coupled: token selection relies on coherent rollouts achieved via the curriculum, and the KL annealing assumes monotonic competence gains, also induced by the curriculum.
AI.result: On OLMo-3 models from 7B to 32B parameters, SEAD outperforms vanilla on-policy distillation by an average of 4.8 accuracy points across six math benchmarks. Ablation studies show that each component contributes and that the combination yields super-additive improvements, indicating strong positive interaction between the three scales of adaptation.
AI.conclusion: SEAD demonstrates that entropy-based competence estimation can effectively structure supervision in on-policy distillation at multiple scales, significantly improving data and compute efficiency and overall performance on math reasoning tasks. Coordinated design of token selection, divergence annealing, and curriculum proves more powerful than applying any of these ideas in isolation.

title: Search for Truth from Reasoning: A Dynamic Representation Editing Framework for Steering LLM Trajectories
summary: Current approaches to enhance Large Language Model (LLM) reasoning, such as Chain-of-Thought and "Wait" prompts, primarily encourage models to think more, yet often fail to guide them toward Truth. While Representation Editing (RepE) offers a intrinsic control, its application to dynamic reasoning trajectories remains underexplored. In this work, we bridge this gap by investigating the geometry of truth within unfolding reasoning chains. We uncover three critical insights: (1) Truth is encoded at the sentence level and is entangled with latent reasoning patterns; (2) Effective intervention follows an Uncertainty Principle and a Decay Effect, requiring localization to early, high-entropy forks; (3) Naive steering vectors suffer from noise, risking collateral damage to correct trajectories. Based on these findings, we propose DynaSteer, a dynamic RepE framework. DynaSteer employs pattern clustering to disentangle reasoning manifolds and utilizes Fisher-LDA to project purified truth. By dynamically monitoring lookahead entropy, it selectively steers and rolls back trajectories only when necessary. Comprehensive experimental results on several MATH benchmark verify the effectiveness of DynaSteer, and experiments on out-of-domain coding tasks further confirm its generalization ability. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/tianlwang/DynaSteer.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces DynaSteer, a dynamic representation editing framework that steers LLM reasoning trajectories toward truthful solutions by intervening early and selectively based on uncertainty.
AI.motivation: Existing methods like Chain-of-Thought and "Wait" prompts make LLMs think more but do not reliably guide them toward truth. Representation editing offers a way to intrinsically control model behavior, but how to apply it to the evolving reasoning process (rather than static representations) is unclear. The authors aim to understand how "truth" is encoded in unfolding reasoning chains and to design interventions that correct erroneous reasoning without harming correct trajectories.
AI.method: The authors empirically analyze the internal representations of LLM reasoning chains, focusing on the geometry of truth in hidden states. They observe that truth is encoded at the sentence level and intertwined with latent reasoning patterns, that interventions must target early high-entropy decision points (Uncertainty Principle and Decay Effect), and that naive steering vectors introduce noise. Building on this, they propose DynaSteer: (1) cluster reasoning patterns to disentangle reasoning manifolds; (2) apply Fisher Linear Discriminant Analysis (Fisher-LDA) to obtain a purified truth direction; (3) dynamically monitor lookahead entropy to detect uncertain forks; and (4) selectively apply representation edits and, if needed, roll back trajectories at those points.
AI.result: On multiple MATH benchmarks, DynaSteer significantly improves LLM reasoning accuracy compared to baselines, demonstrating more effective and safer steering toward correct solutions. Additional experiments on out-of-domain coding tasks show that the learned intervention mechanism generalizes beyond the math domain. The framework reduces collateral damage to correct reasoning paths compared with naive steering methods.
AI.conclusion: Truth-related information in LLMs is encoded in a structured way within the evolving reasoning process and can be exploited through careful, dynamic representation editing. Effective steering requires early, uncertainty-aware, and noise-robust interventions. DynaSteer operationalizes these principles and yields consistent gains on math and coding tasks, suggesting that dynamic RepE is a promising direction for reliably improving LLM reasoning.

title: Correct codes for the wrong reasons? validating LLMs as measurement instruments for theoretical constructs
summary: When a large language model (LLM) codes a construct in text as a human annotator would, that agreement makes the LLM a reliable coder. Yet reliability leaves construct validity untouched. The instrument may be theory-naive, reaching the code through a correlate that meets none of the demands the construct's theory makes, and no current method tells that apart from genuine measurement. We propose grain calibration as a method that closes the gap. It decomposes a construct into clause-level components, tests each against the text with extractive evidence, and combines the results through an explicit, theory-derived rule. Because the rule is stated rather than lodged in one opaque pass, its structure is evidence about the process rather than the output. It shows which components settled a code, and, when the code is wrong, whether a component was missed or an adjacent construct mistaken for it. Validation shifts from scoring an instrument's outputs against an annotator to showing that the instrument runs on the construct its theory specifies.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes 'grain calibration,' a method for validating that large language model (LLM) coders truly measure a theoretical construct rather than just correlates, by decomposing constructs into clause-level components, testing each with extractive evidence, and combining them via an explicit theory-based rule.
AI.motivation: Current practice often judges LLMs as reliable coders when they match human annotations, but such reliability does not guarantee construct validity. An LLM might arrive at the correct code using theory-irrelevant cues or correlates, and existing evaluation methods cannot distinguish genuine construct measurement from such shortcuts. Researchers need a way to demonstrate that an LLM-based instrument actually operates on the intended theoretical construct.
AI.method: The authors introduce 'grain calibration.' First, they decompose the target construct into finer-grained, clause-level components derived from theory. For each component, they test its presence in text using extractive evidence (text spans that support or refute the component). Then they aggregate the component-level assessments using an explicit, theory-derived decision rule to produce the final code. Because the rule and components are explicit, they can examine which elements drove a particular coding decision and how the model processed the construct.
AI.result: Grain calibration yields an instrument whose internal decision structure is partially transparent: it exposes which construct components were detected, which textual evidence supported them, and how these pieces combined to produce a code. This makes it possible to diagnose specific failure modes, such as missing a component or confusing a neighboring construct, and to distinguish cases where the model is tracking the theoretical construct from cases where it is exploiting correlates.
AI.conclusion: By focusing on the internal decomposition of constructs and explicit theory-based aggregation rules, grain calibration shifts validation away from simple output agreement with human annotators toward demonstrating that an instrument is genuinely grounded in the intended construct. This approach strengthens construct validity for LLM-based coding tools and provides richer diagnostic information about when and how they succeed or fail.

title: Aristotelian Virtue Profiling of LLMs through Ethical Dilemmas
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) often face ethical tradeoffs in which several responses may be defensible but express different priorities, such as fairness, honesty, courage, or restraint. We introduce VirtueMap, a framework for describing these patterns through an Aristotelian virtue-ethics lens. Instead of asking for a single correct answer, VirtueMap asks humans or LLMs to rank all five responses to each of seven general, non-lethal, non-political, and non-religious ethical dilemmas. To define the reference orderings used for scoring, we first proposed, for each dilemma and virtue, an ordering of the five responses from most to least expressive of that virtue. We then collected more than 100 respondent evaluations per ordering and retained it as operational ground truth only when at least 95% confirmed it. Rankings are scored against these retained orderings using normalized Borda alignment, yielding profiles over Practical Wisdom, Justice, Truthfulness, Courage, and Temperance. We apply VirtueMap to nine LLM families in a repeated-run evaluation and find high mean rank consistency (90.3%), with the largest differences appearing on Courage, Temperance, and Justice. We also release an interactive website that computes profiles locally in the browser and compares respondents with measured LLM profiles.

AI.tldr: VirtueMap is a virtue-ethics-based framework to evaluate how LLMs prioritize ethical virtues (like justice or courage) by ranking multiple defensible responses to dilemmas, generating a virtue profile for models and humans.
AI.motivation: LLMs often encounter ethical situations with multiple reasonable responses that emphasize different virtues rather than a single clearly right answer. Existing evaluation methods typically look for one correct output or focus on safety vs. harm, missing the nuanced tradeoffs between virtues such as fairness, honesty, or restraint. The authors aim to systematically characterize these differing priorities in LLM behavior, and to do so in a way that is grounded in a well-established ethical theory (Aristotelian virtue ethics) while remaining non-political, non-religious, and non-lethal in content.
AI.method: The authors design seven general ethical dilemmas that are explicitly non-lethal, non-political, and non-religious. For each dilemma, they create five plausible responses. For each combination of dilemma and virtue (Practical Wisdom, Justice, Truthfulness, Courage, Temperance), they hypothesize an ordering of the five responses from most to least expressive of that virtue. They then empirically validate these orderings using human judgments: each proposed ordering is evaluated by more than 100 respondents, and an ordering is kept as ground truth only if at least 95% of respondents agree with it. This yields a set of highly-agreed reference orderings. Human participants or LLMs are then asked to rank the same five responses for each dilemma. Their rankings are compared with the validated reference orderings using normalized Borda alignment, producing a numerical alignment score for each virtue. Repeated-run evaluations on multiple LLM families are conducted to assess consistency and differences across virtues. An interactive website is developed to compute and visualize virtue profiles locally in users’ browsers, allowing comparison between human respondents and LLMs.
AI.result: The validated VirtueMap framework produces stable reference orderings for the dilemmas and virtues, with very high human agreement (≥95%) on the retained orderings. When applied to nine different LLM families, the method yields a high mean rank consistency of about 90.3% across repeated runs, indicating that model behavior under this evaluation is relatively stable. However, notable variation appears in certain virtues—especially Courage, Temperance, and Justice—where models differ more in how they rank responses, revealing distinct virtue-priority profiles across LLM families.
AI.conclusion: VirtueMap offers a practical, empirically grounded way to characterize LLM behavior in terms of Aristotelian virtues rather than single correct answers or simple safety metrics. By mapping how models rank defensible responses to ethical dilemmas and comparing them to human-validated virtue orderings, the framework exposes nuanced differences in models’ ethical prioritization, particularly around Courage, Temperance, and Justice. The high consistency across runs suggests it can serve as a reliable diagnostic tool. The public interactive website extends this utility by letting users compute and compare virtue profiles for both humans and LLMs, supporting broader analysis and discussion of value alignment in language models.

title: Phonological Perception of Sign Language Models
summary: Sign languages are compositional systems where meaning arises by combining sublexical phonological parameters, such as handshape, location, and movement. While deep learning models for Sign Language Recognition (SLR) have achieved increased performance on translation benchmarks, it remains unclear whether these models distinguish abstract phonological features or merely rely on low-level statistical correlations. This work evaluates the phonological perception of SLR models trained on American Sign Language (ASL) by probing phonological sensitivity using minimal pairs and evaluating representational alignment with human behavioral data. Our results reveal that SLR models exhibit emergent phonological sensitivity, but with clear architectural trade-offs: pose-based models are sensitive to handshape contrasts, while pixel-based models better capture location changes. Furthermore, pose-based models learn latent representations that correlate with human perceptual similarity judgments (r~0.49). These findings suggest that while SLR models exhibit emergent phonology, current training paradigms are insufficient to scale them beyond their architectural inductive biases.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates whether deep learning models for American Sign Language recognition develop sensitivity to sublexical phonological features, finding that they do show emergent phonological awareness with different strengths depending on architecture.
AI.motivation: Although SLR models perform well on translation tasks, it is unknown if they truly capture the abstract phonological structure of sign languages or just exploit surface-level statistical patterns. Understanding this is key for building linguistically grounded and generalizable SLR systems.
AI.method: Train different SLR architectures (pose-based and pixel-based) on ASL, then probe their internal representations using phonological minimal pairs and compare model similarity judgments with human behavioral perceptual similarity data to assess phonological sensitivity and alignment with human perception.
AI.result: Both types of SLR models show emergent sensitivity to phonological contrasts, but with distinct biases: pose-based models better capture handshape contrasts, while pixel-based models better capture location contrasts. Pose-based model representations correlate moderately with human perceptual similarity judgments (correlation around 0.49).
AI.conclusion: Current SLR models do develop some level of phonological representation, but their phonological sensitivity is constrained by architectural inductive biases, indicating that standard training regimes are not sufficient for learning fully abstract, human-like phonological structure in sign language; more linguistically informed approaches are needed.

title: An AI agent for treatment reasoning over a biomedical tool universe
summary: Treatment reasoning underpins every therapeutic decision, integrating disease context, comorbidities, medications, contraindications, and evolving biomedical knowledge to select an appropriate therapy. It is inherently iterative: candidates are weighed against many constraints, revised as evidence emerges, and grounded in verifiable sources. Here we introduce ATHENA-R1, an AI agent for treatment reasoning across all FDA approved drugs since 1939, trained by reinforcement learning over a universe of 212 biomedical tools. At each step it identifies missing information, selects and runs relevant tools, and incorporates the evidence. To train it without human-annotated traces, we build a two-level self-learning framework: multi-agent systems construct the tools, tasks, and reasoning trajectories for supervised fine-tuning, then reinforcement learning with scientific feedback rewards reasoning quality (evidence gathering, grounded tool use, logical non-redundancy). Across five benchmarks of 3,168 drug reasoning tasks and 456 patient treatment cases, ATHENA-R1 outperforms language models and tool-use systems, reaching 94.7% accuracy on open-ended drug reasoning and 82.9% on treatment reasoning, 17.8 and 10.7 points above GPT-5. In blinded evaluations by experts from 28 rare disease organizations, it is preferred over reference models on all criteria, and physicians rated it favorably on complex hospitalized cardiovascular and infectious-disease cases. Adverse-event hypotheses it generated, tested in electronic health records from 5.4 million patients, reached adjusted odds ratios of 1.48-1.84, with no elevation among negative controls. Because it requires knowing what evidence to seek before concluding, treatment reasoning has long been hard for AI; we show it can be reframed as a learnable process of iterative evidence gathering that reinforcement learning can train AI to perform.

AI.tldr: ATHENA-R1 is a reinforcement-learned AI agent that performs iterative, tool-based treatment reasoning over all FDA-approved drugs and outperforms strong language models and tool systems on drug and patient-treatment benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Therapeutic decision-making is complex and iterative, requiring integration of disease context, comorbidities, medications, contraindications, and up-to-date biomedical evidence. Existing AI systems struggle because they often generate answers without systematically identifying what evidence is missing, which tools or data sources to query, and how to ground conclusions in verifiable sources. The authors aim to build an AI that can mimic expert-like treatment reasoning as a stepwise, evidence-seeking process across the large space of FDA-approved drugs.
AI.method: The authors design ATHENA-R1, an AI treatment-reasoning agent operating over a universe of 212 biomedical tools (e.g., drug databases, guidelines, literature search). At each reasoning step, the agent identifies missing information, chooses and calls relevant tools, and integrates their outputs into its evolving plan. To avoid human-annotated traces, they propose a two-level self-learning framework: (1) multi-agent systems automatically construct the tools, generate tasks, and synthesize reasoning trajectories for supervised fine-tuning; (2) reinforcement learning with scientific feedback then optimizes the agent for high-quality reasoning, rewarding effective evidence gathering, grounded tool use, and logically non-redundant steps.
AI.result: On five benchmarks comprising 3,168 drug reasoning tasks and 456 patient treatment cases, ATHENA-R1 substantially outperforms strong language models and existing tool-use systems. It achieves 94.7% accuracy on open-ended drug reasoning and 82.9% on treatment reasoning—improvements of 17.8 and 10.7 percentage points over GPT-5, respectively. In blinded expert evaluations from 28 rare disease organizations, ATHENA-R1 is preferred over reference models across all assessed criteria. Physicians also rate it favorably on complex hospitalized cardiovascular and infectious disease cases. Furthermore, adverse-event hypotheses generated by ATHENA-R1, when tested in electronic health records from 5.4 million patients, show significant associations (adjusted odds ratios 1.48–1.84) without spurious elevation in negative controls, suggesting clinically meaningful signal.
AI.conclusion: Treatment reasoning can be framed as a learnable process of iterative evidence gathering and tool use, rather than a one-shot prediction task. By coupling a large tool ecosystem with self-supervised trajectory generation and reinforcement learning guided by scientific feedback, ATHENA-R1 learns to identify missing information, query appropriate biomedical resources, and synthesize grounded therapeutic decisions. Its superior performance on benchmarks, expert preference studies, real-world physician assessments, and validation of generated adverse-event hypotheses indicates that this approach is a promising path toward robust, clinically useful AI for treatment decision support.

title: AnTenA: Actionable and Explainable Tensor Analysis System with Large Language Models
summary:
Accurately explaining hidden patterns in multi-aspect data has typically been done by leveraging labels and/or accompanying auxiliary metadata. However, labels and auxiliary data may be inaccurate (e.g. nonstandard, inconsistent), insufficient (e.g. static tabular metadata for time-dependent recordings), or unavailable. %
We propose \fullmethod (\method), which leverages the knowledge of large language models (LLMs) to explain the hidden patterns in human narratives. \method uses task-agnostic and task-specific prompts to explain extracted co-clustered latent patterns from tensor decomposition. To evaluate these explanations, we test the LLMs on forward and backward inference tasks. % Our demo system is available at https://github.com/dawonahn/ECML_PKDD_AnTenA.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces AnTenA, a method that uses large language models to explain latent patterns discovered via tensor decomposition in multi-aspect narrative data, without relying on labels or metadata.
AI.motivation: Existing approaches to explaining hidden patterns in multi-aspect data typically rely on labels or auxiliary metadata, which can be noisy, inconsistent, static, or entirely missing. This limitation is particularly problematic for complex, time-dependent human narrative data, where rich semantics are present but not fully captured by standard metadata. The authors aim to create a way to generate meaningful explanations of discovered latent patterns without depending on such unreliable or unavailable supervision.
AI.method: The authors propose AnTenA (shortened as the method), which first applies tensor decomposition to multi-aspect human narrative data to extract co-clustered latent patterns. Then, instead of using labels or metadata, they query large language models with a combination of task-agnostic and task-specific prompts to obtain natural-language explanations for these patterns. They further design evaluation protocols based on forward and backward inference tasks, where LLMs must either predict implications of a given explanation or infer explanations from given patterns, to assess the quality of the generated explanations.
AI.result: The method successfully leverages LLMs to produce explanations for latent patterns derived from tensor decomposition, even in the absence of traditional labels or rich auxiliary data. Through forward and backward inference tasks, the authors demonstrate that LLM-generated explanations are coherent and informative enough for LLMs themselves to reason over, indicating that the explanations capture salient aspects of the underlying patterns. (Exact quantitative results are not specified in the abstract.)
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that AnTenA shows the feasibility and promise of using LLMs as explanation engines for unsupervised pattern discovery in multi-aspect narrative data. By combining tensor decomposition with carefully designed prompting strategies and inference-based evaluation, the approach mitigates the reliance on potentially flawed labels and metadata and opens up new directions for interpretable analysis of complex, high-dimensional human narratives.

title: COMPASS: Grounding Composition-Intent Guidance in Unified Multimodal Models
summary: Composition is a high-level visual intent that governs where subjects are placed and how a scene is organized, yet current unified multimodal models remain unreliable at fine-grained composition recognition and struggle to turn such intent into controllable generation. We present COMPASS, the first unified multimodal framework that grounds composition-intent control in a single system spanning both composition perception and composition-guided generation, with a shared expert token $τ_c$ as the central intent anchor. On the perception side, COMPASS injects composition expertise into an MoE backbone in a minimally invasive manner and distills the inferred intent into $τ_c$. On the generation side, COMPASS reuses $τ_c$ as a global conditioning signal that steers the denoising trajectory, effectively converting passive composition analysis into explicit layout control. To support systematic instruction-following composition learning and evaluation at scale, we construct Comp-11, a large-scale dataset with an 11-class taxonomy and reasoning-augmented annotations. Extensive experiments show that COMPASS substantially improves category-level composition understanding and delivers more composition-consistent, prompt-faithful generation than strong baselines.

AI.tldr: Introduces COMPASS, a unified model that both understands and controls image composition using a shared expert token and a new dataset.
AI.motivation: Current multimodal models are poor at recognizing fine-grained visual composition and at turning high-level compositional intent (where subjects go, how a scene is organized) into controllable image generation, so a dedicated, unified framework is needed.
AI.method: Propose COMPASS, a unified perception–generation framework with a shared composition expert token τ_c. On the perception side, composition knowledge is injected into a mixture-of-experts (MoE) backbone, and the inferred compositional intent is distilled into τ_c. On the generation side, τ_c is reused as a global conditioning signal to steer the diffusion denoising trajectory for layout control. They also build Comp-11, a large-scale dataset with an 11-class composition taxonomy and reasoning-augmented labels for instruction-following training and evaluation.
AI.result: Experiments show that COMPASS significantly improves recognition of composition categories and enables image generation that better matches the desired composition and text prompts compared to strong baselines.
AI.conclusion: Grounding a shared expert composition token across perception and generation enables more accurate understanding of visual composition and more controllable composition-guided image synthesis, supported by the new Comp-11 dataset.

title: SEATauBench: Adapting Tool-Agent-User Evaluation Into Low-Resource Southeast Asian Languages
summary: While AI development and evaluation for Southeast Asia (SEA) has grown rapidly, agent capabilities in regional languages are still poorly understood despite its importance to sovereign AI. To fill this gap, we introduce SEATauBench, the first agent-focused evaluation framework for SEA sovereign AI. SeaTau adapts TauBench to five languages -- Mandarin, Vietnamese, Thai, Indonesian, and Filipino -- and evaluates agents across progressively localized settings that vary the language of user-agent interaction, tool specifications, and task domains. Across three recent models, we find that English agent capabilities transfer reasonably well when only the conversation language changes, but quality and robustness degrade sharply as more task contexts are localized, with the largest losses in full domain adaptation. We also the limits of English-only agent assessment for measuring agent capabilities in SEA languages. More broadly, SeaTau provides a diagnostic benchmark and reusable adaptation pipeline for building reliable multilingual agents for linguistically diverse regions. Data and code can be accessed at github.com/SEACrowd/SEATauBench.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SEATauBench, the first benchmark to systematically evaluate AI agents in Southeast Asian languages by adapting an existing agent benchmark (TauBench) to five regional languages and progressively localized task settings.
AI.motivation: Although AI for Southeast Asia is growing, we lack a clear understanding of how well AI agents actually work in key regional languages, which is crucial for developing sovereign, locally reliable AI systems. Existing evaluations mainly focus on English and do not stress-test agents in localized tools and domains. The paper aims to close this gap.
AI.method: The authors adapt the TauBench agent evaluation framework to create SEATauBench in five languages: Mandarin, Vietnamese, Thai, Indonesian, and Filipino. They construct progressively localized scenarios that vary three factors: the language of user–agent interaction, the language of tool descriptions/specifications, and the localization of task domains. They then test three recent AI models as agents on these settings to measure performance, robustness, and transfer from English-centric training and evaluation.
AI.result: The experiments show that when only the conversation language changes (with tools and domains still mostly English-based), English-trained/assessed agent capabilities transfer reasonably well. However, as the evaluation is more fully localized—changing tool specifications and domain contexts into SEA languages—agent quality and robustness drop sharply, with the largest performance losses observed under full domain adaptation. This highlights substantial gaps in current models’ real-world utility in SEA-language settings.
AI.conclusion: English-only agent assessments are insufficient proxies for true multilingual agent capabilities, especially in Southeast Asian languages and localized task environments. SEATauBench serves both as a diagnostic benchmark and a reusable adaptation pipeline to help researchers and practitioners build and evaluate more reliable, multilingual AI agents tailored to linguistically diverse regions like Southeast Asia.

title: BV-Blend: Uncertainty-Weighted Historical Baselines for Stable Critic-Free RL with Verifiable Rewards
summary: Critic-free reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR), exemplified by Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), avoids training a value function (critic) and reduces memory and compute overhead relative to critic-based PPO pipelines for aligning large language models. However, GRPO-style advantage estimation depends on prompt-local (within-prompt-group) reward statistics and can be unstable. In particular, when all rollouts in a prompt group receive identical rewards, the within-group reward variance becomes zero, and group normalization yields zero advantages for that group, impeding learning in cold-start regimes with binary verifiers. We introduce BV-Blend, a critic-free framework that stabilizes advantage estimation by combining prompt-local on-policy statistics with semantic-cluster-conditioned historical moments. BV-Blend maintains EMA-tracked reward moments for each cluster, derives a confidence weight from a standard error of the mean (SEM) proxy, and uses this weight to blend historical and prompt-local baseline and variance statistics into a standardized advantage for PPO-style clipped updates.
