Experiments on verifiable reasoning benchmarks show that BV-Blend improves training stability and performance, and remains robust in regimes where group-normalized methods may stall.

AI.tldr: They propose BV-Blend, a critic-free RL method that stabilizes advantage estimation for aligning large language models by blending prompt-local and historical reward statistics.
AI.motivation: Existing critic-free RL methods like GRPO avoid training a critic and are more efficient, but their reliance on prompt-local reward statistics makes them unstable, especially when all rollouts in a group get identical rewards, leading to zero advantages and stalled learning.
AI.method: Introduce BV-Blend, which clusters prompts semantically and maintains exponentially moving average (EMA) reward moments per cluster. It computes a confidence weight based on a standard error of the mean (SEM) proxy and uses this to blend cluster-level historical and prompt-local baseline and variance statistics to form standardized advantages used in PPO-style clipped policy updates, all without training a critic.
AI.result: On verifiable reasoning benchmarks, BV-Blend yields more stable training dynamics and better performance than GRPO-style group-normalized methods, especially in challenging regimes such as cold-start with binary verifiers where traditional group-normalized advantage estimation can stall.
AI.conclusion: BV-Blend offers a more robust critic-free RL framework for LLM alignment by using cluster-conditioned historical reward statistics to stabilize advantage estimation, improving both stability and performance over existing group-normalized methods while preserving efficiency benefits of critic-free approaches.

title: DriftGuard: Safety-Aware Multi-Monitor Detection and Selective Adaptation for Evolving Toxicity Moderation
summary: Automated toxicity moderation systems operate in dynamic online environments where harmful behavior evolves through coded language, shifting targets, and strategic adaptation to enforcement. Existing drift detection methods often focus on global distributional change, but such signals may miss safety-relevant shifts that emerge in localized harm subspaces or high-risk model-error regions. This paper introduces DriftGuard, a safety-aware adaptive moderation framework that combines multi-monitor drift detection with selective model updating. The framework tracks global text drift, identity-harm drift, model uncertainty, toxic-risk drift, and false-negative-risk drift. When safety-relevant change is detected, the model is updated using a hard-mix adaptation set that prioritizes likely false negatives, identity-related high-risk examples, false-positive-risk examples, and uncertain boundary cases. Experiments on Civil Comments temporal shift and Jigsaw-to-DynaHate cross-dataset shift show that safety-aware monitors detect risks missed by global drift alone. Hard-mix adaptation improves toxic recall and accuracy over no-update and random-balanced baselines, raising toxic recall to 0.8777 on Civil Comments and from 0.7107 to 0.8523 on DynaHate. Bootstrap analysis further shows stable DynaHate safety gains, with toxic recall increasing by 0.1418 and false-negative prevalence decreasing by 0.0781. Overall, DriftGuard links safety-aware drift detection to targeted, lightweight model updating for more robust adaptive toxicity moderation.

AI.tldr: DriftGuard is a safety-aware adaptive toxicity moderation framework that detects nuanced, safety-relevant distribution shifts and selectively updates models to maintain high toxic recall and reduce false negatives over time.
AI.motivation: Online toxicity evolves rapidly through new slang, coded language, changing targets, and adversarial adaptation, making static moderation models brittle over time. Standard drift detection focuses on global distributional changes, which can miss subtle but safety-critical shifts localized in specific harm types or error-prone regions of the model. There is a need for moderation systems that can specifically track safety-relevant drift and update models in a targeted, efficient way to sustain robust performance against evolving harmful content.
AI.method: The paper proposes DriftGuard, a framework that monitors multiple safety-related drift signals—global text drift, identity-harm drift, model uncertainty, toxic-risk drift, and false-negative-risk drift. Upon detecting safety-relevant change, DriftGuard performs selective model updating using a "hard-mix" adaptation set. This set is curated to emphasize likely false negatives, identity-related high-risk samples, examples at risk of false positives, and uncertain boundary cases. The approach is evaluated under temporal shift (within Civil Comments over time) and cross-dataset shift (from Jigsaw to DynaHate).
AI.result: In experiments, safety-aware monitors identify risk patterns that are not captured by traditional global drift monitoring alone. The hard-mix adaptation strategy improves toxic recall and overall accuracy compared with no updating and random balanced updating. Quantitatively, toxic recall reaches 0.8777 on Civil Comments and increases from 0.7107 to 0.8523 on DynaHate. Bootstrap analysis on DynaHate demonstrates that these gains are stable, with an average toxic recall increase of 0.1418 and a reduction in false-negative prevalence by 0.0781.
AI.conclusion: DriftGuard effectively connects multi-faceted, safety-aware drift detection with focused, lightweight model updates, leading to more robust toxicity moderation under distribution shift. By emphasizing high-risk and error-prone regions during adaptation, the framework sustains and improves toxic recall while reducing false negatives, addressing the limitations of global drift-only approaches in dynamic online environments.

title: The Two Genie Game: Adoption and Welfare in Audit-Grounded AI Governance
comment: 36 pages, 3 figures. Lean 4 formalization and figure scripts: https://github.com/dlewissandy/two-genie-scripts
summary:
We ask under what conditions an agent with a harm-minimizing policy can displace an approval-seeking (RLHF) agent in a competitive market, and when that policy is sufficient to prevent community harm. We use evolutionary game theory (finite-population Moran-Fermi pairwise comparison) to formalize this subject to assumptions of wisher hindsight, peer testimony, a monotone harm ledger, sufficient information density of community feedback, and a finite, depleting resource pool, in a negative-sum environment.
  We show that adoption is favored when the prior distributions on how readily wishers attune to community sentiment are monotone, exhibit endpoint inversion, and have a centro-symmetric pairing property, and demonstrate this with several long-tailed priors (Hill, Pareto, Lomax, Frechet). Where it is favored, a critical adoption level separates communities that drift back to the approval-seeking agent from those for which the audited agent fixes; above that level fixation is the overwhelmingly likely outcome. We derive when fixation is attainable as a bound on the effective (informational) size N_c of the community, which must be small enough to allow fixation before depletion. We present these as Theorems 5.4 and 5.5; the algebraic and finite-grid backbone is machine-checked in Lean 4, with the barrier-crossing asymptotics retained as explicit hypotheses.
  We show that a self-audited agent with a community ledger is not, in general, sufficient to prevent community harm. Sufficiency depends both upon the alignment of the agent's audit with community values and the timeframe over which harm is evaluated. Regardless of alignment, once adoption reaches dominance, the state is absorbing. The same policy that reduced harm under alignment becomes a trap, welfare-negative under misalignment and, even under alignment, one that locks in harm deferred past the adoption horizon.

AI.tldr: Analyzes when a harm-minimizing AI policy can outcompete an approval-seeking (RLHF-style) agent, and why such a policy is not always sufficient to prevent community harm.
AI.motivation: Approval-seeking RLHF agents may cause harm by optimizing for human approval rather than true welfare, especially in competitive, resource-limited settings. The authors want to know under what realistic market and community-feedback conditions a harm-minimizing agent can spread and dominate, and whether that dominance actually guarantees reduced harm for the community over time.
AI.method: They build an evolutionary game-theoretic model using a finite-population Moran-Fermi pairwise comparison process to study competition between a harm-minimizing agent and an approval-seeking RLHF agent. The setting includes a negative-sum, finite-resource environment, a monotone harm ledger, wisher hindsight, peer testimony, and dense community feedback. They analytically characterize properties of prior distributions governing how agents update to community sentiment (monotonicity, endpoint inversion, centro-symmetry), and instantiate these with heavy-tailed distributions (Hill, Pareto, Lomax, Fréchet). They then derive critical adoption thresholds and community-size bounds for fixation, and machine-check core algebraic arguments in Lean 4, leaving some asymptotic assumptions as explicit hypotheses.
AI.result: They identify structural conditions on the prior distributions of agents’ responsiveness to community sentiment under which the harm-minimizing agent is more likely to be adopted. Under these conditions, there exists a critical adoption fraction: below it, the system tends to drift back to dominance of the approval-seeking agent; above it, the harm-minimizing agent fixates with overwhelming probability. They derive bounds on the effective informational community size N_c required so that fixation can occur before resources are depleted. They also show that even a self-audited agent with a community harm ledger does not in general prevent community harm: sufficiency depends on the match between the audit metric and community values and on the horizon over which harm is aggregated.
AI.conclusion: Harm-minimizing policies can outcompete approval-seeking RLHF agents only under specific structural conditions on learning priors and community feedback, and only if the effective community size is small enough relative to resource depletion timescales. Even when such a policy fixates, it is not guaranteed to prevent harm: misalignment between the audit metric and community values or a too-short harm evaluation horizon can make the adopted policy welfare-negative or lock in deferred harms once dominance is reached, creating an absorbing but potentially harmful state.

title: 5ting at SemEval-2026 Task 8: Strong End-to-End Multi-Turn RAG via LLM-Based Reranking and Faithfulness Control
summary: We introduce 5ting, our system for the SemEval2026 Task 8 (MTRAGEval), which evaluates multi-turn Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) systems. Multi turn RAG involves context drift, under specification, and hallucination risk. Our system combines BGE-M3 dense retrieval with FAISS indexing, dual-query merged retrieval, and LLM based reranking, followed by role separated generation constrained to retrieved evidence. The retriever achieved nDCG@5 = 0.4719 in Task A, while the end to end system ranked in Task C with a harmonic score of 0.5597 and RL_F = 0.7692.

AI.tldr: 5ting is a system for evaluating multi-turn Retrieval Augmented Generation that combines strong dense retrieval, dual-query retrieval, and LLM reranking with evidence-constrained generation, achieving solid results on the SemEval2026 MTRAGEval tasks.
AI.motivation: Multi-turn RAG systems are prone to context drift, under-specified queries, and hallucinations, which make it challenging to retrieve and use the right information across multiple conversational turns. The paper aims to build an evaluation system that can robustly retrieve relevant context and constrain generation to reliable evidence in this multi-turn setting, and to participate in the SemEval2026 MTRAGEval shared task as a benchmark.
AI.method: The system uses the BGE-M3 dense retriever with FAISS for efficient indexing and search. It performs dual-query merged retrieval (likely generating or using two complementary queries whose retrieved results are combined) and then applies an LLM-based reranker to reorder the retrieved passages. For generation, it separates conversational roles and constrains the language model to use only the retrieved evidence when producing answers, in order to mitigate hallucinations and drift. The performance is evaluated on the subtasks of the SemEval2026 MTRAGEval shared task.
AI.result: On Task A (retrieval evaluation), their retriever achieves nDCG@5 of 0.4719, indicating reasonably strong top-5 ranking quality. On Task C (end-to-end multi-turn RAG), the system obtains a harmonic score of 0.5597 and an RL_F (ROUGE-L F) of 0.7692, placing it in the official rankings of the shared task.
AI.conclusion: The proposed 5ting system, which integrates advanced dense retrieval (BGE-M3 + FAISS), dual-query retrieval, LLM-based reranking, and evidence-constrained generation, performs competitively on the SemEval2026 MTRAGEval multi-turn RAG benchmarks. The results suggest that combining strong retrieval with careful reranking and strict evidence grounding is effective for handling context drift, under-specification, and hallucination in multi-turn RAG.

title: TrajRS: Towards Certified Robustness in Pedestrian Trajectory Prediction
comment: Accepted by 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing
summary: The robustness of trajectory prediction models is crucial for developing safe autonomous driving systems. Adversarial attacks on trajectory prediction can significantly impair the accuracy of predicted trajectories, leading to hazardous driving behaviors. While heuristic defense strategies have been implemented to enhance the robustness of trajectory prediction models, these measures often fail against more sophisticated, targeted adversarial attacks. Hence, there is a pressing need to establish verifiable safety assurances for trajectory prediction models. In this paper, we extend the traditional Randomized Smoothing framework to "TrajRS", which provides a certified robust radius for smoothed trajectory predictors. We clarify and expand the formal definitions of robustness in trajectory prediction and tailor the practical TrajRS scheme specifically to "robustness for the optimal prediction" and "robustness for all possible predictions". An extensive set of experiments demonstrates that TrajRS effectively achieves robustness certification for all smoothed pedestrian trajectory predictors in this work.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces TrajRS, a randomized smoothing-based framework that certifies robustness radii for trajectory prediction models in autonomous driving.
AI.motivation: Trajectory prediction models are critical for safe autonomous driving, but are vulnerable to adversarial attacks that can cause dangerous behaviors. Existing heuristic defenses are insufficient against strong, targeted attacks, so there is a need for formally verifiable robustness guarantees.
AI.method: The authors extend the Randomized Smoothing framework to trajectory prediction, proposing TrajRS. They refine formal robustness definitions for trajectory prediction and design a practical smoothing and certification scheme aligned with two notions: robustness of the optimal prediction and robustness of all possible predictions produced by the model.
AI.result: Experiments on pedestrian trajectory predictors show that TrajRS can successfully certify nontrivial robustness radii for all considered smoothed models, indicating resistance to adversarial perturbations within certified bounds.
AI.conclusion: TrajRS provides a principled way to obtain certified robustness guarantees for trajectory prediction models, going beyond heuristic defenses and enabling verifiable safety assurances for autonomous driving trajectory predictors.

title: Majority Vote Silences Minority Values: Annotator Disagreement at the Hate/Offensive Boundary in HateXplain
summary: Hate speech annotation pipelines routinely collapse annotator disagreement into majority vote labels before training. We show that this aggregation is not neutral: 42.6% of all annotator disagreement in HateXplain concentrates specifically at the hate/offensive boundary, a pattern consistent with annotators applying different thresholds for where hate begins (chi-squared = 135.199, df = 2, p < 0.0001). Both a hard-label BERT model (Model A) and a soft-label model (Model B) drop 22 percentage points in accuracy from agreed posts (~80%) to disagreement posts (~58%), confirmed at p < 0.0001. A per-annotator multi-head model (Model C) widens this gap further to 28 points while collapsing offensive disagreement accuracy to 0.245. Critically, Model A expresses significantly higher confidence on boundary case errors than Model C (0.710 vs. 0.495, p < 0.0001), meaning standard evaluation metrics will not detect the failure. Three downstream interventions of increasing sophistication all fail to recover boundary accuracy. We argue the problem is structural. Majority vote presents a contested judgment as ground truth, and models inherit that false certainty. The intervention must be upstream in annotation design.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how collapsing annotator disagreement into majority-vote labels in hate speech datasets is not neutral and structurally harms model performance, especially at the boundary between hate and merely offensive speech.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether treating majority vote as ground truth in hate speech annotation pipelines hides systematic disagreement among annotators and causes models to learn an overconfident but misleading boundary between hate and offensive content.
AI.method: The authors analyze the HateXplain dataset to quantify where annotator disagreements occur, use statistical tests (chi-squared) to show concentration at the hate/offensive boundary, and train several models: a standard hard-label BERT (Model A), a soft-label model (Model B), and a per-annotator multi-head model (Model C). They compare accuracy on posts with full annotator agreement vs disagreement and measure model confidence on boundary errors. They also test three downstream interventions aimed at improving boundary accuracy.
AI.result: 42.6% of annotator disagreement occurs specifically at the hate/offensive boundary, indicating differing thresholds for what counts as hate. All three models show large accuracy drops from agreed to disagreement posts: about 22 percentage points for Models A and B and 28 points for Model C, which performs particularly poorly on offensive disagreement. Model A is more overconfident on boundary case errors than Model C, making standard metrics unable to expose this failure. None of the three tested downstream interventions meaningfully recover accuracy on boundary cases.
AI.conclusion: Majority-vote aggregation in hate speech datasets is not a neutral operation; it converts contested, subjective judgments into misleading "ground truth" labels, causing models to learn falsely sharp, overconfident boundaries between hate and offensive content. Because the issue is structural and rooted in annotation design, it cannot be fixed by downstream modeling tricks alone; instead, annotation practices must be redesigned to represent disagreement rather than collapse it.

title: ComMem: Complementary Memory Systems for Test-Time Adaptation of Vision-Language Models
comment: A brain-inspired complementary memory framework leveraging fast visual caching and slow textual refinement for VLM test-time adaptation
summary: Test-time adaptation (TTA) of vision-language models (VLMs) is essential for their robust deployment in dynamic, real-world environments. However, existing TTA methods often adapt locally without accumulating knowledge over time, or operating within a single modality without exploiting VLMs' inherently multi-modal nature. Inspired by the \textbf{Com}plementary \textbf{Mem}ory systems of the biological brain, we propose \textbf{ComMem}, an innovative approach that mimics the distinct but cooperative roles of the hippocampus and neocortex to enable effective TTA for VLMs. ComMem consists of two key components: a fast-adapting detailed memory, akin to the hippocampus, that forms a dynamic visual cache from high-confidence test samples; and a slow-integrating abstract memory, akin to the neocortex, that continually refines global textual prototypes. For each test instance, ComMem jointly optimizes both memory systems to ensure cross-modal consistency. Extensive experiments on 15 benchmark datasets show that ComMem significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods under both natural distribution shifts and cross-dataset generalization, offering a promising direction for enhancing VLMs' practical adaptability.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes ComMem, a biologically inspired test-time adaptation method for vision-language models that maintains two complementary memories to improve robustness under distribution shifts.
AI.motivation: Existing test-time adaptation methods for vision-language models either adapt only locally without accumulating knowledge or work in a single modality, failing to fully exploit the models’ multi-modal nature. There is a need for a principled way to continually adapt VLMs in dynamic environments while leveraging cross-modal signals.
AI.method: ComMem is inspired by complementary memory systems in the brain. It has: (1) a fast, detailed memory like the hippocampus that builds a dynamic visual cache from high-confidence test samples, and (2) a slow, abstract memory like the neocortex that continually updates global textual prototypes. For each incoming test instance, ComMem jointly optimizes both memories to maintain cross-modal (visual-text) consistency during adaptation.
AI.result: On 15 benchmark datasets, ComMem yields significant performance gains over state-of-the-art test-time adaptation methods for VLMs, under both natural distribution shifts and cross-dataset generalization scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Biologically inspired complementary memory mechanisms can substantially enhance the practical adaptability of vision-language models. ComMem’s dual-memory design provides a promising path toward robust test-time adaptation that accumulates and transfers knowledge over time across modalities.

title: Structure-Preserving Document Translation via Multi-Stage LLM Pipeline: A Case Study in Marathi
summary: Government documents in India are predominantly issued in regional languages such as Marathi, creating substantial accessibility barriers for non-native readers, interstate administrative bodies, and policy analysts. Although recent advances in neural machine translation have improved sentence-level translation quality, existing systems largely neglect document structure, formatting integrity, and domain-specific terminology, thereby limiting their applicability to official documentation. This paper presents a structure-preserving Marathi-to-English government document translation framework capable of performing end-to-end document transformation while maintaining layout fidelity. The proposed system integrates layout-aware optical character recognition, coordinate-based text extraction, large language model based translation, and structured document reconstruction through HTML representations. By enforcing spatial alignment constraints and preserving hierarchical document elements, the framework ensures structural consistency between the source and translated documents. Experimental evaluation on real-world Marathi government PDFs demonstrates improved structural preservation, translation coherence, and terminological consistency compared to conventional text-only translation pipelines. The proposed framework contributes toward scalable multilingual accessibility solutions for e-governance and administrative document processing.

AI.tldr: A framework for translating Marathi government PDFs to English while preserving layout and structure.
AI.motivation: Government documents in India are often in regional languages, which makes them hard to access for non-native speakers, interstate bodies, and policy analysts. Existing neural machine translation methods translate sentences but ignore document layout, structure, and specialized terminology, reducing usability for official documents.
AI.method: The paper proposes a structure-preserving Marathi-to-English translation pipeline that operates on full documents. It combines layout-aware OCR, coordinate-based text extraction, large language model–based translation, and reconstruction of the translated document via HTML. Spatial alignment constraints and preservation of hierarchical document elements are used to maintain structural consistency between source and translated documents.
AI.result: On real Marathi government PDFs, the framework yields better structural preservation, more coherent translations, and more consistent terminology than standard text-only translation workflows.
AI.conclusion: The framework enables scalable, multilingual access to government documents by providing layout-faithful translation suitable for e-governance and administrative processing, overcoming key limitations of conventional translation systems.

title: Agentic Abstention: Do Agents Know When to Stop Instead of Act?
summary: LLM agents are expected to act over multiple turns, using search, browsing interfaces, and terminal tools to complete user goals. Yet not every goal is well specified or achievable in the available environment. In such cases, a reliable agent should recognize that further interaction is unlikely to help and abstain from additional tool calls. We define Agentic Abstention, the problem of deciding when an agent should stop acting under uncertainty. Unlike standard LLM abstention, which is usually evaluated as a single-turn answer-or-abstain decision, agentic abstention is a sequential decision problem: an agent can answer, abstain, or gather more information at each turn, and the need to abstain may only become clear after interacting with the environment. We study this problem across web shopping, terminal environments, and question answering, evaluating 13 LLM-as-agent systems and 2 agent scaffolds on more than 28,000 tasks. Our results show that the main challenge is not only whether agents can abstain, but also when they abstain. Some agents never abstain when they should, while others do so only after many unnecessary interactions. This gap is especially large on tasks where the instruction appears feasible until the environment reveals otherwise (e.g., no valid result matches the instruction). We further find that model scale, reasoning, and agent scaffolding affect abstention in different ways, where larger or more capable models sometimes perform worse at timely abstention. Finally, we introduce CONVOLVE, a context engineering method for improving agentic abstention that distills full interaction trajectories into reusable stopping rules. On WebShop, CONVOLVE substantially improves timely abstention without updating model parameters, raising Llama-3.3-70B's timely recall rate from 26.7 to 57.4. Our dataset and code are available at https://lhannnn.github.io/agentic-abstention

AI.tldr: The paper defines and studies “agentic abstention”: when multi-step LLM agents should stop acting and decline a task instead of continuing tool use.
AI.motivation: Multi-turn LLM agents can search, browse, and use tools, but many user goals are underspecified or impossible in the current environment. Existing abstention work focuses on one-shot answer-or-abstain. There is a need to understand and improve how agents decide, over multiple steps, whether to continue acting, answer, or stop and abstain when success is unlikely.
AI.method: The authors formalize agentic abstention as a sequential decision problem where, at each step, an agent may answer, abstain, or gather more information via tools. They benchmark 13 LLM-as-agent systems and 2 agent scaffolds across more than 28,000 tasks in web shopping, terminal, and QA environments, analyzing when and how they abstain. They then propose CONVOLVE, a context-engineering method that distills full interaction trajectories into explicit stopping rules which are re-injected into prompts to guide future abstention without model fine-tuning.
AI.result: Experiments show that existing agents struggle with timely abstention: some never abstain despite impossibility, others abstain only after many wasted interactions, especially when tasks initially seem feasible but later prove impossible. Model scale, reasoning ability, and scaffolding have complex, sometimes negative, effects on abstention timing. With CONVOLVE, timely abstention improves substantially; for example, on WebShop it increases Llama-3.3-70B’s timely recall from 26.7 to 57.4 without changing model parameters.
AI.conclusion: Agentic abstention is a distinct, sequential problem that current LLM agents handle poorly. Good agents must not only be able to abstain but do so at the right time. Simple scaling or stronger reasoning does not guarantee better abstention behavior. Context-based distillation of trajectory-derived stopping rules (CONVOLVE) can significantly improve timely abstention, offering a practical way to make tool-using agents more reliable and efficient without retraining.

title: Labeling Training Data for Entity Matching Using Large Language Models
summary:
Recent large language models (LLMs) achieve strong performance on entity matching without requiring task-specific training data. However, applying these models to large sets of candidate pairs remains slow and costly. In contrast, entity matchers using traditional machine learning methods or small language models (SLMs), such as RoBERTa, offer much faster inference but require task-specific training data.
  This paper investigates whether the need to provide task-specific training data can be avoided by using knowledge-distillation workflows, in which an LLM serves as a teacher model to label training pairs that are subsequently used to train a smaller student model. We investigate knowledge distillation for entity matching along the following dimensions: pair-selection strategy, teacher model, label post-processing method, and student model. We evaluate the workflows using the Abt-Buy, Walmart-Amazon, WDC Products, DBLP-ACM, and DBLP-Scholar benchmarks, and compare the performance of student models trained with machine-labeled data to the performance of the same models trained using the benchmark training sets.
  Our experiments show that student models trained using the machine-labeled sets perform approximately on par with models trained on the benchmark training sets, with the remaining differences in both directions staying below two F1 points. Using GPT-5.2 to label the training sets for all five benchmarks costs US\$28.31 to US\$40.88, whereas manually labeling the same training sets is estimated to require 470 hours of work. At inference time, Ditto is 41.5 to 534 times faster than directly using an LLM to perform the matching tasks.
  These results indicate that current LLMs, when combined with a suitable pair-selection method, can substantially reduce or even eliminate the manual effort required to label use case-specific training data for entity matching.

AI.tldr: They use a powerful LLM to automatically label entity-matching pairs, then train a smaller, faster model on these labels, achieving similar accuracy to human-labeled training data but at far lower cost and with huge inference speed-ups.
AI.motivation: Entity matching is important but typically needs substantial task-specific labeled data, which is time-consuming and expensive to create. LLMs can perform entity matching zero-shot but are slow and costly for large-scale use. The authors want to see if LLMs can instead be used once as automatic labelers (teachers) so that smaller, cheaper models can be trained without manual labeling, preserving accuracy while cutting both human effort and inference cost.
AI.method: They design knowledge-distillation workflows where an LLM acts as a teacher that labels candidate record pairs for entity matching. They systematically vary and study four factors: (1) which record pairs to send to the LLM (pair-selection strategy), (2) which LLM to use as teacher, (3) how to post-process the LLM’s labels, and (4) which smaller model architecture to use as student (e.g., Ditto based on RoBERTa). They then train student models on these machine-labeled datasets and evaluate them on multiple standard entity-matching benchmarks, comparing against the same students trained on the official human-labeled training sets. They also measure labeling cost and inference-time speedups relative to directly using an LLM for matching.
AI.result: Across five standard benchmarks (Abt-Buy, Walmart-Amazon, WDC Products, DBLP-ACM, DBLP-Scholar), student models trained on LLM-generated labels reach nearly the same F1 as when trained on the original human-labeled data, with differences under two F1 points, sometimes better and sometimes worse. Using GPT-5.2 as the teacher to label all training sets costs about US$28–41, compared to an estimated 470 hours of human annotation effort. At inference, a distilled model (e.g., Ditto) is about 41.5× to 534× faster than using the LLM directly for entity matching.
AI.conclusion: Knowledge distillation from LLMs to smaller entity-matching models is highly effective: with an appropriate strategy for selecting training pairs and processing the LLM’s labels, one can nearly match the accuracy of human-labeled training data while eliminating most manual labeling effort. This enables practical, scalable entity matching where LLMs are used once as automatic teachers and small models are used for fast, cheap inference.

title: Agent Safety Is Action Alignment
summary: Large language models increasingly act as agents: they call tools, move money, delete records, and send messages on a user's behalf. To keep them safe, practitioners imported the chatbot-era recipe (train the model to refuse unsafe inputs) into the agentic setting, and treat the resulting capability loss as a manageable ``alignment tax.'' We argue this is a \emph{category error}. Refusal is a primitive for \emph{content safety}, where the harm is in the model's output and is therefore a learnable function of it. Agentic harm is different in kind: it lies not in any output but in the relation between the authority an action exercises and the authority the user granted, which is absent from the text the model sees. Importing content-safety methods into this regime does not trade capability for safety; it pays capability and buys negative security. We support this with three lines of evidence spanning the autonomy spectrum: defense-trained models learn surface patterns rather than intent; the same training collapses multi-step agents before any threat appears while leaving them exploitable; and even undefended frontier models exceed granted authority under ordinary use. We conclude that action safety cannot be installed in weights. It must be expressed as \emph{least privilege}, enforced \emph{outside} the model at the action boundary, and evaluated as \emph{action alignment} (a relational, deployment-conditioned property) rather than a refusal score.

AI.tldr: The paper argues that current safety approaches for large language model agents—based on training them to refuse unsafe requests—are fundamentally mismatched to the nature of agentic risk and can even reduce security, and instead proposes that safety must be enforced externally via least-privilege controls and evaluated in terms of action alignment.
AI.motivation: As large language models are increasingly used as agents that can take real-world actions (e.g., making payments, sending messages, modifying data), existing "content safety" methods—training models to refuse harmful outputs—have been naively reused to address these new risks. The authors see a conceptual confusion here: agentic harms arise not from what the model says, but from mismatches between the actions taken and the authority granted by the user. This gap motivates a rethinking of how safety is defined, implemented, and evaluated for agentic LLMs.
AI.method: The paper provides three empirical and conceptual lines of analysis, covering models with different levels of autonomy. First, it studies "defense-trained" models and shows they mostly pick up superficial refusal patterns rather than understanding user intent. Second, it examines multi-step tool-using agents and finds that safety training tends to destabilize their behavior (causing them to fail early) while still leaving them vulnerable to exploitation. Third, it evaluates undefended frontier models in realistic usage scenarios and demonstrates that they frequently exceed the authority actually granted by users. These analyses support the thesis that action safety cannot be reliably embedded in model parameters alone.
AI.result: The studies demonstrate that standard refusal-based safety training: (1) encourages pattern-matching refusals that do not track actual intent or authorization; (2) degrades the robustness and task performance of multi-step agents without preventing harmful or overreaching actions; and (3) fails to stop even advanced, untuned models from overstepping user-granted authority during real-world-like tasks. Overall, these results suggest that current practices impose a capability cost while providing illusory or even negative security benefits in the agentic context.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that treating agentic LLM safety as an extension of content safety is a category error. Safety for action-taking agents cannot be achieved purely via training the model to refuse certain prompts or behaviors, because the key variable—whether an action is within the user's granted authority—is not present in the text the model sees. Instead, the authors advocate designing systems around least privilege, where external mechanisms tightly constrain what actions an agent can take, and assessing safety in terms of "action alignment"—how well actions respect user-granted authority in a specific deployment—rather than via refusal scores or content filters.

title: The Heterogeneous Safety Impacts of Benign Multilingual Fine-Tuning
summary: Fine-tuning a large language model is a ubiquitous method for enhancing its capability on a specific downstream task. However, prior work has shown that this increase in capability comes with a cost: it can increase a model's tendency to respond to unsafe adversarial prompts, even when fine-tuning with non-adversarial data. We present the first comprehensive empirical study of this phenomenon in multilingual settings by fine-tuning Llama-3.2, Qwen3, and Gemma-3 models using benign data translated across nine languages. We find that safety outcomes are highly sensitive to both the choice of fine-tuning language and the evaluation language, with adversarial compliance rates increasing four-fold in some settings. Multilingual safety drift is decoupled from general capability metrics, and occurs heterogeneously across languages and models. Fine-tuning in non-English languages often induces smaller internal representational drifts than English, but these shifts lead models to default to either exaggerated compliance or refusal. As such, assessing fine-tuning impacts solely in English provides inadequate assurance for deployment. To facilitate further research into these cross-lingual safety blind spots, we release the Multilingual-Benign-Tune dataset and the SORRY-Bench-Multilingual evaluation suite.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how fine-tuning large language models on benign, multilingual data can unexpectedly worsen safety, especially for adversarial prompts, and releases datasets and benchmarks to analyze this effect.
AI.motivation: While fine-tuning large language models on task-specific data is standard practice, recent work shows it can unintentionally increase a model’s willingness to answer unsafe or adversarial prompts, even when the fine-tuning data itself is not adversarial. Most existing studies focus on English-only settings, leaving open how this safety degradation behaves across different languages. This gap is critical, since real-world deployments are multilingual and may harbor safety blind spots in non-English use. The paper aims to systematically investigate how fine-tuning in various languages affects safety, and whether assessing safety only in English is sufficient for deployment decisions.
AI.method: The authors conduct an empirical study on multiple large language models (Llama‑3.2, Qwen3, and Gemma‑3) fine-tuned on benign instruction-following data that has been translated into nine different languages. For each model and fine-tuning language, they evaluate safety using multilingual adversarial prompts and measure “adversarial compliance rates”—how often the model complies with unsafe requests. They also analyze internal representational drift caused by fine-tuning, comparing English vs non-English fine-tuning, and investigate how these drifts correlate with safety behavior and general capability metrics. To support the community, they build and release two resources: the Multilingual-Benign-Tune dataset for controlled fine-tuning and SORRY-Bench-Multilingual as a multilingual safety evaluation benchmark.
AI.result: Safety outcomes vary sharply depending on both the fine-tuning language and the language in which the model is evaluated. In some combinations, adversarial compliance rates increase by up to four times compared to the base or differently fine-tuned models. These multilingual safety drifts do not align with standard capability measures, showing that a model can retain or even improve task performance while becoming less safe. The internal representation changes from non-English fine-tuning are often smaller than those from English fine-tuning, yet they still produce distinct safety behaviors, with models tending toward either over-compliance with unsafe prompts or overly broad refusals. The effects are heterogeneous across languages and model families, indicating no simple, universal pattern.
AI.conclusion: Fine-tuning large language models in multilingual settings can introduce significant, language-specific safety degradation, even when using only benign data. The impact depends strongly on the pairing of fine-tuning and evaluation languages and is not predictable from standard capability metrics or from English-only evaluation. Smaller representational shifts from non-English fine-tuning can still cause pronounced and qualitatively different safety behaviors, such as exaggerated compliance or blanket refusal. Therefore, assessing fine-tuning safety solely in English is insufficient for responsible deployment. The released Multilingual-Benign-Tune dataset and SORRY-Bench-Multilingual benchmark provide a foundation for future work on detecting, understanding, and mitigating cross-lingual safety blind spots in fine-tuned LLMs.

title: Self-Supervised Theorem Discovery in a Formal Axiomatic System
summary: Recent artificial intelligence (AI) systems have shown remarkable progress in mathematical reasoning. Many existing approaches, including large language models (LLMs), draw on human prior knowledge in the form of mathematical text, code, or theorem libraries. Although these approaches are highly effective in practice, it remains an open question whether an agent can autonomously discover useful theorems without such human priors. We study this question in a formal axiomatic system by developing an agent that starts from axioms and inference rules alone and gradually grows a library of useful theorems. Concretely, we propose a self-supervised theorem-discovery algorithm that alternates between proof search and useful-theorem extraction, building a theorem library whose entries are reused as lemmas for subsequent proof search. Experiments show that the agent discovers tens of thousands of theorems and finds proofs for human-written benchmark problems, suggesting that its discoveries include theorems meaningful from a human mathematical perspective. Furthermore, the discovered theorems improve LLM proof performance when provided as prompt lemmas, indicating that they can serve as external knowledge for LLM reasoning. Our results provide evidence that useful theorems can emerge from proof search without relying on human-provided theorem libraries. More broadly, they suggest a path toward self-evolving AI systems for mathematics whose discoveries remain formally verifiable.

AI.tldr: They build an AI agent that, starting only from axioms and rules, can discover many useful theorems and use them to solve formal math problems.
AI.motivation: Current math-capable AI systems, especially LLMs, rely heavily on human-curated mathematical text, code, and theorem libraries. It is unclear whether an artificial agent can independently discover mathematically meaningful theorems and build its own reusable knowledge without any such human prior, starting only from formal axioms. This work aims to test and demonstrate that possibility.
AI.method: They work in a formal axiomatic system and design a self-supervised theorem-discovery agent. The agent starts only with axioms and inference rules. It repeatedly (1) conducts proof search to find new derivable statements; (2) selects and extracts particularly useful derived statements as theorems; and (3) stores them in a growing theorem library. These stored theorems are then used as lemmas in subsequent proof searches, creating a feedback loop where the library improves search capability over time. They then evaluate the agent on human-written benchmark problems and also test whether the discovered theorems help LLMs when provided as prompt lemmas.
AI.result: The agent autonomously discovers tens of thousands of theorems. Using its self-grown theorem library, it can find proofs for human-written benchmark problems, indicating that the discovered theorems are nontrivial and align with human mathematical notions of usefulness. Additionally, when the discovered theorems are supplied as lemmas in prompts to LLMs, the LLMs’ proof performance improves, demonstrating that these theorems function as valuable external mathematical knowledge.
AI.conclusion: Useful, human-meaningful theorems can emerge purely from automated proof search in a formal system, without relying on human-provided theorem libraries or textual corpora. This provides evidence that autonomous, self-supervised theorem discovery is feasible and that the resulting knowledge can benefit both automated provers and LLM-based mathematical reasoning. More broadly, this points toward a pathway for self-evolving, formally verifiable AI mathematicians that can grow their own mathematical knowledge base over time.

title: Open but Incompatible: A License Compatibility Analysis of Corpora for Low-Resource African Languages
comment: 12 pages. Published in Proceedings of Resources for African Indigenous Languages (RAIL) 2026 @ LREC-COLING 2026, pages 128-139
summary: Creative Commons licenses dominate African NLP corpus releases, but their compatibility rules are rarely applied. CC-BY-SA and CC-BY-NC cannot be combined in a single published dataset; a NoDerivs clause silently prohibits tokenisation and annotation. This paper audits the license provenance of over twenty corpus families used in African NLP, constructs a six-tier compatibility matrix, and applies it to three case-study languages: Kituba/Munukutuba, Zarma, and Moore. Four failure modes are documented with primary-source evidence: outright prohibition (JW300, removed from OPUS after a legal audit confirmed Terms of Service violation); composite license misrepresentation (WAXAL, whose CC-BY 4.0 claim is contradicted by its own HuggingFace dataset card); a NoDerivs clause hidden behind a CC-BY label (Tanzil); and data persistence failure (the Congolese Radio Corpus, where 402 of 405 source URLs are now dead). A pre-annotation due diligence checklist and a survey of legally clean enrichment opportunities close the paper.

AI.tldr: The paper audits licensing practices in African NLP corpora, revealing widespread incompatibilities and misrepresentations in Creative Commons usage, and offers a compatibility matrix, case studies, and practical guidance for legally sound corpus enrichment.
AI.motivation: To highlight and systematically analyze the legal and licensing problems in African NLP corpora, where Creative Commons licenses are widely used but often misunderstood or misapplied, creating legal risks and barriers to data sharing and reuse.
AI.method: The authors audit the license provenance of over twenty African NLP corpus families, build a six-tier license compatibility matrix focused on Creative Commons variants, and apply this framework to three detailed case studies (Kituba/Munukutuba, Zarma, Moore) to identify concrete failure modes.
AI.result: They identify four main kinds of licensing failures—outright legal prohibition of use, composite license misrepresentation, hidden NoDerivs restrictions, and data persistence failures—illustrated via primary-source evidence on datasets like JW300, WAXAL, Tanzil, and the Congolese Radio Corpus.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that current licensing practice in African NLP is often legally fragile and inconsistent; it recommends a pre-annotation due diligence checklist and points to legally clean avenues for dataset enrichment to support safer, more sustainable corpus creation and sharing.

title: Mechanistic Personality Analysis of LLMs Steering Personality via Latent Feature Interventions
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated the ability to simulate human-like OCEAN personality traits in generated text. Previous efforts have focused on prompt engineering or fine-tuning to shape LLM personality. In this work, we propose a mechanistic interpretability approach that directly intervenes on the model's latent features. Our method identifies latent directions in the residual stream corresponding to a target OCEAN trait using sparse autoencoders (SAEs) and contrastive activation analysis. We formalize an additive steering vector in activation space and demonstrate how applying a small additive shift to the hidden states enhances the target trait while preserving overall language modeling performance. To determine the optimal combination of feature shifts, we explore a linear weighting heuristic with grid search optimization that balances personality expression with task performance. Our approach shows promise in controllably steering personality traits at the mechanistic level while maintaining high performance on standard benchmarks.

AI.tldr: Mechanistic method to steer LLM OCEAN personality traits via latent feature interventions while preserving performance.
AI.motivation: Existing work controls LLM personality mainly via prompts or fine-tuning, which is indirect, brittle, and may hurt task performance. There is a need for more mechanistic, interpretable, and precise control over personality traits in LLMs without degrading their general capabilities.
AI.method: Use mechanistic interpretability tools to act directly on latent representations. Identify sparse, disentangled latent features in the residual stream using sparse autoencoders (SAEs). Apply contrastive activation analysis to find directions associated with specific OCEAN traits. Define an additive steering vector in activation space and apply small additive shifts to hidden states during generation. Optimize the combination and magnitude of feature shifts via a linear weighting heuristic and grid-search to trade off personality strength against task performance.
AI.result: The method successfully steers LLM-generated text toward target OCEAN traits by adding small activation shifts, while largely preserving language modeling quality. The grid-searched linear combination of feature shifts yields controllable personality expression with minimal performance degradation on standard benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Direct, mechanistic interventions on LLM latent features can controllably modulate personality traits like OCEAN dimensions without substantial loss of core model performance. This provides a promising alternative to prompt-based or fine-tuning approaches for fine-grained, interpretable personality control in LLMs.

title: Memory-Managed Long-Context Attention: A Preliminary Study of Editable Request-Local Memory
comment: 14 pages, 2 figures, 4 tables. Preliminary technical report
summary: Long-context language models often conflate two different goals: compressing history into an efficient state, and maintaining reliable long-term memory. Linear, recurrent, and sparse attention reduce the cost of processing long sequences, but they do not by themselves specify when a fact should be written, overwritten, protected from distractors, or discarded. We study memory-managed long-context attention, a research route that separates a fast recurrent or sparse backbone from explicit editable request-local memory slots and query-time sparse fallback. Across structured synthetic tasks, token/chunk/sequence bridges, generated natural language, and local frozen-model diagnostics, pure fixed-state or pure sparse methods fail some overwrite, version, anti-pollution, or no-write-signal cases, while a hybrid covers both routes. A small 2,097,152-token mechanism stress test reaches 50/50 pooled accuracy with 2-132 active chunks. A 2.74M-parameter minimal causal event-token model reaches 595/600 with lite write supervision, supporting proof of trainability rather than scale. A six-family frozen-hidden-state bridge reaches 1079/1080 controlled pointer accuracy, but it uses generator-provided integer key IDs and separately encoded canonical key strings; it is an oracle-metadata probe, not open-text entity resolution. Local non-leaderboard RULER 4K diagnostics remain close to full context, whereas a 33-record LongBench v1 16K subset shows that naive lexical selection is not general. The evidence separates three claims: controlled slot lifecycle is feasible, sparse fallback is needed when writes lack future-query signals, and learned open-domain selection remains the main architectural bottleneck. We do not claim a final generative architecture, global slot-trajectory convergence, or systems superiority.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a hybrid, memory-managed long-context attention mechanism that separates fast recurrent/sparse processing from explicit editable memory slots and sparse fallback, showing it better handles writing, overwriting, and protecting information than pure fixed-state or pure sparse approaches.
AI.motivation: Existing long-context language model methods (linear, recurrent, sparse attention) efficiently process long sequences but conflate compression with reliable long-term memory, lacking explicit control over when to write, overwrite, protect, or discard information. The authors want to disentangle computation from memory management and understand how to design mechanisms that support robust, controllable long-term memory in long-context models.
AI.method: They introduce and study “memory-managed long-context attention,” which combines a fast recurrent or sparse backbone with explicit, editable request-local memory slots and a query-time sparse fallback mechanism. They evaluate this hybrid against pure fixed-state and pure sparse methods on a variety of tests: structured synthetic tasks, token/chunk/sequence bridging problems, generated natural language setups, and diagnostics with frozen models. They run stress tests with very long sequences (up to about 2M tokens), examine a small minimal causal event-token model with light supervision for writing, and probe frozen hidden states using oracle metadata to measure pointer accuracy, as well as benchmark-style diagnostics (RULER 4K, LongBench subset).
AI.result: Pure fixed-state and pure sparse attention each fail on specific memory-management scenarios such as overwriting, versioning, avoiding pollution, or handling cases with no future-write signal, whereas the proposed hybrid mechanism successfully covers both classes of problems. In a 2,097,152-token stress test, their mechanism achieves 50/50 pooled accuracy even with only 2–132 active chunks. A minimal 2.74M-parameter causal event-token model attains 595/600 accuracy with light write supervision, demonstrating that the approach is trainable without large scale. A frozen hidden-state bridge achieves 1079/1080 controlled pointer accuracy using oracle-style integer IDs and canonical key strings. On local RULER 4K diagnostics, performance remains close to full-context baselines, but a 33-record LongBench v1 16K subset reveals that naive lexical selection strategies do not generalize well.
AI.conclusion: The study shows that explicit, controlled memory slots with a hybrid backbone make robust slot lifecycle management (writing, overwriting, and protection from interference) feasible, and that sparse fallback is crucial when write operations cannot rely on clear future-query signals. However, selecting relevant information in open-domain settings remains the central architectural bottleneck. The authors emphasize that they are not presenting a final generative architecture or claiming overall system superiority, but rather providing evidence and tools that separate key design claims and clarify what works and what remains challenging for long-context memory architectures.

title: HyphaeDB: A Living Knowledge Topology for Agent-First Memory
summary: Every existing vector database and agent memory framework treats memory as passive storage that agents query explicitly. No system propagates knowledge between agents through the memory layer itself. We introduce HyphaeDB, an agent-native memory infrastructure that reinterprets the Hierarchical Navigable Small World (HNSW) graph topology the data structure at the core of every modern vector database not as a search optimization, but as a communication fabric for multi-agent AI systems. In HyphaeDB, agents are nodes in the vector space with persistent positions, knowledge propagates via a gossip protocol through the graph's neighbor structure with energy-based attenuation, and emergent behaviors contradiction detection, pattern crystallization, and consensus formation arise from the combination of topology, propagation dynamics, and local interaction rules. We present the architecture built on three primitives (knowledge nodes, topology edges, and memory diffs), a multi-layer abstraction hierarchy with promotion via emergent consensus, and theoretical analysis grounding the system in small-world network theory, epidemic broadcast protocols, and swarm intelligence. We provide a reference implementation on PostgreSQL with pgvector and describe a concrete deployment in Swarm-Driven Development, a multi-agent software engineering methodology. HyphaeDB represents, to our knowledge, the first system to combine navigable small world topology with gossip-based knowledge propagation for multi-agent coordination.

AI.tldr: Proposes HyphaeDB, a vector-database-based, agent-native memory system that uses HNSW graph topology as a communication fabric for multi-agent AI, enabling gossip-based knowledge propagation and emergent coordination behaviors.
AI.motivation: Existing vector databases and agent memory systems treat memory as passive, only supporting explicit queries and not enabling automatic knowledge propagation or coordination between agents. There is a gap in using the underlying graph topology of vector databases as an active medium for multi-agent communication and collective intelligence.
AI.method: Redefine the HNSW graph structure, typically used just for efficient similarity search, as a communication network where agents are persistent nodes in the vector space. Introduce three primitives (knowledge nodes, topology edges, and memory diffs), a gossip protocol with energy-based attenuation for knowledge propagation through neighbors, and a multi-layer abstraction hierarchy where information can be promoted via emergent consensus. Provide theoretical grounding in small-world networks, epidemic broadcast protocols, and swarm intelligence, and implement a reference system on PostgreSQL with pgvector, applying it to a multi-agent software engineering setting called Swarm-Driven Development.
AI.result: Demonstrates a working architecture (HyphaeDB) that operationalizes HNSW as a communication fabric for agents, shows how knowledge can propagate and consolidate across agents, and observes emergent behaviors such as contradiction detection, pattern crystallization, and consensus formation in multi-agent settings. Implements and deploys the system in a real multi-agent software engineering workflow on top of PostgreSQL/pgvector.
AI.conclusion: HyphaeDB is, to the authors’ knowledge, the first system to fuse navigable small-world vector index topology with gossip-based knowledge propagation as a core coordination mechanism for multi-agent AI. This transforms the memory layer from passive storage into an active communication substrate that can support emergent collective behaviors and more coordinated multi-agent systems.

title: PASTA: A Paraphrasing And Self-Training Approach for Knowledge Updating in LLMs
summary: Knowledge updating in pre-trained Large Language Models (LLMs) remains an important challenge. While continual training provides a potential avenue for knowledge updating, it continues to present substantial technical difficulties. Furthermore, LLMs often struggle with accurately answering questions about specific factual information, such as news articles - a capability limitation widely recognized in the research community. This paper proposes PASTA, a simple yet powerful framework for integrating detailed factual information from news articles as new knowledge into LLMs, with the primary goal of building specialized models that accurately answer questions about this knowledge. Our framework combines data augmentation, question-answering generation, and a novel self-learning DPO process that simultaneously enables knowledge overwriting and hallucination suppression. We provide insights into effective knowledge updating through systematic analysis of learning parameters and data configurations. In our experimental evaluation with web articles published after the base model's knowledge cutoff, PASTA achieved remarkable improvement from 0.02 to 0.82 accuracy while maintaining general language capabilities, demonstrating its effectiveness for creating domain-specialized LLMs.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces PASTA, a framework to update LLMs with post-cutoff factual news knowledge, greatly boosting QA accuracy while preserving general abilities.
AI.motivation: Pre-trained LLMs are hard to update with new or corrected factual knowledge. Continual training is technically challenging, and existing LLMs often fail on specific, time-sensitive facts like those in news articles. There is a need for a practical method to inject detailed, recent information into LLMs to build domain- or time-specialized models without degrading their general language skills.
AI.method: The authors propose PASTA, a framework that integrates factual content from news/web articles into LLMs. It uses (1) data augmentation to turn articles into structured training material, (2) automatic generation of question–answer pairs about those articles, and (3) a novel self-learning DPO (Direct Preference Optimization) process. This DPO stage is designed to both overwrite outdated or missing knowledge and suppress hallucinations by optimizing the model’s preferences over candidate responses. They systematically vary learning parameters and data configurations to study what makes knowledge updating effective.
AI.result: On web articles published after the base model’s knowledge cutoff, models trained with PASTA improve QA accuracy from 0.02 to 0.82, a very large gain, while preserving general language capabilities. This shows that their approach can effectively inject new, time-sensitive factual knowledge into an LLM.
AI.conclusion: PASTA is an effective and relatively simple framework for updating LLMs with new factual knowledge from news articles. It can build domain- or time-specialized models that accurately answer questions about newly added information, achieving major accuracy gains without sacrificing broader language competence. The analysis of training setups offers guidance for practical knowledge-updating of LLMs.

title: Primary ICD Category Prediction using LLM-based Probing
comment: 9 pages, 2 figures. Supplementary materials provided as an ancillary file
summary:
Objective: ICD codes are central to reimbursement, research, and population health surveillance, yet automated coding systems often struggle to integrate diagnostic signals from both clinical narratives and structured electronic health record (EHR) variables. We evaluated whether frozen medical large language model (LLM) representations can serve as a shared embedding space for multimodal primary diagnosis category prediction.
  Materials and Methods: We constructed a MIMIC-IV cohort of 13,645 admissions from the 10 most frequent primary ICD-10 codes, consolidated into seven categories. Structured variables were serialized into clinical narratives and combined with leakage-pruned discharge notes. Using a frozen MedFound-Llama3-8B-finetuned backbone, we extracted hidden states from five transformer layers and trained linear probes for structured-only, unstructured-only, and combined inputs, comparing against XGBoost and information-matched PLM-ICD baselines and evaluating MIMIC-III adaptation with a compact bottleneck adapter.
  Results: The combined probe performed best on MIMIC-IV (87.69% strict; 91.45% medical accuracy), exceeding both single-modality probes and baselines. The structured-only probe outperformed its standard baseline by 6.19 points in medical accuracy. Diagnostic information became increasingly linearly separable in deeper layers, and a 2M-parameter adapter restored cross-dataset transfer to MIMIC-III using only 5% of target labels.
  Discussion: LLM embeddings can unify structured and narrative EHR information for multimodal diagnosis prediction, supporting efficient reuse of clinical representations across modalities and datasets through a small representation-level module.
  Conclusion: Multimodal probing of frozen medical LLM representations provides a practical approach for studying EHR modalities and adapting clinical representations across datasets.

AI.tldr: They test whether frozen medical LLM embeddings can act as a shared space to combine structured EHR data and clinical notes for primary diagnosis prediction, showing strong multimodal performance and easy cross-dataset adaptation.
AI.motivation: ICD coding is critical but automatic systems have difficulty jointly using free-text clinical notes and structured EHR variables. A unified representation that works across modalities and datasets would simplify building accurate, transferable diagnosis predictors.
AI.method: Using MIMIC-IV, they selected 13,645 admissions from the 10 most common primary ICD-10 codes, grouped into seven categories. They serialized structured variables into text and concatenated them with cleaned discharge notes. With a frozen MedFound-Llama3-8B-finetuned LLM, they extracted hidden states from five layers and trained simple linear probes to predict diagnosis categories for three input setups: structured-only, unstructured-only, and combined. They compared to XGBoost on structured data and a PLM-ICD model for notes, and tested transfer to MIMIC-III using a small bottleneck adapter trained with 5% labels.
AI.result: On MIMIC-IV, the combined (multimodal) probe achieved the best performance (87.69% strict accuracy; 91.45% medical accuracy), outperforming single-modality probes and both baselines. The structured-only probe beat its structured baseline by 6.19 medical-accuracy points. Diagnostic labels became more linearly separable in deeper LLM layers. A ~2M-parameter adapter enabled effective transfer to MIMIC-III using only 5% labeled data.
AI.conclusion: Frozen medical LLM embeddings form a useful shared space for structured and narrative EHR data, enabling high-quality multimodal diagnosis prediction with simple linear heads and efficient cross-dataset adaptation through a small adapter. This probing setup offers a practical tool to study and reuse clinical representations across modalities and datasets.

title: Latent Bridges for Multi-Table Question Answering
summary: We introduce GRAB, a constructor-encoder-bridge pipeline for table question answering. Our method lifts relational data into an heterogeneous graph, encodes it via message passing, and transfers the signals to an LLM through a small set of query-conditioned latent tokens. This provides the LLM with a compact, task-relevant structural representation together with the flattened text. Crucially, the LLM remains strictly frozen to preserve its general reasoning capabilities; we train only the lightweight graph encoder and latent bridge (91M parameters), allowing the entire pipeline to be trained efficiently. Our pipeline significantly improves performance on relational Question Answering, with the largest gains in demanding multi-table settings, offering an efficient, principled way to connect relational deep learning with LLMs.

AI.tldr: GRAB is a pipeline that turns relational tables into graph representations, encodes them, and feeds compact latent tokens into a frozen LLM for efficient and improved table question answering, especially on multi-table tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing table QA systems struggle with complex, multi-table, relational queries when only using flattened text or when fine-tuning large LLMs is required. There is a need for a method that can exploit relational structure while preserving the general reasoning abilities of large pretrained language models, without incurring large computational costs.
AI.method: The authors construct a heterogeneous graph from relational data (tables), apply a graph neural network–style message passing encoder to produce node/graph representations, and then bridge this graph representation to a frozen LLM via a small set of query-conditioned latent tokens that are prepended or injected into the LLM’s input together with the flattened textual form of the tables and question. Only the graph encoder and latent bridge (about 91M parameters) are trained, while the LLM is kept frozen.
AI.result: The GRAB pipeline yields significant performance improvements on relational question answering benchmarks, with especially large gains in challenging multi-table scenarios compared to baselines that either rely on flattened tables or require full LLM fine-tuning.
AI.conclusion: Integrating a trainable graph encoder with a frozen LLM through compact latent tokens is an efficient and principled approach to leverage relational structure in table QA. GRAB demonstrates that such a modular pipeline can substantially enhance LLM performance on relational and multi-table reasoning tasks without sacrificing general-purpose language abilities or requiring expensive LLM fine-tuning.

title: MedEvoEval: Evaluating Continual Evolution of Doctor Agents through Simulated Clinical Episodes
summary: Doctor agents are moving beyond single-turn answer generation toward evolving clinical decision systems. Within an outpatient episode, they acquire evidence, use examination and consultation resources, and decide when to finalize a diagnosis and management plan. Across episodes, their behavior may change through memory, retrieval, reflection, or other update mechanisms. Current evaluations only partially cover this setting. Fixed-input medical QA benchmarks score final answers from complete inputs, whereas many interactive benchmarks still focus on individual encounters or fixed runs, providing limited support for evaluating how episode-level decisions interact with cross-episode experience. We introduce MedEvoEval, an executable longitudinal evaluation framework based on action-gated simulated outpatient episodes. Each source case is converted into role-specific patient, examination, and manager views; evidence is revealed only through valid actions; and each episode records a structured trace that links observations, actions, final outputs, manager scores, and optional experience write-back. We release a runnable E&D artifact with 700 processed episodes, provenance notes, schemas, an episode runner, scoring scripts, configurations, example logs, analysis code, and trajectory- and step-level derivatives. Experiments show that episode traces expose process costs hidden by final-answer scoring, show how MDT-style consultation reallocates resources, and support longitudinal analyses of memory maturation, held-out transfer, update-stage response, and backward retention. Together, these results show that MedEvoEval provides a concrete basis for evaluating whether doctor agents improve through experience, transfer useful behavior, and retain earlier capabilities over time.

AI.tldr: The paper presents MedEvoEval, a framework to evaluate how AI doctor agents behave and improve over multiple outpatient episodes, not just single questions.
AI.motivation: Existing medical QA and interactive benchmarks mostly assess single-turn answers or isolated encounters, and they do not adequately capture how clinical agents gather evidence, manage process costs, and change their behavior across multiple patient episodes via memory and learning mechanisms.
AI.method: They build an executable longitudinal evaluation framework using simulated outpatient episodes where information is revealed only when the agent performs valid actions (e.g., ordering exams, consulting others). Each clinical case is decomposed into multiple role-specific views (patient, examinations, manager). The system records a structured trace of all actions, observations, final decisions, and any write-back to long-term experience. They package 700 such episodes along with an episode runner, schemas, scoring scripts, configurations, logs, and analysis tools.
AI.result: Using MedEvoEval, their experiments show that (1) detailed episode traces reveal process costs that are invisible if you only score final answers; (2) multi-disciplinary team–style consultations change how resources are allocated; and (3) the framework enables longitudinal analyses of how agents’ memory and behavior mature, how well they transfer to held-out cases, how they respond to updates, and how well they retain earlier capabilities.
AI.conclusion: MedEvoEval serves as a practical benchmark and tooling suite for evaluating clinical agents as evolving systems: it allows researchers to measure whether doctor agents truly improve with experience, can transfer learned behaviors, and maintain prior capabilities over time rather than just producing good one-off answers.

title: FinInvest-GTCN: Explainable Graph-Temporal-Causal Modeling for Risk-Aware Investment Decision Optimization
summary: Venture capital (VC) investment decisions face distinct challenges, such as multi-source heterogeneous data, non-stationary time series, and the demand for explainable predictions in high-stakes, low-data settings. To overcome these issues, we introduce \textbf{FinInvest-GTCN}, a Graph-Temporal-Causal Network that redefines the task from content recommendation to quantitative risk-return assessment. This architecture combines a relational graph encoder to capture the investment ecosystem's topology, a multi-scale temporal fusion module to handle long-term dependencies and non-stationarity, and a causal decision head that generates risk-adjusted predictions with interpretable causal attributions. A core innovation is the Meta-Causal Adaptation (MCA) strategy, which facilitates robust fine-tuning for new, data-scarce sectors by aligning updates with causally-plausible structures derived from meta-pretraining. Comprehensive experiments on proprietary VC datasets show that FinInvest-GTCN delivers state-of-the-art results, markedly lowering the primary Risk-Adjusted Mean Squared Error (RA-MSE) to 2.51 from a baseline of 3.05 and boosting the cumulative return of a simulated portfolio by 18.7\%. Ablation studies underscore the essential role of each component, while additional analyses confirm the model's stability, interpretability, and enhanced adaptability. This work pioneers a data-driven, explainable framework for investment decision support.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes FinInvest-GTCN, a graph-temporal-causal neural network that delivers more accurate and explainable VC investment risk-return predictions, especially in data-scarce new sectors.
AI.motivation: Venture capital decisions rely on complex, heterogeneous, and time-varying information, yet current methods struggle with non-stationary time series, low-data environments, and the need for interpretability in high-stakes investments. The authors aim to build a model that can handle relational structures, temporal complexity, and causal reasoning to improve risk-return forecasting for VC portfolios.
AI.method: They design FinInvest-GTCN, which has three main components: (1) a relational graph encoder that models the VC investment ecosystem as a graph to capture relationships between entities; (2) a multi-scale temporal fusion module that handles long-term dependencies and non-stationarity in financial time series; and (3) a causal decision head that outputs risk-adjusted predictions together with causal attributions for interpretability. Additionally, they introduce Meta-Causal Adaptation (MCA), a meta-pretraining and fine-tuning strategy that constrains adaptation to new, data-scarce sectors using causally plausible structures learned from prior data.
AI.result: On proprietary venture capital datasets, FinInvest-GTCN achieves state-of-the-art performance by significantly reducing Risk-Adjusted Mean Squared Error (RA-MSE) from 3.05 to 2.51 and increasing the cumulative return of a simulated investment portfolio by 18.7%. Ablation studies show that removing any major component (graph encoder, temporal fusion, or causal head/MCA) degrades performance. Additional experiments indicate the model is stable over time, yields interpretable causal explanations, and adapts better to new sectors than baselines.
AI.conclusion: FinInvest-GTCN offers an effective and explainable framework for supporting VC investment decisions by integrating graph, temporal, and causal modeling with a meta-causal adaptation strategy. The approach improves risk-adjusted prediction accuracy, portfolio returns, and interpretability, showing that causally informed, graph-temporal learning is well-suited to high-stakes, low-data financial decision-making contexts.

title: Expert Evaluation of Clinical AI Tools on Real Point-of-Care Clinical Queries
summary: Physicians now pose millions of clinical questions to AI tools each week, yet these tools are evaluated largely on hypothetical or exam-style questions, not those actually asked in practice.
