We report a blinded evaluation built on 620 Real-world Point-Of-Care Queries (Real-POCQi) submitted to the OpenEvidence (OE) platform by physicians spanning 30 specialties, as well as 187 questions from HealthBench. 149 practicing physicians across 36 states made head-to-head comparisons between answers from three frontier general-purpose models (Claude Opus 4.8, Gemini 3.1 Pro, and GPT-5.5) and a specialized clinical tool (OE), with graders matched to each question's specialty. When comparing answers along five dimensions relevant to clinical decision support -- accuracy, clinical utility, source quality, verifiability, & completeness -- physicians scored the specialized tool highest on all axes; in the primary analysis on Real-POCQi, win differences (margins between win and loss rates) ranged from 25 to 39 percentage points (p<0.001). Results remained consistent in sensitivity analyses stratifying by citation display, answer length, OE-user status, and Real-POCQi versus HealthBench. In parallel, LLM judges were found to systematically differ from expert judges, though both generally agreed on the best model. These findings underscore two conclusions: (i) AI tool evaluations should reflect real-world query distributions and use expert judges that mirror the specialization defining modern medicine and (ii) the consistent advantage of the specialized tool over general-purpose models does not necessarily mean that the latter cannot serve similar purposes, but that targeted engineering and customization can yield meaningful gains in performance for its users. We release Real-POCQi as a public benchmark, as well as the prespecified statistical analysis for reproducing results of this study.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates how well general-purpose AI models versus a specialized clinical tool answer real-world physician questions, finding the specialized tool consistently outperforms general models across multiple quality dimensions.
AI.motivation: Most AI evaluations in medicine use synthetic, exam-style, or benchmark questions that may not reflect what clinicians actually ask in practice. This creates a gap between benchmark performance and real-world clinical usefulness. The authors want to understand how current frontier general-purpose models perform on real point-of-care physician queries, compared directly to a specialized evidence-based clinical system, and whether usual evaluation practices (like using non-specialist or LLM judges) misrepresent true clinical quality.
AI.method: They built a dataset of 620 real point-of-care physician questions submitted to the OpenEvidence platform across 30 specialties and combined it with 187 HealthBench questions. Answers from three frontier general-purpose models (Claude Opus 4.8, Gemini 3.1 Pro, GPT-5.5) and the specialized clinical tool OpenEvidence were generated. 149 practicing physicians, matched by specialty to each question, performed blinded, head-to-head comparisons of model answers. They rated the answers along five clinically relevant dimensions: accuracy, clinical utility, source quality, verifiability, and completeness. Statistical analyses estimated win/loss margins and p-values, and sensitivity analyses stratified by citation display, answer length, user status, and dataset source were conducted. LLM-based judges were also used to compare with human expert judgments.
AI.result: Across all five evaluation dimensions on the Real-POCQi dataset, the specialized OpenEvidence tool was rated best by physicians, with win margins over each general-purpose model between 25 and 39 percentage points, all highly statistically significant (p<0.001). These advantages were robust across multiple sensitivity analyses (e.g., whether citations were displayed, answer length, whether the grader was a prior OE user, and whether questions came from Real-POCQi or HealthBench). LLM judges systematically differed from expert physician judgments, although they typically agreed on which model was best overall.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that (1) evaluations of medical AI systems should use real-world physician questions and domain-matched expert judges, reflecting the specialization of modern clinical practice; and (2) a domain-specialized, carefully engineered clinical tool can substantially outperform cutting-edge general-purpose LLMs on clinically relevant dimensions, demonstrating the value of customization and targeted design. However, this does not preclude general-purpose models from being clinically useful; rather, it highlights that tailored systems can deliver meaningful performance gains. The authors release the Real-POCQi benchmark and the prespecified analysis plan to facilitate transparent, reproducible evaluation in this space.

title: EVLA: An Electro-Aware Multimodal Assistant for Physically-Grounded Driving Reasoning and Control
summary: Modern vision-language models (VLMs) for driving assistants typically treat vehicle dynamics as a black box, resulting in decisions that lack awareness of the vehicle's real-time electro-mechanical state. To bridge this gap, we introduce the Electro-Visual-Language Assistant (EVLA) -- a novel framework that combines multi-modal scene understanding with real-time perception of the electrified powertrain state (e.g., motor torque, battery SOC). Our approach features two key innovations: first, a Unified Co-State Encoder (UCSE) that fuses visual, textual, and vehicle-state inputs into a shared latent representation, augmented with an Energy-Efficiency Field to model spatial energy costs; and second, an Electro-aware Structured Reasoning Chain (ESRC), which replaces external chain-of-thought prompting with an internal, deterministic reasoning process grounded in physical constraints and optimization objectives. Trained end-to-end with a physics-guided joint loss, EVLA learns to generate context-aware and energy-optimal driving decisions. Extensive evaluations on a driving QA benchmark demonstrate that EVLA substantially outperforms strong fine-tuned VLM baselines, improving the final score by +0.0871 and accuracy by +5.6\%. Ablation studies validate the necessity of each component, and efficiency analyses show that EVLA achieves 36\% faster inference than multi-stage pipelines. This work underscores that integrating vehicle-state awareness and structured physical reasoning is crucial for developing next-generation, physically-grounded driving assistants.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes EVLA, a vision-language driving assistant that explicitly incorporates real-time electric powertrain states and internal structured physical reasoning to make more energy-efficient, context-aware driving decisions, outperforming standard VLM-based assistants.
AI.motivation: Existing vision-language models for driving assistants treat the vehicle as a black box and ignore real-time electro-mechanical states, leading to decisions that can be suboptimal or even inconsistent with the physical and energy constraints of electric vehicles. There is a need for assistants that understand both the scene and the EV powertrain state to optimize safety and energy efficiency simultaneously.
AI.method: The authors introduce Electro-Visual-Language Assistant (EVLA) with two main components: (1) a Unified Co-State Encoder (UCSE) that fuses visual inputs, textual instructions/queries, and vehicle-state signals (e.g., motor torque, battery SOC) into a single latent space, enhanced by an Energy-Efficiency Field that encodes spatially varying energy costs; and (2) an Electro-aware Structured Reasoning Chain (ESRC), which implements an internal, deterministic reasoning procedure grounded in physical constraints and optimization goals instead of relying on external chain-of-thought prompting. The system is trained end-to-end using a physics-guided joint loss that couples task performance with adherence to physical and energy models.
AI.result: On a driving question-answering benchmark, EVLA significantly surpasses strong fine-tuned vision-language baselines, with a final score improvement of +0.0871 and a +5.6% gain in accuracy. Ablation studies show that each major component (UCSE, Energy-Efficiency Field, ESRC) is necessary to achieve the full performance gains, and efficiency measurements indicate that EVLA yields 36% faster inference compared to conventional multi-stage VLM pipelines.
AI.conclusion: Integrating explicit vehicle-state information and physically grounded structured reasoning into vision-language driving assistants meaningfully improves decision quality, energy efficiency, and computational speed. The results suggest that future driving assistants should move beyond pure perception-language modeling to incorporate real-time dynamics and physics-based reasoning for more reliable, energy-aware behavior, especially in electrified vehicles.

title: Customized Generative AI Agent for Transportation Engineering Practice: A Development and Continued Pre-training Guideline
summary: Recent advancements in generative artificial intelligence (AI) and large language models (LLMs) have shown significant promise in automating complex reasoning, summarization, and question-answering tasks. However, the effectiveness of general-purpose LLMs in specialized engineering domains remains limited due to insufficient exposure to technical standards, engineering terminology, and domain-specific semantics. This study proposes a systematic approach to developing a customized generative AI agent for transportation engineering applications. A curated corpus of U.S. transportation manuals, design guidelines, and regulatory documents is used to conduct continued pretraining of six state-of-the-art LLMs through a unified low-rank adaptation (LoRA) framework. The training process is monitored to ensure convergence and model stability. Performance is evaluated using standard natural language processing metrics, including BLEU-4 and ROUGE, with Qwen2.5-7B and LLaMA-3.1-8B demonstrating the highest domain alignment and response quality. Results validate the effectiveness of LoRA-based adaptation in improving LLM performance on technical content interpretation and context-specific reasoning. This work contributes a reproducible development framework for constructing domain-specialized generative AI agents, supporting broader deployment in transportation research, design, planning, and policy analysis.

AI.tldr: They adapt general LLMs to transportation engineering using LoRA on a curated corpus of manuals and regulations, and show clear gains in domain-specific reasoning and text handling, offering a reproducible framework for similar specialized agents.
AI.motivation: General-purpose LLMs underperform in specialized engineering tasks because they lack exposure to technical standards, terminology, and domain semantics. Transportation engineering has many manuals, guidelines, and regulatory documents that are hard to navigate manually, so there is a need for AI agents that can accurately interpret and reason over this material.
AI.method: The authors curate a corpus of U.S. transportation manuals, design guidelines, and regulatory documents. They then perform continued pretraining (domain adaptation) of six state-of-the-art LLMs using a unified low-rank adaptation (LoRA) setup, monitoring training for convergence and stability. After fine-tuning, they evaluate models with standard NLP metrics like BLEU-4 and ROUGE to quantify domain alignment and response quality.
AI.result: Among the six adapted models, Qwen2.5-7B and LLaMA-3.1-8B achieve the best scores on BLEU-4 and ROUGE, indicating superior fit to the transportation domain and better response quality. Overall, LoRA-based adaptation leads to noticeable improvements in technical content interpretation and context-specific reasoning compared with the base general-purpose models.
AI.conclusion: LoRA-based continued pretraining on a curated, domain-specific corpus is an effective and efficient way to specialize LLMs for transportation engineering. The paper offers a reproducible workflow for building such domain-focused generative AI agents, enabling more reliable use of LLMs in transportation research, design, planning, and policy analysis tasks.

title: A3M: Adaptive, Adversarial and Multi-Objective Learning for Strategic Bidding in Repeated Auctions
summary: Learning to bid in repeated multi-unit auctions with bandit feedback poses a fundamental challenge. Existing methods often rely on rigid explore-then-exploit schedules, assume stationary adversaries, and optimize solely for bidder utility, thereby limiting adaptability and strategic robustness. To address these limitations, we introduce the A3M framework, which integrates adaptive deep reinforcement learning (DRL), explicit adversarial reasoning, and principled multi-objective reward design for online auction strategy optimization. A3M employs an actor-critic DRL backbone to dynamically balance exploration and exploitation, an opponent model for fictitious play against non-stationary adversaries, and a composite reward function to jointly maximize utility, auctioneer revenue, and fairness. We provide the first comprehensive empirical evaluation of this integrated approach against established baselines in both discriminatory and uniform price auctions. Results show that A3M reduces final regret by 30--40\% in standard settings, maintains robust performance against adversarial strategy shifts, scales favorably with the number of units $K$, and enables tunable multi-objective trade-offs. An extensive ablation study confirms the necessity of each core component. Our work establishes A3M as a powerful and flexible framework for learning in complex auction environments.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes A3M, a deep RL-based framework that learns bidding strategies in repeated multi-unit auctions with bandit feedback, improving regret, robustness, and multi-objective control compared to existing methods.
AI.motivation: Learning to bid effectively in repeated multi-unit auctions is challenging because feedback is limited (bandit setting), opponents and environments can be non-stationary, and existing methods rely on rigid exploration schedules, assume stationary adversaries, and optimize only bidder utility. This leads to strategies that adapt poorly, lack robustness to adversarial behavior, and ignore other important objectives like revenue and fairness. The paper aims to design a learning framework that overcomes these limitations and performs well in realistic, complex auction environments.
AI.method: The authors introduce A3M, an integrated framework with three main components: (1) an actor-critic deep reinforcement learning backbone that adaptively balances exploration and exploitation instead of using fixed explore-then-exploit schedules; (2) an explicit opponent model used for fictitious play-style reasoning, allowing the agent to adapt to non-stationary adversaries by modeling and responding to their evolving strategies; and (3) a composite, principled multi-objective reward function that encodes and trades off between bidder utility, auctioneer revenue, and fairness. They evaluate A3M empirically against established baselines in both discriminatory and uniform price multi-unit auctions and conduct an ablation study to isolate the contribution of each component.
AI.result: Empirical results show that A3M reduces final regret by 30–40% compared to baselines in standard multi-unit auction settings. It maintains robust performance under adversarial strategy shifts, scales well with the number of units K being auctioned, and allows practitioners to tune trade-offs between different objectives (utility, revenue, fairness) via its composite reward design. The ablation study indicates that removing any of the key components (DRL backbone, opponent modeling, or multi-objective reward) significantly degrades performance, demonstrating that each is necessary.
AI.conclusion: A3M is presented as a powerful and flexible framework for learning effective bidding strategies in complex repeated multi-unit auction environments with bandit feedback. By combining adaptive deep reinforcement learning, adversarial reasoning via opponent modeling, and multi-objective reward design, it achieves lower regret, robustness to non-stationary and adversarial behavior, scalability with auction size, and tunable trade-offs among competing objectives. The work suggests that such integrated, multi-objective, and opponent-aware learning approaches are promising directions for auction and market design applications.

title: Preventing Error Propagation in Multi-Agent AI through Runtime Monitoring
summary: Multi-agent AI systems can improve answer selection by allowing different language models to exchange reasoning traces, revise initial predictions, and support a final decision. However, such communication may also introduce reliability risks: reasoning from one agent can correct another agent's mistake, but it can also mislead an agent that was initially correct. This paper studies reliable multi-agent AI communication through reasoning exchange and runtime answer revision. We develop a framework in which agents first answer multiple-choice questions independently, then share reasoning traces and revise their decisions. We conduct numerical experiments where we evaluate whether this process improves accuracy, produces more positive than negative answer transitions, and remains effective across domains such as cybersecurity, networking, and general knowledge. The results help identify when multi-agent reasoning improves reliability and when it may propagate errors.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes how multi-agent AI systems that exchange reasoning traces affect answer reliability across domains.
AI.motivation: To understand and improve the reliability of multi-agent AI communication, where sharing reasoning can both fix and propagate errors, especially in high-stakes domains like cybersecurity and networking.
AI.method: They propose a framework where multiple language model agents first answer multiple-choice questions independently, then share their reasoning traces and are allowed to revise their answers based on others’ reasoning. They run numerical experiments to measure accuracy changes, types of answer transitions, and cross-domain performance.
AI.result: The experiments show specific conditions under which sharing reasoning and revising answers increases overall accuracy and yields more beneficial than harmful answer changes, as well as cases where it instead spreads errors among agents. Performance patterns vary across domains such as cybersecurity, networking, and general knowledge.
AI.conclusion: Multi-agent reasoning with trace exchange can improve reliability but is not uniformly beneficial; its effectiveness depends on the domain and interaction dynamics. The findings highlight when multi-agent reasoning is advantageous and when it risks error propagation, informing the design of more reliable multi-agent AI systems.

title: Beyond the Mean: Three-Axis Fidelity for Aligning LLM-Based Survey Simulators from Small Pilot Data
comment: 11 pages, 8 tables, 3 figures; Pluralistic Alignment @ ICML 2026 Workshop
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used to simulate social survey responses, yet their outputs exhibit systematic biases: marginal distributions are skewed, response variance is poorly calibrated, and predictor-outcome relationships are attenuated. We ask a simple question: given a small pilot sample of human responses, can an LLM recover the statistical characteristics of a broader population? We decompose recovery along three axes: structural fidelity, marginal fidelity, and individual fidelity. Using a COVID-19 misinformation survey as a case study, we benchmark three families of approaches: prompting, rectification, and fine-tuning. The findings suggest that fine-tuning on small pilot samples offers a balanced approach for achieving multiple forms of fidelity, but the levels of such fidelity can vary across subsamples, potentially threatening pluralistic alignment.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates whether large language models can, using a small human pilot sample, generate synthetic survey responses that accurately match population-level statistics, comparing prompting, rectification, and fine-tuning methods.
AI.motivation: LLMs are increasingly used to generate synthetic social survey data, but their responses show systematic biases: skewed marginal distributions, miscalibrated variance, and weakened predictor-outcome relationships. Researchers need to know whether and how LLMs can be adjusted, using limited real data, to faithfully reproduce the statistical properties of broader populations.
AI.method: The authors conceptualize "recovery" of population characteristics along three dimensions: structural fidelity (accurate relationships between variables), marginal fidelity (correct univariate distributions), and individual fidelity (realistic variability at the respondent level). They use a COVID-19 misinformation survey as a testbed and benchmark three families of techniques—(1) direct prompting strategies, (2) rectification methods that post-process or reweight LLM outputs, and (3) fine-tuning LLMs on a small pilot sample of real survey responses—then compare how well each method recovers the desired fidelities.
AI.result: Across the benchmark, simple prompting and rectification approaches only partially correct biases in LLM-generated survey data, often failing to simultaneously achieve good structural, marginal, and individual fidelity. Fine-tuning on small pilot samples generally provides a more balanced improvement across all three dimensions, although the performance is not uniform: fidelity varies across different population subsamples and outcome dimensions.
AI.conclusion: Fine-tuning LLMs on small, human-collected pilot surveys is a promising strategy for producing synthetic survey data that better reflect population-level statistics along multiple dimensions of fidelity. However, the uneven fidelity across subgroups and subsamples raises concerns for pluralistic alignment, indicating that additional methods or constraints may be needed to ensure that all segments of the population are represented fairly and accurately in LLM-simulated data.

title: Memory as an Attack Surface in LLM Agents: A Study on Multiple-Choice Question Answering
summary: AI agents extend conventional large language model (LLM) applications by integrating language understanding with task execution, external tool use, and memory mechanisms. While memory allows agents to retain prior interactions and provide more personalized and context-aware responses, it also introduces a new vulnerability: information stored in memory can influence future outputs even when the current query is clean. In this paper, we investigate memory manipulation in LLM-based agents for multiple-choice question answering. We first design and implement an LLM-based AI agent with an external memory component that stores and retrieves task-relevant information. We then introduce basic memory manipulation scenarios in which misleading or corrupted memories are inserted into the agent before it answers multiple-choice questions. Using a controlled experimental setup, we compare the agent's performance before and after memory manipulation and measure changes in answer accuracy, attack success rate, and selection of manipulated options. Our results show that even simple memory manipulations can noticeably affect the agent's final answers, causing it to select incorrect options despite receiving clean and well-formed questions.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how manipulating an AI agent’s external memory can corrupt its answers to multiple-choice questions, even when the questions themselves are clean.
AI.motivation: LLM-based agents use memory to provide personalized, context-aware responses, but this memory can become a vector for attacks. The authors want to understand how vulnerable such memory mechanisms are to manipulation and how much they can affect downstream task performance.
AI.method: They build an LLM-based agent equipped with an external memory that stores and retrieves task-relevant information. They then design simple memory manipulation scenarios where misleading or corrupted entries are inserted into memory before the agent answers multiple-choice questions. In a controlled experimental setup, they compare performance metrics—such as answer accuracy, attack success rate, and the frequency of choosing manipulated options—before and after memory manipulation.
AI.result: Experiments show that inserting even simple misleading or corrupted memories substantially changes the agent’s behavior: its answer accuracy drops, the attack success rate increases, and it more often selects the attacker-manipulated answer choices, despite receiving clean questions.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates that external memory in LLM-based agents is a significant vulnerability: corrupted memories can steer the agent toward incorrect answers without needing to tamper with the input query. This highlights the need for robust memory management, validation, and defense mechanisms in AI agent designs that rely on persistent memory.

title: Can LLMs Hire Fairly? Racial Bias in Resume Screening
summary: We audit fourteen mainstream large language models (LLMs) for hiring discrimination using the paired-resume methodology of Kline, Rose, and Walters (2022). The sole 2023-vintage model reproduces the pro-White callback gap documented in field experiments on labor market discrimination ($+2.12$ pp, significant at the 1\% level). Every model released in 2024 or after shows either a null gap or a significant pro-Black reversal (up to $-3.01$ pp). The same pattern holds on the gender axis. Based on 24,024 paired postings per model across 14 models, our results document a reversal in the direction of algorithmic hiring bias across model generations.

AI.tldr: The paper audits 14 large language models for hiring discrimination and finds that newer (2024+) models no longer reproduce earlier pro-White and pro-male biases, instead showing null or reversed gaps.
AI.motivation: To understand whether mainstream LLMs used in hiring and screening reproduce or alter real-world patterns of labor market discrimination, and how these patterns evolve across successive model generations.
AI.method: Apply the paired-resume audit methodology of Kline, Rose, and Walters (2022) to 14 mainstream LLMs, generating 24,024 paired job postings per model where resumes are equivalent except for demographic signals (race and gender), and measure differential “callback” or hiring recommendations across demographic groups.
AI.result: The only model from 2023 reproduces a statistically significant pro-White callback gap (+2.12 percentage points, 1% significance). All models released in 2024 or later exhibit either no statistically significant difference or a statistically significant pro-Black gap (up to −3.01 percentage points). An analogous pattern appears for gender, indicating a shift in bias direction across model vintages.
AI.conclusion: There has been a documented reversal in the direction of algorithmic hiring bias across generations of mainstream LLMs: older models reproduce pro-White and pro-male gaps seen in field experiments, while newer models tend to equalize or favor historically disadvantaged groups, highlighting the impact of ongoing alignment and debiasing efforts in commercial models.

title: Low-cost concept-based localized explanations: How far can we get with training-free approaches?
comment: 6 pages, 2 figures, 4 tables. Accepted at the 2026 IEEE International Conference on Artificial Intelligence (CAI), 8-10 May 2026, Granada, Spain. Code: https://github.com/darianfgUgr/CoNa
summary: Concept-based Explainable AI (C-XAI) seeks human-understandable explanations grounded in semantic concepts, yet validation is limited by the scarcity of fine-grained concept annotations. We evaluate whether mid-scale Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) can perform localized concept naming under strict zero-shot conditions by assigning labels to bounding-box regions at both object and part levels. We propose a reproducible zero-shot evaluation protocol for Concept Naming (CoNa) with (i) closed-set, category-constrained prompting for moderate vocabularies and (ii) Open-CoNa, an embedding-similarity-based strategy for large label spaces. Experiments with four MLLMs (7B-32B) show consistent performance trends across datasets, reaching 62%-88% object-level exact-match accuracy, highlighting the potential of training-free concept annotation from localized regions. We discuss limitations and failure modes and release a reproducible framework to support future low-cost C-XAI research.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates whether mid-scale multimodal LLMs can accurately name visual concepts (objects and parts) in image regions under strict zero-shot settings, proposing a reproducible concept-naming benchmark and showing promising performance for training-free concept annotation.
AI.motivation: Concept-based explainable AI needs human-understandable concepts linked to model behavior, but current work is limited by the lack of detailed, fine-grained concept annotations. Creating such annotations manually is costly. The authors are motivated to see whether existing multimodal LLMs can automatically and reliably name localized visual concepts, which could greatly reduce the cost of building datasets and tools for concept-based explanations.
AI.method: They frame the task as "Concept Naming" (CoNa), where a model assigns labels to given bounding boxes in images at both object and part levels. They design a strict zero-shot evaluation protocol and test four mid-scale MLLMs (7B–32B) without additional training. Two protocol variants are introduced: (i) a closed-set, category-constrained prompting setup for moderate-size vocabularies, and (ii) Open-CoNa, which uses embedding similarity between model outputs and label descriptions to handle large label spaces. Performance is evaluated as exact-match accuracy on several datasets.
AI.result: Across multiple datasets and four MLLMs, they observe consistent trends and relatively strong results: 62%–88% exact-match accuracy for object-level concept naming under zero-shot conditions. These results suggest that current mid-scale MLLMs can already provide reasonably accurate localized concept labels without any task-specific training.
AI.conclusion: The authors conclude that mid-scale MLLMs have substantial zero-shot capability for localized concept naming, making them promising tools for generating concept annotations needed in concept-based XAI, at low cost and without training. They emphasize remaining limitations and failure modes, but argue that their reproducible protocol and released framework can serve as a standard, facilitating future research on scalable, training-free concept annotation for C-XAI.

title: Fine-Tuning General-Purpose Large Language Models for Agricultural Applications:A Reproducible Framework and Evaluation Protocol Based on Qwen3-8B
summary: General-purpose large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated strong abilities in opendomain question answering, information extraction, and text generation. Agricultural applications, however, are domain-specific, region-dependent, time-sensitive, and safety-critical. Without data governance, expert evaluation, and evidence constraints, an agricultural assistant mayproduce unreliable advice on crop diseases, pesticide use, fertilization, or policy interpretation.To avoid presenting unverified simulated numbers as real experimental findings, this paper doesnot report any model-performance claims that have not been produced by an actual training runand expert evaluation. Instead, we propose AgriTune-R, a reproducible and auditable frameworkfor adapting general-purpose LLMs to agricultural tasks. The framework selects the publiclyverifiable Qwen3-8B model as the recommended base model and integrates agricultural datagovernance, instruction construction, LoRA/QLoRA parameter-efficient fine-tuning, retrievalaugmented generation, expert evaluation, and safety control for high-risk questions. The contributions are: (1) a structured workflow for agricultural LLM adaptation; (2) an evaluationprotocol for agricultural knowledge QA, pest and disease consultation, cultivation management,and policy explanation; (3) an expert-review rubric combining factuality, safety, evidence consistency, and uncertainty expression; and (4) a clear separation between protocol design andempirical conclusions, providing an executable baseline for future empirical studies.

AI.tldr: The paper presents AgriTune-R, a reproducible framework to adapt general-purpose LLMs to agricultural applications using a verified base model, data governance, expert evaluation, and safety controls.
AI.motivation: General-purpose LLMs perform well on many NLP tasks but agriculture is highly domain-specific, safety-critical, and sensitive to region and time. Naively applying LLMs risks unreliable or dangerous advice (e.g., on pesticides, fertilization, or policy). The authors want a trustworthy, auditable way to adapt LLMs to agriculture without making unverifiable performance claims.
AI.method: They design AgriTune-R, a structured workflow for adapting LLMs to agriculture. It uses the publicly verifiable Qwen3-8B as the base model and integrates: agricultural data governance; domain instruction construction; parameter-efficient fine-tuning via LoRA/QLoRA; retrieval-augmented generation for grounding answers; expert evaluation; and safety control mechanisms for high-risk questions. They also design an evaluation protocol and an expert-review rubric emphasizing factuality, safety, evidence consistency, and proper uncertainty expression.
AI.result: They do not emphasize new benchmark scores; instead, the main result is a reproducible, auditable adaptation framework plus an evaluation protocol and expert rubric. The output is a clearly specified baseline workflow and processes that future empirical work can implement and extend, rather than specific performance numbers that might be hard to verify or reproduce.
AI.conclusion: AgriTune-R offers a principled, transparent approach to customize general LLMs for agriculture while prioritizing safety, evidence, and reproducibility. By separating protocol design from empirical claims and embedding expert review and safety controls, it provides an executable baseline for building and rigorously evaluating agricultural LLM assistants in future studies.

title: Managing the Human Fallback: Skill Investment Under Improving AI and Worker Mobility
summary: When firms deploy autonomous AI, they must decide how much work to leave to the system and how much to keep workers engaged. This decision affects current output and future human capital. We develop a parsimonious two-period model in which AI may outperform the worker when it functions, but may fail with positive probability. A firm chooses worker engagement; engagement lowers current output for below-benchmark workers, but changes future skill through learning and erosion. We distinguish two dimensions of AI progress: capability, the system's output when it works, and reliability, the probability that it works. In a single-firm benchmark, engagement is valuable only as fallback investment. The firm engages the least-skilled workers most, because they have the largest skill gaps and are least costly to bring toward a useful fallback level. With worker mobility, engagement also affects labor-market sorting: workers prefer jobs that build more valuable skill trajectories. This sorting motive targets higher-skill workers near the AI frontier, where skill gains are more valuable and engagement is less costly. Mobility can therefore reverse the engagement pattern, shifting investment from the least-skilled toward the most-skilled workers below the AI benchmark. Mobility also reshapes how AI progress affects engagement: greater capability raises engagement by increasing the value of the skill trajectory a firm offers, whereas greater reliability can raise or lower it because it reduces fallback need while also changing learning opportunities. Under worker mobility, human-AI work design becomes a problem of human-capital investment, in which allocating work today shapes future skill.

AI.tldr: Two-period model of firms’ choice of how much to involve workers vs. autonomous AI, showing how this shapes future skills and labor-market sorting.
AI.motivation: As AI systems become more capable and reliable, firms must decide how much to automate versus keep humans engaged. This choice not only affects immediate output but also workers’ future skills, which matters for long-run productivity and how workers sort across jobs. Existing discussions of AI often overlook this dynamic human-capital dimension and how labor mobility can alter firms’ incentives to invest in worker skills when AI is available.
AI.method: The paper builds a parsimonious two-period theoretical model. AI can outperform workers when it functions, but it fails with positive probability. The firm chooses a level of worker engagement with tasks. Engagement reduces current output for under-benchmark workers but affects their future skills via learning (skill accumulation) and erosion (skill loss without engagement). The model distinguishes AI progress along two axes: capability (output when AI works) and reliability (probability that AI works). The analysis compares a single-firm benchmark without worker mobility to a setting with worker mobility, where workers can move based on the value of the skill trajectory offered.
AI.result: In the single-firm benchmark without worker mobility, worker engagement is valuable only as a fallback investment—ensuring workers can perform when AI fails. The firm optimally engages the least-skilled workers most, because they have the largest skill gaps to reach a minimal fallback level and are cheapest to bring up to that level. When workers are mobile, engagement also influences labor-market sorting: workers prefer employers that offer more valuable skill trajectories. This creates a sorting motive for engagement targeted at higher-skill workers near the AI frontier, where additional skill is more valuable and engagement is less costly. As a result, with mobility, the pattern of engagement can flip—investment shifts from the least-skilled toward the most-skilled workers below the AI benchmark. The model also shows that increases in AI capability always tend to raise engagement by enhancing the value of the skill path a firm offers, whereas increases in AI reliability have ambiguous effects, because they simultaneously reduce the need for human fallback and alter learning opportunities.
AI.conclusion: When workers can move between firms, designing human-AI task allocation is fundamentally a human-capital investment problem rather than just an immediate productivity choice. Optimal engagement policies depend on both AI capability and reliability and on labor mobility. Firms may invest more in higher-skill workers’ engagement when workers can sort into jobs with better skill-building trajectories, reversing the pattern seen in an immobile-labor benchmark. This implies that labor-market institutions and the nature of AI progress will critically shape who gets skill investment, how work is organized around AI, and the evolution of human capital in AI-intensive occupations.

title: BERTomelo: Your Portuguese Encoder Best Friend
summary: Encoders have become the state of the art for multiple NLP tasks, especially those requiring deep contextual understanding. While multilingual models offer broad coverage, dedicated monolingual encoders are essential for capturing the unique lexical and syntactic nuances of specific languages. For Portuguese, however, existing monolingual options like BERTimbau and Albertina have not kept pace with recent architectural breakthroughs, often lagging behind English benchmarks in scalability and efficiency. This work introduces BERTomelo, a next-generation monolingual encoder pre-trained from scratch and specifically optimized for the Portuguese language. By leveraging the ModernBERT architecture, BERTomelo overcomes the limitations of previous models, offering Base and Large versions with a 1,024-token context window and hardware-level optimizations like FlashAttention and alternating attention mechanisms. The model was trained on ClassiCC-PT, a massive, high-quality Portuguese corpus of 106 million documents, ensuring superior alignment with the language's contemporary usage. The results demonstrate that BERTomelo not only outperforms previous Portuguese encoders but also provides a more robust and efficient alternative to massive multilingual models in downstream tasks such as STS and NER.

AI.tldr: The paper presents BERTomelo, a new Portuguese-specific encoder based on the ModernBERT architecture that outperforms prior monolingual and multilingual models on several NLP tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing Portuguese monolingual encoders like BERTimbau and Albertina lag behind recent architectural advances and underperform compared to English counterparts, while multilingual models, though broad, do not capture Portuguese-specific lexical and syntactic nuances sufficiently.
AI.method: The authors pre-train a new monolingual encoder, BERTomelo, from scratch using the ModernBERT architecture, with Base and Large variants supporting a 1,024-token context window and hardware-oriented optimizations such as FlashAttention and alternating attention. Training is done on ClassiCC-PT, a large curated corpus of 106 million contemporary Portuguese documents, to better model the language.
AI.result: BERTomelo achieves superior performance over previous Portuguese encoders and large multilingual models on downstream NLP tasks including semantic textual similarity (STS) and named entity recognition (NER).
AI.conclusion: A carefully designed, modern-architecture monolingual encoder trained on a large, high-quality Portuguese corpus can surpass both older monolingual models and massive multilingual systems, providing a more efficient and robust solution for Portuguese NLP tasks.

title: Characterizing Large Language Model Agentic Workflows: A Study on N8n Ecosystem
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) are rapidly being adopted in low-code and no-code automation platforms, where non-expert users design workflows that combine natural language understanding with external services and APIs. LLM agents are LLM systems that use LLMs as a core "brain" to reason, plan, and autonomously execute complex, multi-step tasks. In this paper, we present the first large-scale empirical study of LLM agentic workflows in low-code automation platforms. We analyze more than 6,000 publicly available n8n workflows and examine four aspects of their design: task distribution, structural and tool use patterns, reliability mechanisms, and autonomy levels. Our analysis shows that LLM workflows are not merely prompt response pipelines. Instead, LLMs are commonly embedded within broader automation structures involving control logic, external tools, communication services, storage systems, and human review points. We further find that while many workflows include lightweight post-processing or routing logic after LLM execution, explicit reliability mechanisms such as structured fallback paths, repair loops, failure-specific alerts, and human approval gates remain relatively uncommon. These results reveal a gap between the increasing deployment of LLM agents in practical automation ecosystems and the limited engineering support for reliability, safety, and governance. Overall, our study provides ten empirical findings and five research takeaways for researchers, platform developers, and practitioners seeking to understand and improve real-world LLM agentic workflows.

AI.tldr: The paper empirically studies how LLM-based agentic workflows are actually built and used on a large low-code automation platform (n8n), revealing that while LLMs are embedded in rich automation structures, explicit reliability and safety mechanisms are still rare.
AI.motivation: As LLMs and LLM agents are increasingly integrated into low-code/no-code automation tools for non-expert users, there is little empirical understanding of how these agentic workflows are designed in the wild, especially regarding reliability, safety, and autonomy. This gap hinders principled improvements to platforms and best practices.
AI.method: The authors perform a large-scale empirical analysis of more than 6,000 publicly available n8n workflows that incorporate LLMs. They categorize and examine these workflows along four dimensions: (1) how tasks are distributed between LLMs and other components, (2) structural patterns and tool usage, (3) presence and type of reliability mechanisms, and (4) degrees of autonomy and human involvement.
AI.result: They find that real-world LLM workflows are typically not simple single-shot prompt-response chains. Instead, LLMs appear as components within larger automations that include control logic, use of external tools and APIs, communication and storage systems, and occasional human review points. However, despite the complexity of these workflows, explicit reliability mechanisms—such as systematic fallbacks, repair loops, failure-based alerts, and human approval gates—are comparatively rare. This highlights a mismatch between widespread deployment and the maturity of reliability engineering practices for LLM agents.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that current LLM agentic workflows in low-code platforms exhibit sophisticated integration patterns but lack robust reliability, safety, and governance mechanisms. The authors synthesize ten empirical findings and five high-level research directions aimed at guiding researchers, platform builders, and practitioners toward better tooling, design patterns, and governance for real-world LLM agents.

title: Conversational Domain Adaptation of IndicTrans2 across 21 Indic Languages via Experience Replay and Model Soups
summary: IndicTrans2 is the strongest open English to Indic translation system, but like most systems it is trained on general text and tends to sound stiff on casual, conversational input. We adapt IndicTrans2-1B to conversational register across all 21 Indic languages using only public data (OpenSubtitles, BPCC-H-Daily, Tatoeba). Plain fine-tuning improves conversational chrF but forgets the general domain (it drops 3.9 chrF on FLORES for Hindi). Mixing general data back into training (experience replay) and then averaging the fine-tuned weights with the base (model souping) removes that trade-off: the resulting model beats IndicTrans2-1B on conversational chrF in every one of the 21 languages (mean +6.2) while matching it on FLORES (mean change -0.17, all within 0.7 chrF). Paired bootstrap tests confirm the conversational gains are significant (p <= 0.004) and that FLORES is not significantly degraded. We are deliberate about scope: these are chrF gains, and a blind human plus multi-model LLM check does not confirm them as a perceived quality improvement, so we treat the conversational gain as largely a register match to the references rather than proof of better translation. The techniques are not new; the contribution is the honest, end-to-end study in the Indic conversational setting.

AI.tldr: They adapt the IndicTrans2-1B English→Indic translator to conversational language for 21 Indic languages using public conversational data, combining fine-tuning, experience replay, and model souping to gain conversational performance without hurting general-domain quality, and carefully validate what these gains mean.
AI.motivation: IndicTrans2 is strong for English→Indic translation but is trained on general text, so it performs poorly or sounds unnatural on casual, conversational inputs. There is a need for open, conversational-quality translation for many Indic languages, using only public data, and for an honest assessment of whether metric gains correspond to real perceived quality.
AI.method: Starting from IndicTrans2-1B, they fine-tune on conversational parallel corpora (OpenSubtitles, BPCC-H-Daily, Tatoeba) for 21 Indic languages. They first do plain fine-tuning, observe domain forgetting on FLORES, then mitigate this by (1) mixing general-domain data back into training (experience replay) and (2) averaging the fine-tuned model weights with the base model (model souping). They evaluate with chrF on conversational test sets and FLORES, use paired bootstrap resampling to test significance, and conduct human and LLM-based blind evaluations to see if chrF gains reflect perceived improvements.
AI.result: Plain conversational fine-tuning improves conversational chrF but significantly hurts general-domain performance (e.g., −3.9 chrF on FLORES for Hindi). With experience replay plus model souping, the adapted model outperforms IndicTrans2-1B on conversational chrF across all 21 Indic languages (average +6.2 chrF) while essentially preserving general-domain performance on FLORES (mean change −0.17 chrF, all language changes within 0.7 chrF). Bootstrap tests show conversational gains are statistically significant (p ≤ 0.004) and FLORES degradation is not statistically significant. Human and multi-model LLM evaluations, however, do not clearly confirm a perceived quality improvement matching the metric gains.
AI.conclusion: The combination of conversational fine-tuning, experience replay with general-domain data, and model souping can adapt a strong general-domain translator like IndicTrans2-1B to conversational register for many Indic languages using only public data, boosting chrF on conversational benchmarks without sacrificing general-domain performance. However, these gains largely reflect better register alignment with conversational references rather than clear human-perceived quality improvements, and the key value of the work is an honest, end-to-end empirical study in the Indic conversational translation setting rather than introducing new techniques.

title: HiComm: Hierarchical Communication for Multi-agent Reinforcement Learning
summary: Cooperative multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) often relies on communication to mitigate partial observability, yet most existing protocols treat messages as flat dense vectors detached from the structure of the observations they summarize. This design overlooks an important source of inductive bias in many cooperative environments, where observations naturally follow a hierarchy such as groups and entities. We propose \textsc{HiComm}, a plug-in communication module that grounds messages in the sender's hierarchical observation. \textsc{HiComm} is receiver-driven: the receiver issues a query, and the hierarchy is resolved through a three-stage decoding process that first selects a group, then a sender, and then an entity within that group, returning the corresponding feature slice as the message. This converts communication from unstructured vector transmission into structured information retrieval over the sender's observation hierarchy. We instantiate this mechanism with Straight-Through Gumbel-Softmax for differentiable discrete selection and a lightweight shared projection design that attaches to standard MARL pipelines. Experiments across cooperative MARL tasks with different observation structures and coordination demands show that \textsc{HiComm} matches or outperforms representative learned communication baselines while reducing communication volume by up to $23\times$ per receiver per episode.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces HiComm, a communication module for cooperative multi-agent reinforcement learning that uses the hierarchical structure of observations to make communication more efficient and effective, significantly reducing message volume while matching or improving performance over baselines.
AI.motivation: Existing cooperative MARL methods often use flat, dense message vectors that ignore the natural hierarchical structure of many environments (e.g., groups and entities), missing a useful inductive bias and potentially wasting bandwidth. The paper aims to design a communication protocol that is aligned with hierarchical observations to improve coordination and communication efficiency.
AI.method: The authors propose HiComm, a receiver-driven, hierarchy-aware communication module. Communication is modeled as structured information retrieval from the sender’s hierarchical observation. The receiver issues a query that is decoded in three stages: (1) select a group, (2) select a sender within that group, and (3) select an entity within that group. The chosen entity’s feature slice is returned as the message. The selection is implemented with Straight-Through Gumbel-Softmax to enable differentiable discrete decisions. A lightweight shared projection design allows HiComm to be plugged into standard MARL pipelines.
AI.result: Across several cooperative MARL tasks with varying observation structures and coordination requirements, HiComm achieves performance that matches or exceeds representative learned communication baselines. At the same time, it reduces communication volume by up to 23 times per receiver per episode, indicating much more communication-efficient coordination.
AI.conclusion: Grounding communication in the hierarchical structure of observations provides a beneficial inductive bias for cooperative MARL. HiComm demonstrates that treating communication as structured information retrieval rather than unstructured vector transmission can improve or maintain task performance while greatly reducing communication load, and it can be easily integrated into existing MARL frameworks.

title: How to Leverage Synthetic Speech for LLM-Based ASR Systems?
summary: In regulated domains such as banking and healthcare, where privacy constraints make real speech costly to collect and retain, synthetic speech from modern text-to-speech (TTS) is an appealing alternative for training automatic speech recognition (ASR) without exposing sensitive customer recordings. Yet a persistent distributional gap between synthetic and real data limits how far it can replace genuine recordings. Prior work largely treats this gap as a black box to be engineered around, but in our work, we instead examine its origin directly by probing a SLAM-ASR architecture. Then, we localise where its LLM backbone separates real from synthetic speech and find the discriminative signal concentrated in the early-to-middle layers, where temporal and prosodic perturbations disrupt it most. We further show that representation-level separability, help, but does not directly predict downstream ASR gains. On the other hand, convolving synthetic audio with room impulse responses (RIRs) narrows the gap not by making synthetic speech sound cleaner or more natural, but by reproducing the acoustic irregularities of real recordings. Translating these findings into the training procedure, by adding a layer-selection module combined with RIR augmentation matches a fully real-data baseline using only 25% of the real speech (13.6h) and surpasses it at all higher proportions.

AI.tldr: The paper studies why synthetic TTS data doesn’t fully replace real speech for ASR training, by probing representations inside a SLAM-ASR model and designing training tricks that close the gap.
AI.motivation: In privacy‑sensitive areas like banking and healthcare it’s hard or impossible to store and reuse real customer speech, which limits ASR development. Synthetic speech from modern TTS seems like a safe, cheap alternative, but models trained on it still underperform those trained on genuine recordings due to a distributional gap between synthetic and real speech. Prior work mostly tries to compensate empirically without understanding where the gap arises in the model’s internal representations.
AI.method: The authors probe a SLAM‑ASR architecture with an LLM backbone to see where in the network real and synthetic speech become separable. They analyse layer‑wise representations, apply temporal and prosodic perturbations to see which layers are most affected, and relate representation‑level separability to ASR performance. They also experiment with convolving synthetic speech with room impulse responses (RIRs) to mimic real acoustic conditions, and then incorporate insights into a modified training scheme that uses layer selection plus RIR augmentation while varying the proportion of real vs. synthetic data.
AI.result: They find that the model’s ability to distinguish real from synthetic speech is concentrated in early and middle layers; perturbations affecting timing and prosody most strongly disrupt this separability. Representation‑level separation between real and synthetic data correlates with, but does not directly determine, downstream ASR improvements. RIR convolution reduces the gap mainly by introducing realistic acoustic irregularities rather than by making synthetic audio perceptually cleaner or more natural. Using these insights, a training strategy with layer‑selection and RIR‑augmented synthetic data matches the performance of a full real‑data baseline using only 25% of the real speech and outperforms it when more real data is available.
AI.conclusion: Understanding where and how an ASR model internally differentiates synthetic from real speech enables better use of synthetic data. The gap is rooted in early‑to‑middle layer representations sensitive to prosody and temporal cues, and can be narrowed by adding realistic acoustic artifacts via RIRs rather than purely improving TTS naturalness. Incorporating a layer‑selection mechanism and RIR augmentation allows practitioners in privacy‑constrained domains to significantly reduce their dependence on large real‑speech corpora while matching or surpassing the performance of fully real‑data baselines.

title: Flow Reasoning Models: Scaling Reasoning Through Iterative Self-Refinement
summary: Discrete flow models have recently shown promising performance on few-step text generation; however, when naively applied to structured reasoning tasks such as Sudoku and Zebra puzzles, they converge confidently to incorrect answers (solving only $\sim$36% of Sudoku puzzles). We introduce Flow Reasoning Models (FRMs), a training and test-time-scaling framework for structured reasoning with flow models. We make the observation that, despite their poor solve rate, flow models can act as their own verifiers. A correct answer is a stable fixed point of the denoising dynamics, returning to itself when re-noised and re-solved. This enables a test-time-scaling paradigm: propose many candidate solutions and keep those that are dynamically stable, which alone reaches high solve rates on Sudoku-Shah (~$100\%$) and Zebra ($95.9\%$). This even generalizes to harder out-of-distribution puzzles like Sudoku-Extreme ($96.1\%$), without ever training on that distribution. This pure search, however, wastes a great deal of computation generating incorrect candidate solutions. We therefore design a training recipe to improve the base model's efficiency. First, we train flow models with a self-conditioning channel and close it at inference, letting them refine their own past predictions. Second, we train models to avoid their own failed generations using direct preference optimization. These changes substantially improve the base model's efficiency, letting it reach $99.2\%$ on Sudoku in just $7$ forward passes, over $8\times$ fewer than the strongest matched masked-diffusion baseline we compare needs for the same accuracy. When combined with test-time scaling, this lets flow models solve hard out-of-distribution puzzles (e.g. Sudoku-Extreme) far more efficiently.

AI.tldr: They propose Flow Reasoning Models (FRMs), showing that discrete flow models can solve structured reasoning tasks like Sudoku very accurately by using their own denoising dynamics both as a verifier at test time and as a training signal to improve efficiency.
AI.motivation: Standard discrete flow models work well for few-step text generation but fail badly on structured reasoning tasks such as Sudoku and Zebra puzzles, converging confidently to wrong answers and requiring a lot of compute for search. There is a need for methods that can both improve solve rates and dramatically increase efficiency, ideally generalizing out-of-distribution without retraining.
AI.method: 1) Observe that correct solutions are stable fixed points of the flow model’s denoising dynamics: when re-noised and re-solved, they map back to themselves. 2) Use this as a verifier for test-time scaling: generate many candidate solutions and keep those that are dynamically stable. 3) Improve base-model efficiency via a training recipe: (a) add a self-conditioning channel so that during training the model refines its own past predictions but close that channel at inference; (b) apply direct preference optimization so the model learns to avoid its own failed generations. 4) Combine these training changes with the test-time stability-based search for structured reasoning tasks.
AI.result: Using only the stability-based search, they achieve nearly perfect solve rates: about 100% on Sudoku-Shah and 95.9% on Zebra, and strong generalization to harder out-of-distribution Sudoku-Extreme puzzles with 96.1% accuracy, all without training on that harder distribution. With the improved training recipe, the base flow model becomes much more efficient, reaching 99.2% accuracy on Sudoku in only 7 forward passes—over 8× fewer than a comparable masked-diffusion baseline needs for similar accuracy. When combined with test-time scaling, the method solves hard out-of-distribution puzzles far more efficiently than prior approaches.
AI.conclusion: Flow models, despite initially performing poorly on structured reasoning, contain internal dynamics that can be exploited both as verifiers and as signals to improve training. By using stable fixed points of denoising dynamics for test-time scaling and by refining the model with self-conditioning and direct preference optimization, Flow Reasoning Models achieve near-perfect accuracy on Sudoku and Zebra puzzles and efficiently generalize to harder, unseen puzzle distributions.

title: The strength of clinical evidence is recoverable from language model representations but not from their stated grades
summary: Large language models (LLMs) increasingly summarize clinical evidence, where a claim's weight depends on how strongly it is supported. Yet these models convey confidence poorly, and properties they never state, such as truth, are often readable from their activations. Whether a clinical model registers evidence strength, distinct from truth, and states it when asked is untested, and any such signal could be lexical. We compiled 45,134 clinical claims from six public sources, harmonized 20,611 into a four-level evidence grade under three independent frameworks, and tested 22 local, open-weight LLMs from several developers (0.6-70 billion parameters; general, medical, and reasoning), with lexical, truth, and cross-framework controls. A linear estimator recovered the grade in every model (median AUROC 71.8), yet decodability did not rise with scale and was weakest in reasoning models. The grade the models stated fell to chance, 25-27 percentage points below the estimator. The recoverable signal was largely lexical and did not transfer across topics or frameworks, yet it was distinct from factual truth and still flagged weakly supported claims (AUROC 69.2). Clinical LLMs thus carry an ordered evidence-strength signal they do not express, so their stated grades fail to convey a claim's support even when it is recoverable from their representations and text.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that clinical large language models internally encode how strong the supporting evidence is for medical claims, but they do not reliably express this evidence strength when asked, and the signal is mostly driven by lexical cues and does not generalize well across topics or grading frameworks.
AI.motivation: In clinical settings, summarizing evidence accurately is critical because treatment and policy decisions depend not just on whether a claim is true, but on how strong the underlying evidence is. Current large language models are increasingly used to summarize clinical evidence but are known to convey confidence unreliably, and prior work shows that some properties (like truthfulness) can be decoded from hidden activations even when the model does not state them. It is unknown whether models similarly encode the *strength of evidence* behind clinical claims as a separate concept from truth, whether this encoding is genuinely internal rather than just lexical, and whether models can or do express this evidence strength when prompted. This gap motivates a systematic study of how evidence strength is represented and expressed in clinical and general LLMs.
AI.method: The authors aggregate 45,134 clinical claims from six public datasets and harmonize 20,611 of them into a unified four-level evidence grading scale, aligned across three independent evidence frameworks. They then evaluate 22 locally runnable, open‑weight large language models of varying sizes (0.6–70B parameters), spanning general-purpose, medical-domain, and reasoning-focused models. Using linear probes on model activations, they attempt to decode the evidence grade and measure performance via AUROC, while controlling for confounds such as lexical features, factual truth labels, and differences across grading frameworks. They also compare the decoded grades to grades that models explicitly output when asked to rate evidence strength, and they test cross-topic and cross-framework generalization of the decoded signal.
AI.result: A simple linear estimator applied to model representations can recover the four-level evidence grade in all 22 models, with a median AUROC of 71.8, indicating a consistent but imperfect internal signal of evidence strength. This decodability does not systematically improve with model scale and is actually lowest in reasoning-specialized models. In contrast, when models are directly asked to state the evidence grade, their performance drops to near chance, performing 25–27 percentage points worse than the linear estimator. Analysis shows that much of the decodable signal is driven by lexical cues and fails to transfer robustly across topics or across different evidence frameworks. However, the evidence-strength signal is statistically separable from factual truth and still enables detection of weakly supported claims (AUROC 69.2).
AI.conclusion: Clinical and general LLMs encode an ordered signal corresponding to the strength of clinical evidence behind claims, but they largely fail to expose this information in their explicit outputs, even when it is reliably recoverable from their internal representations. The underlying signal is heavily influenced by lexical features and does not generalize strongly across topics or grading schemes, yet it is distinct from simple factual correctness and can still identify weakly supported claims. Consequently, current clinical LLMs are unreliable at communicating how well claims are supported by evidence, highlighting a mismatch between internal representations and expressed uncertainty and indicating a need for improved training or interfaces that explicitly align evidence-strength representations with model outputs.

title: Pooled Leaderboards Hide System-Specific Winners: A Reporting-Protocol Audit of Offline Root-Cause Analysis Benchmarks
summary: Offline root-cause-analysis (RCA) benchmarks commonly rank methods by a single pooled top-1 accuracy across multiple subsystems, and engineers often read the pooled winner as a recommendation for their own subsystem. We audit that reading on three public RCA benchmark families -- OpenRCA, RCAEval, and PetShop -- covering 11 subsystems and 778 matched scoring units. To keep pairwise comparisons on identical cases, the main analysis retains four methods or comparators with complete coverage: BARO, a CD-1min adapter, max-$|Z|$, and per-service alert-count. All six pairwise comparisons show subsystem-level effects of both signs, every random-effects 95\% prediction interval crosses zero, and case-level interaction tests reject exchangeability in 5 of 6 pairs. Leave-one-system-out selection picks the lower-scoring method on up to 5 of 11 held-out subsystems, with regret reaching 24.8 pp on RCAEval / Sock-Shop. We release a 320-line audit module; given a matched RCA benchmark score table, it recomputes the same per-subsystem stability checks alongside pooled scores.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that aggregated (pooled) accuracy metrics in offline root-cause-analysis benchmarks can be misleading for specific subsystems, and proposes an audit procedure to check stability across subsystems.
AI.motivation: Engineers often choose RCA methods based on a single pooled top-1 accuracy score reported over many subsystems, implicitly assuming that the method that wins overall will also work best in their particular subsystem. The authors suspect this assumption is flawed and want to test how stable these pooled rankings are at the subsystem level.
AI.method: They audit three public RCA benchmark families (OpenRCA, RCAEval, PetShop), covering 11 subsystems and 778 matched scoring units. To ensure fair pairwise comparison, they restrict attention to four methods with complete coverage (BARO, a CD-1min adapter, max-|Z|, and per-service alert-count). They perform pairwise comparisons across methods on identical cases, compute subsystem-level effects and random-effects prediction intervals, test for case-level interaction and exchangeability, and perform leave-one-system-out selection experiments to assess how often pooled winners fail on held-out subsystems.
AI.result: Across all six pairwise method comparisons, subsystem-specific performance differences vary in sign (each method can win or lose depending on the subsystem). Every random-effects 95% prediction interval for subsystem effects crosses zero, and interaction tests reject exchangeability in 5 of 6 method pairs, indicating strong heterogeneity. In leave-one-system-out experiments, selecting based on pooled performance can pick the worse method on up to 5 of 11 subsystems, with performance regret up to 24.8 percentage points on RCAEval / Sock-Shop.
AI.conclusion: Benchmarking RCA methods via a single pooled top-1 accuracy is unreliable for choosing methods for specific subsystems because performance is highly heterogeneous and unstable across systems. The authors provide a small audit module (about 320 lines) that, given a matched RCA score table, recomputes pooled scores and adds per-subsystem stability diagnostics, enabling more cautious and subsystem-aware method selection.

title: Masked Diffusion Decoding as $x$-Prediction Flow
summary: Masked diffusion language models (MDLMs) generate text by iteratively unmasking tokens, but their standard decoder reduces each step to a binary action: a position is either committed to a single token or left fully masked, with no representation of partial belief in between. This all-or-nothing regime discards rich predictive information and forces premature, irrevocable commitments, leading to poor performance under a limited decoding budget. In this paper, we reinterpret mask prediction as clean-state prediction ($x$-prediction) and show that it can be used to induce a continuous flow in input embedding space. Building on this view, we propose a continuous decoding framework for MDLMs where tokens can accumulate partial progress at each diffusion step and remain revisable. To match the uneven contextual constraints across positions in language, we replace the globally synchronous schedule in image diffusion with a confidence-based asynchronous update in which the diffusion progress is token-wise accumulated. Additionally, we introduce a lightweight policy network and formulate its training as a reinforcement learning problem. Applied to pretrained LLaDA, our continuous decoder reaches 97% of its performance on the HumanEval dataset with 25% of decoding budget.

AI.tldr: They redesign how masked diffusion language models decode text, moving from binary mask/unmask steps to a continuous, revisable token update process that uses partial beliefs, asynchronous token-wise progress, and a small RL-trained policy to achieve similar performance with far fewer decoding steps.
AI.motivation: Current masked diffusion language models commit each position to a specific token or keep it fully masked at each step, throwing away intermediate uncertainty and forcing early, irreversible choices. This makes them sample-inefficient and weak when the number of decoding steps (budget) is limited. The authors want a decoding mechanism that preserves and exploits partial beliefs, allows revisions, and adapts per token to differing contextual certainty, thereby improving performance under tight decoding budgets.
AI.method: They reinterpret mask prediction as predicting the clean underlying token embeddings (x-prediction) and use this to define a continuous flow in embedding space rather than discrete mask/unmask actions. On top of this, they design a continuous decoding framework where each token position can accumulate partial progress at each diffusion step and stay revisable over time. Instead of a globally synchronous schedule (like in image diffusion), they use a confidence-based asynchronous update where each token’s diffusion progress is updated token-wise according to its confidence. They also add a lightweight policy network that controls aspects of the decoding process, trained via reinforcement learning to optimize task performance under a decoding budget. They apply this to an existing MDLM, LLaDA.
AI.result: On HumanEval, their continuous decoder achieves 97% of the performance of the base LLaDA model but using only 25% of the decoding budget, showing much higher sample efficiency. This demonstrates that leveraging continuous token-level progress, asynchronous scheduling, and an RL-trained policy can substantially reduce computation while preserving accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Recasting MDLM decoding as continuous x-prediction in embedding space and allowing tokens to accumulate partial, revisable progress leads to far more efficient generation than standard binary mask/unmask decoding. Confidence-based asynchronous token updates better match language’s uneven certainty structure, and a small RL-trained policy further improves budget-aware decoding. Overall, continuous decoding can retain nearly full performance with a fraction of the steps, suggesting a promising direction for more efficient diffusion-based language models.

title: Direct Causation in International Humanitarian Law and the Challenge of AI-Mediated Civilian Cyber Operations
comment: 11 pages, 1 figure, Workshop on Technical AI Governance Research ICML 2026
summary: International humanitarian law protects civilians from direct attack unless and for such time as they take direct part in hostilities, with the ICRC's 2009 Interpretive Guidance operationalising this rule through a three-criterion cumulative test. This paper argues that AI-mediated civilian cyber operations challenge the direct causation element of this test in a structurally specific way: when a civilian deploys an autonomous multi-agent cyber system of the kind recently demonstrated in offensive AI research, the "one causal step" standard fails because harm is produced by system-generated decisions made after human disengagement, and the integral-part requirement does not extend because it presupposes downstream human contributors whose conduct can be independently classified. The framework therefore defaults to treating such deployments as indirect participation, in tension with its purpose of capturing civilians who personally take part in hostilities. Beyond the doctrinal analysis, this paper identifies goal-specification granularity as the property on which the integral-part test's concreteness component implicitly turns, classifies AI-mediated operations along a five-level spectrum, and argues that existing technical AI governance instruments do not log or report this property.

AI.tldr: The paper examines how AI-driven autonomous cyber operations by civilians undermine the current legal test for when civilians lose protection from attack under international humanitarian law (IHL).
AI.motivation: There is a growing use of AI and autonomous systems in cyber operations, including by civilians, and existing IHL doctrine—especially the ICRC's 2009 Interpretive Guidance on Direct Participation in Hostilities—was developed with human-controlled, discrete acts in mind. The author wants to test whether the current three-part test for direct participation (threshold of harm, direct causation, and belligerent nexus) can handle AI-mediated cyber activities where harm results from autonomous system decisions after the human operator has disengaged.
AI.method: The paper conducts a doctrinal and conceptual legal analysis of the ICRC's three-criterion cumulative test, with a focus on the direct causation and 'integral part of a concrete and coordinated tactical operation' elements. It uses recent offensive AI research demonstrating autonomous multi-agent cyber systems as case studies or paradigms to test how the law applies, and then introduces a conceptual framework based on goal-specification granularity and a five-level spectrum of AI-mediated operations.
AI.result: The analysis shows that, for AI-mediated autonomous multi-agent cyber systems, the 'one causal step' standard in the direct causation test breaks down because harm is caused by decisions made by the system after the human has stopped acting. The attempt to save such cases via the 'integral part of a concrete and coordinated tactical operation' notion also fails, since that concept presupposes identifiable downstream human actors whose conduct can be legally classified. As a result, current doctrine tends to treat such AI-mediated deployments as only indirect participation, even though civilians are meaningfully engaging in hostilities. The paper also finds that goal-specification granularity is the key hidden variable in assessing the concreteness of an operation, and maps AI-mediated operations onto a five-level spectrum, while noting that present AI governance tools do not capture or report this variable.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that AI-mediated civilian cyber operations reveal a structural gap in IHL's concept of direct participation in hostilities, particularly in the direct causation and integral-part elements of the ICRC's test. This gap risks under-including civilians who meaningfully contribute to harm via autonomous systems, contrary to the framework's purpose. The author argues that legal doctrine and technical governance need to be updated to recognise and measure goal-specification granularity in AI systems, and to better classify AI-mediated cyber activities along a spectrum of participation so that civilians' loss of protection in armed conflict can be more accurately determined.

title: ThinkProbe: Beyond Accuracy -- Structural Profiling of Open-Ended LLM Reasoning Traces via Non-Generative Thought Graphs
summary: We present ThinkProbe, a framework for structural analysis of LLM reasoning traces.
