ThinkProbe converts each trace into a Thought Graph a directed graph with cycles, 8 node types, and 6 edge types and derives a 19-metric five-dimensional cognitive profile (5D-CP: Breadth, Depth, Structure, Metacognitive, Efficiency) through a fully non-generative pipeline combining rule-based segmentation and discriminative semantic linking. Applied to 4{,}200 traces from 7 native reasoning models across 200 open-ended questions and 10 cognitive domains, ThinkProbe reveals that reasoning structure is a stable, model-level property: between-model variance exceeds between-domain variance by up to fourfold across four of five cognitive dimensions, with Structure showing genuine sensitivity to question domain, exposing qualitatively distinct cognitive profiles invisible to accuracy-based evaluation.

AI.tldr: ThinkProbe is a non-generative framework that converts LLM reasoning traces into structured Thought Graphs and a 5D cognitive profile, revealing stable, model-level reasoning structures beyond accuracy metrics.
AI.motivation: Existing evaluations of large language models focus mainly on accuracy or final answers, failing to capture the internal structure and style of reasoning. There is a need for systematic, scalable structural analysis of reasoning traces that is model-agnostic and does not rely on generative judging models.
AI.method: ThinkProbe turns each LLM reasoning trace into a Thought Graph (a directed, possibly cyclic graph with 8 node types and 6 edge types). It then computes a 19-metric five-dimensional cognitive profile (Breadth, Depth, Structure, Metacognitive, Efficiency) using a non-generative pipeline that combines rule-based segmentation of thoughts and discriminative semantic linking to construct and analyze the graph.
AI.result: On 4,200 reasoning traces from 7 native reasoning models, across 200 open-ended questions in 10 cognitive domains, ThinkProbe shows that structural properties of reasoning are stable at the model level. Variance between models along most cognitive dimensions is up to 4x larger than variance between domains, and the Structure dimension in particular is sensitive to domain differences. These structural profiles expose distinct model reasoning behaviors that accuracy-based evaluation alone cannot see.
AI.conclusion: ThinkProbe provides a practical framework to characterize and compare LLMs by the structure of their reasoning traces. Its 5D cognitive profiles reveal consistent, model-specific reasoning styles and domain-sensitive structural features, demonstrating that structural analysis adds complementary insights beyond traditional accuracy metrics.

title: Selective Memory Retention for Long-Horizon LLM Agents
comment: Accepted at the International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML) 2026
summary: When does retention matter for memory-augmented LLM agents? We study this with TraceRetain, a lightweight framework for bounded external memory in frozen LLM agents that scores entries by interpretable features (success, age, access frequency, redundancy, specificity, similarity, downstream utility) and evicts the lowest-scoring ones at capacity. On clean ALFWorld with gpt-5-mini, external memory robustly improves over no memory across two seeds, but differences among bounded retention policies fall within Wilson 95% CIs: clean ALFWorld at T=100 to T=200 does not naturally exhibit the memory pollution retention is designed to address. Under a controlled noisy-write stress (75% synthetic distractors), unbounded memory and FIFO-K50 degrade on Precision@5 (20.2% to 12.4% and 15.8% to 3.8%) while TraceRetain-CEM is essentially unchanged (16.9% to 16.6%) and preserves 97/100 task success. The mechanism: unbounded memory has the highest mean similarity (0.87) but lowest precision, indicating failed distractors close to the query in embedding space. Held-out in-distribution evaluation shows memory-augmented policies solving 47 to 49 of 50 tasks vs. 39/50 for no memory. Bounded retention buys memory and step efficiency on saturated clean benchmarks at no task-success cost, and only differentiates from cache heuristics when streams contain noise.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces TraceRetain, a lightweight, interpretable retention framework for memory-augmented LLM agents, and shows that sophisticated retention only matters under noisy, distractor-heavy memory streams; on clean tasks, bounded memory helps but retention strategies perform similarly.
AI.motivation: Memory-augmented LLM agents can benefit from external memories, but in practical deployments memory is bounded, and naïve retention policies can cause memory pollution, especially when many irrelevant or failed experiences are written. There is limited empirical understanding of when sophisticated retention schemes actually help over simple caching or FIFO, and under what conditions memory quality impacts downstream task success and efficiency.
AI.method: The authors propose TraceRetain, a frozen-LLM-compatible framework that scores memory entries using interpretable features such as success, age, access frequency, redundancy, specificity, similarity, and downstream utility. When the external memory reaches capacity, the lowest-scoring entries are evicted. They evaluate several retention variants (including TraceRetain-CEM) against baselines like unbounded memory and FIFO-K on ALFWorld tasks using gpt-5-mini, under both clean conditions and a controlled noisy-write setting with 75% synthetic distractors. Metrics include task success, Precision@5 of retrieved memories, similarity statistics, and step efficiency.
AI.result: On clean ALFWorld (T=100–200) with gpt-5-mini, using external memory improves performance over no memory and memory-augmented policies solve 47–49/50 tasks versus 39/50 without memory. However, differences among bounded retention policies lie within Wilson 95% confidence intervals, implying no statistically clear advantage between sophisticated and simple retention on clean data. Under a noisy-write stress test with 75% distractors, unbounded memory and FIFO-K50 suffer substantial drops in Precision@5 (20.2%→12.4% and 15.8%→3.8%), while TraceRetain-CEM remains stable (16.9%→16.6%) and preserves 97/100 task successes. Analysis shows unbounded memory has the highest mean similarity (0.87) but lowest precision, suggesting that many retrieved items are near in embedding space but semantically unhelpful distractors.
AI.conclusion: Bounded external memory reliably improves performance and step efficiency of LLM agents even on saturated, clean benchmarks, and does so without harming task success. However, among different bounded retention policies, sophisticated approaches like TraceRetain only show clear benefits over simple cache heuristics when the memory stream contains substantial noise or distractors. In noisy settings, TraceRetain-like retention maintains high precision and task success by filtering out misleading but superficially similar failures, whereas unbounded or FIFO-based memory becomes polluted and degrades retrieval quality.

title: A Comparative Study on Affective Cues in Text Embeddings Across Psychological Emotion Theories
summary: Text encoders are known for their utility in natural language processing, as they are able to efficiently compress inputs into dense vectors while preserving semantics. These models have been applied to affective computing, in particular to help with solving sentiment analysis and emotion recognition tasks. Nevertheless, it remains unclear to what extent the latent representations produced by modern text encoders capture well-defined psychological theories of affect. In this work, we investigate the affective capabilities of twelve recently released text encoders by probing their generated embeddings as input features for solving regression and classification tasks across three established emotion frameworks, using both word- and sentence-level data. Additionally, we apply a semantic data-leakage prevention technique to improve robustness in word-level evaluations. Our main findings show that the latent manifolds of the latest instruction-aware open-weight encoders enclose an equal or even a larger amount of affective information in comparison with proprietary counterparts when evaluated at word level. In contrast, embeddings of task-tuned and proprietary encoders reach the highest scores on sentence-level affective classification. Furthermore, a qualitative analysis of latent representations and their encoded affective cues is provided.

AI.tldr: Paper probes whether modern text encoders’ embeddings naturally encode psychological theories of affect, comparing 12 encoders on word- and sentence-level emotion tasks.
AI.motivation: Text encoders are widely used and known to capture semantics, and they are increasingly used for sentiment and emotion tasks. However, it is unclear if their latent representations align with well-established psychological models of affect, beyond just task performance. The authors want to systematically assess how much affective information is inherently present in different contemporary encoders, and how this varies across architectures, instruction-tuning, openness, and evaluation granularity (words vs. sentences).
AI.method: Evaluate twelve recent text encoders (including open instruction-aware and proprietary/task-tuned ones) by freezing them and using their embeddings as features in downstream models for regression and classification across three established emotion frameworks. Conduct experiments both at the word level and sentence level. Introduce a semantic data-leakage prevention technique for more robust word-level evaluation. Complement quantitative scores with qualitative analysis of the latent spaces and the affective cues they encode.
AI.result: At the word level, recent instruction-aware open-weight encoders encode as much or more affective information than proprietary encoders. At the sentence level, task-tuned and proprietary encoders achieve the best performance on affective classification tasks. The data-leakage prevention improves robustness of the word-level evaluation. The qualitative examination reveals structured affective organization in the latent manifolds and highlights specific encoded affective cues.
AI.conclusion: Modern text encoders do embed substantial affective information that corresponds to psychological emotion frameworks, but how well this information is expressed depends on the encoder type and granularity. Open instruction-aware encoders are highly competitive or superior at word-level affect representation, while proprietary and task-tuned models excel for sentence-level emotion tasks. Probing and qualitative analysis together suggest that affect is a salient, structured component of current text embedding spaces, with implications for affective computing model selection and evaluation.

title: Measuring Graph-to-Graph Semantic Similarity in Knowledge Graphs: An Empirical Evaluation of Knowledge Graph Embeddings
comment: 9 pages, 2 figures, 6 tables. Accepted as a poster at The 2nd Frontiers in Graph Machine Learning for the Large Model Era (GMLLM'26) Workshop, co-located with KDD 2026
summary: A Knowledge Graph (KG) represents facts as structured triples and is widely used to organize relational knowledge across diverse domains. Just as textual information ranges from words and sentences to complete documents, KG information can be interpreted at multiple levels, from entities, relations, and triples to subgraphs and entire KGs. However, existing KG embedding methods mainly focus on entities, relations, and triples, leaving graph-level semantics largely unaddressed. Conventional graph-level methods, which typically compare graphs based on structural patterns, are also insufficient because structural similarity alone cannot guarantee semantic similarity between KGs. To evaluate how well different methods capture such graph-level semantic information, we study graph-to-graph semantic similarity, which determines whether a pair of KGs represents semantically corresponding underlying information. To obtain reliable ground-truth correspondences, we construct a semantic matching dataset by modifying text documents, extracting KGs from both original and modified documents, and transferring their known correspondences to KG pairs. We compare text-based, structure-based, and KG embedding-based approaches on each dataset. For the KG embedding-based approach, we introduce two scoring functions: \textit{EmbPairSim}, which uses maximal pairwise entity similarity, and \textit{AvgEmbSim}, which uses a frequency-weighted centroid. Experiments on WikiText-2 and CC-News show that \textit{EmbPairSim} achieves up to 5.3 pp higher MRR than Sentence-BERT while using substantially fewer parameters. These results suggest that KGE representations can serve as compact and effective signals for graph-to-graph semantic similarity in KGs. Our code is available at https://github.com/SeungRyeolBaek/KG-to-KG-Semantic-Similarity.

AI.tldr: They study how well different methods capture semantic similarity between whole knowledge graphs by building a KG-to-KG matching benchmark and showing simple KGE-based scores outperform strong text baselines with fewer parameters.
AI.motivation: Most knowledge graph embedding methods model semantics at the level of entities, relations, and triples, but many applications need to compare entire knowledge graphs. Existing graph-level methods focus on structural similarity, which does not necessarily reflect semantic similarity. There is also a lack of reliable benchmarks to evaluate graph-to-graph semantic similarity in KGs. The paper aims to fill this gap by defining the task, constructing a semantic matching dataset, and systematically evaluating different approaches.
AI.method: They define graph-to-graph semantic similarity as detecting whether two KGs encode corresponding underlying information. To obtain ground-truth correspondences, they start from text documents, create modified document versions, and extract KGs from both originals and modifications. Since text-level correspondences are known, they transfer these labels to KG pairs, forming a benchmark. They then evaluate three families of approaches: text-based (e.g., Sentence-BERT on source documents), structure-based graph comparison, and KG-embedding-based approaches. For the KGE-based approach, they propose two scoring functions built on pretrained KG embeddings: (1) EmbPairSim, which computes maximal pairwise entity similarities between graphs, and (2) AvgEmbSim, which compares frequency-weighted centroid embeddings of entities in each graph. They test on KGs derived from WikiText-2 and CC-News corpora.
AI.result: Across datasets, KG embedding-based similarity, particularly EmbPairSim, consistently outperforms strong text-based and structural baselines on ranking metrics such as MRR. On WikiText-2 and CC-News, EmbPairSim improves MRR by up to 5.3 percentage points over Sentence-BERT while using substantially fewer model parameters, indicating a more compact yet effective representation for graph-to-graph semantic similarity. AvgEmbSim also performs competitively but slightly below EmbPairSim.
AI.conclusion: Graph-level semantic similarity between knowledge graphs is not adequately captured by traditional structural methods or solely text-based approaches. KG embeddings, when combined with appropriate graph-level scoring functions, can provide compact and powerful signals for comparing whole KGs. The proposed benchmark construction pipeline and the EmbPairSim/AvgEmbSim scoring functions demonstrate that KGE-based methods are promising for graph-to-graph semantic similarity tasks and can outperform large text encoders with lower parameter budgets.

title: Evolution Fine-Tuning: Learning to Discover Across 371 Optimization Tasks
summary: Would experience designing faster GPU kernels also help close in on a long-standing open mathematical conjecture? Large Language Models (LLMs) integrated into evolutionary search have recently produced state-of-the-art solutions on optimization tasks, including open mathematical conjectures, GPU kernel design, scientific law discovery, and combinatorial puzzles. To achieve this, prior work applied search scaffolds to one target task at a time, so every new problem is approached from scratch and the experience accumulated during search is discarded once the model finishes its attempt. This leaves the capability of iteratively evolving a solution (e.g., knowing which part to mutate and how, deciding when to backtrack) entirely in the scaffold rather than in the model itself. Whether the model itself could acquire this capability and reuse it across different tasks has been largely unexamined. To address this, we introduce Evolution Fine-Tuning (EFT), a mid-training paradigm that teaches LLMs to evolve solutions across tasks by converting evolutionary search trajectories into supervision. We construct Finch Collection, a 156K-trajectory dataset spanning 10 domains and 371 optimization tasks, and fine-tune open-source LLMs from 2B to 9B parameters. Empirically, EFT confers cross-task generalization: across 22 held-out tasks, our models surpass their base counterparts by 10.22% on average. Furthermore, when paired with test-time RL, our model matches state-of-the-art performance on two circle-packing tasks and outperforms its base-model counterpart on the Erdős minimum-overlap problem. EFT thus serves as a "practice phase" for general-purpose discovery agents that do not solve new problems from scratch.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Evolution Fine-Tuning (EFT), a training paradigm where LLMs learn to perform evolutionary search across tasks using past search trajectories, improving cross-task optimization and mathematical discovery performance.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-based evolutionary search systems reach state-of-the-art on diverse optimization and discovery problems, but each task is treated independently: search scaffolds drive the evolution and accumulated experience is discarded after each problem. This prevents the model itself from learning how to evolve solutions (what to mutate, when to backtrack, etc.) and from reusing that capability across tasks. The authors aim to create LLMs that internalize the evolutionary search process so they can generalize and accelerate discovery on new tasks without starting from scratch.
AI.method: They introduce Evolution Fine-Tuning (EFT), a mid-training procedure that converts recorded evolutionary search trajectories into supervised training data. The authors build Finch Collection, a dataset of 156K trajectories spanning 10 domains and 371 optimization tasks, and use it to fine-tune open-source LLMs (2B–9B parameters). During EFT, the model is trained to propose and refine solutions based on prior evolutionary steps, effectively learning patterns of successful mutation, refinement, and backtracking. They also combine the EFT-trained models with test-time reinforcement learning for further performance gains on challenging tasks.
AI.result: On 22 held-out optimization tasks, EFT-tuned models outperform their base models by an average of 10.22%, demonstrating cross-task generalization of the learned evolutionary behaviors. When integrated with test-time RL, the EFT model achieves state-of-the-art performance on two circle-packing problems and surpasses the base model on the Erdős minimum-overlap problem, indicating improved capability on difficult mathematical and combinatorial optimization benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Evolution Fine-Tuning enables LLMs to internalize and reuse evolutionary search strategies across tasks, turning them into more general-purpose discovery agents. Instead of relying solely on external scaffolds and solving each new problem from scratch, models trained with EFT can leverage prior experience encoded in search trajectories to more effectively explore solution spaces and tackle new optimization and mathematical discovery problems.

title: Evidence-Informed LLM Beliefs for Continual Scientific Discovery
summary: Open-ended scientific discovery with large language models (LLMs) increasingly operates as a long-horizon loop of hypothesis search and verification, where a reward signal guides which hypotheses to test next. A notable recent example is AutoDiscovery, which uses "Bayesian surprise" - the belief shift an LLM undergoes after observing evidence for a hypothesis - as both a discovery metric and a reward for search. We first observe that AutoDiscovery treats surprisal as a static quantity, while surprisal in human reasoning is non-stationary - it is defined relative to beliefs that evolve with experience, a prerequisite for continual scientific discovery. We address this mismatch with evidence-informed LLM beliefs: priors updated with evidence from previous hypotheses to compute non-stationary surprisal for new hypotheses. We compare in-context belief-updating mechanisms and find that embedding-based retrieval-augmented generation over prior discoveries best anticipates eventual posteriors, identifying 37.5% of static surprisals as spurious. We then modify search to avoid these spurious rewards and prioritize hypotheses that remain surprising under non-stationary beliefs. Concretely, we introduce two complementary changes to the original search procedure: belief-update filtering and diversity maximization. Across five discovery domains, our method increases accumulated non-stationary surprisal by 30.62% on average compared to the original search procedure, demonstrating that continual scientific discovery with LLMs requires not only better belief measurement but also search procedures that avoid redundancy and encourage diversity.

AI.tldr: The paper improves open-ended scientific discovery with LLMs by making ‘surprisal’ non-stationary via explicit belief updating, and by adapting the search strategy to avoid redundant, spurious rewards, leading to more diverse and informative discoveries across several domains.
AI.motivation: Existing systems like AutoDiscovery use “Bayesian surprise” (belief shift after seeing new evidence) as a static reward to guide hypothesis search. However, human-like scientific discovery is continual: beliefs evolve over time as more evidence accumulates. If an LLM’s surprise measure does not change with prior discoveries, the system can reward redundant or already-explained hypotheses, wasting search budget and limiting true novelty. The authors want a more realistic, dynamic notion of surprise that tracks evolving beliefs, and a search procedure that exploits this to drive ongoing, non-redundant discovery.
AI.method: 1) Formalize ‘non-stationary surprisal’ by explicitly updating the LLM’s prior beliefs with evidence from previously tested hypotheses, then measuring surprise for new hypotheses relative to these updated beliefs. 2) Implement and compare several in-context belief-updating mechanisms; empirically find that retrieval-augmented generation over past discoveries (using embeddings to retrieve relevant prior evidence and conditioning the LLM on them) best predicts the LLM’s eventual posterior beliefs. 3) Use this improved belief model to re-evaluate surprisal scores from the original AutoDiscovery procedure, flagging cases where static surprisal appears high but drops significantly once beliefs are updated; these are labeled as “spurious surprisals.” 4) Modify the hypothesis search algorithm with (a) belief-update filtering, which downweights or removes hypotheses whose surprisal largely disappears under updated beliefs, and (b) diversity maximization, encouraging exploration of hypotheses that differ from previous ones in content or implications. 5) Evaluate across five scientific discovery domains, comparing accumulated non-stationary surprisal under the new search procedure versus the original AutoDiscovery-like baseline.
AI.result: Embedding-based retrieval-augmented belief updating most closely matches the LLM’s eventual posteriors and reveals that 37.5% of hypotheses that appeared surprising under static surprisal are actually spurious once prior evidence is accounted for. Incorporating belief-update filtering and diversity maximization into the search loop increases accumulated non-stationary surprisal by an average of 30.62% across five different discovery domains, indicating that the system discovers more genuinely informative and non-redundant hypotheses over time than the original AutoDiscovery-style search.
AI.conclusion: Static measures of Bayesian surprise are insufficient for long-horizon, open-ended scientific discovery with LLMs because they ignore the continual evolution of beliefs and thus over-reward redundant or already-explained hypotheses. Updating LLM beliefs with evidence from prior discoveries to compute non-stationary surprisal, combined with search strategies that filter spurious rewards and promote diversity, yields significantly more informative and diverse discoveries across domains. Continual scientific discovery with LLMs thus requires both more faithful belief measurement and appropriately adapted search procedures, rather than focusing on scoring functions alone.

title: AB-RAG: Adaptive Budgeted Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Reliable Question Answering
summary: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) has become the standard way to ground large language models in external knowledge, yet most systems retrieve a fixed number of passages for every question regardless of its difficulty. This wastes computation on easy questions, starves hard ones, and gives no signal for when a generated answer can be trusted. With a growing share of question answering systems built on top of commercial language model APIs, a method that can decide how much to retrieve, and how far to trust its own answers, without retraining the underlying model, is of clear practical value. This paper presents AB-RAG (Adaptive Budgeted Retrieval-Augmented Generation), a training-free and backbone-agnostic framework that generates an answer, estimates its confidence from a combination of three signals, and then decides whether to stop or to retrieve more evidence, subject to a fixed retrieval budget. The estimator combines the model's own certainty, the agreement between the answer and the evidence, and the variance of the retrieval scores. For models that expose token probabilities the certainty signal is read directly; for closed APIs it is approximated by self-consistency, so the method works without access to model internals. Across three backbones and two datasets, the central result is that the confidence estimate reliably separates correct from incorrect answers on every backbone, reaching a clean split of 57.6% against 0% Exact Match between high- and low-confidence answers on a factoid dataset. The adaptive policy improves accuracy on capable backbones, and the study reports its negative and nuanced findings honestly, including a confidence signal that proved unsuitable for short answers and a retrieval signal whose sign was found and corrected through measurement. The entire study was carried out on a single consumer laptop with only a few dollars of API spend.

AI.tldr: AB-RAG is a training-free, model-agnostic RAG framework that adaptively decides how much to retrieve and when to trust its own answers using a composite confidence estimator, improving efficiency and reliability without retraining the backbone model.
AI.motivation: Standard RAG systems retrieve a fixed number of passages per question, which is inefficient: easy questions get unnecessary retrieval while hard ones lack sufficient evidence, and users receive no indication of answer trustworthiness. With QA increasingly built on commercial LLM APIs where retraining is impossible or expensive, there is a need for a method that can dynamically allocate retrieval budget and estimate answer confidence without modifying or retraining the underlying model.
AI.method: AB-RAG is a training-free, backbone-agnostic framework that iteratively: (1) generates an answer, (2) estimates confidence using three signals—model certainty, agreement between answer and retrieved evidence, and variance of retrieval scores—and (3) decides whether to stop or retrieve more evidence, under a fixed retrieval budget. Model certainty is taken from token probabilities when available, or approximated via self-consistency for closed APIs. The system then uses this composite confidence score to adapt retrieval depth and decide when an answer is reliable enough to return.
AI.result: Across three different backbone models and two QA datasets, the confidence estimator consistently separates correct from incorrect answers, achieving a strong separation—for example, on a factoid dataset, high-confidence answers achieve 57.6% Exact Match while low-confidence answers achieve 0% EM. The adaptive retrieval policy improves answer accuracy on sufficiently capable backbones. The authors also uncover limitations, such as a confidence signal that fails for very short answers and a retrieval-related signal whose sign had to be corrected after empirical analysis.
AI.conclusion: AB-RAG demonstrates that adaptive, budgeted retrieval and confidence estimation can be added on top of existing LLMs— including closed commercial APIs—without retraining, yielding better use of retrieval budgets and more reliable answers. The work highlights both strengths and limitations of different confidence signals, suggests that simple, inexpensive setups (even a single laptop and low API cost) can support meaningful RAG research, and opens the door to more practical, trustworthy QA systems that know when to retrieve more and when not to trust themselves.

title: AI Trading's Alpha Singularity: Emergent Market Reasoning through Agent-to-Agent Self-Evolution
summary: Automated alpha mining holds the scoring function fixed and varies the search algorithm over it. A search that converges against a fixed scorer overfits whatever the scorer cannot penalize, a primary cause of the out-of-sample generalization gap. We treat the scoring function as a search artifact alongside the alpha factors and study what conditions make this joint search admissible. Sealed Joint Search (SJS) is a framework: a set of structural conditions on information flow in an autonomous-discovery system that prevent joint search from collapsing into self-confirmation while keeping the evaluator sealed. Conditions cover role decomposition, typed inter-role communication, provenance-sealed reads, versioned stores, and substrate-local promotion. Agora tests SJS empirically: five LLM agent classes communicate via three channels, evolving eight skill libraries, with alpha libraries built on AlphaGen operators. Three evaluators write reports aggregated into one brief, carrying forward disagreement instead of voting. We run Agora for 100 rounds on CSI 1000 and evaluate on a 91-day 2026 holdout sealed from all LLM inputs. Agora achieves holdout Sharpe +1.87; best baseline +1.334 at favorable seed and -0.755 cross-seed mean. Pre-loading Agora's two metrics into a frozen-library ablation recovers only +0.40 of the +2.25 Sharpe gap, and adding PPO without library evolution worsens the gap. The two metrics emerge rather than being designed. Caveats: single-seed run, short-side concentrated signal, intended for long-short.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Sealed Joint Search (SJS), a framework that jointly searches over both alpha factors and their scoring function while enforcing strict information-flow constraints to avoid overfitting and self-confirmation, and demonstrates it with an LLM-based system (Agora) that discovers trading alphas with strong out-of-sample performance.
AI.motivation: In automated alpha mining, existing approaches usually hold the scoring function fixed and only optimize the search over alpha factors. This leads to overfitting to whatever the fixed scorer cannot penalize, causing poor out-of-sample generalization. There is a need to let both the factors and the scoring function co-evolve, but without falling into self-confirmation or information leakage that would make evaluation invalid.
AI.method: The authors introduce Sealed Joint Search (SJS), defined as a set of structural constraints on an autonomous discovery system’s information flow: clear role decomposition among agents, typed communication between roles, provenance-sealed reads, versioned storage of artifacts, and substrate-local promotion of artifacts. They instantiate SJS in a system called Agora, where five classes of LLM-based agents communicate via three channels to evolve eight skill libraries, including alpha libraries based on AlphaGen operators. Three distinct evaluators generate reports that are aggregated into a single brief preserving disagreements instead of simple voting. The system is run for 100 rounds on the CSI 1000 universe, and the discovered alphas are evaluated on a fully held-out 91-day 2026 period with no LLM access to this data.
AI.result: Agora, under the SJS constraints, achieves an out-of-sample Sharpe ratio of +1.87 on the 2026 holdout period. Baseline methods achieve a best-case Sharpe of +1.334 under favorable random seed and a mean Sharpe of -0.755 across seeds, indicating poor robustness. An ablation study that pre-loads Agora’s two discovered metrics into a frozen-library setup recovers only +0.40 of the +2.25 Sharpe gap, and adding PPO without allowing library evolution further degrades performance. This suggests that the performance gains come from the emergent joint search dynamics rather than from hand-designed metrics or simple RL fine-tuning.
AI.conclusion: Jointly searching over both alpha factors and their scoring function can substantially improve out-of-sample performance if the search is subject to carefully designed information-flow and structural constraints. The SJS framework provides such constraints, preventing collapse into self-confirmation while keeping evaluators sealed from downstream components. The Agora implementation demonstrates that LLM-driven, library-evolving, multi-agent systems can autonomously discover strong trading signals and even emergent evaluation metrics, though the authors caution that results are based on a single-seed run, with signals concentrated on the short side and designed for long-short strategies.

title: Knowing in Advance When an Evolutionary Outer Loop Will Not Help: A Pre-Registered Cheap-Baseline Screening Rule
summary: We introduce a pre-registered screening rule that decides, before any implementation, whether an evolutionary / population / lifecycle outer loop over neural-network parameters or structure is worth building. Such outer loops cost 10^2-10^3x their gradient inner loop, yet whether they beat a cheap single-shot alternative is usually discovered only after the expense is paid. Our rule computes, at a Phase-0 gate, a single number: the recovery R = s/G, the best single-shot gradient/curvature statistic's gain s divided by the best gain G of any cheap method evaluated, and prescribes skipping the outer loop when R >= 90%. We validate the rule on a within-lab series of pre-registered outer-loop bets (two analyzed cases plus a disclosed file drawer): in both analyzed cases a static or single-shot computation captured the effect on the project's own metric, the gate fired (R approximately 1.0 in both cases; approximately 0.95 under a stricter metric on one), and the outer loop was abandoned, including one case where a companion factorial decomposition localizes the apparent win to a static substrate change with the evolutionary lifecycle contributing no detectable gain. On one project the gate cost about 50-70 GPU-hours and screened out an estimated 400+ GPU-hours (first cell only) plus weeks of implementation, a 6-8x saving. The rule is prospectively falsifiable: a task with R < 90% where the outer loop still fails to beat single-shot would refute it.

AI.tldr: They propose a simple, pre-registered quantitative rule (R = s/G) to decide in advance whether expensive evolutionary or population-based outer loops around neural networks are worth implementing, and show two case studies where it correctly recommended skipping the outer loop, saving substantial compute and time.
AI.motivation: Outer-loop methods (evolutionary algorithms, population-based training, lifecycle methods) wrapped around standard gradient-based training can be 100-1000x more expensive. Currently, researchers often only discover whether these costly methods outperform simpler single-shot baselines after the full implementation and computation, wasting resources when they fail. The authors want an upfront, principled way to predict when such outer loops are unlikely to be worth it.
AI.method: They define a "Phase-0 gate" based on a recovery metric R = s/G, where s is the gain from the best single-shot gradient/curvature-based statistic and G is the best gain from any other cheap method evaluated. If R >= 90%, the rule says to skip implementing the full outer loop. They preregistered this decision rule and applied it prospectively to multiple internal projects, including at least two detailed cases and additional file-drawer results, comparing the predicted decision to what would have happened with full outer-loop implementation or decompositions.
AI.result: In both analyzed case studies, simple static or single-shot methods captured essentially all of the observed performance improvement on the projects' own metrics (R ~ 1.0, or ~0.95 under a stricter metric in one case). The gate therefore recommended skipping the outer loop, and indeed the evolutionary or lifecycle components contributed no detectable additional gain beyond a static change. In one project, using the gate required only ~50-70 GPU-hours but avoided at least 400 GPU-hours plus weeks of engineering time, giving a 6–8x resource saving. Additional undisclosed "file drawer" cases are consistent with the rule.
AI.conclusion: The authors propose a simple, prospectively falsifiable screening rule for deciding whether to invest in expensive evolutionary or population-based outer loops for neural networks. Their initial preregistered case studies suggest that when single-shot methods already recover at least 90% of the best observed gains, outer loops are unlikely to add value, and can be safely skipped, yielding large savings in compute and engineering time. They emphasize that the rule can be refuted by future tasks where R < 90% but the outer loop still fails to outperform single-shot methods.

title: A Cognition-Emotion-Personality Framework for Modeling Human-Like Awareness and Behavior in Emergency Evacuations
summary: Agent-based evacuation simulations are widely used to study crowd behavior during emergencies, but many models rely on assumptions such as perfect event awareness, complete exit knowledge, and fully rational decision-making. This paper presents an extended evacuation framework that integrates cognitive, emotional, social, and personality-related mechanisms into a unified model of human behavior under uncertainty. The framework incorporates a dynamic event-awareness mechanism based on a continuous Event Certainty Level, a memory-based representation of exit knowledge subject to acquisition, forgetting, and recall, a continuous fear model in which panic emerges as a high-intensity state, and an OCEAN-based personality representation. Neuroticism is explicitly integrated into the emotional model, influencing fear generation, escalation, social contagion, and recovery. Behavioral heterogeneity is further captured through individualized decision thresholds that affect responses to perceived risk. The framework is evaluated through simulation experiments examining the effects of spatial familiarity, memory robustness, decision sensitivity, emotional dynamics, and personality variation. Results show that cognitive, emotional, and personality-driven processes substantially influence evacuation dynamics, reducing evacuation efficiency and generating realistic crowd phenomena such as delays, confusion, injuries, and socially influenced behaviors. The proposed framework provides a more realistic representation of human behavior in emergency evacuations and supports systematic investigation of the interactions between cognition, emotion, personality, and crowd dynamics.

AI.tldr: Extended agent-based evacuation framework modeling realistic human behavior under uncertainty.
AI.motivation: Traditional evacuation models assume perfect information and fully rational agents, which fails to capture realistic human responses like partial awareness, limited knowledge of exits, panic, and personality-driven differences. This paper aims to overcome these limitations by embedding richer psychological and social mechanisms into evacuation simulations.
AI.method: Develop an integrated agent-based framework that combines: (1) an Event Certainty Level to represent evolving awareness of the emergency; (2) a memory-based model of exit knowledge with acquisition, forgetting, and recall; (3) a continuous fear/emotion model where panic is high-intensity fear; (4) an OCEAN-based personality model, with a specific focus on integrating neuroticism into emotion dynamics; and (5) individualized decision thresholds governing risk responses. Conduct simulation experiments varying spatial familiarity, memory robustness, decision sensitivity, emotional parameters, and personality distributions to analyze emergent evacuation patterns.
AI.result: Simulations show that adding cognitive, emotional, social, and personality mechanisms significantly alters evacuation outcomes: evacuations become less efficient than in idealized models, and more realistic crowd phenomena emerge, including delays, confusion, injuries, and socially influenced behaviors. Spatial familiarity, memory reliability, emotional dynamics, and personality traits, especially neuroticism, all substantially affect evacuation performance and patterns.
AI.conclusion: The extended framework yields more realistic evacuation dynamics by explicitly modeling cognition, emotion, memory, and personality. It enables systematic exploration of how these factors interact to shape crowd behavior during emergencies, providing a richer basis for both scientific understanding and practical planning of evacuation strategies and safety designs.

title: How Anthropomorphic Language Impacts Public Perceptions of AI
summary: Public discourse about artificial intelligence (AI) often uses anthropomorphic language: language that attributes human capabilities and characteristics to the system. This practice has been criticized for setting misleading expectations, inflating claims, and fueling hype around AI, which may distort public understanding of AI and impact policy priorities. We study the effects of anthropomorphic framing by comparing changes in participants' perceptions (N=815) when reading passages with and without anthropomorphic language, designed to reflect realistic public-facing AI discourse. We further examine whether these effects differ across two types of AI technologies -- large language models and recommendation systems -- and measure changes in perceptions of AI across several dimensions that are prominent in current public discourse. In a separate condition using a text that explicitly discusses the dangers of AI, we show that individuals' views of AI can shift in response to reading a text; yet in the main conditions of the experiment, where we compare anthropomorphic and non-anthropomorphic descriptions, we find that whether the text uses anthropomorphic language does not substantially affect participants' perceptions of AI. Our results indicate that any immediate effects on public opinions of AI are modest, although they leave open the possibility that anthropomorphic language could have an effect in naturalistic settings, or over gradual, continued exposure.

AI.tldr: The paper experimentally tests whether anthropomorphic language in public-facing AI descriptions changes people’s perceptions and finds only modest, non-robust short-term effects.
AI.motivation: Anthropomorphic talk about AI is widely criticized for misleading the public and skewing policy, but there is little empirical evidence about how much wording actually shifts people’s views. The authors want to test whether the way AI is framed—anthropomorphic vs. non-anthropomorphic—meaningfully changes lay perceptions of AI systems.
AI.method: The authors run an experiment with 815 participants who read short passages about AI that either use anthropomorphic language or more neutral, technical wording. The texts are designed to resemble realistic public discourse and cover two AI types (large language models and recommender systems). Participants’ perceptions of AI are measured along several commonly debated dimensions. A separate condition uses a strongly critical, risk-focused text to test whether any text at all can move people’s views.
AI.result: Participants’ views of AI can be shifted when exposed to a text that explicitly emphasizes AI dangers, showing that opinions are somewhat malleable. However, in the main comparisons between anthropomorphic and non-anthropomorphic versions of otherwise similar passages, the wording difference produces little to no substantial change in assessed perceptions across the measured dimensions, for either large language models or recommendation systems.
AI.conclusion: In the short term and in a controlled experimental setting, anthropomorphic language in AI discourse does not strongly alter people’s stated perceptions of AI, even though more strongly valenced content can. The findings suggest that immediate framing effects from anthropomorphism alone are modest, while leaving open the possibility that such language could still matter in real-world contexts or through cumulative exposure over time.

title: PolicyGuard: A Dialogue-Grounded Sub-Agent Verifier for Policy Adherence in LLM Agents
summary: LLM agents handle user requests on behalf of organizations through tool calls and must follow the company policies stated in their system prompts. Prior work approaches this as a safeguarding problem -- external checks that block non-compliant agent actions. We argue that policy adherence is a broader problem: real workflows unfold across many turns, require explicit user confirmation and prerequisite reads, and hinge on the content of the dialogue rather than on any single argument value. Meeting this bar requires (i) full conversation context, (ii) self-reasoning over the policy and the current dialogue, and (iii) conversation-specific remediation that guides the agent's next turn -- three capabilities that prior safeguard work has often underestimated. We introduce POLICYGUARD, a sub-agent verifier that shares the agent's view of the dialogue, reasons over the policy in context, and provides actionable feedback for the agent's next turn. On tau^2-BENCH airline across three vendors (GPT-5.4, Claude Sonnet 4.6, Gemini 2.5 Pro) with four trials per setting, POLICYGUARD improves PASS4 by +12.0 / +6.0 / +12.0 pp. Per-call analyses show POLICYGUARD achieves higher policy-violation recall while blocking roughly half as often as argument-level guards.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes POLICYGUARD, a context-aware sub-agent that verifies and guides LLM agents to better follow organizational policies in multi-turn tool-using workflows.
AI.motivation: Existing approaches treat policy compliance for LLM agents mainly as a safeguarding or filtering problem that blocks non-compliant actions, usually at the level of single tool-call arguments. However, real-world workflows are multi-turn, depend on full dialogue context, need explicit user confirmations and prerequisite steps, and are governed by policies that cannot be reduced to local argument checks. Prior guards often lack full-context reasoning, deep policy understanding, and conversation-specific guidance, leading to either missed violations or excessive blocking.
AI.method: The authors introduce POLICYGUARD, a sub-agent verifier that runs alongside the main LLM agent. POLICYGUARD has access to the same conversation and tool context as the agent, explicitly reasons over the written policy and the current dialogue, and then produces structured, actionable feedback for what the agent should do next (e.g., ask for confirmation, refuse, or proceed under constraints). They evaluate it on the tau^2-BENCH airline benchmark across multiple commercial LLM vendors and multiple trials, comparing against traditional argument-level safeguard methods.
AI.result: On the tau^2-BENCH airline benchmark, across three vendor models (GPT-5.4, Claude Sonnet 4.6, Gemini 2.5 Pro) and four trials per setup, POLICYGUARD improves a key policy-adherence metric (PASS4) by +12.0 / +6.0 / +12.0 percentage points relative to baselines. Per-call analyses show that POLICYGUARD has higher recall of policy violations while also blocking about half as often as argument-level guards, indicating better precision and less over-conservatism.
AI.conclusion: Policy adherence for LLM agents should be framed as a full-conversation, reasoning-intensive control problem rather than as a narrow argument-level safeguard. A dedicated verifier sub-agent that shares context with the main agent and produces targeted remedial guidance can meaningfully improve policy compliance while reducing unnecessary blocking, as demonstrated by POLICYGUARD’s performance on an airline workflow benchmark across several frontier models.

title: DistilledGemma: Balanced Efficiency-Accuracy for Person-Place Relation Extraction from Multilingual Historical Articles
comment: The Conference and Labs of the Evaluation Forum (CLEF) 2026 - HIPE Challenge
summary: We present DistilledGemma, an efficient and accurate system for the HIPE-2026 shared task on person-place relation extraction from multilingual historical newspaper articles in English, German, and French. Our approach adopts a three-stage knowledge distillation pipeline designed to balance classification accuracy with computational efficiency. In the first stage, we systematically explored prompt engineering strategies across eight large language models to identify the most effective reasoning architecture for this challenging task. In the second stage, we applied supervised fine-tuning (SFT) via QLoRA to a Gemma 4 26B A4B teacher model, leveraging its strong multilingual capabilities to generate silver-standard chain-of-thought traces across the training corpus. In the final stage, we performed response-level distillation to transfer these learned reasoning patterns into a compact Gemma 4 E2B student model. In the official evaluation, our team WHEREAMI ranked 3rd on the standard test set with an accuracy profile mean score of 0.688, and 2nd on the binary test set with a mean score of 0.8156. Notably, by distilling knowledge from the 26B teacher to the 2.3B student, we preserved strong reasoning capabilities while reducing the deployed model size to approximately 2.3B effective parameters; the LoRA adapters used during training were merged into the student for inference. This configuration ranked 2nd in the balanced efficiency-accuracy profile across both the standard and binary test sets. These results demonstrate that knowledge distillation provides a practical and scalable solution for historical document processing, achieving competitive performance without excessive computational cost.

AI.tldr: DistilledGemma is a distilled multilingual model pipeline that achieves high accuracy and efficiency for person-place relation extraction in historical newspapers, ranking near the top in the HIPE-2026 shared task while using a compact 2.3B-parameter student model.
AI.motivation: Processing historical multilingual newspapers for person-place relation extraction is computationally expensive if using large language models directly. The authors aim to build a system that maintains strong reasoning and accuracy while greatly reducing model size and computational cost, enabling practical large-scale processing of historical documents.
AI.method: They design a three-stage knowledge distillation pipeline. (1) Prompt engineering experiments across eight LLMs to identify the best reasoning architecture for the person-place relation extraction task. (2) Supervised fine-tuning with QLoRA on a 26B-parameter multilingual Gemma 4 A4B teacher model, which generates silver-standard chain-of-thought reasoning traces for the training data. (3) Response-level distillation from this teacher into a smaller Gemma 4 E2B student model (~2.3B parameters), transferring the reasoning patterns; LoRA adapters are merged into the student for inference to keep deployment simple and efficient.
AI.result: On the HIPE-2026 shared task, their system ranks 3rd on the standard test set with an accuracy profile mean of 0.688 and 2nd on the binary test set with 0.8156. The distilled 2.3B-parameter student preserves strong reasoning while substantially reducing model size compared to the 26B teacher. Their configuration ranks 2nd overall in the balanced efficiency-accuracy profile across both test sets.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that their knowledge distillation pipeline is an effective and scalable way to apply LLMs to historical document processing. Distilling from a large multilingual teacher to a compact student model can retain strong reasoning performance and achieve competitive leaderboard results while greatly improving efficiency and lowering computational costs, making such systems more practical for real-world use.

title: SurgVLA-Bench: Towards Evaluating Vision-Language-Action Models for Laparoscopic Surgical Robotics
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models represent a promising direction for embodied intelligence in surgical robotics. Despite the prevalence of VLA benchmarks for general robotics, standardized evaluation platforms specifically designed for surgical contexts remain absent. To address this limitation, we present SurgVLA-Bench, the first comprehensive benchmark for evaluating VLA models in laparoscopic surgical robotics. Leveraging the SurRoL simulation platform, we construct a hierarchical task taxonomy ranging from atomic actions to complete surgical procedures, complemented by a multi-dimensional evaluation framework assessing action accuracy and semantic consistency. We then systematically evaluate two representative paradigms, including autoregressive models such as OpenVLA, and flow matching models such as $π_{0}$, $π_{0.5}$, and SmolVLA. Our experiments show that autoregressive models tend to excel in semantic understanding, while flow matching models often achieve higher task precision but may face generalization trade-offs. However, even the best-performing models remain far from satisfactory, as the constrained endoscopic field of view, restricted viewing angles, and frequent occlusions persist as fundamental physical bottlenecks. The code and data are available at https://github.com/VCL-HNU/SurgVLA

AI.tldr: Introduces SurgVLA-Bench, the first benchmark to systematically evaluate vision-language-action models in laparoscopic surgical robotics, revealing current models are far from clinically reliable.
AI.motivation: Vision-Language-Action models are promising for controlling surgical robots, but there is no standardized, surgery-specific benchmark to evaluate and compare them. Existing VLA benchmarks are tailored to general robotics and do not capture the unique visual and interaction challenges in minimally invasive surgery, such as limited field of view and frequent occlusions. The paper aims to fill this evaluation gap so that progress in surgical VLAs can be measured and driven systematically.
AI.method: The authors build SurgVLA-Bench on top of the SurRoL surgical simulation platform. They design a hierarchical task taxonomy, from low-level atomic actions up to full surgical procedures, and define a multi-dimensional evaluation framework that measures both action execution accuracy and semantic consistency between instructions and actions. Using this benchmark, they systematically test two key VLA paradigms: (1) autoregressive models (e.g., OpenVLA) and (2) flow-matching models (π_0, π_0.5, SmolVLA), comparing their behavior across the taxonomy and metrics.
AI.result: Autoregressive models generally perform better on semantic understanding—correctly interpreting and aligning with language instructions. Flow-matching models, on the other hand, typically deliver higher task precision in executing motions but suffer more in generalization. Across all models, performance is still inadequate for robust surgical use, especially under realistic constraints like narrow endoscopic views, limited camera angles, and object/organ occlusions.
AI.conclusion: SurgVLA-Bench establishes the first comprehensive, surgery-specific benchmark for VLA models in laparoscopic robotics and reveals a clear gap between current capabilities and the demands of real surgical environments. Different VLA paradigms show complementary strengths—semantic understanding vs. precise execution—but all struggle with the inherent visual constraints of laparoscopy. The benchmark and released code/data provide a foundation for more targeted model development and fair comparison in future surgical VLA research.

title: Can OCR-VLMs Read Devanagari? A Stress-Test Benchmark and Post-Correction Study
comment: 9 pages, 5 figures. Benchmark and code released
summary: OCR systems, ranging from classical engines to specialised OCR vision-language models (OCR-VLMs) and frontier multimodal LLMs, report strong results on English and Chinese document benchmarks, yet their behaviour on Indic scripts is largely uncharacterised. We benchmark ten systems on Devanagari (Hindi): classical EasyOCR; open VLMs (Qwen2.5-VL-3B, Qwen3-VL-8B, olmOCR-7B); specialised OCR-VLMs (DeepSeek-OCR, Unlimited-OCR); and frontier closed models (Gemini 2.5 Flash, Claude Opus 4.7, GPT-5.5, Mistral OCR), across four synthetic degradation conditions and 300 real printed scans. We report four findings. First, on clean rendered text all ten cluster within chrF++ 91 to 98, so synthetic text does not separate them. Second, under degradation the specialised OCR-VLMs are the most fragile: DeepSeek-OCR suffers rare but catastrophic repetition failures (outputs up to 71 the reference length) that wreck its corpus mean even though its median is the best of any system, which is why we report median and catastrophic-rate instead of the mean. Third, on real scans nine of the ten systems collapse (EasyOCR falls from chrF++ 93.6 to 58.3) and the field spreads across a 76-point range, so synthetic renders badly overstate Devanagari quality. Fourth, strong English OCR does not predict Indic OCR: GPT-5.5 drops to chrF++ 58.5 (tying classical EasyOCR) and olmOCR-7B, the model behind olmOCR-Bench, falls to 40.5, while the open Qwen3-VL-8B (75.2, runnable on a single 24 GB GPU) beats GPT-5.5 and approaches Mistral; Gemini and Claude lead at 86.3 and 82.2. An error taxonomy separates surface errors (numerals, punctuation) from structural ones (conjuncts, matras, nukta), and a byte-level (ByT5) post-corrector improves a cheap engine on its own error distribution (chrF++ +1.2 to +1.5) but does not transfer across engines. We release the benchmark, code, and models.

AI.tldr: The paper benchmarks modern OCR and multimodal models on Hindi Devanagari, showing that strong English performance and synthetic benchmarks seriously overestimate Indic performance, and that specialised OCR-VLMs can be fragile under degradation.
AI.motivation: Although OCR and multimodal LLMs claim strong results, their performance on Indic scripts like Devanagari is poorly understood, and current evaluations rely heavily on clean, synthetic English/Chinese text that may not reflect real-world conditions or non-Latin scripts.
AI.method: The authors evaluate ten OCR-related systems—including classical OCR, open-source VLMs, specialised OCR-VLMs, and proprietary frontier multimodal models—on Devanagari (Hindi) using clean rendered text, four types of synthetic degradation, and 300 real printed scans. They measure chrF++ and analyze both typical and catastrophic errors, introduce an error taxonomy (surface vs structural errors), and test a ByT5-based post-correction model trained on engine-specific error distributions.
AI.result: On clean rendered text, all systems achieve similarly high chrF++ (91–98), so synthetic clean benchmarks fail to differentiate them. Under degradation, specialised OCR-VLMs are particularly brittle, with DeepSeek-OCR showing rare but extreme repetition failures that inflate mean error; median and catastrophic error rates are more informative. On real scans, nine of ten systems experience dramatic performance drops, with scores spreading over a 76-point chrF++ range, revealing that synthetic text overestimates Devanagari performance. Strong English OCR ability does not correlate with Indic performance: some leading English models (e.g., GPT-5.5, olmOCR-7B) perform poorly, while Qwen3-VL-8B outperforms GPT-5.5 and approaches Mistral, and Gemini and Claude lead overall. A ByT5 post-corrector modestly improves a given engine’s performance but does not generalize well across engines.
AI.conclusion: Indic-script OCR remains a hard, under-measured problem: clean, synthetic benchmarks and English results give a misleading impression of capability on Devanagari. Evaluation should consider real scanned data, robustness to degradations, and catastrophic failure modes, using metrics like median performance and failure rates. Architectural and training choices that work well for English do not automatically transfer to Indic scripts. Engine-specific post-correction can yield small gains, but broader improvements require models and benchmarks explicitly designed for Indic scripts; the authors contribute such a benchmark, code, and models to support future work.

title: When Summaries Distort Decisions: Information Fidelity in LLM-Compressed Financial Analysis
summary: Financial decision-makers face more information than they can directly inspect, making context compression necessary. Yet when large language models (LLMs) compress financial source material, they can alter the investment judgment supported by the original source. We frame this problem as information fidelity: compression loses fidelity when it changes the decision induced by the source. In agentic systems, such losses may recur across intermediate steps and amplify throughout the decision process. Across financial filings and earnings-call transcripts, we find that LLM-based compression can produce fluent and factually plausible compressed contexts that nevertheless alter downstream decisions. We analyze two diagnostic patterns associated with fidelity loss: decontextualization, where salient evidence is retained but separated from the caveats and contextual qualifiers needed for correct interpretation, and model dependency, where different compressors expose different views of the same source. We then propose Agentic Context Compression, which generates multiple candidate compressions and audits their disagreements against the original source. Our results suggest that financial compression should be evaluated not only by efficiency or factuality, but also by its ability to preserve decision-relevant context.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how large language model (LLM)–based summarization of financial documents can subtly change investment decisions, and proposes an auditing method (Agentic Context Compression) to better preserve decision-relevant information.
AI.motivation: Financial professionals must process very large volumes of text (filings, earnings calls, reports). They rely increasingly on LLMs to compress this information into shorter contexts for downstream decision-making. However, standard evaluations of summarization (fluency, length, or surface factuality) may miss a critical property: whether the compressed text preserves the investment decision that a human or model would make from the original source. The authors are motivated by the risk that low-fidelity compression can cause systematic decision errors, especially in multi-step agentic systems where compression is applied repeatedly and errors may compound.
AI.method: 1) Define the notion of information fidelity in financial context compression: a compression is low-fidelity if it changes the investment judgment (e.g., buy/sell/hold or similar decisions) that the full source would support. 2) Empirically study LLM-based compression on financial filings and earnings-call transcripts, examining how compressed outputs affect downstream decisions. 3) Identify and characterize two recurring diagnostic patterns of fidelity loss: (a) decontextualization, where key facts are preserved but stripped of caveats or qualifying context; and (b) model dependency, where different compressor models produce divergent summaries of the same source, leading to different decisions. 4) Propose Agentic Context Compression: a pipeline that generates multiple candidate compressions from different LLMs or prompts, then audits their disagreements with respect to the original text to detect potential fidelity issues. 5) Evaluate how this approach performs compared with standard single-compression baselines, in terms of preserving decision-relevant information.
AI.result: The study finds that LLM-based summarization of financial texts frequently yields compressed contexts that are linguistically fluent and appear factually correct, yet lead downstream decision-makers (likely models, and potentially humans) to different investment judgments than those induced by the original documents. The authors document concrete instances of decontextualization and model dependency and show that these are systematic patterns rather than rare anomalies. Using their Agentic Context Compression framework, they demonstrate improved preservation of decision-relevant information: by comparing multiple candidate compressions and auditing their disagreements against the source, the system can flag or reduce cases in which compression would otherwise alter the investment decision.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that financial context compression should be judged not only on efficiency (shortness) and surface-level factual accuracy, but more importantly on information fidelity—whether the compressed text preserves the original decision-relevant context. LLMs can produce plausible but decision-distorting compressions through decontextualization and model dependency, and such distortions can compound in multi-step agentic workflows. Agentic Context Compression, which explicitly checks multiple compressions against each other and against the source to detect disagreement, is a promising approach to mitigating these issues. The work argues for decision-centric evaluation and design of LLM-based tools in high-stakes financial applications.

title: Understanding Evaluation Illusion in Diffusion Large Language Models
summary: Despite the capability of parallel decoding, diffusion large language models (dLLMs) require many denoising steps to maintain generation quality, motivating recent research on efficient decoding strategies. However, existing studies have reported inconsistent evaluation results even under seemingly identical evaluation settings, risking biased conclusions about dLLM decoding methods. To understand this evaluation concern, we conduct a rigorous evaluation of current decoding methods for dLLMs across diverse evaluation settings. Surprisingly, our analysis reveals that the ranking of decoding methods is highly sensitive to the choice of prompt templates. Single-template evaluation can lead to an illusion that decoding methods improve inference efficiency without performance degradation. Through comprehensive experiments, we find that current parallel decoding methods consistently underperform the single-token decoding baseline, failing to overcome the speed-quality trade-off. We further identify this evaluation inconsistency as the high sensitivity of parallel decoding methods to minor variations in prompt templates. Our experiments show that an effective prompt template can achieve strong evaluation results even with fewer denoising steps, markedly outperforming the marginal gain from increasing denoising steps. Beyond prompt templates, our experiments indicate that overlooked evaluation settings can also notably affect the assessment of decoding methods. Based on these findings, we propose practical guidelines for the reliable evaluation of decoding methods in dLLMs.

AI.tldr: The paper rigorously evaluates decoding methods for diffusion LLMs and finds that parallel decoding is highly sensitive to prompt templates, often underperforming standard single-token decoding, and proposes guidelines for more reliable evaluation.
AI.motivation: Diffusion LLMs support parallel decoding but need many denoising steps, making inference slow. Recent efficient decoding methods report mixed and sometimes contradictory results, even under similar settings, which risks misleading conclusions about their real advantages. The authors are motivated to understand where these inconsistencies come from and whether parallel decoding actually beats standard decoding under fair and robust evaluation.
AI.method: They conduct systematic experiments comparing existing decoding methods for diffusion LLMs across a wide variety of evaluation settings. In particular, they vary prompt templates, denoising step counts, and other often-overlooked evaluation choices. They analyze how rankings of decoding methods change with these factors and study the sensitivity of parallel decoding methods to small prompt-template variations. They also quantify the relative impact of better prompts versus more denoising steps on speed–quality trade-offs.
AI.result: They find that method rankings are extremely sensitive to the chosen prompt templates; evaluations with a single template can misleadingly suggest that faster parallel decoding methods maintain quality. Across comprehensive settings, current parallel decoding methods systematically underperform a strong single-token decoding baseline and fail to truly break the speed–quality trade-off. They show that minor changes in prompt templates can significantly alter performance, and that good prompt templates can yield large quality gains—even with fewer denoising steps—often larger than the gains from increasing denoising steps themselves. They further show that other neglected evaluation details also substantially affect conclusions.
AI.conclusion: The work concludes that current parallel decoding strategies for diffusion LLMs are far less robust and effective than some prior reports suggest, mainly because their performance is highly sensitive to prompt templates and other evaluation details. Reliable assessment of decoding methods requires multi-template, carefully controlled evaluation protocols. The authors provide practical guidelines for evaluating dLLM decoding methods to avoid misleading claims about speed–quality improvements and to foster more robust future research.

title: The Complexity Ceiling Benchmark: A Multi-Domain Evaluation of Sequential Reasoning Under Depth Scaling
comment: 12 pages, 6 figures. Accepted to the 1st Workshop on Combining Theory and Benchmarks (CTB), CTB@ICML 2026
summary: We introduce the Complexity Ceiling Benchmark (CCB), a controlled evaluation of how language-model reasoning decays as the number of required sequential steps grows. CCB fixes the semantic content of a task and varies only its depth N in {5,...,50} across three structurally distinct regimes: grounded spatial state-tracking, abstract symbolic pointer manipulation, and transitive relational inference. Across 6,000 trials over five frontier and open-weight LLMs we find a consistent pattern of geometric per-step decay with widely separated domain ceilings: on the first two regimes the strongest models retain pd>0.92 across N=50; on the third every model collapses by N=5, with the best model's 50%-success horizon at H0.5~4.7 steps despite pd=0.863. A trace-level metric (TFBC) shows that 14.5% of correct answers across the benchmark are reached via incorrect intermediate reasoning. Forced verbose state-tracking does not move the ceiling (McNemar p=1.000), and the mean step at which reasoning first diverges, k*, predicts within-domain accuracy better than parameter count. CCB and the geometric decay model together reduce a model's long-horizon reasoning profile to one interpretable number per task family.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a benchmark to measure how language-model reasoning accuracy decays as tasks require more sequential reasoning steps, revealing domain-specific ceilings and geometric per-step performance decay.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM evaluations often conflate task difficulty with depth of reasoning, making it hard to quantify how well models handle long chains of reasoning. The authors want a controlled way to isolate and measure the effect of increasing reasoning depth on model performance across different kinds of reasoning tasks.
AI.method: They build the Complexity Ceiling Benchmark (CCB), which keeps task semantics fixed but systematically varies the required reasoning depth N from 5 to 50 in three regimes: grounded spatial state tracking, abstract symbolic pointer manipulation, and transitive relational inference. They run 6,000 trials across five state-of-the-art and open-weight LLMs, fit geometric decay models of per-step accuracy, introduce a trace-level metric (TFBC) to evaluate correctness of intermediate reasoning, and test the effect of forced verbose state-tracking. They also compute a divergence step k* as the first step where the model’s reasoning departs from the correct trace and relate it to overall accuracy.
AI.result: Performance decays approximately geometrically with each additional reasoning step, but with very different ceilings across regimes. In spatial tracking and pointer manipulation, the strongest models keep high per-step accuracy (pd>0.92) even at N=50. In transitive relational inference, all models fail quickly: performance collapses by N=5, and even the best model only maintains 50% success up to about 4.7 steps despite a seemingly high per-step accuracy estimate (pd=0.863). TFBC shows that around 14.5% of correct final answers arise from flawed intermediate reasoning. Forcing verbose state-tracking does not improve the complexity ceiling (no significant difference, McNemar p=1.000). The first divergence step k* is a better predictor of within-domain accuracy than model size.
AI.conclusion: Reasoning performance of LLMs over increasing depth follows a geometric decay pattern with clear, domain-specific complexity ceilings. Some forms of sequential reasoning (spatial, symbolic pointer) are robust to long horizons for strong models, while others (transitive relational inference) break down very quickly. Many apparently correct answers come from incorrect reasoning paths, and prompting for more explicit intermediate steps does not extend the reasoning horizon. The proposed CCB and geometric decay characterization provide a compact, interpretable way to summarize and compare models’ long-horizon reasoning abilities across task families.

title: Travel-Oriented Reasoning Large Language Model via Domain-Specific Knowledge Graphs
comment: Accepted to the Uncertainty Reasoning and Quantification in Decision Making (UDM) Workshop, KDD 2026 (To be presented in August 2026)
summary: Large language models (LLMs) demonstrate broad reasoning abilities but struggle with accuracy and reliability in specialized domains such as travel, where reasoning depends on precise definitions, rules, and expert-defined conceptual frameworks, and where confident but unfounded outputs arise from a reasoning failure in which the model has not internalized the underlying domain graph rather than from missing domain knowledge alone. We propose a modular pipeline for building a travel-domain reasoning LLM grounded in an expert-designed knowledge graph (KG). Our pipeline integrates a travel KG that encodes domain entities and their relationships, a bottom-up construction procedure that walks the KG to produce multi-hop question answer (QA) pairs, a supervised fine-tuning stage that embeds the domain knowledge into a reasoning-capable LLM using the generated QA pairs as auditable reasoning traces, and a travel-domain benchmark dataset that measures the fine-tuned model's accuracy and calibration. We evaluate our approach using Qwen3-4B with LoRA adaptation. Our reasoning model achieves an $82.4\%$ exact match on the benchmark. This performance significantly outperforms the pretrained Qwen3-4B baseline at $22.4\%$. A calibration analysis decomposes the residual $17.57\%$ of errors into two distinct failure modes: an over-confident multi-label decoder that predicts both correct answers plus one spurious option on most dual-answer mistakes, and a smaller reasoning failure on single-answer questions where the supporting facts are present in the KG but the model fails to reconstruct the correct multi-hop path. This split confirms that explicit KG-grounded reasoning substantially improves the accuracy and uncertainty interpretation of LLMs in specialized domains, and isolates per-option calibration and trace-length-aware decoding as the next axes of improvement.

AI.tldr: They build a travel-domain reasoning LLM by grounding it in an expert-designed knowledge graph, auto-generating multi-hop QA traces from that graph, fine-tuning a base model on these traces, and showing large gains in accuracy and calibration over the base model.
AI.motivation: General-purpose LLMs are poor at reliable, precise reasoning in specialized domains like travel, where correct answers depend on strict rules, well-defined concepts, and multi-step reasoning over domain structure. The authors argue that hallucinations are often due not just to missing facts but to the model failing to internalize the domain’s underlying graph of entities and relations. They want a method that embeds this structured domain reasoning into LLMs and yields both higher accuracy and better-calibrated uncertainty.
AI.method: 1) Design an expert travel knowledge graph encoding key entities and their relations. 2) Use a bottom-up graph-walking procedure to automatically generate multi-hop QA pairs, with explicit reasoning traces grounded in the KG. 3) Supervised fine-tuning: adapt a base LLM (Qwen3-4B, with LoRA) on this generated QA data so that it learns to follow KG-based reasoning traces. 4) Build a travel-domain benchmark to measure exact-match accuracy and calibration of the fine-tuned model versus the base model. 5) Perform calibration analysis that decomposes residual errors into categories related to decoding and reasoning failures.
AI.result: On the travel-domain benchmark, their KG-grounded reasoning model reaches 82.4% exact-match accuracy, far above the 22.4% of the original Qwen3-4B. Error analysis shows that about 17.57% of remaining errors split primarily into (a) over-confident multi-label decoding that often includes the right answers plus one extra option for dual-answer questions, and (b) a smaller subset of genuine reasoning failures on single-answer questions where the relevant KG facts exist but the model cannot reconstruct the correct multi-hop reasoning path.
