AI.conclusion: Embedding explicit knowledge-graph-grounded reasoning into an LLM via generated multi-hop QA traces can dramatically improve both accuracy and calibration in a specialized domain (travel). The remaining errors are dominated less by missing knowledge and more by decoding and calibration issues—particularly per-option confidence and handling of trace length—suggesting that future gains will come from refining multi-label decoding and making the model’s confidence more sensitive to reasoning complexity.

title: Process Advantage Signal Shaping: A Paradigm-Agnostic Middleware for Process-Supervised RL in LLM Reasoners
summary: Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) is a default recipe for process-supervised reinforcement learning of LLM reasoners, and dense process supervision -- via learned process reward models (PRMs) or on-policy-distillation KL signals -- is a common way to densify its otherwise weak outcome reward. Layering such a step-level signal on top of GRPO's group-standardized advantage, however, exposes three structural pathologies: \emph{channel contamination} between the pooled process, outcome, and format streams at group standardization; \emph{resolution mismatch} between the granularity of the process signal and the granularity of the logical decisions being credited; and a \emph{cumulative trap} by which GRPO's return-to-go sum surfaces either length inflation or truncated exploration depending on the sign regime of the signal. We propose \textbf{PASS} (\emph{Process Advantage Signal Shaping}), a compact middleware that sits between any scalar step-level process signal and GRPO's clipped surrogate and addresses the three pathologies in turn: \emph{Advantage Fusion} standardizes the three streams independently within each group, \emph{Chunk-by-Value} derives value-homogeneous chunks from the signal itself and broadcasts credit within each chunk, and \emph{Divide-Length} converts the cumulative objective into an average-value-density score. We validate PASS across two domains and two process-signal paradigms -- a learned PRM on mathematical reasoning and an on-policy-distillation KL signal (with a generalized variant) on multi-hop question answering -- and under two group-standardization operators. In every regime PASS delivers a consistent pass@1 gain over the corresponding GRPO baseline.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies three structural problems when combining GRPO with dense process-level supervision for training reasoning LLMs, and proposes PASS, a middleware that reshapes the step-wise signals to fix these issues, consistently improving pass@1 over standard GRPO across tasks and signal types.
AI.motivation: GRPO is widely used for process-supervised RL for LLM reasoning, but its default combination with dense step-level supervision (from PRMs or distillation KL) leads to structural pathologies that hurt learning: mixing of different reward channels during group standardization, misalignment between the resolution of the signal and actual decision points, and length-related artifacts in return-to-go. There is a need for a simple, generic way to correct these structural issues without redesigning GRPO or the supervision signals themselves.
AI.method: The authors introduce PASS (Process Advantage Signal Shaping), a middleware layer that modifies scalar step-wise process signals before they enter GRPO’s clipped surrogate. PASS has three components: (1) Advantage Fusion, which standardizes process, outcome, and format streams separately inside each group to avoid channel contamination; (2) Chunk-by-Value, which segments trajectories into contiguous chunks with homogeneous signal values and spreads credit uniformly within each chunk to align signal granularity with decision granularity; and (3) Divide-Length, which replaces the cumulative return-to-go with an average value-density objective to remove length bias. PASS is designed to be compatible with different process signals and group-standardization operators.
AI.result: PASS is evaluated on two domains (mathematical reasoning and multi-hop QA) and with two kinds of dense process signals: a learned PRM and an on-policy distillation KL signal (including a generalized variant). It is also tested under two different group-standardization operators. Across all tested regimes, PASS consistently improves pass@1 performance compared to the corresponding GRPO baselines without PASS.
AI.conclusion: Dense process supervision on top of GRPO, while powerful, suffers from intrinsic structural issues that limit its effectiveness. By reshaping the step-wise signals through Advantage Fusion, Chunk-by-Value, and Divide-Length, PASS provides a compact, plug-in solution that systematically fixes these issues and yields robust gains in reasoning performance. PASS appears to be a general, method-agnostic enhancement for GRPO-style training with process-level signals.

title: MIThinker: A Plug-and-Play Policy-Optimized Thinker For Motivational Interviewing Counseling
summary: Reasoning large language models (LLMs) have recently made much progress in complex problem-solving, leveraging internal reasoning (or thought) to guide their solution generation. However, existing LLM-based counseling agents, including those using Motivational Interviewing (MI), generate responses without explicitly aligning thoughts with counseling techniques, limiting their effectiveness. We propose MIThinker, a lightweight thinking model that generates therapeutic thoughts to guide MI counseling agents in strategy selection and response generation. To overcome the lack of annotated thought data, we introduce AugR1-MI, an automated pipeline that reverse-engineers counselor's thoughts from observed responses. Through two-stage training combining supervised fine-tuning and reinforcement learning, MIThinker demonstrates improved theory-of-mind assessment and strategy alignment. Comprehensive evaluations show that MindfulMI, our agent leveraging MIThinker, achieves MI competency comparable to state-of-the-art systems with an order of magnitude less computation.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces MIThinker, a lightweight reasoning module that generates explicit therapeutic thoughts to better guide motivational interviewing (MI) counseling agents, achieving strong MI competence with much lower computation.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-based counseling and MI agents respond directly from prompts without explicit reasoning aligned to counseling theory. This weakens their ability to choose appropriate MI strategies and demonstrate theory-of-mind, and there is a lack of thought-annotated data for training such reasoning in counseling.
AI.method: They design MIThinker, a small thinking model that outputs structured therapeutic thoughts guiding a downstream MI agent’s strategy selection and response. To obtain training data, they propose AugR1-MI, an automatic pipeline that infers latent counselor thoughts from observed expert responses (reverse engineering). They train MIThinker in two stages: supervised fine-tuning on these inferred thoughts, followed by reinforcement learning to better align thoughts with MI strategies and theory-of-mind signals.
AI.result: MIThinker shows improved performance on theory-of-mind assessment and alignment of generated strategies with MI principles. When integrated into a counseling agent called MindfulMI, the system matches state-of-the-art MI competency while using roughly an order of magnitude less computation than prior SOTA systems.
AI.conclusion: Explicit, lightweight reasoning modules can substantially enhance MI counseling agents. By reverse-engineering counselor thoughts and using a two-stage SFT+RL training regime, MIThinker enables MindfulMI to achieve high MI competency and efficient, well-aligned strategy selection, suggesting that structured therapeutic thinking is a promising direction for scalable counseling LLMs.

title: Hierarchical Experimentalist Agents
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used to take actions in the real world and support human decision-making, yet most agents rely on parametric knowledge, fixed post-training data, retrieval, or search. This paradigm breaks down in novel domains and for sophisticated queries that cannot be answered from prior knowledge alone. Knowing the laws of physics, for instance, does not by itself enable LLMs to answer queries or complete long-horizon tasks in a complex physical system. To address this, we introduce Hierarchical Experimentalist Agents (HExA), an in-context self-improvement framework to learn from active experimentation. HExA iteratively designs and refines query-relevant experiments, learns a reusable library of composable skills from experience, and integrates experimental evidence to answer queries or take actions. HExA is training-free, compatible with any black-box model, and does not require external supervision, oracles, or offline data. To evaluate active experimentation, we introduce Interphyre, a tool-calling benchmark built on the PHYRE 2D procedural physics environment, where agents propose interventions and test hypotheses through simulation APIs. Experiments show that current LLM agents struggle in these settings, especially on the hardest levels of Interphyre. Claude Sonnet 4.6 achieves only 2% success, while HExA improves the same model to up to 77% success. HExA also improves open-weight models and outperforms agentic baselines such as ReAct and Reflexion. Moreover, using only skills learned from easier levels and transferred without active experimentation, HExA achieves 44% success, demonstrating the reusability and generalization of its learned skills. Overall, HExA shows that learning through active experimentation can help agents discover useful knowledge, acquire reusable skills, and make efficient progress on novel long-horizon tasks.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Hierarchical Experimentalist Agents (HExA), a training-free framework that lets LLM agents actively design and run experiments to learn skills and solve novel, long-horizon physics tasks, dramatically improving success rates over standard LLM agents.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM agents mostly rely on static knowledge sources (model parameters, fixed post-training data, retrieval, search). This fails in novel or complex domains where prior knowledge is insufficient, especially for long-horizon tasks in dynamic physical environments. The authors want agents that can autonomously acquire new, task-relevant knowledge and skills by experimenting, without extra training, supervision, or bespoke datasets.
AI.method: They introduce HExA, a hierarchical, in-context learning framework for active experimentation. An LLM agent: (1) plans and iteratively refines targeted experiments in a physics simulation to test hypotheses about the environment; (2) extracts and abstracts the outcomes into a library of composable, reusable skills; and (3) uses both the experimental results and learned skills to answer queries or act in the environment. HExA works with black-box LLMs, requires no additional training, and uses tool-calling APIs to interact with the PHYRE-based Interphyre benchmark.
AI.result: On Interphyre, a new physics-based tool-calling benchmark where agents propose interventions and test hypotheses via simulation APIs, standard LLM agents perform poorly: Claude Sonnet 4.6 attains about 2% success on the hardest levels. With HExA layered on top of the same base model, success rises to as high as 77%. HExA similarly boosts open-weight models and outperforms existing agent frameworks such as ReAct and Reflexion. Even when restricted to reusing skills learned only from easier tasks and not allowed to actively experiment on new tasks, HExA still reaches 44% success, showing strong skill transfer and generalization.
AI.conclusion: Active experimentation combined with hierarchical skill learning enables LLM agents to autonomously acquire new, reusable knowledge that is not contained in their parametric memory or training data. HExA demonstrates that such training-free, black-box, self-improving agents can greatly enhance performance on novel, complex, long-horizon tasks in physical environments, suggesting a promising direction for building more adaptive and general LLM-based agents.

title: A Hybrid Framework for Song Lyric Annotation Based on Human-LLM Alignment
summary: Emotion recognition of song lyrics is a challenging task since lyrics may not necessarily align with the overall emotion of a song. As a result, lyrics annotation remains largely underexplored. Drawing inspiration from research in large language model (LLM) assisted annotation, we examine the alignment between humans and LLMs for annotation of lyrics by creating a new sentence-level dataset of lyrics. Our observations highlight the subjectivity of the task and the inherent challenges. Following this, we present a hybrid annotation framework that optimizes human and LLM annotation by predicting potential misalignment in annotation.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how well large language models (LLMs) can help annotate emotions in song lyrics and proposes a hybrid human+LLM annotation framework that accounts for likely disagreements.
AI.motivation: Emotion labels for song lyrics are hard to obtain because lyrics do not always reflect the overall song emotion, and emotional interpretation is subjective. Manual annotation is costly and inconsistent, and existing work has not deeply explored lyric-focused emotion annotation. Meanwhile, LLMs show promise as annotation assistants, but their alignment with human judgments is unclear for subjective tasks like emotion in lyrics. The authors want to understand this alignment and improve the efficiency and quality of lyric emotion annotation.
AI.method: The authors construct a new sentence-level lyric emotion dataset annotated by humans and by an LLM. They systematically analyze agreement and disagreement patterns between human annotators and the LLM to characterize the subjectivity and difficulty of the task. Based on these insights, they design a hybrid annotation framework that uses a model to predict when human–LLM emotion labels are likely to misalign, and then selectively allocate human effort or LLM labeling depending on predicted alignment risk, thereby optimizing the division of labor.
AI.result: The analysis shows substantial subjectivity and nontrivial disagreement between humans and LLMs, confirming that sentence-level lyric emotion recognition is challenging. Their hybrid framework can predict likely misalignment cases and use that signal to decide when to rely on LLM annotation versus human review, leading to more efficient annotation with maintained or improved quality compared to purely human or purely LLM-based approaches.
AI.conclusion: Emotion recognition in lyrics is inherently subjective and difficult, and LLMs do not trivially solve the annotation problem. However, by carefully modeling and predicting human–LLM misalignment, a hybrid framework can effectively combine their strengths, improving the scalability and reliability of lyric emotion annotation. The work also provides a new sentence-level dataset and insights into using LLMs for subjective annotation tasks.

title: PHF: Privileged Hidden Flow for On-Policy Self-Distillation
summary: On-policy self-distillation (OPSD) trains a reasoning model on rollouts sampled from its own policy by matching a privileged teacher that also sees verified reference solutions. Existing OPSD objectives supervise only the output distribution, so privileged context affects training through a token-level divergence without directly supervising the internal computation that produced that distribution. We propose Privileged Hidden Flow (PHF), which additionally distills how a privileged teacher's hidden states move along the same rollout. Rather than forcing each student hidden vector to match the teacher vector at the same token position, PHF aligns token-to-token transition directions and trajectory geometry over selected generated positions. The all-layer recipe also includes an adjacent-layer relation computed from these same transitions, without pointwise hidden-state imitation. Under the same 100-step training schedule, PHF improves the Average@12 aggregate over our reproduced OPSD baseline on Qwen3-1.7B, 4B, and 8B, with observed gains of about +2.2, +1.5, and +1.7 points. The transport objective is exactly invariant to shared trajectory offsets; its local geometry term is also invariant to orthogonal transformations of transition directions. Ablations distinguish the fixed PHF recipe from pointwise hidden-state matching, single-channel transition losses, and layer-subset choices, supporting PHF as a compact hidden-flow extension to OPSD.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes Privileged Hidden Flow (PHF), a new on‑policy self‑distillation objective that aligns hidden-state transitions and trajectory geometry between a student and a privileged teacher, improving reasoning performance over standard OPSD without pointwise hidden-state matching.
AI.motivation: Existing on-policy self-distillation (OPSD) methods for reasoning models only supervise the output token distributions of a student using a privileged teacher that has extra information (e.g., verified solutions). This means the influence of the privileged context is indirect, propagating only through token-level divergences, while the internal computation (hidden-state dynamics) is unconstrained. The authors want to leverage the richer internal signal from the privileged teacher to guide how the student reasons over a trajectory, without simply copying hidden states, which can be brittle and overly restrictive.
AI.method: They introduce Privileged Hidden Flow (PHF), an extension to OPSD that distills how the teacher’s hidden states evolve along the same generated rollout. Instead of matching hidden vectors at each token position, PHF aligns (1) token-to-token transition directions of hidden states and (2) the geometry of trajectories at selected generated positions. An all-layer formulation also uses an adjacent-layer relation based on these transitions, again avoiding pointwise hidden-state imitation. The objective is designed so that the transport term is invariant to shared offsets of trajectories, and the local geometry term is invariant to orthogonal transformations of transition directions, focusing on relational structure rather than exact coordinates.
AI.result: Using the same 100-step training schedule as their OPSD baseline, applying PHF to Qwen3 models of sizes 1.7B, 4B, and 8B improves the Average@12 reasoning benchmark aggregate by approximately +2.2, +1.5, and +1.7 points, respectively. Ablation studies show that these gains are not reproduced by simpler alternatives such as pointwise hidden-state matching, using only single-channel transition losses, or restricting to subsets of layers, indicating that the particular PHF design is critical for the observed improvements.
AI.conclusion: PHF is an effective and compact hidden-flow extension to on-policy self-distillation for reasoning models. By supervising how hidden states move rather than their absolute values, and by using geometry- and offset-invariant objectives, it better exploits the privileged teacher’s internal computations. The method consistently improves reasoning performance over standard OPSD across multiple model sizes, with ablations supporting that the gains stem from PHF’s specific hidden-transition and trajectory-geometry design rather than from naive hidden-state imitation.

title: TriageRA-CCF: Source-Side Clinical Confidence and Coverage Signals for Adaptive Rank Budgeting in Medical LLMs
summary: Medical large language models are commonly adapted with a fixed low-rank budget, even though medical questions differ substantially in confidence, clinical coverage, and cross-domain difficulty. We study adaptive rank budgeting for parameter-efficient medical question answering: for each question, the adapter decides whether to activate a small, medium, or large subset of LoRA rank channels. The central challenge is that a naive adaptive budget router can collapse to unstable choices or spend capacity without improving shifted benchmarks. We propose TriageRA-CCF, a source-side teacher for adaptive rank-budgeted LoRA. It combines three signals computed only from source training data: base-model answer confidence, metadata-cell clinical coverage, and a counterfactual close-miss proxy. These signals supervise a straight-through budget router over active ranks {2,4,8}, together with budget-cost, entropy, and rank-balance regularization. Under a matched CMB-source training protocol, TriageRA-CCF achieves the best average accuracy among LoRA, DoRA, and MoELoRA baselines on both Qwen3-8B and Llama3.1-8B. The gains are modest and non-uniform across benchmarks: +0.21 average points over the strongest external baseline on Qwen3-8B and +0.16 on Llama3.1-8B. Component ablations show that confidence, coverage, and counterfactual signals all provide useful budget supervision, but their combination is not monotonically best on every backbone.

AI.tldr: They propose TriageRA-CCF, a method that adaptively selects LoRA rank per medical question using confidence, coverage, and counterfactual signals, modestly improving accuracy over existing parameter-efficient tuning baselines on 8B medical LLMs.
AI.motivation: Existing medical LLM adaptation typically uses a fixed LoRA rank (i.e., fixed parameter budget) for all questions, even though questions vary greatly in difficulty, required medical coverage, and model confidence. A fixed budget can be wasteful on easy or narrow questions and insufficient on hard or broad ones. Naively routing different parameter budgets per question is unstable and may not improve performance on out-of-distribution benchmarks, motivating a principled, data-driven way to adapt the rank per example while remaining parameter-efficient.
AI.method: They introduce TriageRA-CCF, a teacher-style approach for adaptive rank-budgeted LoRA on medical QA. The system has a budget router that, for each question, selects how many LoRA rank channels to activate from {2, 4, 8} (small/medium/large). The router is supervised using three signals computed on source training data only: (1) base LLM answer confidence, (2) clinical coverage of metadata cells (how broad or specialized the question is clinically), and (3) a counterfactual close-miss proxy that approximates how near the model is to a correct answer. Training uses a straight-through estimator for the discrete budget decisions and adds regularization terms for budget cost (discouraging unnecessary high rank use), entropy (preventing deterministic collapse too early), and rank balance (encouraging usage of all budget levels). They train under a matched CMB-source protocol and compare against fixed-rank LoRA, DoRA, and MoELoRA on Qwen3-8B and Llama3.1-8B backbones.
AI.result: On both Qwen3-8B and Llama3.1-8B, TriageRA-CCF yields the best average accuracy across multiple medical QA benchmarks when compared to LoRA, DoRA, and MoELoRA baselines under the same training setup. The improvements are modest and heterogeneous: about +0.21 average accuracy points over the strongest external baseline for Qwen3-8B and +0.16 for Llama3.1-8B, with gains varying by benchmark. Ablation studies indicate that each of the three supervision signals (confidence, coverage, counterfactual proxy) contributes to effective budget routing, though the optimal combination can depend on the backbone model, and combining all three is not consistently superior in every case.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive per-question LoRA rank selection, guided by carefully designed source-side signals (confidence, coverage, counterfactual error), can slightly but consistently improve medical question answering accuracy over strong parameter-efficient baselines on 8B models. TriageRA-CCF demonstrates that using a learned budget router with appropriate regularization avoids degenerate routing behavior and makes better use of a limited adaptation budget. However, performance gains are small and vary across datasets and backbones, and the interaction between different supervision signals is complex, suggesting more work is needed to robustly capitalize on adaptive parameter budgeting in medical LLMs.

title: When LLMs Develop Languages: Symbolic Communication for Efficient Multi-Agent Reasoning
summary: Chain-of-Thought (CoT) improves large language models (LLMs) on difficult reasoning tasks, but it often incurs long natural-language rationales that are poorly aligned with efficient machine reasoning. We propose Communicative Language Symbolism Routing (CLSR), a test-time framework in which multiple LLM agents autonomously invent, evolve, and share compact Language Symbolism Frameworks (LSFs), while a latent-free router adaptively selects and composes these languages per query to optimize the accuracy-token trade-off. Unlike prompt optimization that refines surface instructions, CLSR treats each LSF as a reusable symbolic protocol with compact symbols, usage rules, and a message-passing contract, and improves it through an evolutionary loop driven by correctness and token cost. At inference time, the router may invoke a single low-cost LSF call, ensemble multiple LSFs, or execute a multi-round LSF composition protocol on harder queries. Across challenging benchmarks, CLSR reduces latency-oriented generated token completion by $3\sim 6\times$ compared to standard CoT while maintaining accuracy. We further derive an information-theoretic lower bound on token cost under arbitrary symbolism and show that, under an interpreter-realizability premise, multi-round LSF protocols conditionally subsume program-execution pipelines. Code is publicly available (https://github.com/pzqpzq/LSF_MDia).

AI.tldr: The paper proposes CLSR, a test-time framework where multiple LLM agents invent and share compact symbolic languages (LSFs), and a router selects and composes them to solve queries with far fewer tokens than standard Chain-of-Thought while preserving accuracy.
AI.motivation: Chain-of-Thought reasoning improves LLM performance but produces long, verbose natural-language rationales that are inefficient and poorly matched to how machines could reason more compactly. There is a need for a more token-efficient reasoning process that maintains or improves accuracy, especially for latency- and cost-sensitive applications.
AI.method: They introduce Communicative Language Symbolism Routing (CLSR). Multiple LLM agents autonomously design and iteratively refine Language Symbolism Frameworks (LSFs) – symbolic communication protocols with compact symbols, usage rules, and message-passing contracts. An evolutionary loop updates LSFs based on their correctness and token cost. At inference time, a latent-free router adaptively chooses how to use these LSFs per query: invoking a single cheap LSF, ensembling several, or running multi-round composition protocols for harder problems. They also provide an information-theoretic lower bound on token cost for symbolic communication and analyze the expressive power of multi-round LSF protocols.
AI.result: On challenging benchmarks, CLSR cuts the number of generated completion tokens related to latency by about 3–6x compared with standard Chain-of-Thought, while keeping accuracy roughly the same. Theoretical results include a lower bound on token cost under arbitrary symbolism and a conditional proof that multi-round LSF protocols can subsume program-execution style pipelines.
AI.conclusion: Compact, learned symbolic communication protocols between LLM agents, combined with an adaptive router, can substantially reduce token cost and latency for complex reasoning tasks without sacrificing accuracy. Under reasonable assumptions, such multi-round symbolic protocols can match or generalize traditional program-execution pipelines, suggesting a promising direction for more efficient LLM-based reasoning systems.

title: Cross-Temporal Sinhala OCR: Page-Level Adaptation and Diachronic Analysis
comment: 6 pages, 4 figures, 7 tables, Accepted paper at the 12th Moratuwa Engineering Research Conference (MERCon) 2026
summary: Sinhala is a morphologically rich abugida spoken by roughly 16 million people in Sri Lanka, and to date, there are no publicly available real-world datasets for page-level Sinhala OCR. All previous studies for assessing Sinhala OCR models have used artificially generated data. To bridge the gap, we introduce sinhala-ocr-lk-acts-1010, an annotated dataset of 1,010 page-level images and their transcriptions collected from Sri Lankan Legislative Acts published between 1981-1989 and 2000-2019, split into 707 training examples, 101 validation examples, and 202 testing examples. Three models based on deep learning-based visual language processing, namely DeepSeek-OCR V1, DeepSeek-OCR V2, and LightOnOCR-2-1B, are fine-tuned using QLoRA in 8 experiments conducted on consumer and cloud GPUs. LightOnOCR-2-1B is the top performer, achieving a CER of 1.05% across all test examples, outperforming state-of-the-art open-source OCR models such as Surya-OCR (8.84%) and Tesseract v5 (10.69%), as well as commercially available OCR models such as Google Document AI (2.06%). Our results suggest that LightOnOCR-2-1B outperforms other baselines on real-world OCR tasks and maintains consistent performance across all print periods, even when documents are severely degraded.

AI.tldr: They build and release the first real-world page-level Sinhala OCR dataset from legislative acts and benchmark/fine-tune modern OCR models, showing a large performance gain over existing open-source and commercial systems.
AI.motivation: Sinhala is morphologically rich and lacks publicly available, real-world page-level OCR datasets; prior work relied on synthetic data, which does not reflect real document noise, degradation, and layout complexity. This gap prevents reliable evaluation and fine-tuning of modern OCR models for Sinhala in realistic settings.
AI.method: They curate sinhala-ocr-lk-acts-1010, a dataset of 1,010 page images with transcriptions from Sri Lankan legislative acts (two time ranges: 1981–1989 and 2000–2019), and split it into training/validation/test sets (707/101/202). They fine-tune three deep-learning visual language OCR models (DeepSeek-OCR V1, DeepSeek-OCR V2, and LightOnOCR-2-1B) using QLoRA, running 8 experiments on consumer and cloud GPUs, and then compare character error rate (CER) on the test set against open-source (Surya-OCR, Tesseract v5) and commercial (Google Document AI) baselines.
AI.result: The best model, LightOnOCR-2-1B, achieves a test CER of 1.05%, substantially better than Surya-OCR (8.84%), Tesseract v5 (10.69%), and Google Document AI (2.06%). It also shows stable performance across documents from different publication periods, including heavily degraded ones.
AI.conclusion: Providing a real-world Sinhala OCR dataset enables proper evaluation and adaptation of OCR systems. Fine-tuned LightOnOCR-2-1B significantly outperforms both existing open-source and commercial baselines on this dataset and remains robust across varying document ages and degradation levels, indicating that modern visual language models can deliver high-quality OCR for low-resource scripts when supported by targeted datasets and fine-tuning.

title: Diagnosing and Repairing Factual Errors in RAG under Budget Constraints
summary: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) improves the factuality of large language models by grounding responses in external evidence, yet real-world deployments remain fragile. Failures often stem from missing or weakly relevant evidence, as well as from generation that does not faithfully reflect the retrieved context. Many existing approaches rely on fine-tuning, privileged access to internal model signals, or resource-insensitive escalation strategies, which limits their practicality in black-box and budget-constrained settings. We propose D2R-RAG (Diagnose-to-Repair RAG), a model-agnostic and resource-aware framework that combines lightweight failure diagnosis with adaptive repair. D2R-RAG derives interpretable failure signatures from observable signals in the query, retrieved evidence, and generated response, and then selects from a small set of corrective actions under explicit latency and VRAM constraints. Experiments on FEVER and HotpotQA show that D2R-RAG improves reliability over recent baselines and achieves better accuracy--efficiency trade-offs across multiple compute budgets. The code is available at https://github.com/CyberScienceLab/D2R-RAG/.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces D2R-RAG, a framework to make Retrieval-Augmented Generation more reliable in realistic, budget-constrained, black-box settings by diagnosing likely failure modes and applying targeted fixes under compute limits.
AI.motivation: Although RAG helps large language models be more factual by using retrieved evidence, real deployments are brittle. They often fail when retrieval misses key evidence, when evidence is only weakly related, or when the model’s generation ignores or misuses the retrieved context. Many current solutions need model fine-tuning, internal model access, or expensive fallback strategies, which are impractical when using black-box APIs or strict latency/VRAM budgets. The paper aims to create a practical, general method that improves reliability without these constraints.
AI.method: The authors propose D2R-RAG (Diagnose-to-Repair RAG), a model-agnostic, resource-aware framework that adds a lightweight diagnosis-and-repair layer around an existing RAG pipeline. It extracts observable signals from three components: the input query, the retrieved documents, and the generated answer. From these signals it constructs interpretable “failure signatures” that indicate likely error types (e.g., missing evidence, weakly relevant retrieval, unfaithful generation). Based on the detected signature and explicit constraints on latency and VRAM, the system selects one of a small set of corrective actions (such as re-retrieval, evidence re-ranking, answer regeneration, or more conservative generation) that aim to fix the answer while respecting compute budgets. The framework does not require access to model internals or additional fine-tuning.
AI.result: On the FEVER and HotpotQA benchmarks, D2R-RAG achieves higher reliability than several recent RAG baselines, improving factual accuracy while managing computational cost. Across different compute budgets, it delivers better accuracy–efficiency trade-offs, meaning it gains more accuracy per unit of latency/VRAM than competing methods. The experiments demonstrate that its diagnose-then-repair mechanism is effective in identifying and correcting instances where standard RAG would fail.
AI.conclusion: D2R-RAG is an effective, practical way to harden RAG systems against common failure modes in real-world conditions. By using only observable signals and simple corrective policies, it improves factual reliability without requiring fine-tuning or privileged model access, and it can adapt its repairs to explicit resource limits. This makes it suitable for black-box and budget-constrained deployments while maintaining strong accuracy–efficiency performance.

title: LC-ICL: Label-Guided Contrastive In-Context Learning for Robust Information Extraction
summary: There has been increasing interest in exploring the capabilities of advanced large language models (LLMs) in the field of information extraction (IE), specifically focusing on tasks related to named entity recognition (NER) and relation extraction (RE).Although researchers are exploring the use of few-shot information extraction through in-context learning with LLMs, they tend to focus only on using correct or positive examples for demonstration, neglecting the potential value of incorporating incorrect or negative examples into the learning process.In this paper, we present LC-ICL a novel few-shot technique that leverages both correct and incorrect sample constructions to create in-context learning demonstrations. This approach enhances the ability of LLMs to extract entities and relations by combining positive samples with negative samples annotated by error-cause labels. These labels expose more detailed error features in erroneous examples, enabling the model to understand why similar predictions fail and avoid repeating such errors during inference.Specifically, our proposed method taps into the inherent contextual information and valuable information in hard negative samples and the nearest positive neighbors to the test and then applies the in-context learning demonstrations based on LLMs. Our experiments on various datasets indicate that LC-ICL outperforms previous few-shot in-context learning methods, delivering substantial enhancements in performance across a broad spectrum of related tasks. These improvements are noteworthy, showcasing the versatility of our approach in diverse scenarios.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes LC-ICL, a few-shot in-context learning approach for information extraction that uses both correct and error-labeled incorrect examples to improve LLM performance on NER and relation extraction.
AI.motivation: Most few-shot in-context learning methods for information extraction use only correct (positive) examples, ignoring the informative value of incorrect (negative) examples and their error patterns. The authors aim to exploit these hard negative samples to help LLMs better understand and avoid common extraction errors in NER and RE tasks.
AI.method: LC-ICL constructs in-context demonstrations by combining positive examples with carefully selected hard negative examples that are annotated with error-cause labels. For a given test instance, the approach selects the nearest positive neighbors and related hard negatives, then forms a demonstration set where negative samples explicitly indicate why certain predictions are wrong, leveraging contextual cues and error features to guide the LLM during inference.
AI.result: Experiments across multiple information extraction datasets show that LC-ICL consistently outperforms prior few-shot in-context learning baselines, yielding substantial performance gains on both NER and relation extraction tasks in a variety of settings.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating error-labeled negative examples alongside positive ones in in-context demonstrations allows LLMs to better capture and avoid typical extraction errors, making LC-ICL a more effective and versatile few-shot information extraction method than existing ICL approaches.

title: LLM-Guided Planning for Multi-hop Reasoning over Multimodal Nuclear Regulatory Documents
comment: Accepted at the Second Workshop on Agents in the Wild: Safety, Security, and Beyond @ ICML 2026. 8 pages (main), 3 figures, 1 algorithm
summary: Reviewing nuclear regulatory documents requires multi-hop reasoning across tens of thousands of pages, where judgments depend on evidence assembled across multiple chapters. We frame this task as planning: an LLM-based agent observes the evidence collected so far, picks the next document fragment to inspect, and stops when the evidence is sufficient. The agent operates over a vectorless document tree using browse, read, and search tools, and maintains a dynamic knowledge graph (KG) as state. On a 200-question benchmark over NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report (FSAR) documents, the system reaches 81.5% accuracy with a RAGAS Faithfulness of 0.93. The dominant performance factor is planning: against PageIndex, which uses the same document tree without state-conditioned action selection, the gap is +38.0pp (43.5% to 81.5%, p<0.001). The system also outperforms LightRAG (73.0%, p<0.05), HippoRAG (70.5%, p<0.01), and GraphRAG (49.5%, p<0.001), and matches RAPTOR (75.5%, p=0.11) without offline indexing. Edge inference adds 2.8x cost without raising accuracy; we retain it as a traceability module. Of 7,391 inferred edges, 3 Violates edges (0.04%) flag scope boundaries (Q058) and partial conformance (Q176) as typed annotations that a human reviewer can audit.

AI.tldr: They build a planning-based LLM agent with a dynamic knowledge graph to answer complex multi-hop questions over massive nuclear safety reports, achieving strong accuracy and traceability without heavy offline indexing.
AI.motivation: Regulatory document review, such as nuclear safety analysis, requires combining evidence from many distant sections across tens of thousands of pages. Existing RAG and retrieval methods struggle with such multi-hop reasoning, efficient navigation without dense vector indices, and providing auditable, traceable evidence trails for high-stakes domains. The authors want an LLM system that can plan which parts of the document to inspect, reason over a structured representation of gathered evidence, and offer interpretable, verifiable outputs for regulators.
AI.method: They cast document review as a sequential decision-making and planning problem. An LLM agent operates over a vectorless hierarchical document tree of the NuScale FSAR, using tools to browse, read, and keyword-search sections. As it gathers information, it builds and updates a dynamic knowledge graph representing entities and relations extracted from the text. The agent’s policy conditions on the current KG and evidence to select the next fragment to inspect or to stop when it deems the evidence sufficient. They also experiment with an edge-inference module that infers typed relations such as Violates, aimed at traceability and scope/conformance analysis. The system is evaluated on a 200-question benchmark requiring multi-hop reasoning over the FSAR.
AI.result: On the 200-question benchmark, the system achieves 81.5% answer accuracy and a RAGAS Faithfulness score of 0.93. Planning is identified as the key performance driver: compared to PageIndex, which uses the same document tree but no state-conditioned planning, accuracy improves from 43.5% to 81.5% (+38.0 percentage points, statistically significant with p<0.001). The system also significantly outperforms LightRAG (73.0%, p<0.05), HippoRAG (70.5%, p<0.01), and GraphRAG (49.5%, p<0.001), and performs comparably to RAPTOR (75.5%, p=0.11) while avoiding offline indexing. The edge-inference component increases cost by 2.8x without improving accuracy, but yields a small number of useful typed edges (e.g., Violates) that highlight issues such as scope boundaries and partial conformance.
AI.conclusion: Planning-based LLM agents that operate over structured, vectorless document trees and maintain a dynamic knowledge graph can handle complex, multi-hop question answering over large regulatory documents with high accuracy and faithfulness. The main performance gain comes from intelligent, state-conditioned navigation rather than more elaborate indexing. While automatic edge inference in the knowledge graph does not boost accuracy and is computationally expensive, it contributes to interpretability and traceability by surfacing explicit relations like violations or scope limits that human reviewers can audit. This makes the approach promising for high-stakes regulatory review tasks where both correctness and auditability are critical.

title: EntroRouter: Learning Efficient Model Routing via Entropy Regulation
summary: Model routing balances solution accuracy and computational cost by selecting among models of varying capabilities. While recent multi-round frameworks interleave reasoning and planning, we identify a structural failure mode termed Trust Region Collapse. We demonstrate that the deep coupling of reasoning and routing, exacerbated by the dominance of strong pre-training priors under sparse supervision, leads to degenerate local optima where capable experts are systematically suppressed. To decouple these processes, we propose $\textbf{EntroRouter}$, a single-round routing framework that treats entropy regulation as a core objective. We first initialize the policy via Soft Supervision, fitting a distribution of suitable models to establish a high-entropy prior for exploration. Subsequently, we stabilize Reinforcement Learning using a Soft Anchor, which utilizes offline capability estimates to orchestrate controlled entropy contraction within a safe trust region. Extensive experiments demonstrate that EntroRouter retains 98.3% of the strongest expert's accuracy while reducing computational costs by 48.25%.

AI.tldr: Introduces EntroRouter, a single-round entropy-regularized routing framework that avoids a structural failure mode in model routing (Trust Region Collapse) and achieves near-expert accuracy with about half the compute.
AI.motivation: In model routing systems that choose among models of different strengths to balance accuracy and computation, recent multi-round frameworks tightly couple reasoning and routing. This coupling, combined with strong pre-training priors and limited supervision, can lead to a structural failure mode: the router collapses to overusing weaker models and systematically suppressing stronger experts, converging to degenerate local optima. The paper aims to understand and fix this failure mode while preserving efficiency gains.
AI.method: They first identify and formalize a failure mode called Trust Region Collapse in multi-round reasoning–routing systems. To avoid it, they propose EntroRouter, a single-round routing framework that explicitly optimizes for entropy in the routing policy. The method has two main stages: (1) Soft Supervision, where the routing policy is initialized by fitting to a distribution over suitable models, creating a high-entropy prior that encourages exploration among experts; and (2) a Reinforcement Learning phase with a Soft Anchor, which leverages offline estimates of each model’s capability to guide a gradual, controlled reduction of entropy while keeping the policy within a safe trust region. This design decouples routing from multi-round reasoning and stabilizes learning.
AI.result: Experiments show that EntroRouter achieves 98.3% of the strongest expert model’s accuracy while cutting computational cost by 48.25%, demonstrating that it can nearly match the best model’s performance while using roughly half the compute. The experiments also support the claim that EntroRouter avoids the Trust Region Collapse failure mode seen in multi-round systems.
AI.conclusion: Decoupling routing from multi-round reasoning and explicitly managing entropy in the routing policy can prevent structural collapse in model routing. EntroRouter’s combination of Soft Supervision and Soft Anchor–based RL yields stable, high-entropy exploration followed by safe entropy reduction, achieving near-expert performance with substantially reduced computational cost and mitigating the suppression of capable experts.

title: Mixture of Debaters: Learn to Debate at Architectural Level in Multi-Agent Reasoning
summary: Existing multi-agent debate frameworks suffer from two critical limitations: they rely on static architectures where agent roles and coordination patterns are fixed at design time, and they require instantiating multiple model copies, incurring substantial computational overhead. We propose Mixture of Debaters (MoD), a unified framework that enables dynamic self-debate within a single model by leveraging the Mixture-of-Experts paradigm. We address three key challenges in adapting MoE for dialectical reasoning: (1) dual-routing that decouples role allocation from process flow, dynamically determining when to debate versus when to synthesize; (2) momentum switching that smooths token-level routing with local context, reducing expert-switch jitter; and (3) unified self-debate that encapsulates diverse debating personas into lightweight expert modules, eliminating inter-agent communication while preserving behavioral diversity. Extensive experiments on multimodal benchmarks demonstrate that MoD outperforms both single-model baselines and conventional multi-agent systems, achieving superior accuracy with 3.7x lower latency and 87% reduction in token consumption.The source code can be accessed at https://github.com/YongLD/MoD.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Mixture of Debaters (MoD), a single-model, Mixture-of-Experts based framework that performs dynamic self-debate, improving performance and efficiency over traditional multi-agent debate systems.
AI.motivation: Current multi-agent debate frameworks need multiple full model copies and fixed architectures with predefined roles and coordination patterns, which leads to high computational cost and limited flexibility. The authors want a more efficient and adaptive way to get the benefits of debate-style reasoning without spawning many separate agents.
AI.method: They build MoD, a Mixture-of-Experts architecture tailored for dialectical reasoning. It includes: (1) dual-routing, which separates the decisions of which role an expert plays from how information flows, enabling the model to decide when to debate and when to synthesize; (2) momentum switching, which smooths token-level expert routing over local context to avoid frequent, unstable switching between experts; and (3) unified self-debate, where various debating personas (e.g., different roles or viewpoints) are implemented as lightweight expert modules inside one model, removing explicit inter-agent communication while keeping behavioral diversity.
AI.result: On multiple multimodal benchmarks, MoD yields higher accuracy than both single-model baselines and conventional multi-agent debate systems, while being significantly more efficient: 3.7x lower latency and 87% fewer tokens consumed.
AI.conclusion: Integrating debate-like role diversity and coordination into a single Mixture-of-Experts model can surpass traditional multi-agent debate frameworks in both effectiveness and efficiency. MoD shows that carefully designed routing and expert specialization can realize dynamic self-debate without the overhead of multiple agents.

title: mamabench and mamaretrieval: Benchmarks for Evaluating Medical Retrieval-Augmented Generation in Maternal, Neonatal, and Reproductive Health
comment: 13 pages, 3 tables. Datasets and construction code linked in the paper
summary: Medical question-answering benchmarks rarely cover the maternal, neonatal, child, and reproductive-health questions a nurse-midwife asks, and, to our knowledge, no public chunk-level relevance benchmark exists for maternal-health guideline retrieval. We release two benchmarks that fill these gaps. mamabench is a scope-filtered QA set of 25,949 items assembled from seven existing expert-authored sources across multiple-choice, short-answer, and rubric-graded tracks; to help users calibrate the LLM judge that scores the rubric track, we re-scope HealthBench's physician-labelled meta-evaluation to the domain. mamaretrieval pairs 3,185 clinical queries with graded (0-6) relevance labels over a 63,650-chunk maternal-health guideline corpus, using a decomposed rubric that distinguishes a chunk that answers a query from one merely on its topic. Three decisions shape both: assemble and filter expert sources rather than author questions, grade relevance rather than binarise it, and measure and disclose the limits of the labels -- scope-classifier agreement, a frontier-judge check, and a pooling-completeness audit -- rather than treat them as an oracle. A companion paper uses the benchmarks to evaluate a deployed on-device assistant; both are released openly for research.

AI.tldr: They introduce two new maternal-health-focused benchmarks: one for medical QA and one for guideline retrieval, both with graded relevance labels and careful validation, to support evaluation of LLM-based assistants for nurse-midwives.
AI.motivation: Existing medical QA and retrieval benchmarks largely overlook maternal, neonatal, child, and reproductive-health questions that nurse-midwives encounter, and there is no public benchmark for chunk-level relevance in maternal-health guideline retrieval. This makes it hard to build and rigorously evaluate domain-specific assistants for maternal health.
AI.method: They construct two datasets. (1) mamabench: a 25,949-item QA benchmark focused on maternal health, created by filtering and combining seven existing expert-authored resources and covering multiple-choice, short-answer, and rubric-graded questions. They adapt and rescope HealthBench’s physician-labelled meta-evaluation to calibrate an LLM judge for the rubric-graded track. (2) mamaretrieval: an information retrieval benchmark with 3,185 clinical queries and graded 0–6 relevance labels over a 63,650-chunk corpus of maternal-health guidelines; their rubric differentiates between chunks that fully answer a question and chunks that are only topically related. For both, they prioritize using existing expert material rather than writing new questions, use graded rather than binary relevance, and systematically assess label reliability via scope-classifier agreement, checks with a stronger “frontier” model as judge, and a pooling-completeness audit.
AI.result: They obtain (a) mamabench, a large-scale, domain-scoped maternal-health QA benchmark with multiple answer formats and an LLM-judge calibration protocol, and (b) mamaretrieval, a maternal-health guideline retrieval benchmark with fine-grained relevance scores on a large chunked corpus. They also generate empirical assessments of the quality and limits of their labels through agreement and completeness analyses.
AI.conclusion: The authors provide the first publicly available, rigorously constructed benchmarks focused on maternal-health questions and guideline retrieval, with graded relevance and explicit label-quality analysis. These resources enable more accurate evaluation of LLM-based and IR systems in maternal-health settings, such as on-device assistants for nurse-midwives, and are openly released for research along with a companion paper demonstrating their practical use.

title: FADE: Mitigating Hallucinations by Reducing Language-Prior Dominance in Large Vision-Language Models
summary: Despite the impressive capabilities of Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs), they remain susceptible to hallucination, generating content inconsistent with the input image. Recent studies attribute this to the dominance of language priors over visual inputs and employ contrastive decoding methods to mitigate this dominance, but the mechanistic origin remains unexplored. We investigate the information flow through each transformer layer and find that attention modules consistently aggregate visual evidence, while FFN modules at critical layers act as the source of language priors. These priors can override visual evidence, causing correct predictions in intermediate layers to drift toward incorrect outputs. Based on this insight, we propose FADE (FFN Attenuation for DEcoding), a training-free method that attenuates FFN outputs to reduce language-prior dominance. Evaluations on POPE, CHAIR, and MME benchmarks across LLaVA-1.5, mPLUG-Owl2, and InstructBLIP show that FADE effectively mitigates hallucinations while preserving inference efficiency.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies feed-forward network (FFN) layers as the main source of language priors that cause hallucinations in large vision-language models, and proposes a simple, training-free method (FADE) that attenuates FFN outputs to reduce hallucinations without extra computational cost.
AI.motivation: Large Vision-Language Models are powerful but prone to hallucinations—producing text that conflicts with the input image. Prior work blames an overreliance on language priors and uses contrastive decoding to counteract it, but the internal mechanism of how and where these priors dominate within the model remains unclear. The paper aims to pinpoint the architectural components responsible for hallucinations and to design a lightweight mitigation method that does not require retraining or heavy decoding tricks.
AI.method: The authors analyze information flow layer-by-layer in LVLM transformer stacks, separating the roles of attention and FFN modules. They find attention mainly aggregates visual evidence, while certain FFN layers inject strong language priors that can override visual cues. Using this insight, they introduce FADE (FFN Attenuation for DEcoding), a training-free approach that scales down the contribution of FFN outputs during decoding to weaken language-prior dominance, without modifying attention or the underlying model weights.
AI.result: On standard hallucination and multimodal benchmarks—POPE, CHAIR, and MME—applied to several LVLMs (LLaVA-1.5, mPLUG-Owl2, and InstructBLIP), FADE reduces hallucination rates while maintaining or slightly improving performance on other metrics. It achieves this without retraining or significantly increasing inference time, demonstrating that selectively attenuating FFN outputs can effectively rebalance vision and language influences.
AI.conclusion: Hallucinations in LVLMs arise largely because FFN modules in critical layers inject strong language priors that can override visually grounded intermediate representations, while attention remains mostly faithful to visual evidence. By attenuating FFN outputs at inference time, FADE provides a simple, efficient way to curb hallucinations and improve visual consistency, suggesting that better control of FFN behavior is a key direction for building more reliable vision-language models.

title: To Reason or to Fabricate: Reasoning Without Shortcuts via Hint-Anchored Pairwise Aggregation
summary: While reinforcement learning (RL) significantly enhances LLM reasoning, its efficacy is severely undermined by Pre-RL data overlap, where RL datasets overlap with pretraining or SFT corpora, causing models to exploit shortcuts by memorizing correct answers and fabricating post-hoc reasoning. To address this, we introduce HIPPO, a novel RL framework that integrates hint-injected aggregation with a tailored pairwise reward model. By utilizing hint injection to deliberately trigger overlap-induced behaviors, the resulting traces naturally serve as explicit anchors for pairwise comparison. This provides highly discriminable preference signals, enabling a lightweight judge model to reliably distinguish genuine reasoning deduction from shortcut-driven rationalization, while the pairwise formulation ensures stable and robust optimization compared to standard PRMs. Extensive experiments demonstrate that HIPPO yields substantial improvements over standard baselines and generalizes effectively to out-of-distribution general tasks, showing it extracts authentic, transferable reasoning skills rather than superficial shortcut patterns.

AI.tldr: HIPPO is an RL framework for LLMs that combats data overlap-induced shortcut reasoning by using hint-injected trajectories and a pairwise reward model to better distinguish genuine reasoning from memorization and improve generalization.
AI.motivation: Reinforcement learning can improve LLM reasoning, but in practice RL datasets often overlap with pretraining or SFT data. This overlap allows models to recall answers and then fabricate plausible reasoning, which undermines the goal of learning real, generalizable reasoning skills. The paper aims to mitigate this Pre-RL data overlap problem so that RL actually teaches robust reasoning rather than shortcuts.
AI.method: They propose HIPPO, a reinforcement learning framework with two key components: (1) hint-injected aggregation, where hints are injected to intentionally provoke overlap-driven, shortcut behavior and collect corresponding reasoning traces; and (2) a tailored pairwise reward model that uses those hint-induced traces as negative anchors in pairwise comparisons. A lightweight judge model is trained on these pairs to distinguish genuine deductive reasoning from shortcut-based rationalization, and the pairwise preferences are used as the reward signal during RL optimization, which is claimed to be more stable and robust than standard process reward models (PRMs).
AI.result: Experiments indicate that HIPPO significantly outperforms standard RL baselines on reasoning benchmarks. Models trained with HIPPO show better performance and, crucially, better generalization to out-of-distribution general tasks than models trained with conventional RL or PRMs, suggesting more authentic reasoning ability rather than reliance on memorized shortcuts.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that Pre-RL data overlap leads existing RL methods for LLM reasoning to learn spurious shortcut behaviors, and that HIPPO’s hint-injected, pairwise-reward approach effectively counters this by providing clear preference signals for true reasoning. As a result, HIPPO trains models that acquire more genuine, transferable reasoning skills and that are less dependent on memorization or overlap artifacts.

title: How Much Due Diligence Before You Bid? Learning in Intractable Takeover Auctions
comment: 21 pages, 13 figures, 2 tables. Code and data: https://github.com/zainnab-sparq/imperfect-information-deal-games
summary: When two companies bid to buy the same target, no one knows exactly what the target is worth. Each bidder pays for due diligence: costly, imperfect homework that sharpens its own private estimate before it bids. How much of that homework is worth buying? We build a simple computer model of the bidding contest and let it teach itself to bid well by playing against itself, the way a game engine learns chess. The economic question, how much diligence pays for itself, and the computational question, when the contest becomes too complex to solve exactly, are both controlled by a single thing: how many pieces of private information a bidder carries. Our main finding is that the right amount of diligence is modest and finite. It falls as diligence gets more expensive, and it falls further when both sides are doing their homework, because competition erodes the value of knowing more. We also test a recent claim from AI research: that simple, general self-play methods can rival the specialized, expensive algorithms usually built for games like these. Running on an ordinary laptop with no costly frontier AI, we find the simple methods are the best of the self-learning approaches, though purpose-built exact methods still win whenever the game is small enough to solve outright. The simple methods earn their keep only once the game grows too large to solve exactly, which is the regime real deals live in, and there we show they still find strong bidding strategies. The contribution is threefold: a cheap, reproducible way to study deal-making under uncertainty; a concrete, model-based answer to how much due diligence is worth buying; and evidence about when lightweight, general-purpose AI is good enough to replace specialized methods. We release all the games, code, and experiments.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how much costly due diligence is worth in competitive bidding for a company, using a self-play computer model, and also tests when simple general-purpose AI methods can replace specialized algorithms for such games.
AI.motivation: In real-world M&A bidding, firms must decide how much to spend on due diligence before making offers, but the value of extra information is unclear—especially when rivals are also investigating. At the same time, many such bidding games are too complex to solve exactly, raising the question of whether lightweight, general self-play AI can deliver good bidding strategies without bespoke, expensive algorithms.
AI.method: The authors construct a stylized bidding-game model where each bidder can invest in due diligence to obtain additional private information signals about the target’s value. They then use self-play reinforcement-style learning—where an algorithm repeatedly plays against copies of itself—to learn good bidding strategies and diligence levels. They compare simple, general-purpose self-play techniques to specialized exact-solution methods across games of varying information complexity (number of private signals).
AI.result: They find that the optimal amount of due diligence is limited and moderate: bidders purchase only a finite number of signals, and this level declines as the cost of diligence rises. Optimal diligence falls even more when both bidders invest, because competition erodes the marginal value of extra information. On the computational side, simple, general-purpose self-play methods perform best among self-learning approaches and produce strong strategies for large games, while specialized exact methods dominate only in small games that remain tractable to solve exactly.
AI.conclusion: Due diligence has diminishing and ultimately modest optimal scope in competitive bidding; spending heavily on extra information is not justified, especially when rivals do the same. For economic analysis, the paper offers a cheap, reproducible framework to quantify the value of diligence and bidding strategies under uncertainty. For AI and computation, it shows that lightweight, general self-play methods are practically useful and competitive once games become too complex for exact solution, though they do not outperform specialized exact methods on small, solvable instances. All code and game instances are released to facilitate replication and extension.

title: Which Tokens Need Context? A Reference-Based Analysis of Translation Responsibility Using Fertility and Entropy
comment: This is a work in progress. An extended version with machine translation output analysis and attention correlation is in preparation
summary: When humans translate, not every word depends equally on the surrounding context. Some tokens, particularly function words like pronouns and auxiliaries, rely heavily on preceding or following sentences, while others, such as proper nouns, do not. Understanding this inherent context sensitivity is essential for evaluating whether machine translation systems use context in human-like ways. However, existing approaches to analysing context usage rely on discourse-specific test sets or model internals, making them narrow or model-dependent. We propose a post-hoc, model-agnostic framework to quantify context sensitivity at lexical and syntactic levels using two measures derived from word alignments: fertility (number of target tokens generated per source token) and entropy (stability of fertility patterns across contexts). Using reference translations for three language pairs (German $\leftrightarrow$ English, English $\rightarrow$ Hindi) under four context conditions, we show that context selectively redistributes generative responsibility from source to context tokens without altering overall fertility. Function words show the largest fertility reductions, while content words remain stable, suggesting that context resolves ambiguity rather than adding new information. Our framework provides a ground-truth characterisation of selective context usage in human translation, establishing a diagnostic baseline for evaluating machine translation models.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a model-agnostic, post-hoc framework to quantify how much individual words in human translations depend on broader context, using fertility and entropy from word alignments, and establishes a human baseline for selective context usage to evaluate machine translation systems.
AI.motivation: Existing methods to study how translation uses context either depend on special discourse-focused test sets or on internal signals of specific MT architectures, limiting generality. Humans do not rely equally on context for all words: some items (e.g., pronouns, auxiliaries) are highly context-sensitive, while others (e.g., proper nouns) are not. The authors want a principled, model-independent way to measure this differential context sensitivity at the lexical and syntactic levels in order to understand and evaluate whether MT models use context in a human-like, selective way.
AI.method: They develop a post-hoc, model-agnostic analysis framework based on word alignments between source and reference translations. For each source token, they compute two measures: (1) fertility, the number of target tokens aligned to that source token; and (2) entropy, capturing how stable the fertility patterns are across different context conditions. They apply this to reference human translations for three language directions (German↔English, English→Hindi) created under four different context conditions, allowing them to see how adding or removing context changes the fertility and its variability for different token types (function vs content words, syntactic categories, etc.).
AI.result: Across languages and directions, adding context does not change total fertility overall but redistributes which tokens bear generative responsibility. Function words (e.g., pronouns, auxiliaries) show the largest decreases in fertility when more context is available, whereas content words (e.g., proper nouns, main nouns, verbs) maintain relatively stable fertility. Entropy analyses indicate that context mainly stabilises fertility patterns for those context-sensitive function words. This pattern suggests that context use in human translation is highly selective and concentrated on resolving ambiguities for specific lexical/syntactic categories rather than spreading additional information throughout the sentence.
AI.conclusion: The framework successfully captures selective context sensitivity in human translation in a model-agnostic, post-hoc way, using only alignments between source sentences and human references under different context conditions. The findings show that human translators mainly use larger discourse context to resolve ambiguity for function words, while leaving content word generation largely unchanged. This provides a ground-truth diagnostic of how context should be used and establishes a principled baseline for assessing whether machine translation systems exhibit similarly selective, human-like context usage.

title: Agent-Computer Observation Interfaces Enable Dynamic Computer Use
summary:
SWE-agent established the action interface as an underexplored design axis for software-engineering agents; we make the analogous case for the observation interface in computer-use (CU) agents. Current CU agents, closed and open-source alike, tie observation to action--one screenshot every 3-5 s, no audio--leaving them blind and deaf between screenshots to video, animations, transient UI events, meetings, and spoken instructions. We introduce the Agent-Computer Observation Interface (AOI), a model-agnostic perception layer that decouples continuous, adaptive observation from discrete actions through three gated components: inter-step keyframe capture, volume-gated audio transcription, and CU-model-generated visual narration that persists as text. Each produces almost nothing on static, silent content, reducing to the standard loop without degrading it.
  On DynaCU-Bench (100 dynamic browser tasks plus a 50-task static control), CU models from 7B to frontier scale gain +17 to +48 pp over their screenshot baselines with zero retraining, turning tasks that are near-impossible from periodic screenshots into largely solved ones. The gap is starkest on audio: on a spoken-content subset AOI agents solve every task, whereas streaming voice models hear accurately but cannot act on what they hear without the scaffold. The decomposition is as informative as the headline gain: keyframe selection turns out not to matter--the value comes from narrating captured frames into persistent text--and the interface is not a fixed bundle, since on a newer model (Gemini 3 Flash) the keyframe stream actively regresses through image-token dilution, so its components must be selected per model rather than shipped as one configuration.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes AOI, an improved observation interface for computer-use agents that continuously and adaptively captures keyframes, audio, and narrated visual context, dramatically boosting performance on dynamic browser tasks without retraining.
AI.motivation: Existing computer-use agents only see periodic screenshots and lack audio, leaving them unable to perceive dynamic UI changes, video, animations, transient events, and spoken instructions between frames. This observation bottleneck makes many realistic tasks nearly impossible, even as action interfaces improve. The authors argue that, analogous to how SWE-agent innovated on the action side, the observation interface itself is a crucial and underexplored design dimension for CU agents.
AI.method: They design the Agent-Computer Observation Interface (AOI), a model-agnostic perception layer that decouples continuous observation from discrete actions. AOI has three gated components: (1) inter-step keyframe capture to select informative frames between actions, (2) volume-gated audio transcription to capture speech only when relevant, and (3) CU-model-generated visual narration that converts visual content into persistent text. Each component is adaptive and produces little output when content is static or silent, effectively reverting to the traditional screenshot loop. They evaluate AOI on DynaCU-Bench, a benchmark of 100 dynamic browser tasks plus a 50-task static control, and test with CU models of various sizes (7B to frontier scale).
AI.result: Across model scales, adding AOI on top of existing CU models yields performance gains of +17 to +48 percentage points over screenshot-only baselines, with no model retraining. Tasks that were near-impossible using periodic screenshots become mostly solved. AOI particularly shines on audio-heavy tasks: on a spoken-content subset, AOI-equipped agents solve all tasks, while standard streaming voice models can hear the speech but fail to use it to act. Ablations show that the main value is in narrating captured frames into persistent text rather than in the specific keyframe selection strategy. They also observe that AOI is not a one-size-fits-all bundle; for instance, on Gemini 3 Flash, adding the keyframe image stream can hurt performance via image-token dilution, implying components should be tuned per model.
AI.conclusion: Observation design is a central but overlooked factor in CU agent performance. By decoupling and enriching the observation channel with adaptive keyframes, audio transcription, and textual visual narration, AOI substantially improves agents’ ability to handle dynamic, multimedia tasks without requiring model changes. Persistent textual narration is the key contributor, and AOI should be treated as a configurable interface whose components are chosen per model rather than a fixed recipe. This reframes progress on CU agents as not just a modeling problem, but also an interface and perception engineering problem.

title: The Verbose Context Problem in Medical Records
summary: The verbose context problem occurs when structured concepts have token-inefficient textual representations. This bottleneck is acute in population health: cohort-level analysis of longitudinal patient records requires reasoning over thousands of medically-coded events, often exceeding 400K tokens in total. We present PopMedQA, a benchmark isolating this problem through computational tasks on groups of longitudinal patient records. We construct the benchmark using neopatient, a new library for language-controlled generation of artificial patient records. Through extensive ablations -- including prompting strategies, prompt compression, and agentic decomposition -- we find that domain-independent methods fail to alleviate the verbose context problem. There remains significant opportunity to exploit domain-specific structure in language model inputs for population-scale reasoning.

AI.tldr: They introduce PopMedQA, a benchmark to study how language models struggle with extremely long, structured medical records, and show that generic methods don’t solve this verbose context problem, suggesting the need for domain-specific approaches.
AI.motivation: Population health research needs models that can reason over huge cohorts of patients with long longitudinal records, but current language models are limited by token length and inefficient textual representations of structured medical data. The authors want to isolate and rigorously study this “verbose context problem,” especially as it appears when aggregating and analyzing many patient records.
AI.method: They build PopMedQA, a benchmark consisting of computational question-answering tasks defined over groups of longitudinal patient records. To generate data in a controlled way, they create neopatient, a library for language-controlled generation of artificial patient records. They then run extensive ablation experiments over this benchmark, testing different domain-independent strategies like varied prompting, prompt compression techniques, and agentic decomposition to see how well they mitigate the verbose context bottleneck.
AI.result: Across their experiments, generic, domain-agnostic techniques—such as alternative prompting strategies, compressing prompts, or decomposing tasks into smaller agentic steps—do not meaningfully overcome the verbose context problem when dealing with large-scale longitudinal medical data in PopMedQA.
AI.conclusion: The verbose context problem remains a major obstacle for language models working on population-level health tasks that require reasoning over large, structured medical records. Domain-independent methods are insufficient; future progress will likely require leveraging the specific structure and semantics of medical and cohort data to design more efficient input representations and model interfaces.

title: Faults in Our Formal Benchmarking: Dataset Defects and Evaluation Failures in Lean Theorem Proving
summary: Benchmarks for LLM-assisted theorem proving in Lean are often treated as intrinsically reliable because every solved instance comes with a machine-checked proof. However, the kernel only checks that a proof establishes a \emph{formal} statement; it does not verify that the statement faithfully encodes the intended informal problem, nor that evaluation harnesses are robust to trivial or adversarial solutions. We audit five widely used Lean theorem-proving benchmarks and their forks, using corpus-scale static checkers to surface 4,833 findings, including 398 mechanically certified issues such as counterexamples, vacuous theorems, and unsound axioms. We also document semantic defects such as missing hypotheses, problem simplification, incomplete or incorrect translations, and Lean-specific specification hazards. Beyond dataset construction, we survey evaluation-time failure modes and show, on corrected subsets, that defects can both inflate and deflate reported prover scores. We propose a fault taxonomy, a suite of automated checkers and recall-oriented semantic audit prompts, and release standards to guide the creation of formal math datasets and to make evaluation more reproducible and trustworthy. Our checkers, audit prompts, and corrected dataset snapshots are available at https://github.com/Shashi456/atp-checkers.

AI.tldr: They audit popular Lean theorem-proving benchmarks and show they contain many latent flaws, then provide tools, taxonomy, and corrected datasets to make evaluations more reliable.
