AI.motivation: LLM-assisted theorem proving benchmarks in Lean are widely trusted because proofs are machine-checked, but this only guarantees correctness of the formal statement, not that the benchmark problems faithfully represent the intended informal mathematics or are free of trivial/adversarial loopholes. As a result, benchmark scores may be misleading, and there is a need to systematically uncover and categorize such defects and strengthen dataset and evaluation practices.
AI.method: They perform a large-scale audit of five widely used Lean theorem-proving benchmarks and their forks using corpus-scale static analysis tools. These tools act as automated checkers to detect issues like counterexamples, vacuous truths, and unsound axioms. They also conduct a qualitative audit to uncover semantic and specification-level problems (e.g., missing hypotheses, translation errors) and analyze evaluation-time failure modes. Finally, they design a taxonomy of faults, a suite of automated checkers, and semantic-audit prompts, and package these along with corrected dataset snapshots and standards for dataset construction and evaluation.
AI.result: The audit surfaces 4,833 potential issues, including 398 that are mechanically certified as concrete problems such as counterexamples, vacuous theorems, and unsound axioms. They identify additional semantic defects like missing assumptions, oversimplified problems, incorrect or incomplete translations from informal to formal statements, and Lean-specific specification pitfalls. On corrected subsets of the benchmarks, they empirically demonstrate that these defects can both artificially increase and decrease reported performance of theorem provers, highlighting that existing scores are not fully trustworthy.
AI.conclusion: Formal proofs in Lean benchmarks do not by themselves guarantee that the underlying benchmark problems are semantically sound or robust, and current LLM-assisted theorem-proving evaluations can be significantly distorted by dataset and harness defects. Systematic auditing—via fault taxonomies, automated static checkers, and structured semantic review—reveals widespread issues and is essential for creating trustworthy benchmarks. The authors provide tools, audit prompts, standards, and corrected snapshots to improve the reliability, reproducibility, and interpretability of formal-math evaluations going forward.

title: Preference-ASR: A Preference-Aware Test Set for Benchmarking ASR in the Era of Speech LLMs
summary: Popular ASR test sets adopt inconsistent conventions for numbers, disfluencies, entities, and casing, while standard normalizers erase the format distinctions users care about. Current benchmarks therefore cannot measure whether a model follows user preferences for output style. We introduce PreferenceASR, a test set evaluating ASR systems on their ability to follow natural-language preference instructions across four categories: normalization, entities, disfluencies, and case. Built from seven open-source corpora via a two-stage LLM-assisted pipeline with human verification, it is evaluated with a preference-aware normalizer that selectively skips steps matching the active instruction. Benchmarking four models shows rankings shift across preference types, exposing quality differences traditional evaluation obscures. We publicly release the dataset.

AI.tldr: PreferenceASR introduces a new ASR benchmark that tests whether models can follow user-specified style preferences (normalization, entities, disfluencies, casing), revealing quality differences missed by standard metrics.
AI.motivation: Existing ASR test sets use inconsistent conventions and standard text normalizers remove stylistic distinctions, so current benchmarks cannot tell whether models honor user preferences about how transcripts should look (e.g., numbers written out vs. digits, handling of disfluencies, entity formatting, and casing).
AI.method: The authors build PreferenceASR, a test set derived from seven open-source corpora using a two-stage pipeline assisted by large language models, followed by human verification. They also design a preference-aware text normalizer that can skip specific normalization operations depending on the active natural-language instruction (e.g., “keep disfluencies,” “preserve casing”). They then benchmark four ASR models under various preference instructions.
AI.result: The evaluation shows that model rankings change depending on the preference type (normalization, entities, disfluencies, case). This indicates that some systems are better at following certain stylistic instructions than others, and that these differences are not captured by traditional ASR evaluation pipelines that use fixed normalization. The dataset and tooling are released publicly.
AI.conclusion: PreferenceASR provides a way to measure how well ASR systems follow natural-language style and formatting preferences, going beyond conventional error rates after fixed normalization. This reveals previously hidden quality differences and encourages development of ASR models that can flexibly adapt their outputs to specific user formatting needs.

title: Cognitive World Models for Process-Level Social Influence Evaluation
summary: Social influence dialogue changes user behavior by altering internal cognitive states. The central evaluation question is whether the user's beliefs, desires, intentions, and emotions measurably change over the course of conversation, a process-oriented criterion that neither surface-level text metrics (BLEU/ROUGE) nor single-score LLM judgments can capture. We propose the \textbf{Cog}nitive \textbf{W}orld \textbf{M}odel \textbf{(CogWM)}, an LLM-based user model that reframes multi-turn dialogue evaluation from ``what did the user say'' to ``how did the user's internal cognitive state evolves.'' CogWM jointly predicts BDI/E cognitive states and user utterances and serves as both a user simulator and an evaluation platform, using a three-tier evaluation framework that covers turn-level fidelity, trajectory-level state dynamics, and task-level composite scoring. Trained via our \textbf{S}ummarize-\textbf{a}nd-\textbf{A}llocate \textbf{(SaA)} annotation pipeline on 150,454 user-turn samples across four social influence scenarios, CogWM achieves 77.6\% emotion accuracy (2.1$\times$ over GPT-5.5). In 3600 multi-agent discrimination trials, it distinguishes six commercial agents by their cognitive influence, with Llama-4-Scout ranking first (CTS +0.233). CogWM moves social influence dialogue evaluation from terminal judgment to process tracking. We have released our code\footnote{\scriptsize Code: https://github.com/lucianma05-create/CogWM} and models\footnote{Model: https://www.modelscope.cn/models/LucianMa/CogWM-14B}.

AI.tldr: CogWM is an LLM-based cognitive world model to evaluate social influence dialogues by modeling users' internal cognitive states instead of just surface text, offering process-level metrics of influence.
AI.motivation: Existing dialogue evaluation metrics like BLEU/ROUGE or single-score LLM ratings only assess surface text or terminal outcomes and fail to capture how a user's internal beliefs, desires, intentions, and emotions (BDI/E) change during social influence conversations. The authors want a process-oriented, cognitively grounded evaluation that can track and quantify how dialogue agents shape user cognition over time.
AI.method: They introduce CogWM, an LLM-based user model that explicitly represents and predicts users' BDI/E cognitive states at each turn, jointly with their utterances. CogWM is trained via a Summarize-and-Allocate (SaA) annotation pipeline that labels cognitive states for each user turn across four social influence tasks, producing 150k+ samples. They design a three-tier evaluation framework: (1) turn-level fidelity of predicted cognitive states and utterances, (2) trajectory-level dynamics of state evolution over the conversation, and (3) task-level composite scores reflecting overall social influence performance. CogWM is also used as a user simulator and as an evaluation engine in multi-agent settings.
AI.result: CogWM, trained on 150,454 annotated user turns, achieves 77.6% accuracy on emotion prediction, which is 2.1x higher than a GPT-5.5 baseline. In 3,600 multi-agent discrimination experiments, CogWM can reliably distinguish six commercial dialogue agents based on their cognitive influence profiles; among them, Llama-4-Scout is ranked highest with a Cognitive Task Score (CTS) improvement of +0.233.
AI.conclusion: CogWM demonstrates that modeling users' internal cognitive trajectories enables finer-grained, process-based evaluation of social influence dialogues than traditional text or one-shot LLM metrics. It provides both a user simulator and an evaluation platform, supporting multi-level analysis of how agents alter user beliefs, desires, intentions, and emotions. This shifts evaluation from final judgments to tracking cognitive processes throughout the interaction, and the released code and models facilitate further research in cognitively grounded dialogue evaluation.

title: AURORA: Asymmetry and Update-Induced Rotation for Robust Hallucination Detection in Large Language Models
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable capabilities across a wide range of natural language processing tasks. However, their tendency to generate hallucinations, namely factually incorrect or unfaithful outputs, poses a critical obstacle to their deployment in high-stakes applications. Although recent hallucination detection methods have made encouraging progress, they typically rely on costly output-level consistency checks or static hidden-state probes that capture shallow dataset-specific patterns, leading to substantial degradation under cross-dataset evaluation. In this work, we propose AURORA, a novel hallucination detection framework that shifts the focus from static representations to the weight-gradient dynamics of LLMs. Our key insight is that hallucinated and faithful answers induce qualitatively different gradient update patterns on the model's parameters. Specifically, hallucinated samples trigger asymmetric and structurally misaligned gradients, which can be captured through two complementary features: (1) the skewness of the cosine similarity distribution between weight matrices and their gradient update directions, and (2) the rotation ratio, which quantifies how much the gradient update reorients the singular-vector basis of weight matrices via SVD. AURORA achieves strong hallucination detection performance across four model families and four benchmark datasets. Further analyses demonstrate that our method scales effectively across model sizes and transfers to out-of-domain tasks, including mathematical reasoning and vision-language scenarios.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes AURORA, a hallucination detection method for large language models based on analyzing gradient dynamics rather than static outputs or hidden states, achieving strong and transferable performance across models and datasets.
AI.motivation: Large Language Models often hallucinate—produce factually incorrect outputs—which limits their use in high-stakes domains. Existing detectors either run expensive consistency checks on outputs or use static probes that overfit and generalize poorly across datasets. There is a need for a more robust, generalizable, and efficient way to detect hallucinations that works across different models, tasks, and domains.
AI.method: The authors introduce AURORA, a framework that characterizes hallucinations via the gradient update patterns induced by model outputs. For a given answer, they compute the gradients of the model’s loss with respect to its weights and derive two key features from these gradients: (1) skewness of the cosine similarity between each weight matrix and its gradient, capturing asymmetry and misalignment, and (2) a rotation ratio based on SVD that measures how much the gradient changes the singular-vector basis of weight matrices. These gradient-based features are then used as signals to classify outputs as hallucinated or faithful. The approach is evaluated on multiple LLM families and benchmarks, and also tested on out-of-domain tasks.
AI.result: AURORA delivers strong hallucination detection accuracy across four different LLM families and four benchmark datasets, outperforming or matching prior methods while being more robust. It scales well with increasing model size and maintains effectiveness when transferred to substantially different settings, such as math reasoning tasks and multimodal vision-language tasks.
AI.conclusion: Gradient dynamics encode discriminative signals of hallucination in LLMs: hallucinated answers systematically induce asymmetric, structurally misaligned gradients that can be captured via skewness of cosine similarity and rotation ratio metrics. By exploiting these properties, AURORA provides a scalable, model- and dataset-robust framework for hallucination detection that generalizes better than static probes or output-level consistency checks and extends to out-of-domain and multimodal scenarios.

title: UCOB: Learning to Utilize and Evolve Agentic Skills via Credit-Aware On-Policy Bidirectional Self-Distillation
summary: Skill memories can improve agentic reinforcement learning by reusing past experience as textual guidance, but retrieved skills are not oracular: they may help in one state while misleading the same policy in another. This makes the common privileged-teacher assumption fragile, namely that a skill-conditioned prompt can be treated as a fixed teacher for the no-skill prompt. We introduce UCOB, a framework for learning to utilize and evolve agentic skills via credit-aware on-policy bidirectional self-distillation. UCOB treats skill-conditioned and no-skill prompts as two on-policy context views of the same model, compares their return-to-go within the same task and anchor state, and uses the higher-return view as the local teacher. This local credit signal internalizes useful skill-conditioned behavior, corrects misleading skill usage, and guides task/state skill memory updates, utility-aware retrieval, and reflection self-training. Experiments on agentic tasks, including ALFWorld, WebShop, and Search-QA, show that UCOB outperforms skill-free RL, skill-memory baselines, and self-distillation methods across model scales, with up to 23.5 and 18.0 point gains over SOTA baselines on ALFWorld and WebShop. Ablations and analyses further validate its core mechanisms and efficiency.

AI.tldr: UCOB is a reinforcement-learning framework that dynamically decides when to trust or ignore skill-memory guidance by comparing returns between skill-conditioned and no-skill prompts, using the better one as a local teacher for self-distillation and skill updating.
AI.motivation: Existing agentic RL systems use skill memories—retrieved textual guidance from past experience—to improve performance. However, these retrieved skills are not always reliable: a skill that helps in one state can mislead in another. Prior work typically assumes a privileged-teacher setting, where the skill-conditioned policy is treated as an inherently better teacher for the base policy. This assumption breaks down when skills are noisy or partially applicable, leading to degraded learning and brittle behavior. The motivation is to design a principled way to exploit useful skills while avoiding or correcting harmful ones, and to update the skill memory itself based on actual task performance.
AI.method: UCOB (credit-aware on-policy bidirectional self-distillation) treats the agent with a skill-conditioned prompt and the same agent with a no-skill prompt as two alternative context views (policies) for the same underlying model. For each task and anchor state, it evaluates both views on-policy and computes their return-to-go. The view with the higher return is designated as the local teacher; the other acts as the student. Using this local credit signal, UCOB performs bidirectional self-distillation—sometimes distilling from skill to no-skill, other times from no-skill to skill—so that good behaviors are shared and bad skill usages are corrected. The same signal also drives updates to the skill memory: deciding which experiences become or remain skills, how retrieval is prioritized (utility-aware retrieval), and how reflection-based self-training is carried out.
AI.result: Across multiple agentic benchmarks—ALFWorld, WebShop, and Search-QA—UCOB consistently surpasses several baselines: standard skill-free RL, existing skill-memory approaches, and other self-distillation methods. It achieves up to 23.5 and 18.0 point performance improvements over state-of-the-art baselines on ALFWorld and WebShop, respectively. Ablation studies and analyses confirm that the key components—credit-aware teacher selection, bidirectional distillation, and utility-aware skill memory updates—are each important and together provide an efficient and robust performance gains.
AI.conclusion: UCOB demonstrates that treating skill-conditioned guidance not as an always-correct teacher but as one of two competing context views, and assigning teaching roles based on local credit (return) signals, leads to more robust and effective use of skill memories in agentic RL. By jointly optimizing the policy and the skill memory through bidirectional self-distillation and credit-driven updates, the framework improves performance, reliability, and data efficiency across diverse agentic tasks and model scales.

title: Coverage-Driven KV Cache Eviction for Efficient and Improved Inference of LLM
summary: Large language models (LLMs) excel at complex tasks like question answering and summarization, thanks to their ability to handle long-context inputs. However, deploying LLMs is costly, not only due to the high computational demands of quadratic complexity of self-attention and auto-regressive generation, but also because of the significant memory overhead required for storing the key-value (KV) cache during inference. To reduce the memory cost, existing KV-cache eviction strategies leverage the sparsity in attention to selectively store a subset of tokens. While reducing the memory footprint, such approaches show a considerable drop in performance, especially in tasks that require long-context reasoning. We identify that the drop in performance is linked to a reduction in the coverage of unique tokens. Additionally, we theoretically show that reduced coverage limits the mutual information between inputs and outputs, thereby impairing predictive accuracy. To this end, we introduce K-VEC, a novel coverage-aware KV-cache eviction strategy that prioritizes token coverage while evicting tokens in the cache. K-VEC introduces a cross-head and a cross-layer coverage module to enhance token retention across attention heads and model layers, mitigating performance degradation caused by low coverage. Evaluated on 16 LongBench subsets, K-VEC exhibit up to 10.35 points improvement over the existing methods under the same eviction rate and memory constraint. Comprehensive evaluations validate the effectiveness of our approach and demonstrate its potential for efficient LLM deployment in resource-constrained settings.

AI.tldr: K-VEC is a coverage-aware KV-cache eviction strategy for LLMs that reduces memory while preserving performance on long-context tasks.
AI.motivation: LLMs need large KV caches during inference, which creates high memory and deployment costs. Existing KV-cache eviction methods reduce memory but hurt performance, particularly on long-context reasoning, likely because they fail to preserve enough informative tokens. The paper aims to design an eviction scheme that saves memory without sacrificing long-context accuracy.
AI.method: The authors analyze the relation between token coverage in the KV cache and model performance, theoretically linking reduced coverage to lower mutual information between inputs and outputs. They then propose K-VEC, a KV-cache eviction strategy that explicitly prioritizes coverage of unique tokens across the context. K-VEC uses a cross-head and cross-layer coverage module to coordinate which tokens are retained across attention heads and layers, aiming to maximize effective coverage under a fixed memory budget and eviction rate.
AI.result: On 16 LongBench subsets, K-VEC achieves up to 10.35-point improvement over existing KV-cache eviction baselines under the same memory and eviction constraints, indicating better preservation of long-context reasoning ability while reducing KV-cache size.
AI.conclusion: Token coverage in the KV cache is critical for LLLM performance, especially for long-context reasoning. By designing a coverage-aware eviction strategy with cross-head and cross-layer coordination, K-VEC significantly mitigates the performance loss typically associated with KV-cache compression, enabling more memory-efficient LLM deployment in resource-constrained environments.

title: OSWorld2.0: Benchmarking Computer Use Agents on Long-Horizon Real-World Tasks
comment: 68 pages, 42 figures. Equal contribution: Mengqi Yuan, Zilong Zhou, and Xinzhuang Xiong
summary: Existing computer-use benchmarks fail to capture the realism, complexity, and long-horizon demands of real-world computer use, limiting their ability to reveal the limitations of frontier agents. We introduce OSWorld 2.0, a benchmark of 108 long-horizon computer-use workflows across everyday and professional tasks, designed to capture complex and challenging real-world phenomena. Each task represents a realistic end-to-end workflow that takes human users a median of about 1.6 hours to complete and requires an average of 318 tool calls with Claude Opus 4.7 using maximum thinking, compared with about 30 in OSWorld 1.0. OSWorld 2.0 targets challenge phenomena that are common in real workflows yet underrepresented in prior benchmarks, spanning interaction-design challenges such as streaming interaction and dynamic environments, as well as agent-pattern challenges such as cross-source reasoning, implicit-state inference, and visual-spatial precision. Tasks are grounded in authentic input artifacts and cross-referenced against realistic stateful user profile data, and include separate safety reports auditing safety-sensitive execution. Under our primary binary-completion metric at 500 steps, Claude Opus 4.8 with maximum thinking and batched tool calls scores best but still completes only 20.6% of tasks at a 54.8% partial score; GPT-5.5 is far more token-efficient yet plateaus near 13%. These results show that current agents are still far from professional-level computer use: rather than stumbling on basic GUI control or coding, they lose track of constraints, miss information that arrives mid-task, guess rather than ask the user, and skip verification, struggling most when a task hinges on hidden state they must recover.

AI.tldr: OSWorld 2.0 is a much harder, more realistic benchmark of long, complex computer-use workflows that exposes current AI agents’ limitations in real-world software use.
AI.motivation: Previous computer-use benchmarks are too short and simple, so they fail to reflect the complexity, duration, and dynamic nature of real professional computer use and therefore cannot adequately stress-test frontier AI agents.
AI.method: The authors construct OSWorld 2.0, a suite of 108 realistic end-to-end workflows based on everyday and professional tasks, each grounded in real artifacts and user profile data, including safety audits. They explicitly design tasks to exhibit underrepresented challenge phenomena—like streaming interaction, dynamic environments, cross-source reasoning, implicit-state inference, and visual–spatial precision—and evaluate multiple frontier agents (e.g., Claude Opus variants and GPT-5.5) under a fixed step budget, logging tool calls and performance metrics such as binary completion and partial scores.
AI.result: Human users take a median of 1.6 hours per workflow, while Claude Opus 4.7 requires around 318 tool calls on average (vs ~30 in OSWorld 1.0), confirming substantially higher complexity. Under a 500-step cap, Claude Opus 4.8 performs best among tested systems but fully completes only 20.6% of tasks (with 54.8% partial completion), while GPT-5.5, though more token-efficient, saturates around 13% completion.
AI.conclusion: OSWorld 2.0 reveals that current frontier agents remain far from professional-level computer-use competence. Their main failure modes are not low-level GUI manipulation or coding, but high-level workflow management: losing track of constraints, missing mid-task information, guessing instead of querying the user, skipping verification, and failing when success depends on recovering or reasoning about hidden state.

title: Anisotropy Decides Cosine vs. Rank Metrics for Text Embeddings
summary: The standard way to compare two text embeddings is cosine similarity. Scattered studies report that a different metric does better, but never pin down the geometric condition that decides when, or why. We settle both with a comprehensive empirical study: nineteen parameter-free similarity metrics on nineteen encoders, from compact sentence transformers up to seven-billion-parameter large language models, across seven datasets. The answer is geometric. When an encoder spreads its variance evenly across directions, cosine is the best parameter-free choice and no other metric helps by a usable margin. When the variance concentrates into a few dominant directions, a property known as anisotropy, rank-based and L1-type metrics beat cosine by a clear margin. The absolute gain is modest, but because cosine starts low on these encoders it is a sizable relative improvement, around twenty percent on average and largest where cosine is weakest. What decides this is the geometry of the embedding space, not how the model was trained: where the two disagree, the metric follows the geometry. One number, the fraction of variance held by the single most dominant dimension, predicts how much the alternatives help across all nineteen encoders, with a rank correlation of 0.86 and a linear correlation of 0.95. To test this as the cause rather than a correlate, we project out the dominant directions: cosine recovers and the advantage of the other metrics nearly vanishes, but only on the encoders that were anisotropic to begin with. The effect is directional, not magnitude based, since it survives normalizing every vector to unit length. Among parameter-free metrics, then, cosine is the right tool wherever an encoder is well spread, which includes the fine-tuned embedders commonly deployed for retrieval, and we give a one-number diagnostic for when it is not.

AI.tldr: The paper systematically compares 19 parameter-free similarity metrics across 19 text encoders and 7 datasets, showing that the right metric is determined by the embedding space’s geometry, especially its anisotropy.
AI.motivation: Cosine similarity is the de facto standard for comparing text embeddings, but scattered results suggest other metrics can sometimes perform better without clearly explaining when or why. There is no unified understanding or simple diagnostic to choose among similarity metrics for different encoders.
AI.method: The authors run a comprehensive empirical study of 19 parameter-free similarity metrics (e.g., cosine, rank-based, L1-type metrics) across 19 different text encoders, ranging from small sentence transformers to 7B-parameter LLMs, on 7 benchmark datasets. They analyze the geometry of each embedding space, focusing on variance distribution across dimensions, measure anisotropy (dominant directions), compute correlations between anisotropy and metric performance, and perform interventions by projecting out dominant directions and by normalizing vector magnitudes to isolate directional vs magnitude effects.
AI.result: They find that cosine similarity is optimal among parameter-free metrics when an encoder’s variance is evenly spread across embedding dimensions (low anisotropy). When variance is concentrated in a few dominant directions (high anisotropy), rank-based and L1-type metrics clearly outperform cosine, with average relative improvements around 20%, especially where cosine initially performs poorly. A single scalar—the fraction of total variance in the most dominant dimension—predicts the benefit of alternative metrics with rank correlation 0.86 and linear correlation 0.95 across all 19 encoders. Removing dominant directions makes cosine competitive again and largely eliminates the advantage of alternative metrics, but only for originally anisotropic encoders. The effects persist after unit normalization, showing they are directional rather than magnitude-based.
AI.conclusion: The choice of similarity metric for text embeddings should be guided by the geometry of the embedding space, particularly its anisotropy, not by the training method or model family alone. Cosine similarity is the best parameter-free metric when embeddings are well spread (low anisotropy), which includes many fine-tuned retrieval embedders. For anisotropic encoders, rank-based and L1-type metrics perform better. The paper provides a simple one-number diagnostic—the variance fraction of the top dimension—to decide when cosine is appropriate and when alternatives are likely to yield meaningful gains.

title: Learned Coordination Conventions in Cooperative MARL: Measuring the Translation Gap Between Theory-Informed Roles and Learned Routing
comment: 8 pages, 1 figures. Poster Accepted at NExT-Game 2026: New Frontiers in Game-Theoretic Learning, ICML 2026 Workshop
summary:
Role-semantic assignments provide priors over how heterogeneous agents may coordinate, but cooperative MARL systems instead settle on conventions through decentralized, non-stationary learning, with no guarantee that the resulting structure matches those priors. We study this translation gap between theory-informed role expectations and learned coordination structure through a diagnostic combining a role-routing matrix, formation sensitivity ($Δ_{\max}$), and gradient/occlusion attribution across three-role MiniGrid and SMACv2 (Terran) environments.
  We show that label-conditioned attention produces substantially more concentrated and role-specific routing than flat MLP baselines, remains stable under 3v3--9v9 scaling, transfers zero-shot across team sizes, and is invariant to ally-slot padding. A 5-seed re-evaluation shows partial alignment between learned conventions and designer-specified priors while revealing where small-n noise can manufacture apparent strategic divergence. We present these results as an empirical framework for measuring coordination structure in cooperative MARL rather than as a new equilibrium concept or causal explanation.

AI.tldr: They propose an empirical diagnostic framework to compare designer-specified roles with the coordination structures that emerge in cooperative multi-agent reinforcement learning, showing label-conditioned attention better matches role priors and scales/transfer well.
AI.motivation: In cooperative multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL), designers often have prior expectations about agent roles (role semantics), but learned coordination patterns may not match these priors because training is decentralized, non-stationary, and unconstrained. There is a lack of tools to quantitatively analyze how emergent conventions align with or diverge from role-based theories. The paper aims to bridge this gap by providing a way to measure and visualize the coordination structure that MARL policies actually implement, relative to role expectations.
AI.method: They design a diagnostic framework that includes: (1) a role-routing matrix capturing how information/attention flows between agents of different roles; (2) a formation sensitivity metric (Δ_max) to quantify how policy behavior changes under role/position perturbations; and (3) gradient- and occlusion-based attribution analyses to see which agents/roles influence decisions. They apply this framework to cooperative environments with three roles (MiniGrid and SMACv2 Terran scenarios), comparing standard MLP-based policies to policies using label-conditioned attention (where explicit role labels condition communication/attention). They also test robustness across different team sizes (3v3 up to 9v9), zero-shot transfer across team sizes, and invariance to ally-slot padding. A 5-seed re-evaluation is used to assess variability and the risk of over-interpreting small-sample results.
AI.result: Label-conditioned attention leads to more concentrated and role-specific routing patterns in the role-routing matrix than flat MLP baselines, indicating clearer specialization and alignment with role semantics. These structured coordination patterns remain stable when scaling from 3v3 to 9v9 agents, transfer zero-shot across different team sizes, and are robust to changes in how ally slots are padded. The multi-seed analysis shows that, while there is partial alignment between emergent conventions and designer-defined role priors, some previously apparent strategic differences can be attributed to noise from small numbers of training seeds rather than true structural divergence.
AI.conclusion: The paper does not propose a new theoretical equilibrium concept or causal mechanism for coordination. Instead, it contributes an empirical measurement framework for diagnosing and quantifying coordination structure in cooperative MARL. This framework helps clarify how and when learned conventions align with designer role priors, highlights the benefits of label-conditioned attention for realizing role semantics in practice, and cautions against over-interpreting single-seed results due to noise-driven artifacts.

title: MAM-AI: An On-Device Medical Retrieval-Augmented Generation System for Nurses and Midwives in Zanzibar
comment: 36 pages. Video demo: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=M_Kruluel28 ; browser demo, code, models, and benchmarks linked in the paper
summary: Maternal and newborn mortality remain among the highest in sub-Saharan Africa, where midwifery care is often delivered by nurses who lack midwifery training to international standards, and consulting authoritative guidance at the point of care is hard: the guidelines are long and connectivity is intermittent. We present MAM-AI, a medical question-answering assistant for nurse-midwives in Zanzibar that runs entirely on a commodity Android device: a question is embedded (EmbeddingGemma, 300M) and matched against a curated corpus of 87 guideline documents (63,650 passages), then answered with citations by a 4B int4 generator (Gemma 4 E4B), fully offline, with no query leaving the device. We evaluate the exact deployed configuration with a layered methodology -- retriever, generator under oracle context, end-to-end, and latency -- scored by LLM judges validated against physician rubrics. The evaluation relocates the hard problem. On-device retrieval is essentially solved: the 300M embedder ranks third of seven retrievers and rivals cloud systems, so the passages the system needs are usually found. The small generator is what remains in doubt: adding retrieved context does not improve its answers, and at 4B it cannot be both helpful and safe at once -- of two same-size candidates, the more helpful one commits genuine dangerous errors, so we deploy the other, which is about twice as faithful to its sources (as faithful as a frontier model), and recover its helpfulness with a redesigned prompt that cuts deflection from 33% to 3%. Corpus quality is decisive for the same reason: where the corpus holds the right passage the answer is specific and actionable, and where it does not it goes vague. MAM-AI is a thoroughly evaluated, open-source research prototype, not a fielded product; the system, knowledge base, benchmarks, and evaluation harness are released.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces MAM-AI, an offline, on-device medical QA assistant for nurse-midwives in Zanzibar, showing that retrieval can work very well on small devices but that safely generating helpful answers with a 4B model is still challenging and depends heavily on corpus quality and prompting.
AI.motivation: Maternal and newborn mortality are very high in sub-Saharan Africa, where midwifery care is often provided by nurses without full, internationally‑standard midwifery training. Accessing long, complex clinical guidelines is difficult, especially with intermittent internet connectivity. The authors want to provide point-of-care, trustworthy decision support that works entirely offline on low-cost Android devices while respecting privacy (no queries leaving the device).
AI.method: They build MAM-AI, an on-device retrieval‑augmented generation (RAG) system. User questions are embedded using a 300M-parameter EmbeddingGemma model and matched against a curated corpus of 87 guideline documents split into 63,650 passages. A 4B-parameter int4-quantized Gemma model (Gemma 4 E4B) generates answers with citations, all locally on the device. They evaluate with a layered methodology: (1) retriever performance, (2) generator with oracle (ideal) context, (3) full end-to-end system, and (4) latency. Outputs are judged by LLM evaluators whose scoring is validated against physician rubrics. They also experiment with different 4B generators and prompt designs to trade off helpfulness vs safety/faithfulness.
AI.result: The 300M embedding model achieves near–state-of-the-art retrieval, ranking 3rd of 7 retrievers and competing with cloud systems; needed passages are typically retrieved. However, the 4B generator struggles: providing retrieved context does not reliably improve answer quality, and a more helpful 4B model introduces dangerous medical errors. They instead choose a more conservative 4B model that roughly doubles faithfulness to sources (comparable to a frontier model), and then recover much of the lost helpfulness by redesigning the prompt to reduce deflective answers from 33% to 3%. They find that corpus coverage critically determines answer specificity: when the right passage exists, answers are concrete and actionable; otherwise, answers become vague. Latency remains acceptable for on-device use.
AI.conclusion: On-device retrieval for medical QA is essentially a solved problem even with a 300M-parameter embedder on commodity Android hardware, but safe and useful answer generation with small 4B models is still a key bottleneck. Safety and faithfulness require careful model selection, prompt engineering, and high-quality, comprehensive guideline corpora. MAM-AI is released as an open-source, thoroughly evaluated research prototype with its system, knowledge base, benchmarks, and evaluation harness, but is not yet ready as a deployed clinical product.

title: SCARCE: Scalable Cascade Analysis for Rare-event Characterisation via Embeddings
summary: Rare events govern the safety profile of modern AI systems, yet their probabilities are extremely difficult to estimate: direct Monte Carlo requires prohibitive sample budgets. Subset Simulation (SS) addresses this by decomposing a rare-event probability into moderate conditional probabilities over nested intermediate events. However, classical SS requires a handcrafted scalar performance function whose sublevel sets define those events, demanding detailed knowledge of the failure geometry and limiting transfer to new domains. We propose SCARCE (Scalable Cascade Analysis for Rare-event Characterisation via Embeddings), which replaces the performance function with learned latent representations and geometric rulers that score proximity to failure regions. Adaptive thresholding constructs nested intermediate events directly from data. We formalise SCARCE through a non-negative supermartingale, yielding a high-probability upper envelope that remains valid under early stopping. On MNIST misclassification, where dense Monte Carlo provides ground truth, SCARCE achieves approximately 400--500 times lower mean absolute error than grid-searched traditional SS while eliminating systematic over-counting. We then study PAIR-style LLM jailbreaks under a fleet-level threat model with adversarial fraction $η$. On Llama-Guard-3-8B hidden states, a PCA-based ruler attains 2.6% mean relative error for $η\geq 10^{-3}$ against finite-sample references whose average bootstrap relative half-width is 27.9%, and transfers to a GCG-style corpus with 2.93% relative error after recalibration. A directional criterion $\mathrm{KL}(p_{\mathrm{good}}\,\|\,p_{\mathrm{bad}})$ ranks rulers consistently with estimation error (Spearman $ρ=0.83$).

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SCARCE, a method to estimate extremely low failure probabilities of AI systems much more accurately and sample‑efficiently than traditional Subset Simulation, by learning latent representations and geometric “rulers” of proximity to failure instead of hand‑crafted performance functions.
AI.motivation: Rare failures in AI systems are critical for safety but are too infrequent to estimate accurately with naive Monte Carlo, which would need an enormous number of samples. Subset Simulation is a more efficient approach, but it relies on a manually designed scalar performance function that encodes detailed knowledge of how failures arise, which is hard to design, brittle, and not easily transferred to new models or domains. The authors want a scalable, data‑driven alternative that works directly with high‑dimensional AI model representations and generalises across threat models and datasets.
AI.method: They propose SCARCE, which replaces the hand‑crafted performance function in Subset Simulation with learned latent embeddings of model states (e.g., hidden states of a neural network) and geometric rulers that measure distance or separation from failure regions in that embedding space. Using these rulers, SCARCE adaptively constructs a cascade of nested intermediate events via data‑driven thresholding, enabling efficient estimation of very small failure probabilities. The method is formalised as a non‑negative supermartingale, which provides a high‑probability upper envelope on the estimated failure probability and remains valid even under early stopping. They also introduce a directional information‑theoretic criterion, KL(p_good || p_bad), to rank and select rulers based on how well they separate safe and failure distributions.
AI.result: On MNIST misclassification, where the true rare‑event probability can be estimated by dense Monte Carlo, SCARCE reduces mean absolute error by about 400–500× compared to a grid‑searched classical Subset Simulation baseline and also avoids its systematic overestimation bias. In a large‑language‑model jailbreak setting under a fleet‑level threat model with adversarial fraction η, using PCA‑based rulers on Llama‑Guard‑3‑8B hidden states yields only 2.6% mean relative error for η ≥ 10^{-3}, even though the finite‑sample Monte Carlo reference itself has an average bootstrap relative half‑width of 27.9%. The same ruler structure transfers to a different jailbreak corpus (GCG‑style) after simple recalibration, achieving 2.93% relative error. The KL‑based directional criterion strongly correlates with empirical estimation quality, with Spearman ρ = 0.83 between the criterion and estimation error across rulers.
AI.conclusion: SCARCE makes rare‑event probability estimation for AI safety substantially more accurate and sample‑efficient by learning geometric structure in latent spaces instead of relying on hand‑engineered performance functions. Its martingale‑based formalisation provides conservative uncertainty control and allows safe early stopping. The method performs well on both vision (MNIST) and LLM jailbreak tasks and demonstrates transfer across datasets. The KL‑based ruler selection heuristic offers a principled way to choose good geometric rulers, suggesting SCARCE can serve as a practical, scalable tool for quantifying low‑probability failures in modern AI systems.

title: How much of an LLM-generated clinical corpus is actually new? A production-scale measurement of content redundancy for provenance classification
summary: Clinical machine learning increasingly relies on training corpora generated by large language models (LLMs) rather than annotated by clinicians, and such corpora are described and reused largely on the basis of their reported scale. We test whether volume reflects information content. Analysing the complete output of a multi-agent clinical extraction pipeline applied to 167,034 patient narratives, 2.51 billion generated tokens across the ten text-bearing channels of an eleven-channel pipeline, we introduce Provenance-based Redundancy Decomposition, a token-level classification of the entire output by source. Only 10.9% of the output is trainable-unique content while 79.4% is redundant; raw token count overstates information content by roughly ninefold. The redundancy arises through two distinct mechanisms, verbatim copying of source context into per-item fields, and duplication of generated text across records, of which only the former is losslessly removable. An independent, model-free analysis based on lossless compression confirms the redundancy, recovering the two mechanisms without reference to the provenance labels. One pipeline channel carries almost no redundancy, showing that the level of redundancy depends on how each channel is structured rather than being a fixed property of LLM extraction. Because uncorrected redundancy up-weights the longer, more complex presentations that generate the most items, it skews the token-level training distribution of the corpus, a property we measure directly. In a controlled downstream test, de-duplicating the corpus before adaptation improved a clinical encoder on external disease-recognition benchmarks at equal token budget, robustly across adaptation depths and replicated on a second benchmark, confirming that the redundancy carries a measurable cost beyond storage. The classification tool is released openly.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that LLM-generated clinical training corpora contain massive redundancy, so raw token count greatly overestimates real informational content, and that de-duplicating such corpora improves downstream model performance at the same token budget.
AI.motivation: As LLMs are increasingly used to generate large clinical training datasets that are reused and compared mainly by total token count, there is a risk that size metrics are misleading if they do not reflect true informational content. Understanding how much of these corpora is genuinely unique, trainable signal versus redundant repetition is crucial for fair corpus comparison, efficient training, and preventing bias due to over-representation of certain patient narratives.
AI.method: The authors apply a multi-agent clinical information extraction pipeline with eleven channels (ten text-bearing) to 167,034 patient narratives, yielding 2.51 billion generated tokens. They introduce Provenance-based Redundancy Decomposition, which classifies each generated token by its source (e.g., original narrative, copied context, duplicated generation). They quantify redundancy at token level, distinguishing verbatim copying from cross-record duplication. They then validate these findings with an independent, model-free lossless compression analysis that recovers the same redundancy mechanisms. Finally, they de-duplicate the corpus and evaluate clinical encoder performance on external disease-recognition benchmarks under a fixed token budget, across different adaptation depths and on multiple benchmarks.
AI.result: They find that only 10.9% of generated tokens constitute trainable-unique content, whereas 79.4% are redundant, so raw token counts overstate information content by about ninefold. Redundancy comes from two main mechanisms: verbatim copying of source context into multiple per-item fields and duplication of generated text across different records; only the first is losslessly removable. A compression-based analysis independently confirms high redundancy and identifies the same mechanisms without using provenance labels. One pipeline channel is found to be almost redundancy-free, indicating that redundancy depends on specific channel design. Redundancy disproportionately amplifies longer, more complex patient cases, skewing the token-level training distribution. After de-duplicating the corpus, clinical encoders achieve better performance on external disease-recognition benchmarks at the same token budget, and this improvement is robust across adaptation depths and replicates on a second benchmark.
AI.conclusion: Token count is a poor proxy for informational content in LLM-generated clinical corpora due to extensive redundancy, which both wastes storage and degrades downstream training by skewing data distribution. Careful accounting for provenance and redundancy—through techniques such as Provenance-based Redundancy Decomposition and deduplication—can yield more efficient, fairer, and better-performing clinical models at the same computational cost. Corpus design and channel structure strongly influence redundancy, implying that extraction pipelines should be rethought to minimize unnecessary repetition. The authors provide an open-source classification tool to facilitate such analyses.

title: SFBench: The SciFy Scientific Feasibility Benchmark
summary: We present SFBench, a benchmark dataset for evaluating systems that assess the feasibility of scientific claims. SFBench includes 197 claims in materials science, each annotated with a ground-truth feasibility score on a five-point scale along with an explanation of that assessment. The collection differs from previous collections in several important ways: 1) it defines a complex task that requires reasoning over claims of varying scientific feasibility; 2) its claims are not extracted from existing scientific publications but are created de novo, greatly reducing the chances that LLMs have trained on them; 3) claims and ground truth are established by subject matter experts, not by artificial intelligence; and 4) unlike many benchmarks that ask about question/answer pairs, provide multiple choice answers, or ask questions requiring short, fixed answers, SFBench explanations are completely open-ended. We describe the benchmark design, data creation process, and evaluation metrics, and we report baseline results using recent GPT models.

AI.tldr: SFBench is a new expert-annotated benchmark of 197 de novo materials-science claims with 5-point feasibility labels and free-form explanations, designed to test systems that judge scientific claim feasibility.
AI.motivation: There is a need to evaluate and improve systems (especially LLMs) that assess how scientifically feasible a claim is, but existing benchmarks are limited, often derived from existing literature, may overlap with LLM training data, and typically use simple QA or multiple choice formats. A benchmark that focuses on nuanced scientific feasibility, avoids data leakage, and uses expert-created, open-ended annotations can better measure real reasoning ability.
AI.method: The authors construct SFBench, a dataset of 197 claims in materials science that are written from scratch (not extracted from papers). Subject matter experts assign each claim a feasibility score on a five-point scale and provide free-form textual explanations. The paper details how the benchmark is designed, how claims and annotations are generated and validated, and how evaluation metrics are defined. They then run recent GPT models as baselines on this benchmark.
AI.result: The output of the work is the SFBench dataset itself, complete with feasibility scores and rich explanations, plus baseline performance numbers for recent GPT models on the task of scoring and/or explaining claim feasibility using the benchmark’s metrics.
AI.conclusion: SFBench offers a more challenging, leakage-resistant, and realistic benchmark for assessing systems that judge the feasibility of scientific claims, particularly in materials science. Its expert-created, de novo claims and open-ended explanations aim to better test and drive progress in scientific reasoning capabilities of AI models compared with prior, simpler benchmarks.

title: Do We Still Need Fine Tuning? Turkish Sentiment Analysis in the Era of Large Language Model
comment: Accepted to the 34th IEEE Signal Processing and Communications Applications Conference
summary: This study examines whether supervised fine-tuning remains necessary for Turkish sentiment analysis in the era of large language models. We compare classical machine learning methods, fine-tuned pretrained language models, and prompted large language models on a Turkish e-commerce review dataset with negative, neutral, and positive labels. Fine-tuned BERTurk models perform best overall and outperform all prompted large language models in the full three-class task. The neutral class emerges as the main difficulty: while several large language models are much more competitive in binary positive--negative classification, they degrade substantially in the three-class setting by collapsing neutral reviews into polarized categories. The findings suggest that, in realistic Turkish sentiment classification, prompted large language models do not yet match supervised fine-tuning in the zero-shot setting, and that including the neutral class is crucial for robust evaluation.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates whether supervised fine-tuning is still needed for Turkish sentiment analysis compared to directly prompting large language models (LLMs). Fine-tuned BERTurk outperforms prompted LLMs, especially on three-class sentiment including neutral.
AI.motivation: With the rise of strong general-purpose LLMs, many NLP tasks can be attempted via prompting without task-specific training. It is unclear whether this eliminates the need for supervised fine-tuning in non-English, low-resource languages like Turkish, particularly for nuanced tasks like sentiment analysis with a neutral class. The authors aim to clarify if prompting alone is sufficient and how evaluation design (binary vs. three-class) affects conclusions.
AI.method: The authors use a Turkish e-commerce review dataset labeled as negative, neutral, or positive. They compare three paradigms: (1) classical machine learning classifiers; (2) supervised fine-tuned pretrained language models, notably BERTurk; and (3) various large language models used via prompting in a zero-shot setting. Performance is evaluated on both three-class and binary (positive vs. negative) setups, with attention to how models handle the neutral class.
AI.result: Fine-tuned BERTurk models achieve the highest overall performance and clearly outperform all prompted LLMs on the three-class sentiment task. In binary positive–negative classification, several LLMs become competitive with BERTurk. However, when the neutral class is included, the performance of LLMs drops markedly, mainly because they misclassify neutral reviews as either positive or negative, leading to a collapse of the middle category.
AI.conclusion: Supervised fine-tuning remains important for realistic Turkish sentiment analysis: prompted LLMs in a zero-shot configuration do not yet reach the performance of fine-tuned models, especially when a neutral class is present. The study also underscores that excluding the neutral class can give an overly optimistic view of LLM capabilities, so three-class setups are essential for robust evaluation of sentiment models in Turkish.

title: Budgeted Act-or-Defer Multi-Agent LLM Deliberation with Local Reliability Bounds
summary: Multi-agent deliberation among LLMs can improve reasoning, but deployment requires deciding when the current answer is reliable enough to act on and when it should be escalated to human review. We formulate this as budgeted act-or-defer decision making. At each round, the system maps the debate prefix to a low-dimensional state, computes a $k$-nearest-neighbor lower confidence bound on state-conditional correctness using calibration data, and acts only when the bound exceeds a user-specified reliability threshold. The certificate controls wrong actions through the decomposition $β= δ+ α+ \varepsilon_{\mathrm{act}}$, separating calibration failure, residual action risk, and representation gap. The guarantee is conditional, not distribution-free: it relies on a valid local bias envelope and an action-region representation-gap bound, and each assumption is paired with falsification-style diagnostics. Because the same absolute wrong-action budget has different meanings across tasks of different difficulty, we set budgets relative to each task's final-round error using training data only, and evaluate safety by normalized budget usage $\mathrm{WA}/β$. On six benchmarks against nine baselines, the method uses 9--12% of the pre-declared budget on activated datasets, reaching up to 84% automation and 96% acted-on accuracy; on stress-test datasets, it defers rather than forcing unreliable automation. Rather than relying on per-task post-hoc threshold search, the method prospectively converts a user-declared wrong-action budget into an auditable act-or-defer operating point before deployment, under explicitly stated assumptions.

AI.tldr: They design a calibrated act-or-defer controller for multi-agent LLM systems that turns a user’s allowed wrong-action budget into when to act autonomously versus defer to humans, with provable, diagnosable guarantees under explicit assumptions.
AI.motivation: Multi-agent LLM debates can give strong answers but are sometimes confidently wrong. In real deployments you must decide when to trust the current answer and when to escalate to a human, and you often have a strict tolerance on how many wrong actions are acceptable. Existing approaches typically tune thresholds post-hoc per task and lack clear, auditable guarantees. The authors want a principled, prospective way to map a user-specified risk budget into an operational automation / deferral policy, with diagnostics for when guarantees might fail.
AI.method: They cast the problem as budgeted act-or-defer decision making over rounds of multi-agent LLM debate. At each round they compress the debate prefix into a low-dimensional state representation. Using calibration data, they estimate for each state a k-nearest-neighbor lower confidence bound (LCB) on the probability that the current action is correct. The system acts only if this LCB exceeds a user-chosen reliability threshold; otherwise it continues debating or defers to humans. They decompose the total wrong-action budget β into three parts: δ (calibration failure), α (residual action risk in calibrated regions), and ε_act (representation gap from using low-dimensional states). The guarantees are conditional on two explicit assumptions: a local bias envelope on the k-NN estimator and a bound on the representation gap over the region where actions are taken. Each assumption is accompanied by falsification-style diagnostics using data. To make budgets comparable across tasks of varying difficulty, they normalize them relative to each task’s final-round error rate estimated from training data, and evaluate safety via normalized budget usage WA/β, where WA is the realized wrong actions.
AI.result: Across six benchmarks and nine baselines, their method uses only about 9–12% of the pre-specified wrong-action budget on activated (non-trivial) datasets, while achieving up to 84% automation rate and 96% accuracy on the cases where the system chooses to act. On stress-test datasets where LLM performance is unreliable, the controller prefers to defer rather than automate, avoiding overconfident wrong actions. Compared to baselines, it better respects the declared risk budget without per-task post-hoc tuning.
AI.conclusion: A calibrated act-or-defer controller can turn a user-declared wrong-action budget into a concrete, auditable operating point for multi-agent LLM systems before deployment. By using k-NN-based lower confidence bounds over a learned debate-state representation and decomposing the error budget into calibration, residual risk, and representation-gap components—each with associated diagnostics—the method offers conditional but transparent safety guarantees. It achieves high automation and high acted-on accuracy when the model is reliable, and defers appropriately under stress, avoiding brittle post-hoc threshold search and making deployment-time risk control more principled.

title: Two-Stage Prompt Optimization for Few-Shot Relation Extraction: From Reasoning-Guided Search to Gradient-Guided Refinement
summary: Automatic prompt optimization is still underexplored for episodic few-shot relation extraction with smaller language models. We propose a two-stage framework that combines reasoning-based prompt optimization with gradient-based prompt optimization. The first stage can use any reasoning-based optimizer to make broadprompt improvements in natural language. The second stage applies our GradPO, which uses loss and gradient signals to identify high-impact prompt spans and refine them with local edits. Experiments on FS-TACRED and FS-FewRel show that local refinement usually improves prompts found by the first stage, and GradPO is the most consistent refiner. Our framework achieves state-of-the-art performance on FS-TACRED with Qwen3-4B and remains competitive on FS-FewRel.

AI.tldr: A two-stage framework automatically optimizes prompts for episodic few-shot relation extraction with small LMs, combining reasoning-based and gradient-based refinement to reach SOTA on FS-TACRED.
AI.motivation: Episodic few-shot relation extraction with smaller language models relies heavily on carefully engineered prompts, which are difficult and time-consuming to design manually. Existing automatic prompt optimization methods are underexplored in this setting, and there is a need for a principled way to improve prompts for small models to close the performance gap with larger LMs.
AI.method: The authors propose a two-stage prompt optimization framework. Stage 1 uses any reasoning-based optimizer to make broad, natural-language-level improvements to an initial prompt (e.g., via heuristic, rule-based, or LLM-style rewriting). Stage 2 introduces GradPO, a gradient-based optimizer that leverages loss and gradient information from the model to detect high-impact spans within the current prompt and refine them through local edits. This combination allows global restructuring first, followed by fine-grained local improvements guided by gradients.
AI.result: On the FS-TACRED and FS-FewRel episodic few-shot relation extraction benchmarks, the framework consistently improves prompts discovered in Stage 1, with GradPO being the most reliable refiner among compared methods. The approach achieves state-of-the-art performance on FS-TACRED using the Qwen3-4B model and remains competitive with prior work on FS-FewRel.
AI.conclusion: Combining reasoning-based and gradient-based prompt optimization yields more effective prompts for episodic few-shot relation extraction with smaller language models. The proposed GradPO-driven local refinement reliably enhances prompts from a broad first-stage optimizer and can push small models like Qwen3-4B to state-of-the-art performance on at least one key benchmark, demonstrating the practical value of automatic prompt optimization in this setting.

title: Safety from Honesty in a Disinterested AI Predictor
summary: As AI systems become more capable, training procedures that optimize for downstream outcomes risk introducing implicit agency: goal-directed behavior that designers never specified. We present a formal safety argument for the Scientist AI (SAI) Predictor, trained to approximate the Bayesian posterior conditioned on a dataset of "epistemically contextualized" natural-language statements. We argue that such a Predictor can honestly predict agents, actions, and their consequences without itself being an agent that selects outputs to achieve goals. This rests on data representation and on the training procedure. Epistemic contextualization of text distinguishes latent factual claims from communication acts, so expressions of goals are treated as evidence to be explained rather than drives the model adopts. With a posterior-seeking training objective, this is intended to drive the Predictor toward calibrated, cautious predictions. Training proceeds so downstream effects of deploying a prediction never serve as a reward signal; any agency the system needs is supplied by explicit scaffolding constrained by guardrails. We prove that, under assumptions on the training dynamics and on the argued sparsity of dangerous Predictors, the probability that training produces a Predictor whose guarded deployment carries residual harm above a specified threshold is small: a dangerous Predictor would have to underestimate harm in a coordinated way across many queries while such coordinated patterns are rare under the initialization distribution and receive no direct training signal. Safety and accuracy are jointly supported in this framework, since the constraints that secure accuracy are the same ones that make coordinated deception costly. These guarantees against misalignment and agency arising from within the Predictor itself do not preclude the use of the Predictor as part of an agentic system.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes and analyzes a non‑agentic “Scientist AI (SAI) Predictor” that approximates Bayesian posteriors over epistemically contextualized text, aiming to avoid implicit agency while enabling accurate prediction of agents and outcomes, and provides a formal safety argument bounding the probability of harmful misalignment.
AI.motivation: As AI systems grow more capable, standard outcome-optimizing training can inadvertently give models implicit agency, leading them to pursue goals not specified by designers. There is a need for architectures and training procedures that allow powerful predictive modeling of agents and consequences without turning the predictor itself into an agent that acts strategically based on its outputs’ downstream effects.
AI.method: The authors define a Scientist AI (SAI) Predictor trained to approximate the Bayesian posterior over latent factual states given “epistemically contextualized” natural-language data, which separates factual content from communicative intent. They use a posterior-seeking training objective (rather than outcome-based rewards) and design the dataset and training pipeline so that downstream deployment effects never influence the training signal. They then construct a formal safety argument: assuming certain properties of training dynamics and that dangerous Predictors are sparse under initialization, they analyze how unlikely it is for training to yield a Predictor that systematically underestimates harm across many queries in a coordinated fashion.
AI.result: Under their assumptions, they show that the probability of training producing a Predictor whose guarded deployment causes residual harm above a target threshold is small. The argument rests on the rarity (under the initialization distribution) of Predictors that are both accurate in general yet coordinatedly deceptive or harm-underestimating across many queries, combined with the absence of any training incentive for such patterns. They also argue that the same constraints that improve calibration and accuracy also make coordinated deception costly and thus unlikely.
AI.conclusion: The SAI Predictor framework can, in principle, model and predict agents, actions, and consequences without itself becoming an agent that selects outputs to achieve goals. By epistemically contextualizing training data and using a posterior-seeking objective insulated from deployment outcomes, they claim to achieve joint safety and accuracy: the design both promotes honest, calibrated prediction and suppresses emergent internal agency or misalignment. These guarantees only cover the non-agentic Predictor itself and do not rule out building separate, explicitly constrained agentic systems that use the Predictor as a component.

title: Hybrid Retriever Evolution for Multimodal Document Reasoning Agents
summary: Different retrievers, including lexical, semantic, and multimodal approaches, provide highly complementary strengths for multimodal document understanding, yet most systems combine them through fixed pipelines that cannot adapt to the demands of individual reasoning steps. In this work, we ask whether retrieval orchestration itself can be learned as part of the reasoning process. We introduce a failure-driven evolution framework in which a meta-agent autonomously discovers how a tool-using task agent should coordinate diverse retrievers during multi-step document question answering. The meta-agent analyzes incorrect reasoning trajectories, actively probes the same tool environment to diagnose root causes, and iteratively rewrites the task agent's instructions, turning retrieval from a fixed front-end stage into an adaptive, step-wise reasoning decision. The evolved agent learns when to invoke each retriever, how to combine them, and how to compose evidence across modalities and pages. On MMLongBench-Doc and DocBench, the evolved agent achieves gains of up to +19.6 points over the unevolved baseline and consistently outperforms recent systems including MACT, MDocAgent, and SimpleDoc. Detailed retrieval analyses confirm that these improvements arise from adaptive routing and evidence composition rather than reliance on any hard coded retrieval mode, and evolution dynamics reveal a progressive shift from narrow lexical behavior to rich multi-tool coordination. These findings establish autonomous multi-agent coordination as a promising paradigm for multimodal document reasoning.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a learnable, failure-driven framework that automatically orchestrates multiple retrievers (lexical, semantic, multimodal) within a multi-step reasoning agent for multimodal document QA, yielding large performance gains over fixed retrieval pipelines.
AI.motivation: Existing multimodal document QA systems use multiple retrievers but typically wire them in fixed, hand-designed pipelines that cannot adapt which retriever to use at each reasoning step or how to combine them. This limits performance, especially on complex, multi-step, multi-page, and multimodal queries. The authors are motivated to make retrieval orchestration itself part of the learnable reasoning process, so the system can autonomously discover how to best coordinate diverse tools over time and tasks.
AI.method: They introduce a failure-driven evolution framework with two main components: (1) a task agent that performs multi-step question answering using various retrieval tools (lexical, semantic, multimodal) and (2) a meta-agent that operates on top. The meta-agent inspects failed reasoning trajectories, replays and probes the same tool environment to diagnose where and why retrieval or reasoning failed, and then rewrites or updates the task agent’s instructions and policies. Through iterative evolution, the task agent learns an adaptive retrieval policy: when to call each retriever, how to combine their outputs, and how to aggregate evidence across pages and modalities. This transforms retrieval from a fixed pre-processing stage into a step-wise decision integrated into the reasoning loop.
AI.result: On multimodal document benchmarks MMLongBench-Doc and DocBench, the evolved agent improves performance by as much as +19.6 points over its unevolved baseline. It also consistently surpasses recent strong baselines such as MACT, MDocAgent, and SimpleDoc. Analyses of retrieval behavior show that the gains are due to more effective adaptive routing among retrievers and better evidence composition, rather than overfitting to a single retrieval type or relying on any pre-specified retrieval mode.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that retrieval orchestration can and should be learned as part of the reasoning process. Their failure-driven evolution approach enables a meta-agent to autonomously discover effective multi-tool coordination strategies for multimodal document QA. The evolved agents move from rigid, lexical-dominated behavior to flexible, multi-retriever coordination, achieving strong empirical gains. This demonstrates that autonomous multi-agent coordination is a powerful paradigm for complex multimodal document understanding and opens the door to more general, self-improving tool-using reasoning systems.

title: Diversity is the Strength of the AI Crowd
comment: Accepted at the ICML 2026 Workshop on Forecasting as a New Frontier of Intelligence, Seoul, South Korea, 2026
summary: Top AI forecasting systems are approaching superforecaster-level accuracy on future world events, but still rely primarily on off-the-shelf LLMs combined with forecasting-specific context gathering and scaffolding. We study how to improve this recipe through ensembling: given a fixed number of samples, which off-the-shelf model forecasts should be combined to maximize accuracy? On binary questions from the Metaculus AI Benchmark, we find that individual accuracy is not enough: many frontier LLMs make highly correlated predictions, limiting the value of additional forecasts from the same or similar models. Instead, the strongest ensembles combine accurate but diverse forecasters, with models such as \model{Grok 4} contributing disproportionately because their predictions are less correlated with other frontier LLMs. These results suggest that the strength of the AI crowd comes not from sampling more forecasts indiscriminately, but from combining forecasts across models with complementary errors, motivating forecasting systems that explicitly optimize for both model quality and diversity.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates how to best ensemble different large language models (LLMs) for forecasting future events, showing that combining accurate but diverse models outperforms using many similar frontier models whose errors are highly correlated.
AI.motivation: While AI forecasting systems are nearing superforecaster-level performance, they mostly rely on single off-the-shelf LLMs plus tooling. It is unclear how to systematically combine multiple LLM forecasters when computational budget (number of samples) is fixed, and whether simply adding more forecasts from similar frontier models truly improves accuracy.
AI.method: The authors evaluate multiple off-the-shelf LLMs on binary questions from the Metaculus AI Benchmark. They measure both each model’s individual forecasting accuracy and the correlations between their predictions. They then construct and compare ensembles under a fixed total number of forecasts, varying which models are included and how many samples each contributes, to study the tradeoff between model accuracy and prediction diversity.
AI.result: They find that many frontier LLMs, despite being accurate, produce highly correlated forecasts, so additional samples from these models add limited marginal value. Ensembles that mix accurate but less correlated models substantially improve performance. Models like Grok 4 are especially valuable because their predictions are less correlated with other strong LLMs, boosting ensemble accuracy disproportionately.
AI.conclusion: Effective AI forecasting ensembles should explicitly optimize not just for the accuracy of component models, but also for diversity in their predictive patterns. The best performance comes from combining strong but complementary forecasters rather than naively scaling the number of forecasts from similar frontier LLMs, guiding the design of future AI forecasting systems toward quality-plus-diversity model selection.

title: Resolution Thresholds in VLM Detection of Harmful ASCII Art Across Construction Modes and Languages
summary: Large Vision-Language Models (VLMs) are increasingly deployed as content moderation tools, yet they remain vulnerable to jailbreak attacks in which harmful text is visually encoded as ASCII art. This can allow inappropriate or harmful content to bypass moderation systems. To address this vulnerability, this paper investigates how image resolution affects VLM detection of harmful ASCII art across eight character construction modes (L1-L8), ranging from dense block characters to word-embedded designs. We evaluate eight state-of-the-art VLMs on English and Chinese corpora using a pipeline that generates ASCII art images at ten resolution scales, probing whether a consistent detection-failure threshold exists across models, modes, and languages. Results indicate that detection rates decline sharply above certain resolution thresholds, and that word-based modes are the most resistant to detection across the full resolution range. These findings reveal a systematic vulnerability in VLM-based content moderation systems and motivate resolution-aware evaluation standards.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how image resolution affects the ability of large vision-language models to detect harmful content hidden as ASCII art, revealing clear resolution thresholds where moderation fails.
AI.motivation: Large vision-language models are used for content moderation but can be bypassed when harmful text is encoded as ASCII art images, especially at certain resolutions. There is limited understanding of how image resolution and different ASCII construction styles influence detection failures, which is critical for building robust moderation systems.
AI.method: The authors construct a pipeline that transforms harmful English and Chinese text into ASCII art images using eight character construction modes (L1–L8), from dense block-like characters to word-embedded designs. For each mode, they generate images at ten different resolution scales and evaluate eight state-of-the-art VLMs on whether they correctly detect the harmful content, analyzing performance trends across resolutions, modes, and languages to search for consistent failure thresholds.
AI.result: Experiments show that VLMs’ detection performance drops sharply after certain image resolution thresholds are exceeded, and that word-based ASCII modes remain particularly difficult to detect across almost all resolutions. This pattern appears across multiple models, styles, and both English and Chinese corpora, indicating a robust, systematic weakness.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that current VLM-based content moderation systems have a structural vulnerability to high-resolution ASCII-art-based jailbreaks, especially for word-embedded constructions. It argues that moderation pipelines and benchmarks should incorporate resolution-aware evaluations and defenses to mitigate these attacks.

title: Sample-Efficient Learning of Probabilistic Causes for Reachability in Markov Decision Processes with Probabilistic Guarantees
comment: Accepted to UAI2026 as oral presentation
summary: Probabilistic model checking for Markov decision processes (MDPs) provides quantitative guarantees, but often offers limited insight into why undesired outcomes occur. Probability-raising (PR) causality addresses this by identifying states whose visitation increases the probability of reaching designated states.
