Existing PR-cause identification methods, however, use MDP modifications not well-suited for learning: the gap between conditional and unconditional reachability probabilities can be hard to detect from transition samples, and construction requires reachability probabilities of the MDP, which are unavailable when transition probabilities are unknown. We study unknown MDPs and propose a learning approach with probabilistic guarantees for PR-cause identification. Our key ingredient is a restart-based MDP modification that reduces PR-cause checking to two conditional reachability queries without using reachability values of the original MDP. We prove correctness, establish sample-complexity bounds, and develop an anytime learning-and-checking algorithm based on two-sided value iteration that progressively classifies states as causal, non-causal, or undecided. Experiments on two benchmarks demonstrate reliable and fast identification of PR causes.

AI.tldr: The paper develops a learning-based method to identify probability-raising (PR) causes in Markov decision processes with unknown transition probabilities, using a restart-based modification and providing probabilistic guarantees, sample-complexity bounds, and an anytime algorithm.
AI.motivation: Classical probabilistic model checking on MDPs can tell whether bad states are reached with high probability but gives little explanation of *why* they occur. PR causality helps explain which states increase the chance of reaching certain outcomes, but existing methods assume known transition probabilities and require computing reachability probabilities, which is infeasible when the MDP is unknown and must be learned from samples. There is thus a need for a causality analysis method that works directly in the learning setting with theoretical guarantees.
AI.method: The authors consider MDPs with unknown transition probabilities and propose a learning-based framework for probability-raising cause identification. They introduce a restart-based modification of the MDP that transforms the PR-cause checking problem into two conditional reachability probability queries, avoiding the need for global reachability values of the original MDP. Building on this, they design an anytime learning-and-checking algorithm using two-sided value iteration to estimate these conditional probabilities from sampled transitions and to incrementally classify states as causal, non-causal, or still undecided, while controlling estimation error.
AI.result: They formally prove that the restart-based reduction correctly characterizes PR causality and derive sample-complexity bounds that relate the number of samples needed to reliably identify causes to problem parameters. The anytime algorithm is shown, both in theory and experiments, to progressively refine classifications with probabilistic correctness guarantees. Empirical evaluation on two benchmark MDPs demonstrates that the method can reliably and efficiently identify PR causes from data, achieving fast convergence and practical runtimes.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that probability-raising causality analysis can be effectively extended to settings where the MDP is unknown and must be learned. The restart-based MDP modification enables cause identification using only conditional reachability estimates, sidestepping full reachability computation. With proven correctness, finite-sample guarantees, and good empirical performance, the approach provides a practical and theoretically sound tool for causal explanation in probabilistic model checking under model uncertainty.

title: How LLMs See Creativity: Zero-Shot Scoring of Visual Creativity with Interpretable Reasoning
summary: Evaluating the originality of visual images poses enduring challenges for creativity assessment. Automated scoring using AI models has proven effective in the verbal domain, yet key questions remain about evaluating visual creativity and understanding how models arrive at their ratings. The present research asks whether multimodal large language models (LLMs) can serve as judges of visual creativity zero-shot (without any fine-tuning or examples of human ratings) and whether their "reasoning" output offers an interpretable window into their evaluation process. We tested six multimodal LLMs (Gemini 3 Flash, Gemma 4 31B IT, GPT-5.4 Mini, GLM-5v Turbo, Kimi K2.5, and Qwen 3.6 Plus) on 992 AI-generated images (based on human-written prompts) and 1,500 hand-drawn sketches scored for creativity by human raters. In Study 1, all models showed substantial alignment with human creativity ratings on both datasets (r = .57-.68 on AI-generated images; r = .29-68 on sketches). In Study 2, we analyzed the step-by-step reasoning processes of three LLMs evaluating the same images and drawings. Although reasoning made model evaluations interpretable -- showing what they attend to, how they balance originality vs. quality, and how they justify their ratings -- reasoning did not improve alignment with human ratings. In sum, our findings indicate that multimodal LLMs can match human judgments of visual creativity without any additional training, and that their reasoning reveals how AI models evaluate creativity. An open scoring app implementing this pipeline is available at https://review-visual-eval-scoring.hf.space.

AI.tldr: The paper tests whether multimodal LLMs can act as zero-shot judges of visual creativity and how their step-by-step reasoning relates to their scoring.
AI.motivation: Creativity assessment for visual images is hard, and current automated scoring methods work better in the verbal domain. There is a need to see if multimodal LLMs can reliably judge visual originality and to understand the internal logic behind their ratings to make AI-based assessment transparent and interpretable.
AI.method: The authors used six multimodal LLMs (Gemini 3 Flash, Gemma 4 31B IT, GPT-5.4 Mini, GLM-5v Turbo, Kimi K2.5, Qwen 3.6 Plus) to rate creativity in two datasets: 992 AI-generated images based on human prompts and 1,500 hand-drawn sketches with existing human creativity scores. In Study 1, they correlated each model’s zero-shot creativity ratings with human ratings. In Study 2, they prompted three of the models to provide step-by-step reasoning while rating, then analyzed these explanations to see what aspects of the images they focused on and whether reasoning changed alignment with human judgments.
AI.result: All six models showed substantial correlation with human creativity ratings: r = .57–.68 for AI-generated images and r = .29–.68 for hand-drawn sketches. Adding explicit step-by-step reasoning made the evaluations more interpretable but did not increase the strength of alignment with human ratings.
AI.conclusion: Multimodal LLMs can approximate human judgments of visual creativity in a zero-shot setting, without additional training. Their generated reasoning provides a transparent view into what they consider (e.g., balance of originality and quality) but does not enhance predictive alignment with human raters. The authors provide an open app implementing their evaluation pipeline for practical use.

title: Toward Secure and Reliable PDDL Formalization of Large Language Models with Planner-in-the-Loop Feedback
summary: Planning often requires symbolic specifications that are both executable and verifiable. For large language models deployed in autonomous or decision-support systems, failures in such formalization may lead to unverifiable decisions, execution failures, or unsafe downstream behavior. We present NL-PDDL-Bench, a multi-domain benchmark for natural-language-to-PDDL specification construction with planner-verified executability and controlled difficulty scaling by object count. We further propose a planner-in-the-loop framework that uses validator and planner diagnostics to revise non-executable specifications through localized edits. Building on this infrastructure, we develop a planner-grounded optimization recipe that combines parameter-efficient Low-Rank Adaptation supervised fine-tuning, offline planner-derived preference pairs for Direct Preference Optimization, and inference-time planner-in-the-loop repair, without requiring online planner calls during training. We also provide a unified evaluation suite for parseability, solvability, specification similarity, and outcome-aware plan-level consistency against planner references. Experiments on representative model families show substantial gains in planner success and plan-level agreement, with improved robustness under difficulty scaling and cross-domain variation. These results highlight the value of externally verifiable formalization for reliable deployment of LLMs in safety- or security-sensitive planning systems. Code and data are available at: https://github.com/ibasicplan/NL-PDDL-Bench

AI.tldr: The paper introduces NL-PDDL-Bench, a benchmark and method for converting natural language task descriptions into executable PDDL planning specifications, and shows that planner-feedback-grounded training and inference significantly improve reliability and safety of LLM-based planners.
AI.motivation: LLMs are increasingly used in autonomous and decision-support systems where they must generate plans or instructions. However, planning typically requires symbolic, formally specified representations (like PDDL) that must be executable and verifiable. If the mapping from natural language to such formal specifications fails, systems can produce plans that cannot be executed, are unverifiable, or cause unsafe behavior. There is a lack of standardized benchmarks and methods that tightly couple LLM planning with automated planners and validators to ensure external verifiability and robustness across domains and task complexities.
AI.method:
1) Construct NL-PDDL-Bench, a benchmark spanning multiple domains with natural language descriptions paired with PDDL specifications, designed so that problem difficulty can be systematically scaled by varying object counts.
2) Introduce a planner-in-the-loop framework where automated planner and validator diagnostics are used to automatically repair non-executable or incorrect PDDL specifications through local edits, without fully regenerating them.
3) Propose a planner-grounded optimization pipeline: (a) apply parameter-efficient LoRA fine-tuning using supervision from PDDL data; (b) derive offline planner-guided preference pairs and apply Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) to better align the model with planner-validated outputs; (c) use planner-in-the-loop repair at inference time to fix residual errors, while avoiding online planner calls during training.
4) Design a unified evaluation suite assessing not only parseability and solvability of the generated PDDL, but also specification similarity to references and outcome-aware plan-level consistency when run through a planner.
AI.result: Across several representative LLM families, the proposed training and inference pipeline substantially increases the rate at which generated PDDL is syntactically valid, planner-solvable, and behaviorally aligned with reference plans. The models show higher planner success rates, greater plan-level agreement with gold-standard planner outputs, and are more robust when tasks become harder (more objects) or change across domains, compared to baselines without planner-grounded optimization or planner-in-the-loop repair.
AI.conclusion: Integrating external symbolic planners and validators into both the training and inference processes of LLMs leads to more reliable, verifiable formal task specifications. NL-PDDL-Bench and the planner-grounded optimization framework demonstrate that grounding LLM outputs in planner feedback improves executability, safety, and robustness for planning tasks, making LLMs more suitable for deployment in safety- and security-critical planning and decision-support systems.

title: Can MLLMs Critique Like Humans? Evaluating Open-Ended Aesthetic Reasoning in Multimodal Large Language Models
summary: Open-ended aesthetic critique is a challenge for multimodal large language models (MLLMs): unlike multiple-choice aesthetic benchmarks, it has no single correct answer, and most aesthetic evaluation has measured models against numeric scores rather than the written critiques people actually give. We evaluate MLLM critiques against ranked human references and ask whether they are close to human ones. Using the Reddit Photo Critique Dataset, we score five open-weight MLLMs against multiple ranked human critiques per photo with reference-based similarity metrics, under six prompt conditions that disentangle persona framing, aspect hinting, length control, and single- versus multi-pass generation, and add an image-grounding control that feeds each model the wrong photograph. We find that reference-based similarity gives a misleading picture. Stricter lexical and learned metrics show only weak alignment with human critiques, while a coarse embedding cosine reports broad topical overlap that the grounding control traces to a stable house style rather than image-specific observation. Behaviorally, the models diverge from humans in consistent ways the scores do not surface: even under a length cap they write two to three times as much, cover nearly every aesthetic aspect where humans are selective, engage each aspect more uniformly and at greater depth, and repeat themselves across critiques of the same photo where humans vary. We argue that reference-based similarity rewards a fluent, comprehensive critique style rather than the selectivity and specificity of human critique, and discuss implications for evaluating and training open-ended multimodal generation.

AI.tldr: The paper examines how well multimodal LLMs produce open-ended aesthetic photo critiques compared to humans, showing that standard reference-based similarity metrics are misleading and reward verbose, generic ‘house style’ responses rather than human-like, selective, image-specific critique.
AI.motivation: Most evaluations of aesthetic understanding in MLLMs use multiple-choice questions or numeric scores, which do not capture how people actually give open-ended written critiques. There is a need to understand whether MLLM-generated critiques resemble human critiques in content and style, and whether common automatic metrics are appropriate for judging this alignment.
AI.method: Using the Reddit Photo Critique Dataset, the authors test five open-weight MLLMs on open-ended image critique. For each image they collect multiple human reference critiques and compare model outputs to these using several reference-based similarity metrics, ranging from strict lexical/learned metrics to coarse embedding cosine similarity. They design six prompt conditions to manipulate persona framing, hints about aesthetic aspects to cover, length constraints, and single vs. multi-pass generation, plus an image-grounding control where each model is given the wrong image to see how image-dependent its critiques are.
AI.result: Strict lexical and learned similarity metrics indicate only weak alignment between MLLM and human critiques, while coarse embedding cosine similarity suggests high topical overlap. However, a grounding control—feeding the wrong photo—shows that this overlap stems largely from a stable, generic critique style rather than genuine image-specific observations. Behaviorally, models produce critiques that are much longer than humans, systematically cover almost all aesthetic aspects instead of being selective, treat aspects more uniformly and in greater depth, and repeat similar content across critiques of the same photo, unlike humans who vary their focus and wording.
AI.conclusion: Reference-based similarity metrics are poorly suited for evaluating open-ended aesthetic critiques from MLLMs because they mainly reward fluent, exhaustive, house-style critiques rather than the selective, specific, and image-grounded critiques humans write. The authors suggest that evaluation and training methods for open-ended multimodal generation need to be redesigned to capture human-like selectivity, variability, and genuine grounding in the visual input, instead of optimizing for generic, comprehensive responses that superficially resemble human text.

title: GUICrafter: Weakly-Supervised GUI Agent Leveraging Massive Unannotated Screenshots
summary: Data, as the fundamental substrate of modern intelligence, has greatly driven the development of current foundation models. Naturally, researchers aim to extend this paradigm to the domain of GUI agents, hoping to build strong GUI agents through a similar paradigm. However, GUI agent data cannot be directly harvested from the internet, making it costly and difficult to collect at scale. As a result, current GUI agents suffer from poor cross-device generalization and limited visual grounding ability for fine-grained GUI elements. As an attempt to address data challenge in GUI agents, we propose GUICrafter, a weakly-supervised GUI agent leveraging massive unannotated screenshots to substantially reduce the reliance on expensive human annotations. GUICrafter explores a curriculum learning framework for training GUI agents through two progressive stages. First, the model learns visual grounding from large-scale unannotated screenshots and webpages, leveraging the rich contextual signals inherent in GUI interactions without human annotations. Then, in Stage 2, we leverage a small amount of high-quality data to calibrate the model via reinforcement learning. Experiments show that GUICrafter achieves competitive, or even superior, performance to advanced systems like UI-TARS while using only 0.1% of its data. Furthermore, under the same amount of annotated data, GUICrafter surpasses all previous methods such as GUI-R1. Code, data, and models are available at https://github.com/fansunqi/GUICrafter.

AI.tldr: GUICrafter is a weakly supervised GUI agent that learns from massive unannotated screenshots via curriculum learning and a small amount of annotated data, achieving strong performance with greatly reduced annotation cost.
AI.motivation: Foundation-model-style progress in GUI agents is limited by the difficulty and expense of collecting large-scale, richly annotated GUI interaction data, which leads to poor cross-device generalization and weak fine-grained visual grounding in existing systems. The paper seeks a way to leverage easily obtainable, unannotated GUI screenshots to reduce dependency on costly human-labeled data while still training strong GUI agents.
AI.method: The authors propose GUICrafter, a weakly supervised GUI agent trained in a two-stage curriculum. In Stage 1, the model learns visual grounding from large-scale unannotated screenshots and webpages by exploiting contextual signals inherent in GUI layouts and interactions without explicit human labels. In Stage 2, a small but high-quality annotated dataset is used to further calibrate and refine the agent via reinforcement learning. This combination of self/weak supervision and RL fine-tuning aims to build robust visual grounding and decision-making with minimal labeled data.
AI.result: GUICrafter attains performance comparable to or better than advanced GUI agent systems like UI-TARS while using only 0.1% of their annotated data. With the same quantity of annotated data, it outperforms prior approaches such as GUI-R1, demonstrating superior data efficiency and strong visual grounding and generalization capabilities.
AI.conclusion: Massive unannotated GUI screenshots can be effectively exploited through a curriculum learning framework and minimal reinforcement learning-based calibration to train competitive GUI agents with drastically reduced annotation requirements. GUICrafter demonstrates that weakly supervised training can close or surpass the performance gap with heavily supervised systems, suggesting a scalable path toward more general and capable GUI agents. The authors release code, data, and models to support further research.

title: Why Struggle with Continuous Latents? Interpretable Discrete Latent Reasoning via Rendered Compression
summary: Large language models achieve high reasoning performance via explicit chain-of-thought and reinforcement learning, but require long output sequences and extended inference time. Latent reasoning reduces this cost by shifting computation into a latent space; however, continuous latent methods are hard to train, suffering from unstable and uninterpretable reasoning trajectories. We argue these issues stem from a misalignment between continuous-space reasoning and discrete symbolic supervision, as continuous states lack explicit anchors for step-by-step alignment. To resolve this, we propose \textbf{Discrete Latent Reasoning~(DLR)}, the first method that converts continuous latent states into explicit discrete tokens. Inspired by render-based compression, we render textual chains of thought into images, extract visual features, and construct a discrete latent vocabulary via clustering-based fine-tuning. Expanding the vocabulary and output head enables standard autoregressive modeling over both natural language and latent tokens, supporting pretraining alignment, SFT, and RL. Experiments on five reasoning benchmarks and two model series~(Qwen3-VL and LLaMA-3) confirm that \textbf{DLR} outperforms prior latent reasoning baselines with up to \textbf{20$\times$ compression}. Furthermore, the learned latent trajectories retain an interpretable semantic structure. Overall, discrete latent tokens provide a controllable and interpretable basis for efficient latent reasoning.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Discrete Latent Reasoning (DLR), which converts continuous latent reasoning states into discrete tokens to make latent reasoning more stable, efficient, and interpretable, achieving up to 20× compression while improving performance on reasoning benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing chain-of-thought and RL-based reasoning in large language models require long text outputs and slow inference. Latent reasoning can in principle reduce this cost, but current continuous latent approaches are hard to train and produce unstable, uninterpretable reasoning trajectories. The authors argue this is due to a mismatch between continuous latent spaces and discrete, symbolic supervision signals used for stepwise reasoning.
AI.method: They introduce Discrete Latent Reasoning (DLR), which turns continuous latent states into explicit discrete tokens. The method renders textual chains of thought into images, extracts visual features, and builds a discrete latent vocabulary using clustering-based fine-tuning. The model’s vocabulary and output head are expanded so that it can autoregress over both normal language tokens and these new latent tokens, enabling the usual training regimes (pretraining alignment, supervised fine-tuning, and reinforcement learning) to operate directly on the discrete latent space.
AI.result: On five reasoning benchmarks and across two model families (Qwen3-VL and LLaMA-3), DLR surpasses prior latent reasoning baselines while providing up to 20× compression of reasoning sequences. The learned latent trajectories also organize into a semantically meaningful and interpretable structure.
AI.conclusion: Converting continuous latent reasoning states into discrete tokens yields a more controllable, stable, and interpretable basis for latent reasoning in large language models. DLR shows that discrete latent spaces can simultaneously provide strong reasoning performance, significant compression, and compatibility with standard training pipelines.

title: DeepTrans Studio: Turning Expert Interventions into Shared Team Knowledge in Agentic Translation Workflows
comment: 4 pages, 2 figures. Accepted to CSCW 2026 Demo. Code and demo video: https://github.com/hint-lab/deeptrans-studio, https://youtu.be/cNpafhHAEjg
summary: Professional translation is often a team-based process: translators, reviewers, and project managers must coordinate terminology, legal force, and accountability across documents. Yet many LLM-based translation tools treat human corrections as isolated edits. Expert decisions made in one segment or by one member are rarely captured as reusable knowledge for the rest of the team. We present DeepTrans Studio, a collaborative translation workspace that lets professionals intercept selected nodes in an agentic translation workflow, review evidence, revise AI outputs, and save approved decisions to a shared team memory. During the demo, attendees will role-play translators and reviewers, resolve preset terminology and legal-modal risks, and see how their decisions are propagated to downstream segments and surfaced in a teammate's workspace as reusable precedents. The demo illustrates how human interventions in AI-mediated work can become shared, traceable knowledge rather than one-off corrections.

AI.tldr: DeepTrans Studio is a collaborative LLM-based translation workspace that turns human edits into shared, reusable team knowledge instead of isolated corrections.
AI.motivation: Current LLM-based translation tools treat expert human corrections as one-off edits, failing to capture translators’ and reviewers’ decisions as reusable knowledge for the broader team. Professional translation, however, is highly collaborative and requires consistent handling of terminology, legal force, and accountability across documents and roles.
AI.method: They build DeepTrans Studio, an agentic translation workflow environment where professionals can intercept specific nodes in the workflow, inspect supporting evidence, modify AI-generated translations, and store approved decisions into a shared team memory. The system then reuses these stored decisions to influence downstream translation segments and exposes them as precedents in collaborators’ workspaces.
AI.result: In a live demo setting, participants act as translators and reviewers, resolve predefined terminology and legal-modal issues, and observe how their corrections are propagated to subsequent segments and appear in teammates’ interfaces as recommended or enforced precedents, demonstrating propagation and reuse of human decisions.
AI.conclusion: DeepTrans Studio shows that human interventions in AI-mediated translation can be structured as persistent, team-wide, and traceable knowledge. This transforms corrections from ephemeral, local fixes into shared precedents that systematically guide future translations and collaborative decisions.

title: SEVA: Self-Evolving Verification Agent with Process Reward for Fact Attribution
comment: Accepted at AI4GOOD@ICML 2026 and FAGEN@ICML 2026. Code: https://github.com/Justin0504/Verifiable_agent
summary: Hallucination is the reliability bottleneck for LLM-based agents, and fact attribution verifiers are the last line of defense -- yet today's verifiers emit only opaque binary labels, leaving agents unable to self-correct and operators unable to audit. We present SEVA, a structured verification agent that emits evidence alignments, step-by-step reasoning chains, calibrated confidence, and a six-category error diagnosis with actionable fixes. Training such an agent with RL is non-trivial: standard binary reward on multi-component output triggers advantage collapse -- within-group reward variance vanishes and the GRPO gradient disappears. We resolve this with a process reward that decomposes verification quality into five independent components weighted 70/30 toward process signals, restoring the gradient and inducing an implicit curriculum -- the agent first masters verification behavior (alignment 0.917 -> 0.997, format 72% -> 100%), then outcomes (F1 64.9 -> 69.0). Structured output further enables a Verify -> Reflect -> Probe -> Refine self-evolution loop, which over four rounds on a 7B model surfaces an unexpected structural finding: each round produces a benchmark-specialist, not a generalist (+15 pp on HaluEval, -10 to -14 pp on TruthfulQA in the same model, persistent at 4x data). On ClearFacts, SEVA-3B matches GPT-4o-mini (69.0 vs. 69.8 F1) while producing substantially richer, auditable output -- confirming a principle that should generalize: for any RL task with multi-component generation, reward granularity must match output granularity.

AI.tldr: SEVA is a structured verification agent for LLMs that outputs detailed, auditable verification signals and uses a decomposed process reward in RL to avoid advantage collapse, improving verification quality and revealing surprising specialization dynamics.
AI.motivation: LLM-based agents suffer from hallucinations, and existing fact attribution verifiers provide only binary judgments that are opaque and non-actionable, limiting self-correction and human auditing. There is a need for verifiers that output structured, interpretable signals and for RL methods that can effectively train such multi-component verification behaviors.
AI.method: The authors design SEVA, a verification agent that produces structured outputs: evidence alignments, step-by-step reasoning, calibrated confidence, and a six-way error diagnosis with suggested fixes. To train it, they introduce a process reward that decomposes verification quality into five independent components, emphasizing process (70%) over outcome (30%). This granular reward avoids advantage collapse in GRPO by maintaining within-group reward variance, effectively shaping learning and creating an implicit curriculum. They also implement a self-evolution loop (Verify -> Reflect -> Probe -> Refine) over multiple rounds on a 7B model to study specialization dynamics.
AI.result: With the decomposed process reward, SEVA first improves process metrics (alignment from 0.917 to 0.997 and format adherence from 72% to 100%) and then improves outcome metrics (F1 from 64.9 to 69.0). The structured outputs enable a self-evolution loop that, across four rounds, creates models that are specialists on specific benchmarks rather than generalists, gaining about +15 percentage points on HaluEval while losing 10–14 points on TruthfulQA even when trained with 4x data. On the ClearFacts benchmark, SEVA-3B achieves performance comparable to GPT-4o-mini (69.0 vs. 69.8 F1) while offering much richer, auditable verification output.
AI.conclusion: Structured verification with rich, decomposed signals can substantially improve both the transparency and performance of LLM-based fact verifiers. For RL on tasks involving multi-component generation, rewards must be similarly multi-component (aligned with output granularity) to avoid optimization pathologies like advantage collapse and to support effective curriculum-like learning. SEVA demonstrates that such design can reach near–state-of-the-art performance with smaller models while yielding more interpretable, auditable behavior, and it uncovers an important phenomenon of benchmark-specific specialization under self-evolution loops.

title: DEEPMED Search: An Open-Source Agentic Platform for Medical Deep Research with Introspective Verification
comment: 5 pages, 2 figures, 2 tables. Accepted to IJCAI-ECAI 2026 Demo Track. Project website: https://www.deepmedsearch.cloud. Demo video: https://youtu.be/4U4aok8yLpk
summary: Navigating the deluge of heterogeneous medical data, from academic literature (PubMed) to clinical guidelines (Web) and private knowledge bases, remains a critical bottleneck for evidence-based medicine. While commercial black-box tools lack transparency, standard open-source RAG implementations frequently suffer from reasoning drift when handling complex, long-tail queries. We present DEEPMED Search, a fully open-source, agentic platform designed for transparent medical deep research. Built on a high-performance Next.js architecture, DEEPMED Search features a source-adaptive router that autonomously dispatches sub-queries to PubMed, web search, or local graph-based knowledge bases based on information density. Crucially, the platform integrates an introspective verification module, powered by a causal-consistent multi-agent debate framework, to validate retrieved evidence against diagnostic logic before synthesis. To demonstrate its robustness, we showcase DEEPMED Search's ability to autonomously decompose high-difficulty rare disease queries, filter out confounding noise, and generate structured, citation-backed research reports in minutes. By open-sourcing this software, we provide the community with a robust infrastructure to democratize access to trustworthy, glass-box medical reasoning in research and prototyping settings.

AI.tldr: DEEPMED Search is an open-source, agentic medical research platform that routes queries across multiple data sources and uses multi-agent debate for evidence verification, enabling transparent, citation-backed answers to complex clinical questions.
AI.motivation: Medical practitioners and researchers face an overwhelming volume of heterogeneous data across PubMed, web-based clinical guidelines, and private knowledge bases. Existing commercial tools are black boxes, and standard open-source RAG systems often fail on complex, rare, or long-tail medical queries due to reasoning drift and poor evidence validation. There is a need for a transparent, open, and robust system for deep medical research that can reliably handle challenging diagnostic and evidence-based questions.
AI.method: The paper proposes DEEPMED Search, an agentic platform built on a Next.js architecture. It introduces a source-adaptive router that decomposes user queries into sub-queries and automatically dispatches them to appropriate sources (PubMed, web search, or local graph-based knowledge bases) depending on information density. The system incorporates an introspective verification module based on a causal-consistent multi-agent debate framework, which cross-checks retrieved evidence against diagnostic logic before producing an answer. The platform then synthesizes the validated evidence into structured, citation-backed research reports.
AI.result: DEEPMED Search can autonomously handle high-difficulty, rare disease and long-tail medical queries. It decomposes them into sub-problems, filters noisy or confounding evidence, and produces structured outputs that include supporting citations. The paper demonstrates robustness through case-style demonstrations of rare disease queries, showing improved reliability and transparency compared to standard RAG systems, though exact quantitative metrics are not specified in the abstract.
AI.conclusion: DEEPMED Search delivers a transparent, open-source infrastructure for complex medical information retrieval and reasoning. By combining source-adaptive routing with a multi-agent introspective verification process, it mitigates reasoning drift and supports trustworthy, glass-box medical research workflows. Open-sourcing the system aims to democratize access to high-quality, explainable medical reasoning tools for researchers and prototyping environments.

title: How Far Do On-Prem Open LLMs Get on Text-to-SQL? A Cross-Family Size x Technique Frontier on BIRD
comment: 9 pages, 4 figures, 3 tables. Code: https://github.com/beskvladimir-create/nl2sql-onprem-bench Data DOI: https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.20952794
summary: Organizations that cannot send data to a cloud API increasingly ask: how good is Text-to-SQL if the model must run on-premises on open weights, and which popular accuracy "recipes" are worth their compute? We answer with an honest, fully reproducible benchmark on the BIRD development split (n=1534, Execution Accuracy), evaluating three open model families across two generations -- Qwen2.5-Coder (7B/14B/32B), CodeLlama-Instruct (7B/13B/34B), and Llama-3.x (8B, 70B) -- under one matched protocol, ablating a model-agnostic recipe (schema linking, self-correction, self-consistency) component by component, with every difference tested by the paired McNemar test. Four findings stand out. (i) Generation matters more than raw size, and the recipe is family-robust: Qwen2.5-Coder dominates the older CodeLlama at matched size (39.1 vs 20.9 at 7B), but a modern non-Qwen model (Llama-3.3-70B, 49.2 on a matched serving) is competitive, so CodeLlama's weakness reflects its 2023 generation, not "non-Qwen = weak". (ii) Self-correction is a robust, near-free win, significant on all three families where there is room to improve. (iii) Schema linking does not help, and a stronger linker does not rescue it: a retrieval/embedding linker with 96.5% gold-table recall is statistically indistinguishable from no linking, ruling out the "weak lexical strawman" objection across three families. (iv) Self-consistency is poor value (+0.13 pp for ~5x tokens, not significant). We report real per-stage cost ($/1k queries) and release all code, predictions, and summaries; archived code and data: https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.20952794

AI.tldr: The paper benchmarks open-weight, on-premise Text-to-SQL systems on the BIRD dev set, systematically testing common accuracy-boosting techniques (schema linking, self-correction, self-consistency) across several modern LLM families and sizes, and finds that newer model generations matter more than sheer size, self-correction is reliably helpful, schema linking brings no benefit, and self-consistency is not compute-efficient.
AI.motivation: Many organizations cannot send data to cloud APIs and must rely on on-premise, open-weight models for Text-to-SQL, but there is little clear, reproducible evidence about how good these setups can be and which commonly used accuracy-improving “recipes” are actually worth their computational cost. The paper aims to give such organizations practical guidance grounded in rigorous benchmarking rather than anecdotal claims.
AI.method: The authors perform an on-premise Text-to-SQL benchmark on the BIRD development split (1,534 queries), measuring Execution Accuracy. They evaluate three open model families across two generations—Qwen2.5-Coder (7B/14B/32B), CodeLlama-Instruct (7B/13B/34B), and Llama-3.x (8B, 70B)—under a matched prompting and serving protocol. They construct a model-agnostic “recipe” comprised of schema linking, self-correction, and self-consistency, and then systematically ablate each component. For every comparison, they apply the paired McNemar test to check statistical significance. They also record real per-stage monetary cost per 1,000 queries and publicly release all code, predictions, and experiment artifacts for full reproducibility.
AI.result: They find that (i) model generation (how recent/advanced the family is) is more important than raw parameter count, and their recipe transfers robustly across families; Qwen2.5-Coder strongly outperforms the older CodeLlama at the same size (e.g., 39.1 vs. 20.9 execution accuracy at 7B), while a modern non-Qwen model (Llama-3.3-70B) achieves 49.2 under comparable conditions, showing CodeLlama’s weakness is due to its vintage, not being non-Qwen. (ii) Adding a self-correction step yields a consistent, statistically significant accuracy gain wherever there is headroom, at negligible extra compute. (iii) Schema linking fails to improve performance; even a strong retrieval-based linker with 96.5% recall for gold tables performs statistically on par with having no schema linking at all across all three model families. (iv) Self-consistency (sampling multiple outputs and voting) delivers only a tiny accuracy gain (+0.13 percentage points) at roughly 5× token usage, and the gain is not statistically significant.
AI.conclusion: For on-premise Text-to-SQL with open-weight LLMs, organizations should prioritize using a recent-generation model family over simply increasing model size, and should almost always include a self-correction step as a cheap, reliable improvement. Common wisdom around schema linking and self-consistency is challenged: sophisticated schema linking brings no measurable benefit on BIRD, and self-consistency is generally not a cost-effective use of compute. The study offers a transparent, statistically grounded baseline and practical cost numbers to guide deployment choices, supported by fully released code and artifacts.

title: Rethinking Generative Reconstruction Attacks against Graph Neural Network Models
summary: The application of graph data in numerous disciplines raises the need for gathering and analyzing huge volumes of data, some of which is private and sensitive. The non-Euclidean nature of the graph data makes the analysis computationally challenging, leading to the use of Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) in the age of AI. GNNs may inadvertently leak sensitive data they are trained on, which raises serious data security issues, including the model inversion attack. In this study, we analyze GNNs' vulnerabilities by introducing two novel graph inversion (i.e., reconstruction) attacks: graph-label conditioned (GLC) attack and embedding-label conditioned (ELC) attack, utilizing targetmodel predictions and their intermediate representations, respectively. We perform a comprehensive analysis of our introduced privacy attacks and compare them with existing baselines across three benchmark graph datasets (i.e., NCI1, PROTEINS, and AIDS) and four graph distributional/structural metrics (i.e., FGD, EGD, MMD, and GKS). Our work demonstrates that an adversary can use the generator-discriminator technique to reconstruct high-quality graphs in real-world black-box attack scenarios against GNNs. Additionally, we present a variant of our attacks (Ours--) with 50% reduced queries, achieving good or comparable reconstruction attack performance. In addition, we show that GNNs are highly vulnerable to privacy attacks, varying Laplacian noise-scales.

AI.tldr: The paper studies privacy risks of Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) and proposes two powerful graph reconstruction (inversion) attacks that can recover high-quality graphs from a target GNN in black-box settings.
AI.motivation: Graph data (e.g., molecules, proteins, social networks) often contain sensitive information. GNNs are widely used for such data but may leak private training data through their outputs or internal representations. Existing inversion/privacy attacks for GNNs are limited. The authors want to rigorously evaluate how vulnerable GNNs are and design stronger, realistic attacks that work even in black-box conditions.
AI.method: They propose two novel inversion attacks on trained GNNs: (1) Graph-Label Conditioned (GLC) attack, which conditions a generator on graph-level labels and target model predictions to reconstruct graphs; (2) Embedding-Label Conditioned (ELC) attack, which uses intermediate GNN embeddings plus labels as conditions. Both attacks use a generator–discriminator framework to synthesize graphs whose statistics match those of real graphs. They evaluate their attacks on three benchmark datasets (NCI1, PROTEINS, AIDS) using four graph distribution/structure similarity metrics (FGD, EGD, MMD, GKS), and also develop a query-efficient variant (Ours--) that uses about half as many queries. They further study how adding Laplacian noise affects vulnerability.
AI.result: The proposed GLC and ELC attacks can reconstruct high-quality graphs from target GNNs in realistic black-box settings, outperforming existing baselines on standard graph datasets under several distributional/structural metrics. The query-efficient variant with 50% fewer queries still achieves good or comparable reconstruction performance. Experiments show that even when Laplacian noise is added (with various noise scales), GNNs remain highly vulnerable to privacy attacks.
AI.conclusion: GNNs pose serious privacy risks: adversaries can effectively reconstruct sensitive training graphs using only black-box access to a GNN’s predictions or embeddings, via generator–discriminator based inversion attacks. Existing simple defenses like adding Laplacian noise are insufficient to fully protect against these attacks. Stronger, more principled privacy-preserving techniques are needed for graph learning applications involving sensitive data.

title: Fast Numbers, Slow Language: Bridging Quantitative and Qualitative Earnings Signals
summary:
Earnings announcements release two types of information sequentially: quantitative surprise (numeric earnings-per-share (EPS)/revenue versus analyst estimate) arrives first in press releases and financial news, processed by algorithmic traders within minutes; qualitative language (management tone, guidance, question-and-answer (Q&A) credibility) arrives 30-90 min later in the earnings conference call transcript (ECT), requiring human interpretation overnight. Financial economists have studied quantitative surprise for 50 years; natural language processing (NLP) researchers have studied qualitative ECT signals for a decade. Despite studying the same event, the two communities used incompatible frameworks: different targets (return vs. volatility), trading setups (long top-decile and short bottom-decile vs. trade-all), and metrics (return spread between top and bottom 20% (Q5-Q1) vs. mean squared error (MSE)), making direct comparison and connection challenging.
  We bridge these communities with EarningsInOne, the first corpus aligning earnings news, ECTs, and intraday and next-day prices across SP 1500 (broad U.S. equity universe, 2022-2025). Applying unified trading and evaluation tools to both signal types, we confirm a clean speed separation, fast numbers, slow language: quantitative surprise peaks at announcement and is largely eliminated by the next market open; qualitative ECT sentiment peaks on the next trading day, real and tradeable, but hidden under prior transcript-based evaluation that optimised sign-agnostic volatility with pointwise MSE.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces EarningsInOne, a unified corpus and framework to jointly study quantitative and qualitative information from earnings announcements and show how each impacts prices at different speeds.
AI.motivation: Financial economists and NLP researchers both study earnings announcements but from different angles: economists focus on immediate quantitative surprises and returns, while NLP focuses on qualitative conference call language and volatility forecasts. Their different targets, trading setups, and metrics make results hard to compare, obscuring how numeric and linguistic signals jointly move markets over time.
AI.method: The authors build EarningsInOne, a dataset that aligns press-release earnings numbers, earnings call transcripts, and intraday/next-day stock prices for S&P 1500 firms. Using a unified backtesting and evaluation framework (same trading rules and performance metrics), they apply it to both quantitative surprise signals and qualitative NLP sentiment/credibility signals extracted from transcripts, then compare the timing and tradability of the two signal types.
AI.result: They find a clear temporal separation. Quantitative surprises about EPS/revenue are incorporated into prices very quickly around the announcement, leaving little exploitable signal by the next market open. In contrast, qualitative signals from conference call language—tone, guidance, Q&A credibility—peak in their pricing impact on the next trading day and remain tradeable, but this effect was previously obscured by evaluation setups that emphasized sign-agnostic volatility prediction with MSE rather than directional returns.
AI.conclusion: A unified dataset and evaluation framework reveals that markets process numbers fast but language slowly. Quantitative earnings surprises are efficiently priced almost immediately, while qualitative information in earnings calls offers delayed, exploitable return predictability. Prior NLP work missed this because it optimized for volatility MSE rather than directional trading performance, suggesting future research should align targets, setups, and metrics with economic use cases.

title: CLQT: A Closed-Loop, Cost-Aware, Strategy-Consistent Benchmark for Diagnostic Evaluation of LLM Portfolio-Management Agents
summary: LLM agents are increasingly cast as autonomous portfolio managers, and benchmarks have moved from financial question-answering to sequential trading. Yet most still rank agents by returns over a fixed window -- a weak proxy, since a period's return is dominated by the market path and apparent alpha can dissolve once look-ahead leakage is controlled. Such a ranking certifies neither sound reasoning, nor a consistent strategy, nor a durable edge. We introduce CLQT, which reframes closed-loop trading evaluation as diagnosis rather than ranking: an instrument that localizes where and why an agent's process succeeds or fails. CLQT is a fully closed-loop, cost-aware, strategy-consistent, temporally-gated environment whose agents run a five-stage cycle: gather, synthesize, allocate, execute, reflect. Each round emits a complete DecisionRound sealed into a recompute-verifiable hash chain, so every metric is reconstructable from the trail. Six pillars form the substrate: a hard TimeGate, institutional transaction- and financing-cost modeling, strategy-consistency scoring, three-tier memory, a Model-Context-Protocol tool layer, and mandate-aware synthesis. The same agent runs as a constrained committee of specialized roles or a single full-autonomy orchestrator, making process scaffolding an experimental variable. From the audit trail we compute a five-axis capability scorecard (APM-CS: Coherence, Acuity, Composure, Discipline, Reliability), with Coherence judged partly by a held-out, out-of-cohort LLM to curb self-preference bias. We validate it on a contamination-controlled multi-model backtest with an ablation grid and a live broker track on unseen, post-cutoff data, against a repeated-run noise floor. CLQT separates outcome from capability, yielding not a model ranking but a durable, extensible map of agent competencies and limitations.

AI.tldr: CLQT is a diagnostic benchmark for LLM trading agents that evaluates *how* they trade, not just *how much* they earn, producing a structured capability profile instead of a simple return-based ranking.
AI.motivation: Current evaluations of LLM-based trading agents mostly rely on realized returns over fixed windows, which are heavily confounded by market path, look-ahead leakage, and luck. Such metrics do not certify good reasoning, stable strategy, or persistent edge, and they fail to reveal where agents are fragile or mis-specified. The authors want an evaluation framework that disentangles outcome from capability and provides fine-grained, interpretable diagnostics of an agent’s trading process.
AI.method: They design CLQT, a fully closed-loop trading environment where LLM agents operate in repeated five-stage cycles: gather information, synthesize views, allocate capital, execute trades, and reflect on performance. Each decision cycle is logged as a structured DecisionRound and cryptographically chained so the entire process is auditable and recomputable. The environment incorporates six key design elements: (1) strict temporal gating to prevent future information leakage, (2) realistic institutional transaction and financing cost models, (3) metrics for strategy consistency, (4) a three-tier memory architecture, (5) a Model-Context-Protocol tool layer, and (6) mandate-aware synthesis so agents remain within specified constraints. The same underlying model can be deployed either as multiple specialized roles coordinated in a committee or as a single orchestrator, enabling tests of process scaffolding. From the audit trail, they derive a five-dimensional capability scorecard (APM-CS: Coherence, Acuity, Composure, Discipline, Reliability), where Coherence is partially evaluated by an out-of-cohort LLM to reduce self-assessment bias. They validate CLQT through backtests with controlled data contamination, an ablation grid over design choices, and live trading with a broker on post-cutoff data, comparing against a noise-floor from repeated runs.
AI.result: CLQT produces a rich diagnostic map of trading-agent behavior rather than just a PnL curve. It separates luck and market path from intrinsic agent capabilities, quantifies multiple dimensions of trading competence, and exposes how design choices like role-based committees vs. monolithic orchestrators affect behavior. In experiments on multi-model backtests and live trading, CLQT yields stable, interpretable capability profiles across models and conditions while controlling for data leakage and noise, and it shows that some agents that appear strong by raw returns actually have weak or inconsistent process metrics.
AI.conclusion: The authors conclude that evaluating LLM trading agents solely by realized returns is inadequate and often misleading. CLQT offers a more robust alternative by treating evaluation as process diagnosis rather than leaderboard ranking, providing an extensible, auditable framework that characterizes where agents are strong or fragile across multiple capability axes. This enables more principled comparison, debugging, and improvement of LLM-based trading systems and can serve as a durable foundation for future research on autonomous financial agents.

title: Managing Map Cardinality in Automatic Disease Classification Mapping: Balancing Precision, Recall and Coverage
comment: Main text: 8 pages, 1 table and 3 figures; Appendix: 8 pages, 11 tables, 2 figures
summary: Automatic mapping between disease classification systems, such as the International Classification of Diseases (ICD), is a challenging yet essential task for integrating health data and conducting longitudinal data analysis. Existing embedding-based methods primarily focus on \emph{one-to-one} mappings, overlooking more complex \emph{one-to-many} scenarios. The threshold-based and top-K methods offer natural extensions; however, they involve inherent trade-offs between \emph{precision}, \emph{recall} and \emph{mapping coverage} -- the proportion of source codes with at least one mapping to a target code. To address this challenge, we introduce a novel method, which is inspired by the \emph{blocking-and-matching} pipeline commonly used in \emph{entity resolution}. In particular, we first generate a block of candidate matches (\emph{blocking}) and then employ a large language model (LLM) to identify all valid mappings within each block (\emph{matching}). Empirically, we show that the proposed method achieves higher precision with comparable recall and broader coverage across multiple ICD version pairs (ICD-9-CM$\leftrightarrow$ICD-10-CM and ICD-10-AM$\leftrightarrow$ICD-11). Our source code and dataset is available at: https://tinyurl.com/46kyn7wp.

AI.tldr: They propose a two-stage, LLM-based blocking-and-matching method to automatically map disease codes between ICD versions, improving precision while maintaining recall and increasing coverage, especially for one-to-many mappings.
AI.motivation: Automatic mappings between different ICD versions are crucial for integrating health data and supporting longitudinal analyses, but current embedding-based methods mainly handle one-to-one mappings and struggle with one-to-many relationships, also facing trade-offs between precision, recall, and mapping coverage.
AI.method: They adapt the classic blocking-and-matching pipeline from entity resolution: (1) generate a block (set) of candidate target codes for each source code using embeddings or similar techniques (blocking); (2) within each block, use a large language model as a semantic matcher to decide which candidates are valid mappings, enabling one-to-many mappings and reducing errors from naive threshold or top-K selection.
AI.result: Across multiple ICD version pairs (ICD-9-CM↔ICD-10-CM and ICD-10-AM↔ICD-11), their approach attains higher precision than baselines while achieving similar recall and larger mapping coverage (more source codes receive at least one target mapping).
AI.conclusion: An LLM-based blocking-and-matching framework is effective for complex ICD code mapping, particularly for one-to-many scenarios, delivering more accurate and comprehensive mappings than simple embedding thresholds or top-K rules; they release code and data to support further research and application.

title: The CRISTAL Method: Neurosymbolic analysis from AI-synthesized world models
summary: This project introduces the CRISTAL Method (Coherent Reliable Intentional Synthesis of Truthful Analysis Logic), a neurosymbolic framework for automating complex analysis workflows, with fundamental investment analysis as a primary use case. This domain poses major challenges: high structural uncertainty, noisy and subjective data, tight attention budgets, and the need for justified, reproducible decisions. Human analysts often struggle in this domain due to cognitive biases and limitations, suggesting significant value in automation. But while LLM-based agents have been proposed as analytical aids, their limitations -- poor numerical reasoning, unawareness of uncertainty, and lack of reproducibility -- hinder their effectiveness in this context. CRISTAL addresses these gaps through a principled blend of statistical model synthesis, continuous learning, and active learning. Starting from a natural-language prior knowledge curriculum, CRISTAL builds a dynamic, interpretable probabilistic program that enables full Bayesian inference, including uncertainty quantification and budget-aware data acquisition. CRISTAL continually refines its world model during analysis, leveraging LLMs for code synthesis and learning. We validate CRISTAL on a novel benchmark of synthetic equities with rich financial and textual data. On a company classification task, CRISTAL achieves Bayes-optimal accuracy with just 5 examples and a 5-second budget, outperforming state-of-the-art LLMs that plateau around 40\% accuracy even with order-of-magnitude more input data and compute.

AI.tldr: CRISTAL is a neurosymbolic framework that uses probabilistic programming, Bayesian inference, and LLM-based code synthesis to automate complex analysis workflows, validated on synthetic equity analysis where it dramatically outperforms standard LLMs.
AI.motivation: Fundamental investment analysis is hard to automate due to noisy and subjective data, structural uncertainty, tight attention/computation budgets, and the need for justified, reproducible decisions. Human analysts are limited by cognitive biases, while existing LLM agents lack strong numerical reasoning, explicit uncertainty handling, and reproducibility, leaving a gap for more principled, reliable automation.
AI.method: CRISTAL combines statistical model synthesis, continuous learning, and active learning in a neurosymbolic framework. It starts from a natural-language prior knowledge curriculum and automatically constructs a dynamic, interpretable probabilistic program that supports full Bayesian inference, including uncertainty quantification and budget-aware data acquisition. During analysis, it uses LLMs to synthesize and refine code, continually updating its world model as new information is acquired.
AI.result: On a benchmark of synthetic equities with detailed financial and textual features, CRISTAL is evaluated on a company classification task. It achieves Bayes-optimal accuracy using only 5 training examples and a 5-second computation budget, significantly outperforming strong LLM baselines, which plateau at around 40% accuracy even when given an order of magnitude more data and compute.
AI.conclusion: The CRISTAL Method shows that a neurosymbolic, probabilistic-programming-based approach can deliver sample-efficient, compute-efficient, and reproducible analysis in complex, uncertain domains such as fundamental investment analysis. By explicitly modeling uncertainty, leveraging prior knowledge, and using LLMs for targeted code synthesis rather than end-to-end reasoning, CRISTAL overcomes key limitations of pure LLM agents and suggests a promising path for automating other complex analytical workflows.

title: Are Humans Evolved Instruction Followers? An Underlying Inductive Bias Enables Rapid Instructed Task Learning
comment: 4 pages, Position Paper, Published at Neurips 2025 Workshop on Interpreting Cognition in Deep Learning Models - https://neurips.cc/virtual/2025/loc/san-diego/129741
summary: Human adults can often perform a novel task correctly on the first attempt after only receiving verbal or written instructions. This rapid instructed task learning (RITL) is a hallmark of human cognitive flexibility, yet its mechanisms and parallels in artificial systems remain under-explored across disciplines. In this position paper, we argue that humans possess an evolved instruction-following bias -- an inductive bias shaped by evolution to interpret and execute linguistic instructions which critically enables fast generalization of behavior from language. This bias functions analogously to the way large language models (LLMs) leverage instruction tuning to achieve zero-shot task performance. We synthesize evidence from cognitive science, neuroscience, and machine learning research to support this hypothesis. While instruction-following in AI is currently achieved via specialized training protocols, we posit that in humans it arises as an innate cognitive architecture feature. We outline testable predictions and call for more interdisciplinary research to investigate Instruction-Following as a unifying mechanism enabling rapid task learning in both natural and artificial neural networks.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes that humans have an evolved, innate bias to follow linguistic instructions, analogous to how LLMs use instruction tuning, and argues this bias underlies our ability for rapid instructed task learning.
AI.motivation: To explain how humans can rapidly learn and correctly perform novel tasks from instructions alone, and to connect this ability to mechanisms in artificial systems like LLMs, which also show instruction-following behavior.
AI.method: A conceptual and synthetic position-paper approach: the authors review and integrate evidence from cognitive science, neuroscience, and machine learning to argue for an evolved instruction-following inductive bias in humans and draw analogies to instruction-tuned LLMs.
AI.result: They present a unified hypothesis that an innate instruction-following bias in humans parallels instruction-tuning in LLMs, and derive testable predictions about cognitive and neural mechanisms of rapid instructed task learning in humans and artificial networks.
AI.conclusion: Instruction-following is proposed as a core, possibly innate, cognitive mechanism that enables humans’ rapid task learning from language, and this mechanism has strong analogies in modern AI systems; the authors call for interdisciplinary research to empirically test and develop this framework.

title: Beyond Triplet Plausibility: Relation Set Completion in Knowledge Graphs
summary: Knowledge graphs (KGs) organize real-world knowledge as triplets and underpin many downstream applications. Due to their inherent incompleteness, knowledge graph completion (KGC) is widely studied and is typically formulated as triplet prediction, with link prediction as the dominant paradigm. However, this formulation focuses on the incompleteness of triplet-wise information and overlooks the incompleteness of entity-relation compatibility information. To address this limitation, we introduce a relation set completion task (RSC), which complements the link prediction task and aims to reason about missing relations that are semantically compatible with a given entity. We further propose a Relation Set Embedding model (RelSetE), which models latent patterns among the observed relations of entities to infer missing ones. To evaluate RelSetE, we derive three benchmark datasets from standard KG benchmarks. Extensive experiments demonstrate that RelSetE effectively captures entity-relation compatibility patterns and performs favorably in inferring missing relations of entities. Code and data are publicly available.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies a new type of incompleteness in knowledge graphs—missing but compatible relations for entities—and proposes a new task (relation set completion) and a dedicated embedding model (RelSetE) to tackle it, showing strong performance on derived benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing knowledge graph completion mainly uses link prediction framed as triplet prediction, which focuses on missing (head, relation, tail) facts. This overlooks whether the set of relations attached to an entity is itself incomplete, i.e., which relations are semantically compatible with that entity regardless of specific tails. The authors aim to explicitly model and recover these missing compatible relations at the entity level, filling a gap in how KG incompleteness is treated.
AI.method: They define a new task, Relation Set Completion (RSC), where the goal is to infer which relations are semantically compatible with a given entity, beyond individual triplets. They propose RelSetE (Relation Set Embedding), an embedding model that learns latent patterns within the observed relation sets of entities, and uses these patterns to predict additional, unobserved but compatible relations. They then construct three benchmark datasets for RSC by transforming standard KG benchmarks into relation-set form.
AI.result: Across the three derived benchmarks, RelSetE is empirically shown to capture compatibility patterns between entities and relations and to outperform baselines in predicting missing relations for entities. The experiments suggest that modeling relation sets provides additional signal beyond standard link prediction.
AI.conclusion: Relation incompleteness at the entity level is a distinct and important aspect of KG incompleteness. By introducing the RSC task and the RelSetE model, the paper shows that explicitly modeling relation sets can effectively uncover missing but compatible relations for entities, complementing traditional link prediction approaches.

title: Fund2Persona: A Framework for Building and Refining Financial Advisor Personas from Fund Disclosure Data
summary: Demand for personalized financial advising is growing, but consistent advisor expertise is difficult to obtain, scale, and encode in LLM systems. Simple persona prompts rarely specify how a financial advisor should reason and often drift toward generic recommendations. We propose Fund2Persona, a framework that grounds financial-advisor personas in fund disclosures, holdings transitions, market context, and manager commentary, then refines them through an agentic actor--scorer--patcher loop. We evaluate the resulting personas on held-out holdings-transition reconstruction and manager-commentary alignment, where they better recover portfolio decisions and grounded manager interpretation than generic baselines. We further study two downstream diagnostics: market-scenario generation, where persona retrieval broadens plausible investment views beyond repeated generic rollouts, and advisory dialogues grounded in investor profiles, where matched personas give more specific and useful advice than a generic advisor. These results suggest that fund-data-grounded financial-advisor personas can make manager-specific investment expertise portable rather than merely changing an LLM's surface style.

AI.tldr: They build financial-advisor personas grounded in real mutual-fund data and show these personas better reflect manager behavior and give more specific, useful advice than generic LLM prompts.
AI.motivation: Personalized financial advice is in demand, but LLM-based advisors usually rely on shallow persona prompts that lack real investment process grounding and drift into generic recommendations. There is a need for a way to capture and port specific fund managers’ decision patterns and expertise into LLMs in a scalable, data-driven manner.
AI.method: They introduce Fund2Persona, a framework that constructs advisor personas from structured fund data: disclosures, portfolio holdings transitions over time, market context, and manager commentary. The system uses an agentic loop of actor–scorer–patcher: an actor proposes persona descriptions or behaviors, a scorer evaluates them against fund behavior and commentary, and a patcher revises them iteratively. They then test these personas in several tasks, comparing them to generic persona baselines.
AI.result: Fund2Persona personas more accurately reconstruct held-out portfolio holdings transitions and align better with the tone and content of manager commentary than generic personas. In downstream tasks, using these personas for market-scenario generation yields more diverse and plausible investment views versus repetitive generic outputs. In advisory dialogues tailored to investor profiles, personas matched to relevant funds provide more specific and helpful advice than a generic advisor persona.
AI.conclusion: Grounding advisor personas directly in fund data can capture manager-specific investment expertise in a way that LLMs can use, going beyond style mimicry. Such personas make portfolio-manager expertise portable across tasks—improving behavioral fidelity, scenario exploration, and the quality of personalized financial advice compared with generic LLM personas.

title: AI Training Manager: Bounded Closed-Loop Control of Adaptive Training Recipes
summary: We present the AI Training Manager, a bounded LLM-based supervisory controller for adaptive machine learning training. Standard training pipelines often rely on fixed recipes or single-axis schedulers, which can struggle with mid-run failures such as severe overfitting, loss imbalance, exploration collapse, or unsafe exploration. Rather than replacing mathematical optimizers or acting as an unconstrained coding agent, the manager operates through a schema-conditioned interface: it reads structured telemetry snapshots from an active run, audits a constrained action space, and returns validated updates to training parameters such as learning rate, regularization strength, loss-weight coefficients, and exploration settings. We evaluate this architecture across supervised language modeling and reinforcement learning. On TinyStories, the manager detects and corrects overfitting, achieving a validation loss 60% lower than the baseline while producing auditable intervention logs. In this supervised setting, we additionally show that manager inference does not need to block the training loop: training can continue while a manager response is pending, and validated updates can be applied asynchronously once available. In a robotic manipulation reinforcement-learning task, we use the same bounded decision interface in an episodic closed-loop setting, where manager updates are applied at evaluation or checkpoint boundaries. The manager mitigates both conservative and unsafe exploration regimes. These results suggest that schema-conditioned LLMs can serve as bounded supervisory managers for live training runs, complementing conventional optimizers and schedulers with interpretable, multi-axis intervention capabilities

AI.tldr: AI Training Manager is a bounded LLM-based controller that supervises and adapts ML training runs, correcting issues like overfitting and unsafe exploration via a schema-limited action interface.
AI.motivation: Conventional training pipelines rely on fixed schedules or simple heuristics and cannot robustly handle mid-training failures such as overfitting, loss imbalance, or problematic exploration behavior. There is a need for a more flexible yet safe and interpretable controller that can adjust training in real time without replacing the underlying optimizer or acting as an unconstrained agent.
AI.method: Introduce a schema-conditioned LLM (“AI Training Manager”) that receives structured telemetry from an ongoing training run and can only act through a constrained, validated action space. The manager suggests changes to hyperparameters (e.g., learning rate, regularization, loss weights, exploration parameters). The system is evaluated in supervised language modeling (TinyStories) and robotic RL, with both synchronous and asynchronous manager interaction patterns, and with episodic closed-loop updates in RL.
AI.result: On TinyStories, the manager detects and corrects overfitting, achieving a 60% reduction in validation loss compared to a baseline while providing interpretable logs of its interventions. In a robotic manipulation RL task, using the same bounded interface, the manager improves exploration by mitigating both overly conservative and unsafe regimes.
AI.conclusion: Schema-conditioned LLMs can function as bounded supervisory controllers for active ML training, working alongside traditional optimizers and schedulers. They enable interpretable, multi-axis, and safe interventions during training, improving robustness to mid-run failures in both supervised and reinforcement learning contexts.

title: How Far Can You Get Without a GPU? A Systematic Benchmark of Lightweight Hallucination Detection Across Question Answering, Dialogue, and Summarisation
summary: Hallucination detection has become a pressing requirement for trustworthy AI deployment at scale. The most accurate detection methods depend on GPU-intensive inference, proprietary API calls, or white-box access to the generating model. This puts them out of reach for resource-constrained researchers and practitioners. In this paper, we explore a practical alternative: how well can hallucination detection perform using only lightweight, CPU-feasible methods built on publicly available models? We systematically benchmark five such methods: ROUGE-L, semantic similarity, BERTScore, a Natural Language Inference (NLI) detector based on a FEVER-trained DeBERTa model, and a score-level ensemble of similarity and NLI. We evaluate them across all three tasks of the HaluEval benchmark: question answering (QA), dialogue, and summarisation. We calibrate each method on a held-out validation split and evaluate it on 2,000 test instances per task. We find that no single method dominates and performance is highly task-dependent. The ensemble performs best on QA (F1 = 0.792, AUC-ROC = 0.873), the NLI detector leads on dialogue (AUC-ROC = 0.713), and all five methods degrade to near-random performance on summarisation (AUC-ROC between 0.469 and 0.574). This task-dependence and the systematic failure on summarisation map the practical frontier of GPU-free hallucination detection. They give practical guidance for method selection under computational constraints. All experiments run on a standard laptop CPU using public models.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates how well lightweight, CPU-only, publicly available methods can detect hallucinations from language models across multiple tasks, mapping the limits and best choices under resource constraints.
AI.motivation: Accurate hallucination detection methods often require heavy GPU resources, paid APIs, or white-box access to large models, which are inaccessible to many practitioners. There is a need to understand how far one can go using only cheap, CPU-feasible, open models so that hallucination detection becomes more widely deployable and practical.
AI.method: The authors benchmark five lightweight hallucination detection methods—ROUGE-L, semantic similarity, BERTScore, an NLI-based detector using a FEVER-trained DeBERTa model, and a score-level ensemble of similarity and NLI. They evaluate these on the HaluEval benchmark across three tasks: question answering, dialogue, and summarisation. Each method is calibrated on a held-out validation set and then tested on 2,000 instances per task, comparing F1 and AUC-ROC metrics. All models run on a standard laptop CPU and are based on publicly available implementations.
AI.result: No single method consistently outperforms the others across all tasks. The ensemble detector works best for question answering (F1 = 0.792, AUC-ROC = 0.873), the NLI-based detector performs best for dialogue (AUC-ROC = 0.713), and all five methods perform poorly and close to random on summarisation (AUC-ROC between 0.469 and 0.574).
AI.conclusion: Lightweight, GPU-free hallucination detection can be reasonably effective for some tasks like QA and, to a lesser extent, dialogue, but largely fails on summarisation, indicating a strong task dependence. This defines a practical frontier for hallucination detection under computational constraints and provides guidance: use ensembles or NLI for certain tasks, but do not rely on these methods for summarisation without further advances.

title: SafePyramid: A Hierarchical Benchmark for In-context Policy Guardrailing
summary: In real-world applications, guardrails are often expected to identify unsafe user-model interactions according to application-specific safety policies, rather than relying on predefined risk taxonomies. In this work, we study this setting under the paradigm of in-context policy guardrailing, where guardrails predict safety violations based on policy specifications provided in context. To systematically evaluate this capability, we introduce SafePyramid, a safety benchmark comprising 1,000 multi-turn conversations across 10 domains and 3,000 corresponding application-specific policies, which together contain 61,699 distinct natural-language rules. SafePyramid organizes the evaluation into three difficulty levels: L0 evaluates individual-rule understanding, L1 evaluates reasoning over rule dependencies, and L2 evaluates adaptation of full novel policy frameworks defined in context. To ensure benchmark quality, we employ a rigorous multi-stage pipeline to construct and validate the benchmark. Using SafePyramid, we evaluate 10 frontier LLMs and 5 policy-configurable guardrails and find that in-context policy guardrailing remains highly challenging: even the best-performing model, GPT-5.5, exactly identifies the full set of violated rules in only 54.0%, 35.3%, and 12.9% cases on L0, L1, and L2, respectively. These results highlight the limitations of current guardrails and call for stronger in-context policy guardrails that can reliably execute policies, resolve rule dependencies, and adapt to novel policy frameworks.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SafePyramid, a benchmark to test whether LLM-based guardrails can follow arbitrary, in-context safety policies, and shows that current models perform poorly, especially on more complex policy reasoning tasks.
AI.motivation: Most deployed guardrails need to enforce custom, application-specific safety policies rather than fixed, pre-coded taxonomies of risk. Existing evaluations do not adequately measure how well models can interpret and apply arbitrary natural-language policies given in context, especially when rules interact or when entire policy frameworks are novel. The paper aims to fill this gap with a systematic benchmark.
AI.method: The authors propose the paradigm of in-context policy guardrailing, where a model receives both a user–assistant conversation and a natural-language policy specification, and must identify which rules are violated. They construct SafePyramid: 1,000 multi-turn conversations across 10 domains paired with 3,000 application-specific policies containing 61,699 distinct rules. The benchmark is structured into three levels: L0 for single-rule understanding, L1 for reasoning over dependencies among rules, and L2 for adapting to entirely new policy frameworks. They use a multi-stage pipeline to author, review, and validate conversations, policies, and rule–violation labels. They then evaluate 10 state-of-the-art LLMs and 5 configurable guardrail systems on the benchmark.
