AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows that all tested systems struggle with in-context policy guardrailing. Even the strongest model evaluated, GPT-5.5, perfectly identifies the full set of violated rules in only 54.0% of L0 cases, 35.3% of L1 cases, and 12.9% of L2 cases. Performance drops sharply as reasoning over rule dependencies and adaptation to new policy frameworks are required.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that current guardrail approaches are far from reliably enforcing arbitrary, in-context safety policies. The poor performance, especially on higher difficulty levels, underscores the need for more capable methods that can execute complex policies, handle dependencies among rules, and generalize to unfamiliar policy frameworks. SafePyramid is proposed as a challenging, standardized benchmark to drive and measure progress in this direction.

title: SrDetection: A Self-Referential Framework for Data Leakage Detection in Code Large Language Models
summary: Evaluating code large language models (Code LLMs) requires reliable detection of data leakage, where benchmark performance is artificially inflated by exposure to benchmark data during pre-training. Existing approaches either assume access to proprietary training corpora, rely on brittle heuristics such as timestamp filtering, or use external reference sets with manually tuned, non-generalizable thresholds. To address these limitations, we introduce \textbf{SrDetection}, a unified \textbf{s}elf-\textbf{r}eferential leakage detection framework for both gray-box (access to model logits) and black-box (access to model outputs) settings. SrDetection generates semantically equivalent variants of a benchmark sample and detects leakage by contrasting the model's behavior on the original versus its variants, flagging cases where the original is disproportionately easier for the model. We further design a controlled leakage detection testbed and evaluate SrDetection in this environment. Across different models and training stages, SrDetection improves average F1 by 21.52 points in the gray-box setting and 14.46 points in the black-box setting over strong baselines, demonstrating robust, threshold-independent leakage detection. Finally, a gray-box study of 15 widely used Code LLMs on four popular benchmarks reveals benchmark-specific leakage patterns beyond prior overlap-based analyses\footnote{\footnotesize Source code and data are available at https://github.com/SMinL/SrDetectionCode

AI.tldr: This paper proposes SrDetection, a framework to detect data leakage in code large language model benchmarks without requiring access to training data, improving F1 over baselines and revealing benchmark-specific leakage patterns.
AI.motivation: Benchmark results for code LLMs can be misleading when models have seen benchmark examples during pre-training. Current leakage detection methods are limited by needing access to proprietary training corpora, using unreliable heuristics like timestamp filters, or relying on manually tuned thresholds on external reference datasets. A more robust, unified, and threshold-independent method is needed, especially for both gray-box and black-box scenarios.
AI.method: The authors introduce SrDetection, a self-referential leakage detection framework that works in both gray-box (with logits) and black-box (with only outputs) settings. The core idea is to generate semantically equivalent variants of each benchmark sample and compare the model’s performance on the original sample versus its variants. If the original is disproportionately easier for the model than its variants, the sample is flagged as likely leaked. They also build a controlled leakage detection testbed to systematically evaluate SrDetection across models and training stages.
AI.result: In controlled experiments, SrDetection significantly improves leakage detection performance compared to strong baselines: an average F1 gain of 21.52 points in gray-box settings and 14.46 points in black-box settings. The framework is robust and does not depend on manually chosen thresholds. Applying SrDetection in a gray-box setting to 15 widely used Code LLMs across four popular benchmarks uncovers nuanced, benchmark-specific leakage patterns that go beyond what simple overlap-based analyses can detect.
AI.conclusion: SrDetection provides a unified, self-referential framework for detecting data leakage in code LLM benchmarks without access to training data or fragile heuristics. It works effectively in both gray- and black-box settings, yields substantially better F1 scores than existing methods, and reveals non-trivial leakage patterns in widely used models and benchmarks, suggesting that current evaluation practices may overestimate true generalization performance.

title: A causal modeling perspective on decision theory
summary: Decision theory provides a formal framework for how agents should make choices under uncertainty, drawing on ideas from philosophy, probability, and causality. Despite significant progress, the field still lacks a unified modeling language, and key concepts - such as the distinction between subjective and objective elements, or what it means for a decision theory to perform well - are often left implicit. This can make it difficult to evaluate and compare competing theories, particularly in controversial cases. In this paper, we address these issues by introducing a formal framework for decision theory based on nonparametric structural equation models (NPSEMs), a well-established tool in causal inference. NPSEMs provide a unified foundation for representing agents, counterfactuals, and causal relationships, allowing for unambiguous definitions of EDT and CDT. Building on this foundation, we propose a novel decision theory - personal decision theory - which instructs agents to maximize a subjective model of their own counterfactual utility. We introduce a formal performance metric based on hypothetical interventions that enforce a given decision theory across a population - such as might be achieved through education or policy -- and show that, under certain assumptions, personal decision theory is optimal with respect to this metric. Throughout, we use the smoking lesion problem as a running example and conclude with a formal analysis of Newcomb's problem. Our aim is to provide decision theory with a clearer modeling language and firmer evaluative ground, thereby enabling more rigorous comparisons and facilitating conceptual progress in the field.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a unified causal-model-based framework for decision theory and proposes a new theory, personal decision theory, which is shown to be optimal under a formal performance metric.
AI.motivation: Decision theory lacks a unified modeling language and leaves many core concepts implicit, which hinders clear evaluation and comparison of existing theories like evidential decision theory (EDT) and causal decision theory (CDT), especially in controversial cases (e.g., smoking lesion, Newcomb’s problem). The authors want a framework that cleanly separates subjective and objective components, makes counterfactuals precise, and allows rigorous performance evaluation of decision rules.
AI.method: They adopt nonparametric structural equation models (NPSEMs) from causal inference as the core modeling language for agents, counterfactuals, and causal relationships. Within this causal framework, they give precise formulations of EDT and CDT. They then define a new rule, personal decision theory, where agents maximize expected utility according to a subjective model of their own counterfactual utility. Finally, they define a performance metric based on hypothetical interventions that impose a given decision theory on a population (as via education or policy) and analyze which theory scores best under this metric.
AI.result: Using NPSEMs, they obtain unambiguous formalizations of standard decision theories and define personal decision theory within the same language. Under certain assumptions, analyzing the hypothetical-intervention-based performance metric shows that personal decision theory yields better outcomes than the alternatives and is optimal with respect to that metric. They demonstrate the framework and comparative results on canonical puzzles such as the smoking lesion problem and Newcomb’s problem.
AI.conclusion: NPSEMs can serve as a unified and precise language for decision theory, clarifying the role of causality, counterfactuals, and subjective modeling. Within this framework, personal decision theory emerges as an optimal decision rule under a natural intervention-based performance criterion. This approach promises clearer definitions, more rigorous comparisons among decision theories, and improved conceptual progress in understanding rational choice under uncertainty.

title: Neural Procedural Memory: Empowering LLM Agents with Implicit Activation Steering
summary: While Large Language Models (LLMs) excel as static solvers, transforming them into autonomous agents remains challenging. This transition requires continuous environmental interaction, yet current agents lack the necessary persistent procedural memory. Existing approaches predominantly employ Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) to inject explicit textual guidelines into model contexts. However, relying solely on symbolic instructions can introduce a text-action disconnect, frequently failing to activate the internal representations necessary for correct task execution. To address this, the paper introduces Neural Procedural Memory (NPM), a training-free framework that represents agent memory through implicit activation steering rather than explicit instructions. By distilling procedural skills from historical contrastive experiences into steering vectors in the activation space, NPM directly activates the task-relevant neural mechanisms to guide task execution. Evaluations across four agent benchmarks show that NPM performs comparably to baselines using explicit textual instructions. Furthermore, the results show that combining implicit steering with explicit workflows provides complementary advantages, leading to more robust task execution. Representational analyses indicate that these steering vectors encode consistent task logic, forming organized structures within the activation space. These findings suggest that implicit activation steering provides a promising approach for managing agent memory.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Neural Procedural Memory (NPM), a training-free method that stores and recalls agent skills via activation steering vectors instead of textual instructions, improving robustness of LLM-based agents.
AI.motivation: LLMs are good at single-turn problem solving but struggle as autonomous agents that must interact over time and remember procedures. Existing memory mechanisms rely mainly on Retrieval-Augmented Generation with explicit textual instructions, which can cause a mismatch between instructions and the model’s actual internal behaviors, leading to unreliable task execution. A more direct way to invoke the model’s latent skills for agents is needed.
AI.method: The authors introduce Neural Procedural Memory (NPM), a training-free framework that encodes procedural skills as steering vectors in the model’s activation space. They collect historical contrastive experience traces (successful vs. unsuccessful or alternative behaviors) and distill the differences in internal activations into steering vectors. During inference, these vectors are injected to steer the model’s hidden states, directly activating task-relevant neural mechanisms instead of relying on explicit text instructions. They compare pure implicit steering, pure explicit textual workflows, and combinations of both.
AI.result: Across four agent benchmarks, NPM alone achieves performance on par with baselines that use explicit textual instructions with RAG. When NPM is combined with explicit workflows, performance becomes more robust and often better than either alone. Analysis of the learned steering vectors shows they form organized structures in activation space and consistently encode task logic, suggesting that the implicit memory is systematic rather than ad hoc.
AI.conclusion: Neural Procedural Memory offers a promising, training-free way to give LLM-based agents persistent procedural memory by steering internal activations instead of adding more text instructions. Implicit activation steering can match or complement traditional RAG-style explicit guidance, and combining both yields more reliable agent behavior. The structured nature of the steering vectors indicates that agent skills can be stored and managed as organized neural memories in activation space.

title: HippoSpark: An On-Demand Experience System for LLM Reasoning
summary: Distilling historical trajectories into reusable experience to enhance future problem-solving has become a focal point of recent LLM research. However, existing methods predominantly operate at the task level, leveraging general summaries or rules under the assumption that analogous tasks share universal solution patterns. This approach often fails in complex reasoning, which typically falters at local bottlenecks that require precise, state-specific guidance rather than broad heuristics. We introduce HippoSpark, a state-level experience system that performs on-demand retrieval tailored to the immediate needs of the current reasoning state. Across mathematical, scientific, and programming benchmarks, HippoSpark consistently outperforms both standard prompting and task-level experience baselines. Our findings reveal that the most effective experience systems are those that provide actionable guidance at critical bottlenecks rather than serving as generic task-level context. Our code is available at https://github.com/DanlingMeng/HippoSpark.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes HippoSpark, a system that retrieves and reuses past reasoning experiences at the fine-grained state level instead of the whole-task level, leading to better performance of LLMs on complex reasoning tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing experience-based methods for LLMs mostly summarize prior tasks into global rules or task-level hints. These approaches assume similar tasks can share universal solution patterns, but they often fail on complex reasoning problems where errors arise from local bottlenecks that require precise, context-specific help. The authors aim to design an experience system that targets these local reasoning states directly.
AI.method: The authors design HippoSpark, a state-level experience system. It logs and structures historical reasoning trajectories and, during new problem solving, performs on-demand retrieval of experience fragments that closely match the current reasoning state. Instead of providing broad heuristics, the retrieved experiences offer concrete, state-specific guidance at the point where the model is currently stuck or making a decision.
AI.result: Across benchmarks in mathematics, science, and programming, HippoSpark achieves consistent performance gains compared to standard prompting and to prior task-level experience systems. This demonstrates that finer-grained, state-aware retrieval of past reasoning is more effective than generic task-level summaries for improving complex reasoning.
AI.conclusion: Actionable, state-specific guidance at critical reasoning bottlenecks is more valuable than generic, task-level context when reusing experience in LLMs. HippoSpark’s state-level, on-demand retrieval framework provides a more effective way to distill and apply historical trajectories, leading to improved performance on complex reasoning tasks.

title: Revealing the Technology Development of Natural Language Processing: A Scientific Entity-Centric Perspective
summary: Most studies on technology development have been conducted from a thematic perspective, but the topics are coarse-grained and insufficient to accurately represent technology. The development of automatic entity recognition techniques makes it possible to extract technology-related entities on a large scale. Thus, we perform a more accurate analysis of technology development from an entity-centric perspective. To begin with, we extract technology-related entities such as methods, datasets, metrics, and tools in articles on Natural Language Processing (NLP), and we apply a semi-automatic approach to normalize the entities. Subsequently, we calculate the z-scores of entities based on their co-occurrence networks to measure their impact. We then analyze the development trends of new technologies in the NLP domain since the beginning of the 21st century. The findings of this paper include three aspects: Firstly, the continued increase in the average number of entities per paper implies a growing burden on researchers to acquire relevant technical background knowledge. However, the emergence of pre-trained language models has injected new vitality into the technological innovation of the NLP domain. Secondly, Methods dominate among the 179 high-impact entities. An analysis of the z-score trend about the top 10 entities reveals that pre-trained language models, exemplified by BERT and Transformer, have become mainstream in recent years. Unlike the trend of the other eight method entities, the impact of Wikipedia dataset and BLEU metric has continued to rise in the long term. Thirdly, in recent years, there has been a remarkable surge in popularity for new high-impact technologies than ever before, and their acceptance by researchers has accelerated at an unprecedented speed. Our study provides a new perspective on analyzing technology development in a specific domain.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an entity-centric, fine-grained analysis of technology development in NLP using automatically extracted and normalized technology-related entities, revealing rising complexity, dominance of method entities (especially pre-trained models like BERT and Transformer), and increasingly rapid adoption of new high-impact technologies.
AI.motivation: Existing studies of technology development are mostly thematic and use coarse-grained topics, which do not accurately represent specific technologies. With the advent of automatic entity recognition, it is now feasible to extract technology-related entities (e.g., methods, datasets, metrics, tools) at scale. The authors want a more precise, data-driven way to characterize and track technological evolution within a domain, here NLP.
AI.method: 1) Collect NLP research articles. 2) Automatically extract technology-related entities (methods, datasets, metrics, tools) using entity recognition, then semi-automatically normalize them. 3) Build co-occurrence networks of entities and compute each entity’s z-score as an impact measure. 4) Identify high-impact entities and analyze their temporal trends from the early 2000s onward, focusing on new technologies and their adoption dynamics.
AI.result: The average number of technology entities per paper has steadily increased, indicating rising complexity and background knowledge load for researchers. Among 179 high-impact entities, methods are the dominant type. Trend analysis of top entities shows that pre-trained language models (e.g., BERT, Transformer) have recently become mainstream technologies in NLP. In contrast to other methods, the Wikipedia dataset and BLEU metric exhibit long-term, steadily increasing impact. Additionally, the emergence and acceptance of new high-impact technologies are now faster and more intense than before.
AI.conclusion: An entity-centric, fine-grained approach based on extracted and normalized technology-related entities, coupled with network-based impact (z-score) analysis, offers a powerful lens for understanding technology development within a domain. In NLP, this reveals a growing technical complexity, dominance of methods (especially pre-trained language models), persistent importance of certain datasets/metrics, and an accelerating pace of technological diffusion. The framework can be applied to other domains to study their technological evolution.

title: SAGA: Scene-Aware, Goal-Evolving Agents for Long-Horizon CivRealm Strategy Planning
summary: Long-horizon strategic planning in complex strategy games demands concurrent reasoning across multiple decision domains under imperfect information and sparse reward. Existing LLM-based agents suffer from three systematic failures: scene blindness from raw tile coordinates, context overflow and domain coupling from monolithic state dumps, and shallow cross-game learning that treats each episode in isolation. We present SAGA, an LLM multi-agent framework with three mechanisms each directly targeting one class of failure: (i) a Map-Semantic Scene Graph that encodes typed spatial relations among game entities into per-unit natural-language context, resolving spatial blindness without global token inflation; (ii) a Tool-Augmented Planner that pulls fine-grained domain state on demand and dispatches per-domain directives to dedicated specialist controllers, eliminating context overflow, domain coupling, and mechanical constraint violations; and (iii) a Dual-Horizon Feedback Loop that combines periodic within-game goal generation with structured cross-game causal post-mortem, enabling principled strategic evolution without manual reward engineering. Evaluated on FreeCiv, SAGA attains the highest mean civilization score -- the environment's sole sparse objective reward -- with lower variance than the two strongest baselines, and is the only method that significantly surpasses every baseline on infrastructure construction, the resource axis most readily sacrificed under multi-objective conflict. It outscores the two strongest baselines in most head-to-head games while cutting output tokens (the dominant decoding cost) by 27%. Equipped with the cross-game evolution module, SAGA reaches the highest end-of-chain score across five successive episodes. Ablation studies confirm that each architectural component contributes independently to this advantage.

AI.tldr: SAGA is an LLM-based multi-agent framework that improves long-horizon planning in complex strategy games via structured scene representation, modular tool-based planning, and cross-game learning, achieving higher scores and efficiency than baselines in FreeCiv.
AI.motivation: To address limitations of existing LLM game agents that struggle with spatial reasoning, overlong and entangled context, and lack of learning across episodes in long-horizon, sparse-reward strategy games.
AI.method: Introduce SAGA, composed of: (i) a Map-Semantic Scene Graph turning raw spatial data into per-unit natural-language context; (ii) a Tool-Augmented Planner that queries domain tools and delegates to specialist controllers to avoid context overflow and constraint violations; and (iii) a Dual-Horizon Feedback Loop that mixes in-game goal setting with structured cross-game post-mortem analysis for strategy evolution.
AI.result: On the FreeCiv benchmark, SAGA achieves the highest mean civilization score with lower variance than strong baselines, uniquely improves infrastructure building, wins most head-to-head matches, reduces LLM output tokens by 27%, and with cross-game evolution reaches the best end-of-chain score over five episodes; ablations show each component helps.
AI.conclusion: Structured scene encoding, modular tool-augmented planning, and explicit cross-game feedback together substantially improve the effectiveness, robustness, and efficiency of LLM agents in complex strategy games compared to prior monolithic approaches.

title: MATCH: Modulating Attention via In-Context Retrieval for Long-Context Transformers
summary: The quadratic computational cost of traditional attention mechanisms poses a major bottleneck to the scalability and practical deployment of large language models (LLMs), particularly in long-context scenarios. To improve efficiency, existing approaches often enforce rigid structural constraints such as local attention windows. However, these strategies typically lead to substantial performance degradation on tasks requiring precise long-range recall. In this work, we propose MATCH, a scalable and efficient framework that augments sparsified attention mechanisms with dynamically integrated in-context information through an efficient retrieval system. Empirical results show that MATCH significantly improves the performance of sparse-attention models on both synthetic and real-world natural-language tasks. These findings highlight the versatility of MATCH as a general approach for enhancing in-context retrieval capabilities while maintaining the efficiency benefits of sparse attention architectures.

AI.tldr: MATCH is a framework that makes sparse attention models more efficient and accurate on long-context tasks by combining them with a fast retrieval system that injects relevant in-context information.
AI.motivation: Standard attention in large language models scales quadratically with sequence length, making long-context processing computationally expensive and limiting scalability and deployment. Existing efficiency methods like local or fixed sparse patterns reduce cost but hurt performance on tasks that need accurate long-range recall. The paper aims to keep the efficiency of sparse attention while recovering or improving performance on long-range reasoning and retrieval.
AI.method: Introduce MATCH, a framework that augments sparse attention architectures with an efficient retrieval module. Instead of relying only on fixed sparse patterns (e.g., local windows), MATCH dynamically fetches and injects relevant in-context information during processing. This blends sparsified attention with retrieval-based augmentation to better capture long-range dependencies without incurring full quadratic attention cost.
AI.result: Experiments on both synthetic benchmarks and real-world natural language tasks demonstrate that MATCH substantially boosts the performance of sparse-attention models, especially on tasks that rely on long-context recall, compared with using sparse attention alone.
AI.conclusion: MATCH provides a general and scalable way to enhance the in-context retrieval and long-range reasoning capabilities of sparse-attention language models while preserving their computational efficiency advantages. It shows that coupling sparse attention with dynamic retrieval can mitigate the usual accuracy–efficiency trade-off in long-context LLMs.

title: First-Order Temporal Logic Tensor Networks
summary: Most of the existing neuro-symbolic AI methods focus on the scenario of static knowledge where objects do not change according to a temporal dimension. Temporal neuro-symbolic works are still under explored and are mainly developed for time-interval logic or propositional linear temporal logic. There is a lack of models studying linear temporal logics with predicates that deal with objects whose properties and relations change through the time. We present First-Order Temporal Logic Tensor Networks (FOT-LTN) that is an extension of Logic Tensor Networks (LTN) that fills this gap by considering a linear-temporal dimension. In particular, FOT-LTN joins the syntax of First-Order Linear Temporal Logic with the fuzzy (and real-valued) semantics of LTN obtaining a framework that supports both temporal operators and quantifiers and is totally differentiable. A first evaluation regards a temporal knowledge graph completion task on two synthetic datasets showing better performance of FOT-LTN with respect to dedicated (purely neural) methods.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces First-Order Temporal Logic Tensor Networks (FOT-LTN), a differentiable neuro-symbolic framework that extends Logic Tensor Networks with first-order linear temporal logic to model objects whose properties and relations change over time, and shows improved performance over neural baselines on temporal knowledge graph completion.
AI.motivation: Existing neuro-symbolic AI methods largely assume static knowledge and do not effectively handle temporal evolution of objects and relations. Current temporal neuro-symbolic approaches are limited to propositional temporal logics or time-interval formalisms, lacking support for first-order predicates that vary over time. There is a need for a differentiable framework that combines temporal reasoning, quantification, and learnable representations to better model dynamic relational domains, such as temporal knowledge graphs.
AI.method: The authors extend Logic Tensor Networks (LTN) by incorporating the syntax and operators of First-Order Linear Temporal Logic (FOLTL). They define fuzzy, real-valued semantics for temporal operators (such as next, always, eventually, until) alongside first-order quantifiers over objects, ensuring the resulting system is fully differentiable and trainable with gradient-based methods. This framework, called FOT-LTN, can be applied to temporal reasoning tasks such as temporal knowledge graph completion, where facts and relations are indexed by time steps.
AI.result: In experiments on two synthetic temporal knowledge graph datasets, FOT-LTN outperforms specialized purely neural approaches tailored for temporal KG completion. The results indicate that explicitly modeling first-order temporal logic structure with fuzzy semantics yields better predictive performance than using only neural sequence or embedding models without symbolic temporal structure.
AI.conclusion: FOT-LTN successfully bridges first-order linear temporal logic with differentiable neuro-symbolic learning, enabling reasoning over objects and relations that evolve over time. The empirical results on synthetic temporal knowledge graph tasks demonstrate that incorporating explicit temporal logical structure can improve performance over purely neural baselines, suggesting that the framework is a promising foundation for more advanced temporal neuro-symbolic systems and applications involving dynamic relational data.

title: Smooth Scaling Laws Hide Stepwise Token Learning
summary: Language model loss follows remarkably regular scaling laws over model and data size, yet it remains unclear why the aggregate loss should exhibit a power-law form. Existing explanations often attribute this regularity to a heavy-tailed spectrum of pattern difficulty in natural language, but this view has not been directly validated at token-level granularity in large-scale real-data training. We present a token-level framework that decomposes scaling laws into localized learning events of individual contextualized tokens. By fitting token loss trajectories with sigmoids, we show that token learning is concentrated in localized transitions, giving rise to a learning-time spectrum that dominates the scaling-law shape. Across more than one hundred pre-training runs on large and diverse real-language corpora with modern LLM architectures, scaling up to 6B parameters and 300B training tokens, the measured learning-time spectrum quantitatively reconstructs the validation loss derivative along the training-step $T$, data-scale $D$, and model-scale $M$ axes. We further show that the same signal is actionable: by reshaping the training distribution according to when tokens become learnable, we alter the optimization trajectory and achieve 11\% faster validation-loss reduction. These results provide direct empirical evidence that scaling laws are governed primarily by the distribution of token-level learning times, and that this distribution can be used not only to explain scaling behavior but also to improve training performance.

AI.tldr: The paper explains why language model loss follows power-law scaling by analyzing token-level learning dynamics and shows this understanding can speed up training.
AI.motivation: Although language models empirically obey smooth power-law scaling laws over data, model size, and compute, the underlying cause of these laws is poorly understood. Prior hypotheses appeal to a heavy-tailed distribution of pattern difficulty in language, but they lack direct validation at the level of individual tokens in large-scale, real-world training. A deeper mechanistic and empirical understanding is needed both to explain where scaling laws come from and to exploit them to improve training efficiency.
AI.method: The authors introduce a token-level analytical framework that tracks the loss trajectory of each contextualized token during pre-training. They fit these per-token loss curves with sigmoids to identify when each token undergoes a sharp transition from unlearned to learned, defining a ‘learning time’ for each token. Aggregating these learning times yields a learning-time spectrum. They then run over one hundred large-scale LLM pre-training experiments (up to 6B parameters and 300B tokens) and compare the empirically measured learning-time spectrum with observed scaling laws along training steps (T), data size (D), and model size (M). Finally, they modify the training data schedule by up-weighting tokens near their learnable time and measure the impact on optimization speed.
AI.result: They find that individual tokens exhibit localized, sigmoidal learning transitions rather than gradual, diffuse learning. The distribution of these token-level learning times forms a spectrum that effectively predicts and quantitatively reconstructs the derivative of the validation loss with respect to training step, data scale, and model scale. This indicates that the observed power-law scaling of aggregate loss is largely determined by the shape of the learning-time spectrum. Moreover, by reshaping the training distribution to present tokens closer to when they become learnable, they accelerate optimization, achieving an 11% faster reduction in validation loss compared with standard training.
AI.conclusion: Scaling laws for language model loss are primarily a consequence of the distribution of token-level learning times: a spectrum of when different contextualized tokens become learnable as model capacity and data increase. This spectrum not only reproduces the observed power-law scaling behavior but also provides a practical signal for improving training efficiency. By scheduling data according to token learning times, one can steer the optimization trajectory to reach a given validation loss in fewer steps. The work thus connects macro-level scaling laws with micro-level token learning dynamics and demonstrates actionable benefits from this understanding.

title: Exploration and Online Transfer with Behavioral Foundation Models
summary: Zero-shot Transfer in Reinforcement Learning (RL) aims to train an agent that can generate optimal policies for any reward function, without additional learning at transfer time, while training only on reward-free trajectories. For their generality over tasks, such models are sometimes called ``Behavioral Foundation Models'' (BFMs). While they have shown strong performances and improvements in recent years, the current framework and algorithms still assume that, during the transfer phase, the agent is informed offline about the reward (the task to solve) through a dataset of state-reward pairs, which it uses to pick the best policy to deploy. However, in practice if the reward is a black-box (e.g. direct user feedback), it is not possible to generate such a dataset: it is necessary to observe the reward through interactions with the environment. In other words, the current framework of offline transfer is not aligned with the traditional RL setting of online learning through trial-and-error, which requires exploration in order to find rewards. This paper proposes to tackle this new online transfer in zero-shot RL, with the key insight that the BFM itself can be used to generate exploration policies. We show that it is possible to frame this online learning problem in terms of a bandit-like exploration-exploitation problem. More precisely, at each step the bandit algorithm recommends a policy, the BFM executes it in the environment, which yields a reward and a new state; we repeat the process until we converge to the optimal policy. In the popular context of linear reward approximation, we derive a formulation inspired by Upper Confidence Bound and show that exploration can be achieved through the minimization of the eigenvalues of an uncertainty matrix. We evaluate qualitatively and quantitatively our framework on a simple environment to validate the concept of our method.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces an online version of zero-shot transfer in reinforcement learning, where a pre-trained behavioral foundation model (BFM) is used not just for policy deployment but also to drive exploration under unknown, black-box rewards, formulating the problem as a bandit-style exploration–exploitation process.
AI.motivation: Existing zero-shot transfer RL methods (BFMs) assume that, at transfer time, the reward function is known offline through a dataset of state–reward pairs, enabling the agent to directly select an optimal policy without further interaction. This is unrealistic in settings where rewards are black-box (e.g., user feedback) and can only be observed via interaction. Thus, there is a gap between current offline transfer frameworks and the standard online RL paradigm where the agent must explore to discover rewards.
AI.method: The authors propose an online transfer framework for zero-shot RL where the BFM is repeatedly queried to execute different policies while interacting with the environment. They cast the problem as a bandit-like exploration–exploitation loop: at each step, a bandit algorithm selects a policy; the BFM runs that policy in the environment, yielding a new state and reward; the bandit algorithm updates its beliefs and recommends the next policy. Under a linear reward approximation assumption, they design an algorithm inspired by Upper Confidence Bound (UCB), where exploration is guided by minimizing eigenvalues of an uncertainty matrix associated with the reward model.
AI.result: They validate the proposed framework conceptually and empirically on a simple environment, presenting both qualitative and quantitative evidence that the bandit-style approach with BFM-driven exploration can identify good policies and supports effective online transfer under black-box rewards.
AI.conclusion: It is feasible to perform online zero-shot transfer in RL by using a behavioral foundation model as a source of exploratory policies and framing the transfer as a bandit exploration–exploitation problem. In settings with linear reward approximation, UCB-inspired strategies based on uncertainty matrix eigenvalues can effectively drive exploration. The initial experiments on a simple environment support the viability of the approach and the overall framework.

title: Exploring Motivations for Algorithm Mention in the Domain of Natural Language Processing: A Deep Learning Approach
summary: With the rise of data-intensive science, algorithms have become central to scientific research. In academic papers, algorithms are mentioned for different purposes, such as describing, using, comparing, or improving methods for specific research tasks. Identifying these purposes can reveal relationships among algorithms and help assess their roles and value. Taking natural language processing (NLP) as an example, this study proposes a sentence-level framework for identifying, analyzing, and tracing the evolution of motivations for mentioning algorithms. We first identify algorithm entities and algorithm-related sentences from full-text papers through manual annotation and machine learning. We then classify mention motivations using pretrained models and data augmentation, and analyze their distribution and temporal evolution. The results show that deep learning models trained with augmented data outperform traditional machine learning models in motivation classification. In NLP papers, more than half of algorithm-related sentences express direct use, whereas improvement is the least frequent motivation. The diversity of motivations has increased over time. For specific algorithm categories, grammar-based algorithms are more often mentioned for description, while machine learning algorithms are more often mentioned for use. Over time, use motivations have gradually replaced description motivations across different algorithms, and the number of motivation types associated with individual algorithms has declined significantly. This study reveals how authors mention algorithm entities in academic writing and provides a basis for future research on algorithm relationship identification and algorithm impact evaluation.

AI.tldr: The paper builds a framework to automatically classify why algorithms are mentioned in NLP papers (e.g., use, description, comparison, improvement), and analyzes how these motivations change over time and across algorithm types.
AI.motivation: In modern data-intensive science, algorithms are central, but academic papers mention them for diverse reasons (describing, using, comparing, improving). These different motivations hint at relationships among algorithms and their roles in research, yet they are rarely analyzed systematically. The authors aim to automatically identify these motivations to better understand algorithm usage patterns and their evolution in the NLP field.
AI.method: The authors design a sentence-level analysis pipeline. They manually annotate full-text NLP papers to identify algorithm entities and algorithm-related sentences, then train machine learning models to automatically detect them. Next, they classify each algorithm-related sentence by its “mention motivation” using pretrained language models combined with data augmentation. They compare deep learning models with traditional machine learning approaches and then perform distributional and temporal analyses of motivation types across time and algorithm categories.
AI.result: Deep learning models, especially when trained on augmented data, outperform traditional machine learning models in classifying mention motivations. Empirically, more than half of algorithm-related sentences in NLP papers indicate direct use of algorithms, while improvement is the least common motivation. The variety of motivations has increased over time. Grammar-based algorithms are mainly mentioned for description, whereas machine learning algorithms are more often mentioned for use. Over the years, motivations related to use have increasingly replaced those related to description, and individual algorithms have become associated with fewer distinct motivation types.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates an effective framework for automatically identifying and analyzing why algorithms are mentioned in NLP research papers. It reveals clear patterns and temporal shifts in motivations—particularly a move from describing algorithms to using them—and systematic differences across algorithm types. This provides a foundation for future work on mapping relationships among algorithms and evaluating their scientific impact based on how they are referenced in scholarly writing.

title: Be Faithful When Response: Returning Fluent and Grounded Answers for Vision-Language Models Reinforcement Learning
summary: Reinforcement Learning (RL) is an important paradigm for improving the reasoning capabilities of Vision-Language Models (VLMs). However, directly applying RL to rollout multimodal reasoning can lead to instability, due to the exploitation of language priors, the neglect of visual evidence, and the generation of reasoning traces that are fluent yet not visually grounded. The question arises: Can initially steer the policy toward visually faithful reasoning regime before applying reinforcement learning? To this end, we propose a Faithful Warm-Start (FWS) strategy that first curates samples with explicit vision-language causal relationships from six general VQA benchmarks to construct the FaithfulQA dataset, where each of the image-question pairs gains a certain degree of visual observations, question requirements, commonsense knowledge, domain knowledge, and the final answer. Subsequently, a VLM-based judge is employed to further purify the dataset, ensuring strong causal consistency and visual faithfulness. This warm-start stage equips the model with the capability to understand causally grounded vision-language patterns before subsequent RL optimization under sparse answer-level rewards. Experimental results show that such faithful supervision improves answer accuracy, stabilizes RL training, and reduces visually unsupported reasoning.

AI.tldr: They propose a warm-start strategy using a specially filtered, visually grounded VQA dataset to stabilize and improve reinforcement learning for vision-language reasoning.
AI.motivation: Directly applying reinforcement learning to vision-language models for multimodal reasoning can be unstable. Models exploit language priors, ignore visual evidence, and produce fluent but visually ungrounded reasoning traces. There is a need to guide the policy toward visually faithful reasoning before RL under sparse answer-level rewards.
AI.method: 1) Curate a FaithfulQA dataset from six general VQA benchmarks, selecting image-question pairs that exhibit explicit vision-language causal relationships and providing visual observations, question requirements, commonsense knowledge, domain knowledge, and final answers. 2) Use a VLM-based judge to further filter and purify this dataset, enforcing strong causal consistency and visual faithfulness. 3) Use this purified dataset in a Faithful Warm-Start (FWS) training stage to pretrain the model on causally grounded patterns, then apply RL for answer-level optimization.
AI.result: The warm-started models achieve higher answer accuracy, more stable RL training dynamics, and produce fewer reasoning traces that are not supported by the visual input.
AI.conclusion: Initializing VLMs with a faithfully curated, causally grounded vision-language dataset before reinforcement learning effectively steers the policy toward visually faithful reasoning, leading to more stable RL and better multimodal reasoning performance.

title: KbSD: Knowledge Boundary aware Self-Distillation for Behavioral Calibration in Agentic Search
summary: Agentic search equips large language models with dynamic retrieval abilities, but existing reinforcement learning methods remain limited by reward sparsity in knowledge boundary calibration -- deciding when to trust parametric memory, when to rely on retrieved evidence, and when to abstain. Binary rewards can penalize undesirable outcomes, but provide little guidance on the reasoning process required to make calibrated decisions across different knowledge states. To address this, we propose KbSD (Knowledge boundary Self-Distillation), a framework that tackles this limitation through dense token-level supervision, outcome-level sparse rewards, and quadrant-adaptive optimization. KbSD constructs a hint-augmented teacher, architecturally identical to the student, that receives explicit knowledge boundary signals -- including parametric certainty, retrieval quality, and ground-truth answers -- to generate calibrated reasoning demonstrations. This information-asymmetric self-distillation enables dense supervision without requiring a larger external model. To further account for the heterogeneous reasoning distributions across knowledge states, we introduce a quadrant-adaptive distillation objective: reverse KL for concentrated integration, forward KL for diverse refusal, and Pareto-optimal bidirectional KL for asymmetric quadrants requiring both precision and coverage. Experiments on multiple benchmarks show that KbSD consistently improves both task accuracy and hallucination mitigation over strong baselines, with the largest gains appearing in the challenging quadrants where sparse rewards are least informative.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes KbSD, a self-distillation framework that gives LLMs dense, token-level supervision and adaptive objectives so they can better decide when to trust their own knowledge, when to use retrieval, and when to abstain, thereby improving accuracy and reducing hallucinations in agentic search.
AI.motivation: Existing RL-based methods for agentic search suffer from sparse, outcome-level rewards, which do not provide fine-grained guidance for calibrating knowledge boundaries: deciding whether to trust parametric memory, retrieved evidence, or to abstain. This leads to hallucinations and poor calibration, especially in difficult knowledge states. The authors aim to provide richer supervision and better optimization tailored to different knowledge states.
AI.method: They introduce Knowledge boundary Self-Distillation (KbSD). A hint-augmented teacher model, architecturally identical to the student but given extra signals (parametric uncertainty, retrieval quality, ground truth), generates calibrated reasoning traces. The student learns from these traces via dense token-level distillation combined with sparse outcome rewards. They further design a quadrant-adaptive distillation objective, partitioning knowledge states into four quadrants and applying different KL divergences: reverse KL where a sharp, high-precision distribution is needed, forward KL where diverse refusal behavior is desired, and a bi-directional Pareto-optimal KL mixture in asymmetric regions requiring both precision and coverage.
AI.result: On multiple benchmarks, models trained with KbSD outperform strong baselines in both end-task accuracy and hallucination reduction. The largest improvements occur in difficult knowledge quadrants where standard sparse RL rewards are least effective, showing that dense, knowledge-state-aware distillation improves knowledge boundary calibration.
AI.conclusion: Dense, information-asymmetric self-distillation with knowledge-boundary hints and quadrant-adaptive KL objectives is an effective way to train agentic search models. It overcomes limitations of sparse RL rewards, leading to better-calibrated decisions on when to rely on parametric knowledge, retrieval, or abstention, thereby improving accuracy and mitigating hallucinations across diverse knowledge states.

title: AlgoSkill: Learning to Design Algorithms by Scheduling Human-Like Skills
summary: Designing an algorithm from a natural-language problem statement requires identifying the problem structure, reading constraints, choosing a suitable paradigm, checking correctness, and refining complexity. Existing large language model (LLM) methods often rely on direct generation or generic self-refinement, leaving these steps implicit. We propose AlgoSkill, which models algorithm design as sequential decision-making over a typed library of algorithmic skills, including abstraction, constraint analysis, state design, data-structure selection, proof checking, counterexample construction, and complexity refinement. A learned scheduler proposes skills from the current design state, while a Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) controller explores skill sequences using verification feedback from compilation, testing, stress testing, and complexity analysis. Experiments on competitive programming and combinatorial optimization benchmarks show that AlgoSkill improves over direct LLM generation, chain-of-thought prompting, self-refinement, and MCTS without typed skills. Ablations show that typed skills, verification-based repair, and search-based scheduling each contribute to performance. These results support treating automatic algorithm design as verification-guided skill scheduling rather than one-shot code generation.

AI.tldr: AlgoSkill models algorithm design as a sequence of explicit, typed skills guided by search and verification, outperforming direct LLM code generation and generic self-refinement on algorithmic benchmarks.
AI.motivation: LLMs struggle with algorithm design when they treat it as one-shot code generation or vague chain-of-thought reasoning, implicitly skipping key steps like constraint analysis, state design, and complexity refinement. The authors aim to make these steps explicit and structured so that LLMs can more reliably synthesize correct and efficient algorithms, especially for competitive programming and combinatorial optimization tasks.
AI.method: The paper introduces AlgoSkill, which represents algorithm design as sequential decision-making over a library of typed algorithmic skills (e.g., abstraction, constraint analysis, state design, data-structure selection, proof checking, counterexample construction, complexity refinement). A learned scheduler selects which skill to apply at each design state, and a Monte Carlo Tree Search controller explores sequences of skills. Verification feedback from compilation, unit tests, stress tests, and complexity analysis is used to guide search, refine partial designs, and repair errors.
AI.result: On competitive programming and combinatorial optimization benchmarks, AlgoSkill achieves higher solution rates and better quality algorithms compared to baselines including direct LLM generation, chain-of-thought prompting, self-refinement, and MCTS without typed skills. Ablation studies show that (1) explicit typed skills, (2) verification-based repair, and (3) search-based skill scheduling each provide significant performance gains.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly structuring algorithm design as verification-guided scheduling over typed skills yields more reliable and effective automatic algorithm synthesis than treating it as monolithic code generation. The results suggest that future systems for program and algorithm synthesis should leverage modular skills, systematic search, and rich verification feedback to orchestrate LLMs, rather than relying on single-shot or loosely guided generations.

title: ARKD: Adaptive Reinforcement Learning-Guided Bidirectional KL Divergence Distillation for Text Generation
summary: Knowledge distillation (KD) is a key technique for compressing Large Language Models (LLMs), yet methods relying on a single KL objective often fail to balance primary distribution fitting with long-tail probability modeling, limiting both generation quality and generalization. To address this, we analyze the complementary roles of forward and reverse KL divergence (FKL/RKL) in distribution alignment from theoretical and empirical perspectives. We then propose a reinforcement-learning-based adaptive KL-weighted distillation framework, in which a policy network dynamically assigns weights to FKL and RKL based on teacher-student distributional characteristics, guided by immediate reward signals to achieve dual alignment on principal and long-tail modes. Extensive experiments demonstrate consistent improvements across Rouge-L and BertScore metrics, surpassing greedy heuristics by 0.4-0.6 points and outperforming other baseline methods on diverse benchmarks.

AI.tldr: The paper improves knowledge distillation for LLMs by adaptively combining forward and reverse KL divergence via reinforcement learning to better capture both main and long-tail distributions, yielding better text generation metrics than baselines.
AI.motivation: Existing knowledge distillation for LLMs typically uses a single KL divergence objective, which struggles to simultaneously fit the primary (high-probability) token distribution and accurately model low-probability long-tail tokens, harming both generation quality and generalization. The paper aims to understand and remedy this imbalance by exploring the complementary strengths of forward and reverse KL divergences in aligning student with teacher distributions.
AI.method: The authors theoretically and empirically analyze how forward KL (mode-covering) and reverse KL (mode-seeking) behave in teacher-student distribution alignment. Building on this, they propose an adaptive distillation framework based on reinforcement learning: a policy network observes characteristics of the teacher and student distributions and outputs dynamic weights for combining forward and reverse KL losses. These weights are updated using immediate reward signals that reflect how well the student aligns with both principal and long-tail modes, effectively learning when to emphasize FKL versus RKL during training.
AI.result: Experiments on multiple benchmarks show that the adaptive KL-weighted distillation consistently outperforms fixed or greedy KL weighting strategies and other KD baselines. It achieves improvements of about 0.4–0.6 points on Rouge-L and BertScore, indicating better generation quality and alignment with the teacher across both frequent and rare outputs.
AI.conclusion: Balancing forward and reverse KL divergences is crucial for effective knowledge distillation of LLMs, as each emphasizes different aspects of the teacher distribution. A reinforcement-learning-based policy that adaptively weights FKL and RKL according to teacher-student characteristics leads to better dual alignment on both main and long-tail modes, resulting in improved text generation metrics and generalization over standard single-KL or static-weighting approaches.

title: ACPO: Agent-Chained Policy Optimization for Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning
summary: Cooperative tasks in Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL) require agents to collectively maximize a shared return. Under the Centralized Training with Decentralized Execution (CTDE) paradigm, policy gradients have remained difficult to compute directly. Prior methods largely follow two approaches: independent factorized updates with centralized critics, which lack general joint-improvement guarantees without value decomposition assumptions, or alternating best-response updates, which can converge to suboptimal Nash Equilibria. In this paper, we show the joint policy gradient admits an exact decentralized decomposition of per-agent terms, each formed from per-agent score functions and decentralized critics. Based on this decomposition, we develop Agent-Chained Policy Optimization (ACPO), where actors are trained independently, with their updates together constituting a single step on the joint policy gradient. Central to this result is a serialized view of the simultaneous joint decision in which agents commit actions one at a time, each conditioning on a belief over preceding actions. The belief acts as the coordination mechanism which ties the independent per-agent updates into a joint gradient step. We evaluate ACPO on Multi-Robot Warehouse, SMACv2, and MA-MuJoCo, where it outperforms strong baselines, with the gap widening as the number of agents grows.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a new MARL algorithm, Agent-Chained Policy Optimization (ACPO), that computes exact joint policy gradients via decentralized per-agent updates coordinated through beliefs over other agents’ actions, achieving strong performance in challenging cooperative benchmarks.
AI.motivation: In cooperative MARL with CTDE, directly computing and optimizing the true joint policy gradient is hard. Existing approaches either (1) use independent factorized updates with centralized critics, which require strong value-decomposition assumptions and offer no general guarantees of joint improvement, or (2) rely on alternating best-response schemes that may converge to suboptimal Nash equilibria. There is a need for a method that can both respect the true joint policy gradient and still train agents in a decentralized, scalable way without restrictive assumptions.
AI.method: The authors first derive a theoretical decomposition of the joint policy gradient into a sum of per-agent terms, each depending on that agent’s own score function and a decentralized critic. To make this work, they introduce a serialized perspective of the joint action selection: instead of acting simultaneously, agents are conceptually ordered, and each one chooses its action conditioned on a belief over the actions of previously ordered agents. This belief serves as the coordination mechanism. Building on this decomposition, they design Agent-Chained Policy Optimization (ACPO), where each agent updates its policy independently using its own term in the decomposed gradient. When these independent updates are applied together, they provably correspond to a single step along the exact joint policy gradient. The method is implemented under CTDE with appropriate critic architectures that remain decentralized at execution time.
AI.result: ACPO is empirically evaluated on three challenging cooperative MARL benchmarks: Multi-Robot Warehouse, SMACv2, and MA-MuJoCo. Across these environments, ACPO outperforms strong baseline algorithms representative of existing CTDE approaches. The performance advantage becomes more pronounced as the number of agents increases, suggesting that ACPO scales better with team size and coordination complexity than competing methods.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that the joint policy gradient in cooperative MARL can be exactly decomposed into per-agent terms compatible with decentralized training, provided agents coordinate through appropriately modeled beliefs over each other’s actions. Leveraging this insight, ACPO offers a principled way to train decentralized agents whose independent updates align with true joint policy improvement, avoiding both the limitations of value-decomposition-based methods and the suboptimality risks of alternating best responses. The strong empirical results indicate that this framework is particularly beneficial in large-scale multi-agent systems and provides a promising direction for scalable cooperative MARL under CTDE.

title: Clinical Reasoning Graphs: Structured Evaluation of LLM Diagnostic Reasoning Reveals Competence Without Consistency
comment: Spotlight Paper, Proceedings of the Workshop on Structured Data for Health at the 43rd International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML), Seoul, South Korea
summary: Modern large language models (LLMs) reach 60-70% diagnostic accuracy on complex clinical case benchmarks, but accuracy alone cannot distinguish stable clinically-grounded reasoning from pattern matching. We introduce clinical reasoning graphs, structured graph representations extracted from free-text LLM diagnostic traces using a domain-grounded ontology with 5 node types and 7 edge types. We apply this pipeline to 750 traces from five LLMs across 50 New England Journal of Medicine Clinicopathological Conference cases and three prompt conditions, and test whether diagnostic traces show stable structured reasoning patterns, or diagnostic schemas, for clinically similar cases. We operationalize this as higher graph similarity among clinically similar cases than among clinically dissimilar ones. Across 15 model-condition comparisons, within-cluster and between-cluster composite similarity are nearly equal, and no comparison survives multiple-testing correction; a component-level analysis finds any residual content signal far below schema scale. Graph similarity is also nearly identical for pairs of models that are both correct (0.488) and both incorrect (0.484), suggesting that graph structure captures a dimension not reflected in diagnostic accuracy. Structured reflection prompting increases explicit discriminating-feature analysis within traces (+33%) but does not increase cross-case consistency. These results show diagnostic competence without schema-scale reasoning consistency, and indicate that final-answer accuracy should be complemented by process-level evaluation. We release the ontology, extraction pipeline, validation protocol, and the extracted reasoning graphs and similarity artifacts as resources for structured evaluation of LLM clinical reasoning.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a graph-based framework to analyze whether LLMs use stable, clinically grounded reasoning schemas in diagnostic tasks, finding that their internal reasoning structures are inconsistent across similar cases despite reasonable accuracy.
AI.motivation: Existing evaluations of large language models in medicine focus mainly on diagnostic accuracy, which cannot distinguish genuine, schema-based clinical reasoning from shallow pattern matching. The authors want a process-level way to see if models apply stable diagnostic schemas across similar cases, which is crucial for clinical trust and safety.
AI.method: They define a domain-grounded ontology with 5 node types and 7 edge types to represent elements of clinical reasoning as graphs. They extract "clinical reasoning graphs" from free-text diagnostic traces generated by five different LLMs on 50 NEJM Clinicopathological Conference cases under three prompting conditions, giving 750 traces. They then compute similarity between graphs and compare within-cluster similarity for clinically similar cases to between-cluster similarity for dissimilar cases, including composite and component-level analyses, and compare graph similarity for correctly vs incorrectly diagnosed cases. They also test a structured reflection prompting condition to see if it improves schema consistency.
AI.result: Across 15 model–prompt condition comparisons, the similarity of reasoning graphs among clinically similar cases is about the same as among dissimilar cases; no comparison remains significant after correcting for multiple tests, and any remaining signal appears only at a small component level, not at whole-schema scale. Graph similarity is nearly identical for pairs of traces where both model outputs are correct versus both incorrect (0.488 vs 0.484). Structured reflection prompting leads to a 33% increase in explicit discussion of discriminating clinical features but does not improve cross-case consistency of graph structures.
AI.conclusion: LLMs can achieve solid diagnostic accuracy yet lack stable, schema-level consistency in their clinical reasoning across similar cases. Graph-structured analysis reveals a reasoning dimension that accuracy alone misses. Therefore, final-answer metrics should be complemented by process-level, structured evaluations. The authors release their ontology, extraction pipeline, validation protocol, and reasoning graph artifacts as resources to support such evaluations of LLM clinical reasoning.

title: SAT-RTS: A systematic framework for tactical knowledge extraction and visualization-based analysis in real-time strategy games
summary: Efficient tactical knowledge extraction and analysis in real-time strategy (RTS) games micromanagement are constrained by the high-dimensional coupled state-action sequential data and the black-box decision-making process. Current research rarely provides a hierarchical visualization-based attribution analysis from the perspective of data decoupling and abstraction. To facilitate interpretable tactical knowledge extraction and visualization-based analysis in RTS games, a systematic framework named state-action-tactic analysis pipeline (SAT-RTS) is proposed. To decipher the deep-seated drivers of critical decisions in RTS learning systems, this work integrates interpretable visualization with the automated extraction of latent tactical patterns from high-dimensional sequence data. By adapting a cluster-centric BK-tree algorithm and incorporating specialized distance metrics designed to quantify multi-aspect similarities, the proposed framework facilitates robust state-stream abstraction. Furthermore, a rule-based multi-label extraction method is developed to transform unstructured state-action sequences into discrete and interpretable tactical labels, effectively bridging the gap between raw behavioral data and high-level tactical insights. By holistically integrating these computational methods into a hierarchical visualization-based pipeline, the proposed framework effectively addresses the challenges of processing massive real-time data streams while providing fitness landscape visualizations and analytical insights to decipher deep-seated tactical drivers. Comprehensive experiments demonstrate that the proposed SAT-RTS significantly enhances the interpretability and efficiency of tactical analysis in complex RTS environments.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes SAT-RTS, a hierarchical visualization-based framework that extracts and analyzes interpretable tactical knowledge from high-dimensional state-action sequences in RTS game micromanagement.
AI.motivation: RTS game micromanagement generates massive, high-dimensional, tightly coupled state-action sequences, and existing methods treat decision-making as a black box, offering little interpretable, hierarchical, visualization-based attribution of tactics. There is a need to decouple and abstract these data to understand the deep drivers behind critical tactical decisions.
AI.method: The authors design SAT-RTS, a state-action-tactic analysis pipeline. They adapt a cluster-centric BK-tree algorithm with customized multi-aspect distance metrics to abstract continuous state streams into robust state clusters. They then apply a rule-based multi-label extraction procedure that maps unstructured state-action sequences into discrete, interpretable tactical labels. These components are integrated into a hierarchical visualization pipeline that includes fitness landscape visualizations and other visual analytics tools to attribute and interpret tactical behavior of RTS learning systems.
AI.result: Using comprehensive experiments in complex RTS environments, SAT-RTS is shown to efficiently process massive real-time data streams and to extract meaningful latent tactical patterns. The framework yields robust state abstractions, interpretable multi-label tactical descriptions, and visualizations that reveal the drivers of critical decisions, thereby improving both interpretability and the efficiency of tactical analysis compared to prior approaches.
AI.conclusion: The SAT-RTS framework enables interpretable, hierarchical, visualization-driven tactical analysis of RTS game micromanagement by bridging raw high-dimensional state-action data with high-level tactical concepts. Through BK-tree-based state abstraction and rule-based multi-label tactic extraction, it effectively decouples and structures complex behavioral data, offering clearer insights into the underlying tactical drivers of RTS learning systems and improving the practicality and scalability of tactical analysis in such environments.

title: Timesteps of Mamba Align with Human Reading Times
summary: This study demonstrates an alignment of per-word processing time in a popular state-space language model Mamba and human readers. In Mamba, the recurrent state transition at each layer conceptually takes some duration of time, the discretization timestep $Δ_t$, determined dynamically in response to the input. Using a naturalistic reading dataset, we show that the per-word timestep from Mamba is a significant predictor of human reading times, and remains significant even when known predictors such as GPT-2 surprisal are controlled for. We further suggest, through formal analysis of Mamba's architecture and internal dynamics, that Mamba can serve as a new, valuable lens to look at human real-time language processing with ever-updated memory, because it allows us to look at how each module (layer) weighs short- and long-term information retention, and how noise may interact with dynamic, continuous memory representation. Code is available online.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that the per-word internal time step of the Mamba state-space language model aligns with human reading times, suggesting Mamba as a mechanistic model of real-time language processing.
AI.motivation: To understand whether and how modern state-space language models, specifically Mamba, capture aspects of human real-time language processing, and to explore if their internal timing dynamics can explain human reading behavior beyond standard predictors like surprisal.
AI.method: They use a naturalistic reading dataset with human per-word reading times and compute per-word discretization timesteps (Δ_t) from the Mamba language model. They run statistical analyses to test whether Mamba’s per-word timestep predicts human reading times, including models that control for established predictors such as GPT-2 surprisal. They also conduct a formal architectural and dynamical analysis of Mamba to interpret how its layers manage short- vs long-term memory and how noise interacts with continuous memory representations.
AI.result: Per-word timestep values from Mamba significantly predict human reading times, and this predictive power remains after controlling for GPT-2 surprisal and other known predictors. Their architectural analysis shows interpretable patterns in how different layers balance short- and long-term information retention and how noise interacts with the model’s dynamic memory.
AI.conclusion: Mamba’s internal timing dynamics provide a meaningful alignment with human reading behavior, indicating that Mamba can serve as a useful computational lens on human real-time language processing. Its continuous, dynamically updated memory and layer-wise control over information retention make it a promising tool for studying cognitive processes in language comprehension.

title: Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning in StarCraft Micromanagement with Influence Maps and Cluster-based Scripts
summary: Real-time strategy (RTS) games present significant AI challenges, characterized by expansive state-action spaces arising from multi-unit coordination in continuous battlefields, and sparse delayed rewards stemming from final win/lose signals. Existing approaches face a trade-off between managing the dimensionality explosion of joint actions and maintaining the interpretability of complex state representations. This complexity is further intensified by the limitation of traditional hierarchical structures in adaptively decomposing tasks into effective tactical modules. Such difficulties are compounded by the black-box nature of deep learning models and their reliance on sparse rewards, which together result in limited sample efficiency and a lack of decision-making transparency. To address these limitations, this paper proposes HRL-IM/CBS, a hierarchical reinforcement learning framework with influence map hashing and cluster-based scripts for StarCraft micromanagement. Influence map hashing encodes global battlefield situations into compact hexadecimal codes, capturing spatial control and relative advantage. Cluster-based scripts enable dynamic local coordination through adaptive unit partitioning. The hierarchical multi-Q-table architecture decomposes decision-making into upper-level clustering strategy selection and lower-level tactical execution, with reward allocation providing dense learning signals. Experiments across six asymmetric scenarios demonstrate competitive performance against deep RL baselines while offering advantages in sample efficiency and interpretability through transparent Q-table representations.

AI.tldr: Proposes an interpretable, sample-efficient hierarchical RL framework (HRL-IM/CBS) for StarCraft micromanagement using compact influence-map hashing and cluster-based scripts to decompose global strategy and local tactics.
AI.motivation: RTS games like StarCraft are hard for AI because of huge joint action spaces from many units, continuous spatial battlefields, and sparse, delayed rewards (win/lose only). Existing deep RL methods either struggle with the dimensionality of joint actions, lose interpretability with complex neural representations, or rely on rigid hierarchies that don’t adaptively decompose tasks. Deep models are also black boxes and sample-inefficient under sparse rewards, limiting practical use and transparency in decision making.
AI.method: Introduce HRL-IM/CBS, a hierarchical RL architecture tailored to micromanagement. 1) Encode global battle situations using influence map hashing, which aggregates spatial control and relative advantage into compact hexadecimal codes; this serves as a low-dimensional, interpretable state representation. 2) Use cluster-based scripts that dynamically partition units into local clusters and assign each cluster predesigned tactical scripts, enabling adaptive local coordination. 3) Organize control via a hierarchical multi-Q-table: an upper level selects clustering/coordination strategies based on the influence-map code; lower levels handle tactical execution for clusters via Q-tables over scripts, with shaped rewards to provide denser learning signals than win/lose outcomes.
AI.result: On six asymmetric StarCraft micromanagement scenarios, HRL-IM/CBS achieves performance competitive with deep RL baselines while learning from fewer samples. The method maintains clear, table-based value structures that can be inspected to understand strategy and tactic choices. Empirically, it improves sample efficiency and provides more transparent decision policies relative to black-box deep RL methods.
AI.conclusion: Hierarchical RL with structured, interpretable state and action abstractions—via influence-map hashing and cluster-based tactical scripts—can handle complex RTS micromanagement with competitive strength, better sample efficiency, and improved transparency compared to deep RL baselines. The work shows that carefully designed hierarchical decompositions and dense reward allocations can mitigate large state-action spaces and sparse rewards without sacrificing interpretability.

title: MemDelta: Controlled Baselines and Hidden Confounds in Agent Memory Evaluation
summary: Agent memory systems are increasingly evaluated against RAG and full-context baselines, but reported gains often mix changes in the memory method with changes in the language model, embedding model, or retrieval pipeline, making it unclear what is actually being measured. We present MemDelta, a controlled evaluation protocol that varies one component at a time on LongMemEval-S (500 questions, 50+ sessions, three model families). Four findings emerge: (1) verbatim RAG matches full-context GPT-4o-mini (47.2% vs. 49.8%, p = 0.34), but the ranking reverses across models: Gemini gains +14pp from full context, while Sonnet gains +31pp from RAG, partly because it refuses 63% of full-context queries; (2) swapping only the embedding model in an identical pipeline shifts accuracy by +6.2pp at n = 500 (p = 0.004), and Mem0 beats MiniLM-RAG by +11pp but loses to cloud-RAG by 1.2pp, so one variable flips the conclusion; (3) agent self-memory (42%) underperforms basic retrieval (47%); (4) on 2 of 6 question types (n = 88), Mem0 matches cloud RAG (72.7% vs. 73.9%, p = 1.0) at 50x the cost, suggesting narrow rather than general gains. We recommend memory evaluations fix embedding models across comparisons, stratify by model family, and report write-path cost before attributing gains to architecture.

AI.tldr: MemDelta is a controlled evaluation protocol for agent memory systems that isolates the effect of each component (model, embeddings, retrieval, memory) and shows that many reported memory gains actually come from confounded changes, especially embeddings, model families, and refusal behavior.
AI.motivation: Existing evaluations of agent memory systems against RAG and full-context baselines often change multiple components at once (language model, embedding model, retrieval pipeline), so improvements in performance cannot be clearly attributed to the memory mechanism itself. This lack of experimental control makes it hard to understand what architectural choices actually matter and to compare systems fairly.
AI.method: The authors introduce MemDelta, an evaluation protocol that changes one component at a time while holding others fixed.
