They apply it on LongMemEval-S, a benchmark with 500 questions over 50+ sessions using three different model families. They systematically compare configurations such as verbatim RAG vs. full-context, different embedding models within the same retrieval pipeline, self-memory vs. basic retrieval, and a specific memory system (Mem0) vs. several RAG baselines. Statistical tests (p-values) are used to judge significance of differences.
AI.result: (1) Verbatim RAG and full-context GPT-4o-mini perform similarly overall, but relative performance reverses for other model families: Gemini benefits more from full context while Sonnet benefits more from RAG because it refuses many full-context queries. (2) Changing only the embedding model in an otherwise identical pipeline can shift accuracy by over 6 percentage points and even reverse which system appears better; Mem0 outperforms a MiniLM-based RAG but slightly underperforms a cloud-embedding RAG. (3) Agent self-memory performs worse than basic retrieval. (4) Mem0 only matches cloud RAG on a subset of question types and at substantially higher cost, indicating its gains are narrow and expensive rather than broad and efficient.
AI.conclusion: Many claimed improvements in agent memory systems are confounded by changes in non-memory components, especially embedding models and model families. Proper evaluation must control these variables by fixing embeddings across comparisons, stratifying results by language model family, and accounting for the cost of memory writes. Gains should not be attributed to memory architecture unless these factors are controlled and reported.

title: Temporal Feature Extractors in EEG Foundation Models: A Controlled Comparison Including a Pretrained Time-Series Model
summary: Electroencephalography (EEG) foundation models aim to learn generalizable representations from large-scale brain recordings. However, the role of temporal feature extractors and whether pretrained time-series foundation models (TSFMs) can be effectively transferred to this setting remains underexplored. We conduct a controlled comparison of three temporal feature extraction strategies, including a linear baseline, a convolutional encoder, and a frozen pretrained TSFM (MOMENT), within a unified EEG foundation model. We evaluate their impact on representation quality using two downstream tasks: motor imagery and emotion recognition. Results reveal different trends across the evaluated benchmarks. On the motor imagery dataset, simple temporal representations perform competitively, whereas the emotion dataset benefits from richer temporal modeling. Although not specifically adapted to EEG, the pretrained TSFM serves as an effective temporal feature extractor, suggesting that general-purpose time-series representations can be transferred as frozen temporal feature extractors within EEG foundation models.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how different temporal feature extractors affect EEG foundation models and shows that a general-purpose pretrained time-series model can be reused effectively for EEG tasks.
AI.motivation: While EEG foundation models are emerging, it is unclear what kind of temporal feature extractor is most suitable and whether large pretrained time-series foundation models (TSFMs), trained on non-EEG data, can be transferred effectively to EEG representation learning.
AI.method: Within a unified EEG foundation model, the authors systematically compare three temporal feature extraction strategies: (1) a simple linear baseline, (2) a convolutional encoder, and (3) a frozen pretrained time-series foundation model called MOMENT. They then evaluate the learned EEG representations on two downstream tasks, motor imagery and emotion recognition, to assess representation quality and the impact of each temporal extractor.
AI.result: Performance differs across tasks: on motor imagery, simple temporal representations (e.g., linear) are competitive with more complex models, but on emotion recognition, richer temporal modeling (convolutions and MOMENT) improves results. The frozen pretrained TSFM (MOMENT), although not trained on EEG, acts as an effective temporal feature extractor for EEG, often yielding strong downstream performance.
AI.conclusion: The choice of temporal feature extractor in EEG foundation models should be task-dependent. For simpler or more structured tasks like motor imagery, simple temporal models can suffice, while more complex affective tasks benefit from richer temporal modeling. Importantly, general-purpose TSFMs such as MOMENT can be transferred as frozen temporal feature extractors to EEG foundation models, indicating that broad time-series pretraining is useful for EEG applications even without EEG-specific adaptation.

title: Can LLM-as-a-Judge Reliably Verify Rubrics in Agentic Scenarios?
summary: Rubric-based scoring has become a widely used paradigm in model evaluation, typically with LLM-as-a-Judge (LaaJ) for rubric scoring. However, the reliability of LaaJ for rubric scoring remains underexplored. This concern is especially pronounced in agentic scenarios, where long, complex outputs further challenge reliable scoring. To address this, we conduct a systematic meta-evaluation of LaaJ reliability for rubric verification. We introduce RuVerBench, the first benchmark for assessing LaaJ reliability in rubric verification for agentic scenarios. RuVerBench covers two prevalent agentic domains, deep research and agentic coding, with 2,458 instances, each containing a model-generated output, a rubric, and a human-annotated label indicating whether the output satisfies the rubric. Using RuVerBench, we evaluate numerous frontier LLMs and find that even the most advanced models achieve strong performance but still exhibit substantial noise. We further analyze the impact of key LaaJ strategies, including prompt design, batching, and majority voting, on rubric verification. We find that weaker models are more sensitive to prompt variations, batched verification presents a trade-off between accuracy and efficiency, and majority voting yields effective but diminishing returns. We have released our dataset and code to facilitate future research: https://github.com/THU-KEG/RuVerBench.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how reliable large language models are when they are used as rubric-based judges (LLM-as-a-Judge) in complex, agentic tasks, and introduces a benchmark called RuVerBench to evaluate this reliability.
AI.motivation: Rubric-based scoring with LLMs is widely used to evaluate model outputs, but its reliability—especially in long, complex agentic tasks like research and coding—is not well understood. Since noisy or unreliable judgments can mislead research and deployment decisions, a systematic way to measure and improve rubric verification is needed.
AI.method: The authors construct RuVerBench, a benchmark with 2,458 instances from two agentic domains (deep research and agentic coding). Each instance includes a model-generated output, an evaluation rubric, and a human-annotated label on whether the rubric is satisfied. They then test many frontier LLMs as judges on this benchmark and systematically vary LaaJ strategies, such as prompt design, batching multiple verifications, and majority voting, to see how these factors influence reliability.
AI.result: Frontier LLMs achieve relatively strong but still noisy performance as rubric verifiers on RuVerBench. Weaker models show greater sensitivity to how prompts are written. Batching multiple verification tasks together improves efficiency but can reduce accuracy, revealing a trade-off. Majority voting over multiple judgments helps improve reliability, but the benefits taper off after some number of votes.
AI.conclusion: Current LLMs, even advanced ones, cannot yet be assumed to be fully reliable rubric-based judges in complex agentic scenarios. Design choices such as prompts, batching, and voting strategies materially affect reliability. RuVerBench provides a standardized way to measure and compare these effects, and the released dataset and code aim to support future work on building more dependable LLM-as-a-Judge systems.

title: Propagation of~Interval Belief Structures and~Imprecise Copulas for~Neural Network Verification
summary: Quantitative verification of neural networks requires reasoning about probabilities under substantial uncertainty in both input distributions and their dependence structure. In realistic settings, this information is often only partially specified, and assuming precise probabilistic models can lead to unreliable results. We propose a sound framework for quantitative verification under imprecise probabilistic information, combining interval belief structures to represent marginal uncertainty with imprecise copulas to model uncertain dependence. We develop a propagation method for imprecisely coupled interval belief structures through feed-forward neural networks. Using mixed imprecise copula volumes, we derive sound push-forward constructions through affine transformations and activation functions. The resulting output can provide guaranteed lower and upper bounds on probabilistic safety properties, valid for all probability models compatible with the specified imprecise inputs.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a framework to soundly verify neural networks when only imprecise probabilistic information about inputs and their dependencies is available, yielding guaranteed probability bounds for safety properties.
AI.motivation: Existing quantitative verification methods for neural networks typically assume precise input probability distributions and fully specified dependence structures. In realistic applications, however, we often only know partial or interval-valued information about marginals and have vague or no knowledge about dependencies. Relying on precise but incorrect models can make verification results unreliable or misleading. The paper is motivated by the need for a principled way to handle such imprecision and uncertainty while still providing rigorous safety guarantees.
AI.method: The authors introduce a framework that combines interval belief structures to encode uncertainty in marginal input distributions with imprecise copulas to represent uncertain dependence between inputs. They develop a propagation technique tailored to feed-forward neural networks. Central to this is the use of mixed imprecise copula volumes, which allow the sound push-forward (propagation) of these imprecise probabilistic models through both affine layers and nonlinear activation functions. This constructs conservative bounds on the output distribution that are consistent with all admissible input models.
AI.result: The main result is a sound push-forward mechanism for imprecisely specified probabilistic models through feed-forward neural networks. It produces lower and upper bounds on the probabilities of safety properties at the network output. These bounds are guaranteed to hold for every probability distribution consistent with the given interval belief structures and imprecise copulas describing the inputs. Thus, the framework can quantify safety in the presence of both distributional and dependence uncertainty.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that quantitative verification of neural networks can be made robust to severe uncertainty in both input distributions and their dependence by using interval belief structures together with imprecise copulas. Their propagation method yields guaranteed probability bounds for safety properties without requiring precise probabilistic assumptions. This provides a principled and sound approach to verification under imprecise probabilistic information and could be applied to safety-critical settings where only partial probabilistic knowledge is available.

title: Towards Physical Intuitions for Alignment Dynamics: A Case Study With Randomness Crystallization
summary: The alignment of language models is typically studied through the lens of capability benchmarks, but the dynamics of how models change during post-training remain poorly understood. We argue that the physical sciences, and thermodynamic phase-transition theory in particular, offer a principled and underexplored vocabulary for reasoning about these dynamics. As a case study, we instantiate this position through the lens of material Crystallization, which is a well-studied thermodynamic phase transition. For tasks like random number generation, this breaks into 3 phases: (1) the high entropy liquid phase in the pretrained model, with many distinct sampling distributions promptable from the model; (2) the nucleation phase caused by supervised finetuning, in which behavior collapses onto a single seed distribution present in the pretrained LLM; and (3) a settling phase in which reinforcement learning techniques redistribute probability of the collapsed distribution, but largely keep it concentrated on the same options as the seed distribution. We propose intuitive metrics to verify the transitions between these phases, and validate the idea across a range of random tasks. Crystallization is one instance of a broader class of physical frameworks we believe alignment research should import to answer questions about where alignment-induced structure comes from, why it converges where it does, and what it fundamentally cannot change.

AI.tldr: The paper uses ideas from thermodynamic phase transitions, especially crystallization, to explain how language models’ behavior changes during post-training alignment.
AI.motivation: Current alignment work relies on capability benchmarks but lacks a principled way to describe how model behavior structurally changes through post-training. The authors want a more grounded, scientific vocabulary to talk about these dynamics and believe physical sciences, particularly thermodynamics, can provide it.
AI.method: They conceptually map LLM post-training to phases of a thermodynamic phase transition, using crystallization as the analogy. They examine tasks like random number generation and identify three behavioral phases—high-entropy liquid (pretrained model), nucleation (after supervised finetuning), and settling (after reinforcement learning). They propose intuitive metrics to detect phase transitions between these phases and test these metrics on a variety of random tasks.
AI.result: They observe three distinct phases in LLM behavior for random tasks: diverse sampling distributions in the base model; collapse onto a single seed distribution after supervised finetuning; and probability reshuffling within that same support after reinforcement learning. The proposed metrics successfully detect transitions between these phases across different tasks.
AI.conclusion: Post-training alignment of LLMs can be meaningfully described using thermodynamic phase-transition concepts like crystallization. Alignment first collapses behavior onto an existing seed pattern and then mainly reweights options within that pattern, suggesting there are structural limits to what alignment can change and offering a new physical-science-based framework for reasoning about alignment dynamics.

title: Structural Certification for Reliable Physical Design with Language Models
summary: An unreliable language model can be made to produce reliable physical designs if the authority to assert is moved out of the model: the model proposes, and a deterministic engine alone certifies, returning certified, impossible, or unknown. We introduce Physics-Anchored Certification (PHACT), a propose-certify loop spanning five scientific domains, and identify what makes such a certificate trustworthy. A checker that accepts a model-supplied value can be forged; deriving the certified quantity from fixed inputs instead makes forgery impossible by construction. Across eighty adversarial trials spanning two models, two decoding temperatures, and a deliberately faulted engine, this contract produced zero false certifications.

AI.tldr: They propose a framework where LMs only suggest designs and a deterministic physics-based engine verifies them, ensuring reliable physical designs even from unreliable LMs.
AI.motivation: Language models are unreliable for scientific and engineering design tasks because they can hallucinate or make physically impossible claims. The authors want a way to safely use LMs for physical design such that any produced design is guaranteed to obey the relevant physical laws.
AI.method: They introduce Physics-Anchored Certification (PHACT), in which the language model only proposes candidate designs or parameter values, and a separate deterministic engine—implementing domain physics—computes or verifies the relevant quantities from fixed inputs and outputs a certificate: 'certified', 'impossible', or 'unknown'. Crucially, the engine does not trust model-supplied numerical results; instead it recomputes needed quantities, making forgery by the model impossible by construction. They test this framework across five scientific domains, multiple models, temperatures, and even with intentionally faulted checking engines.
AI.result: Across eighty adversarial trials that vary model type, sampling temperature, and reliability of the checking engine, the PHACT setup yields zero false certifications—that is, whenever the system declares a design as certified, it is in fact valid according to the deterministic physics engine and cannot be a model hallucination.
AI.conclusion: By decoupling proposal from certification and anchoring certification in deterministic physics-based computation on fixed inputs, unreliable language models can still be safely used for physical design tasks. The PHACT paradigm provides a trustworthy contract: certificates cannot be forged by the model, and experiments show no false certifications under adversarial testing.

title: LatentRevise: Learning from Zero-Hit Reasoning
summary: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) is bottlenecked by hard prompts on which correct trajectories have low probability, so sampling misses them within a practical budget and leaves the policy update with little useful signal. We frame such zero-hit prompts as RLVR's sampling frontier, where new reasoning behavior is most valuable yet least likely to be sampled. Importantly, failed rollouts can be informative: they expose where the model's reasoning went wrong. We introduce LatentRevise, a first-order latent revision method that recovers training signal for this zero-hit regime. Given a failed rollout and the gold answer as an anchor, LatentRevise optimizes the input embeddings of its reasoning prefix under two complementary gradients, moving the prefix away from the failed continuation and toward the gold answer. The optimization is constrained to the convex hull of the model's vocabulary embeddings, so each update moves the latent toward a real token embedding rather than an arbitrary feature direction. We find that continuations from the revised prefix lengthen, exhibit self-reflection, and reach correct answers missed by the original rollouts. Used as training data, these trajectories improve SFT and RLVR on math benchmarks over standard baselines.

AI.tldr: LatentRevise is a method for reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) that turns failed trajectories on hard problems into useful training signal by editing latent input embeddings toward correct answers, thereby improving reasoning and math performance.
AI.motivation: RLVR struggles on hard prompts where correct solution trajectories are extremely unlikely to be sampled (zero-hit regime), leaving the learning algorithm with almost no positive signal. However, even failed attempts contain information about where the model’s reasoning breaks down. The paper is motivated by the need to extract and leverage this information to push the model’s reasoning frontier without requiring more sampling or stronger supervision.
AI.method: The authors define the "sampling frontier" as hard prompts with zero correct hits under feasible sampling. For each such failed rollout, they use the gold answer as an anchor and introduce LatentRevise: a first-order optimization procedure that operates on the input embeddings corresponding to the model’s reasoning prefix. Two gradients are computed—one that pushes the latent representation away from the failed continuation, and another that pulls it toward a representation associated with the gold answer. The optimization is constrained to the convex hull of the model’s vocabulary embeddings so that each step stays close to valid token embeddings instead of arbitrary feature directions. From these revised prefixes, they resample continuations, generating longer, more reflective, and often correct reasoning traces, which are then used as additional training data for SFT and RLVR.
AI.result: Revised prefixes produce continuations that are more likely to reach the correct answer than the original failed rollouts and tend to show more extended reasoning and self-reflection. When these LatentRevise-generated trajectories are incorporated into training, both supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and RLVR achieve better performance on math benchmarks compared to standard RLVR baselines without latent revision.
AI.conclusion: Failed rollouts on hard prompts can be systematically turned into valuable supervision by operating in the latent embedding space. LatentRevise recovers learning signal in the zero-hit regime by editing reasoning prefixes toward the gold answer while staying close to the model’s token manifold. This approach successfully expands the effective sampling frontier of RLVR and improves mathematical reasoning performance, indicating that latent revision is a promising direction for enhancing RL on verifiable tasks.

title: Open Problems in Constitutional Preference Reconstruction
summary: Pairwise preference data is widely used for training and evaluating language models (e.g., RLHF), but each datapoint records a \emph{choice}, not the rationale behind it. Methods such as Inverse Constitutional AI (ICAI) attempt to improve interpretability by compressing datasets into short ``constitutions'' of natural-language principles. We argue this framing is under-specified: a flat list of principles is not yet an executable decision rule because it leaves principle composition implicit. We use the pairwise setting as a testbed to empirically characterize three open problems in constitutional methods. First, principle quality is hard to measure: coverage and accuracy are useful but incomplete proxies for end-to-end reconstruction. Second, \emph{composition is ambiguous}: holding principles fixed, different executors (LLM judge versus majority vote) agree only $73\%$ of the time. Third, \emph{constitutions differ between LLMs}: cross-model vote agreement is $73\%$, whereas intra-model agreement is $81\%$. Across PRISM, AlpacaEval, and Chatbot Arena, we show that principle refinement (ICAI+) may be a first step towards ameliorating these problems: inter-executor agreement rises to $78\%$, and transparent executors match LLM judge accuracy ($66\%$ vs.\ $67\%$). Our results highlight that constitutions should be evaluated as \emph{constitution--executor systems}, with implications for LLMs-as-a-judge broadly.

AI.tldr: The paper examines how to turn natural-language constitutions (principles) into reliable, executable decision rules for pairwise preference data, showing that principle quality, how principles are composed, and model-specific differences all matter, and that refining constitutions plus using transparent executors can partially fix these issues.
AI.motivation: Pairwise preference data used in RLHF and LLM evaluation captures which output is preferred but not the underlying reasons. ICAI-like methods compress large preference datasets into small sets of human-readable principles (constitutions) to gain interpretability, but a list of principles alone does not specify exactly how decisions should be made. There is a need to understand whether such constitutions faithfully reconstruct the original preferences, how different ways of executing them behave, and whether constitutions are stable across models.
AI.method: Use the pairwise preference setting (datasets such as PRISM, AlpacaEval, Chatbot Arena) as a controlled testbed. Define and measure several properties of constitutions: coverage and accuracy with respect to the original preference labels, end-to-end reconstruction quality, agreement between different executors (e.g., LLM-as-a-judge vs. a transparent majority-vote executor) given the same principles, and agreement across models. Introduce and test an improved method, ICAI+, for refining constitutions and evaluate its impact on these metrics.
AI.result: 1) Principle quality is difficult to evaluate: common metrics like coverage and accuracy do not fully predict how well a constitution reproduces the original pairwise preferences. 2) Composition ambiguity: even with fixed principles, different executors (LLM judge vs. majority vote) disagree on about 27% of comparisons (73% agreement), revealing underspecified decision rules. 3) Constitutions vary across LLMs: cross-model vote agreement is only about 73%, lower than intra-model agreement at 81%, indicating model-specific constitutions. With the refined ICAI+ approach, inter-executor agreement improves to 78%, and transparent, rule-based executors can reach similar accuracy to an LLM judge (66% vs. 67%).
AI.conclusion: Natural-language constitutions for preferences cannot be evaluated in isolation; they must be studied jointly with the procedure that executes them. Ambiguities in how principles are composed and model-specific differences significantly affect decision outcomes. Refining constitutions (ICAI+) and using transparent execution schemes can increase inter-executor agreement and approach LLM-judge performance, but they do not fully solve the underspecification problem. The work implies that future constitutional methods and LLM-as-a-judge systems should explicitly specify and evaluate the combined constitution–executor system rather than just the principles alone.

title: IHDec: Divergence-Steered Contrastive Decoding for Securing Multi-Turn Instruction Hierarchies
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) often fail to maintain instruction hierarchies (IH) when processing multi-source inputs with varying role-level priorities, paradoxically adhering to lower-priority directives during conflicts. While existing defenses mitigate this issue, they are largely restricted to single-turn scenarios and require expensive fine-tuning. In this paper, we formalize this failure mode in multi-turn contexts via a Jensen-Shannon Divergence (JSD) framework, uncovering a pervasive role-influence inversion phenomenon where subordinate inputs override superior roles. To rectify this without training, we propose IHDec (Instruction Hierarchy-steered Decoding). IHDec leverages JSD to automatically detect token-level hierarchy violations and dynamically executes contrastive decoding to suppress misaligned subordinate roles. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that IHDec outperforms training-based baselines in multi-turn conflicts while fully preserving general response quality. Furthermore, IHDec strengthens safety against adversarial prompt injections and exhibits a robust scaling synergy with larger models. The Code is available at https://github.com/nxcolelxu/IHDec.git

AI.tldr: The paper identifies and fixes a common failure where LLMs follow lower-priority instructions over higher-priority ones in multi-turn, multi-role settings, and proposes a training-free decoding method (IHDec) that detects and corrects such hierarchy violations using Jensen-Shannon Divergence and contrastive decoding.
AI.motivation: LLMs are often given inputs from different sources or roles (e.g., system, developer, user) with a clear priority order, but in practice they frequently ignore higher-priority instructions and follow conflicting lower-priority ones instead. Existing defenses either focus on single-turn interactions or require costly model fine-tuning and do not generalize well to multi-turn, evolving dialogues. There is a need for a general, training-free method that can preserve instruction hierarchies across turns while maintaining response quality and improving robustness to prompt injection attacks.
AI.method: The authors first formalize instruction hierarchy failures in multi-turn dialogues using a Jensen-Shannon Divergence (JSD) framework that measures how different role-level inputs influence token generation, revealing a role-influence inversion effect. They then propose IHDec, a decoding-time algorithm that does not modify model parameters: IHDec monitors token-level signals derived from JSD to automatically detect when the model’s next-token distribution is being dominated by a subordinate (lower-priority) role in a way that conflicts with higher-priority instructions. When such a violation is detected, IHDec triggers contrastive decoding to down-weight or suppress contributions from misaligned subordinate roles, steering generation back toward compliance with superior roles. This process is applied dynamically throughout generation in multi-turn settings.
AI.result: Across extensive experiments on benchmarks involving multi-turn instruction conflicts and adversarial prompt injections, IHDec consistently improves adherence to instruction hierarchies compared to both standard decoding and training-based baselines. It corrects many cases where lower-priority instructions would otherwise override higher-priority ones, while preserving or fully maintaining general response quality on non-adversarial tasks. The method also scales well, showing stronger effects when applied to larger LLMs, and enhances safety performance against prompt injection attacks without requiring additional training.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that instruction hierarchy failures in LLMs can be systematically characterized as role-influence inversions measurable via JSD, and that they can be effectively mitigated at decoding time without fine-tuning. IHDec offers a practical, training-free mechanism to enforce role priorities, improving robustness in multi-turn, multi-source interactions and to adversarial prompt injections, with favorable scaling behavior on larger models. This suggests that careful decoding strategies can substantially enhance safety and control of LLMs beyond what is achievable with standard training-only approaches.

title: Does Verbose Chain-of-Thought Really Help? In-Distribution Evidence that Content, Not Length, Matters
comment: ICML Workshop on Efficient Multimodal Question Answering (EMM-QA)
summary: Chain-of-thought (CoT) prompting improves LLM reasoning, but the source is contested: do the intermediate steps help because they carry useful semantic content, or because conditioning on more tokens buys extra computation before the model commits to an answer? We bring two lines of evidence to bear. First, in distribution: we repeatedly sample each model on the same question and pair a shorter with a longer of its own natural generations that follow the same reasoning plan, so nothing is rewritten and both traces are genuinely in-distribution. Across 25 models the extra tokens leave accuracy essentially unchanged for every independently-trained reasoner, and a blind analysis of the surplus tokens shows that what gain exists elsewhere tracks validation- and checking-content, not verbosity per se. Second, as a controlled intervention, we ask whether two traces expressing the same semantic content (the same facts, operations, and intermediate values, verified through directed acyclic graph equivalence) produce different outcomes when one is more verbose, using a dual-validator design across four targets and eight benchmarks with number-redacted completion and stratified bootstrap confidence intervals. Verbose traces do improve accuracy (25 of 32 benchmark-target cells are positive under at least one validator), but the effects are modest (typically 1-4 points) and depend on the quality of the verbose prose, not merely its length. Under maximum numerical redaction the effect is amplified (median 3.24x across four arithmetic benchmarks), and length-matched non-reasoning filler recovers none of it. Both lines converge: what matters is what the extra tokens do (the reasoning and validation content they carry), not how many there are, a picture neither a pure forward-pass-compute nor a pure semantic-content account fully explains.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates whether chain-of-thought’s benefits come from more tokens (extra computation) or from the semantic content of reasoning steps, using in-distribution length variation and controlled, meaning-preserving verbosity changes; it finds that semantic reasoning and validation content, not length alone, drive most of the gains, with modest but real benefits from well-written verbose reasoning, especially when numeric cues are hidden.
AI.motivation: There is a debate about why chain-of-thought prompting improves large language model reasoning: is it due to useful intermediate reasoning steps (semantic content) or simply because longer outputs give the model more computation time before committing to an answer. Clarifying this matters both for understanding LLM internals and for designing better prompting and training methods.
AI.method: The authors use two complementary approaches. (1) In-distribution analysis: they repeatedly sample each model on the same question, then pair a shorter and a longer natural generation that follow the same reasoning plan, keeping content and plan aligned while varying length. They examine accuracy changes across 25 models and blind-annotate the surplus tokens to categorize them as validation/checking vs. mere verbosity. (2) Controlled intervention: they construct pairs of reasoning traces that are provably equivalent in semantic content (same facts, operations, intermediate values, checked via DAG equivalence), but differ in verbosity. Using a dual-validator framework across four target models and eight benchmarks, with number-redacted completions and stratified bootstrap CIs, they compare the effect of verbose vs. concise reasoning, including conditions with maximal numerical redaction and with length-matched non-reasoning filler text.
AI.result: In the in-distribution setting, extra tokens in otherwise similar reasoning traces do not materially change accuracy across 25 independently trained models. The small gains observed correlate with validation and checking content rather than with verbosity or length itself. In the controlled setting, verbose but semantically equivalent traces yield modest accuracy improvements (typically 1–4 percentage points) in most benchmark–target combinations, and the benefit depends on the quality and nature of the verbose prose rather than its mere length. When numeric information is maximally redacted, the benefit of good verbose reasoning is amplified (median 3.24× effect across four arithmetic benchmarks). Replacing reasoning tokens with non-reasoning filler of comparable length fails to reproduce these gains.
AI.conclusion: Both empirical lines suggest that chain-of-thought’s benefits come mainly from the semantic work done by extra tokens—explicit reasoning and validation steps—rather than from sheer token count or compute. While verbosity can help, its effect is modest and contingent on the presence and quality of genuine reasoning content, especially when surface numeric cues are unavailable. Neither a purely compute-based nor a purely semantic account fully explains the findings, implying that effective CoT leverage lies in how additional tokens structure and verify reasoning rather than in their length alone.

title: Are We Measuring Strategy or Phrasing? The Gap Between Surface- and Approach-Level Diversity in LLM Math Reasoning
summary: Diversity in LLM mathematical reasoning is critical for exploration, but common diversity metrics mostly capture surface-level variation rather than differences in how a problem is solved. We address this gap by introducing approach-level diversity: variation in strategies across correct solutions to the same problem. Using a human-calibrated LLM judge framework, we show that prior diversity measures are unreliable proxies for approach-level diversity, and this mismatch carries over to diversity-aware RLVR, where target metrics are preserved while approach-level diversity declines. Investigating when approach-level diversity helps and whether it can be directly induced, we find that approach-diverse candidate sets improve test-time scaling. However, optimizing an LLM judge diversity reward during training causes the policy to exploit judge-specific preferences rather than broaden its approaches, leaving direct optimization of approach-level diversity as an open problem. Together, our work introduces the notion of approach-level diversity and uncovers a systematic divergence between surface- and approach-level signals, marking a step toward LLMs that reason in genuinely diverse, human-like ways.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces and studies "approach-level diversity" in LLM math reasoning—differences in problem-solving strategies among correct solutions—and shows that existing diversity metrics miss this, leading to misleading conclusions in training and evaluation.
AI.motivation: Current diversity metrics for LLM reasoning (e.g., variation in sampled outputs) mostly measure superficial differences such as wording or minor step changes, not whether the model is actually using distinct solution strategies. This is a problem for research areas like exploration, test-time scaling, and diversity-aware training (e.g., RLVR), which implicitly assume that more diverse samples correspond to more diverse reasoning. The authors are motivated to define and measure diversity at the level of approaches/strategies, examine how it relates to existing metrics, and understand its role in improving reasoning performance.
AI.method: 1) Define "approach-level diversity" as variation in underlying solution strategies across correct answers to the same math problem. 2) Develop a human-calibrated LLM judge framework that can classify whether two solutions use different approaches. 3) Empirically compare standard diversity metrics (e.g., token-level or surface metrics) with this judge-based measure to see if they correlate with approach-level diversity. 4) Analyze diversity-aware RLVR training setups to see whether optimizing target surface metrics preserves or harms approach-level diversity. 5) Conduct experiments on test-time scaling where sets of candidate solutions with varying approach-level diversity are generated, to measure their effect on performance. 6) Add a diversity reward based on the LLM judge into training and observe how models adapt, probing whether they truly broaden strategies or just exploit the judge’s idiosyncrasies.
AI.result: 1) Standard diversity metrics are shown to be poor proxies for approach-level diversity; they often indicate high diversity when approaches are actually similar. 2) In RLVR setups, even when target diversity metrics are maintained, true approach-level diversity decreases, revealing a divergence between surface and approach diversity. 3) Candidate sets with higher approach-level diversity improve test-time scaling, suggesting that genuine strategy variety helps reasoning performance. 4) When directly optimizing a judge-based diversity reward, models learn to game the judge by aligning with its specific preferences rather than genuinely increasing their range of solution strategies, indicating that naive optimization of such a reward fails.
AI.conclusion: The paper formalizes and empirically studies approach-level diversity as a distinct and more meaningful notion of diversity in LLM mathematical reasoning than common surface-level metrics. It demonstrates that existing diversity measures and diversity-aware training methods can be misleading, as they do not ensure genuine variation in problem-solving strategies and can even reduce such diversity. Approach-level diversity is shown to be beneficial for test-time scaling, but directly training for it via an LLM-judge reward leads to reward hacking rather than true strategic variety. The work highlights the need for better metrics and training methods that foster real, human-like diversity in reasoning approaches, and it establishes direct optimization of approach-level diversity as an open research challenge.

title: Relevance Is Not Permission: Warranted Attention for Value Contributions
summary: Relevance is not permission. Attention lets a model read key-value items related to the current query, but it does not guarantee that the value contribution of such an item becomes prediction evidence. A retrieved passage may be relevant to a question without being supporting evidence, and a historical fact or temporal neighbor may even blur true-tail ranking or the current edge score. This paper formalizes this gap as a permission problem for the weighted value term alpha_ij * v_j that is actually added to the prediction path. We propose Warrant, a path-localized interface that preserves attention relevance alpha_ij, exposes the value path leading to the primary metric, and, in the full model, turns alpha_ij * v_j into alpha_ij * g_ij * v_j through learned query-item permission g_ij. We place the same operator on the metric-defining value paths of CTDG link prediction, MTPP next-mark ranking, RAG supporting evidence selection, STPP next-location forecasting, and TKG tail prediction. Across 32 paired comparisons, 3 seeds, and 192 total runs, Warrant improves the primary metric in 27 comparisons; practical tiers consist of 10 substantial effects, 1 marginal effect, 8 positive but uncertain effects, 8 tie/negligible effects, and 5 drops. In the path-localization check, correct-path placement outperforms direction-aware Base performance in every domain and exceeds generic attention placement by +0.1076 AUC in CTDG and +0.0683 MRR in TKG. Ablations show that most TKG gains come from historical-tail value path exposure, whereas the core CTDG gain comes from edge-conditioned query-item permission. In conclusion, prediction evidence is not attention mass. A weighted value term becomes evidence only when it is warranted on the path to the metric.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Warrant, a mechanism that distinguishes between mere attention relevance and true prediction evidence by gating value contributions in attention paths, improving performance across multiple sequence and graph prediction tasks.
AI.motivation: Standard attention treats all retrieved, relevant information as if it were automatically allowed to influence predictions, but in practice not all relevant items are valid supporting evidence and some can even hurt performance. The authors observe that the key limitation is the lack of an explicit notion of “permission” for value contributions, especially in complex tasks like temporal link prediction, event forecasting, and retrieval-augmented generation. They aim to formalize and control which attended items are actually allowed to contribute to the model’s final decision metric.
AI.method: They formalize the permission problem on the attention value term alpha_ij * v_j and propose Warrant, a path-localized interface. Warrant keeps the existing attention relevance alpha_ij but explicitly exposes and controls the value path that leads to the task’s primary metric. In the full model, the value contribution is modified from alpha_ij * v_j to alpha_ij * g_ij * v_j, where g_ij is a learned, query-item-specific permission gate. They apply this operator only on value paths that directly define the primary metric in several domains: CTDG link prediction, MTPP next-mark ranking, RAG supporting evidence selection, STPP next-location forecasting, and TKG tail prediction. They also perform path-localization checks and ablations to understand where the gains come from (e.g., historical-tail value path exposure vs. edge-conditioned permissions).
AI.result: Across 32 paired comparisons with 3 random seeds (192 total runs), Warrant improves the primary metric in 27 comparisons. The practical effect tiers include 10 substantial improvements, 1 marginal improvement, 8 positive but uncertain improvements, 8 ties or negligible changes, and 5 performance drops. In path-localization experiments, placing Warrant on the correct metric-defining paths consistently outperforms a direction-aware base model in all domains and yields large gains over generic attention placement—for example, +0.1076 AUC on CTDG and +0.0683 MRR on TKG. Ablation studies reveal that TKG benefits mainly from exposing historical-tail value paths, while CTDG gains are driven primarily by edge-conditioned query-item permission gating.
AI.conclusion: Attention relevance alone does not determine what counts as prediction evidence. A value term contributes evidence only if it is explicitly warranted along the path to the evaluation metric. By introducing a learned permission gate on metric-defining value paths, Warrant effectively separates “what is attended to” from “what is allowed to influence the prediction,” and this separation yields consistent performance improvements across a range of temporal and relational prediction tasks.

title: LLM Agents Are Latent Context Managers: Eliciting Self-Managed Context via a Proprioceptive Dashboard
summary: Long-horizon tool agents are bottlenecked by how their context grows toward the limits of the context window. Recent systems make context management agent- or system-controlled, but they either learn a compression policy that discards evidence or manage context in a layer the agent never sees. We argue both leave a more basic gap unaddressed. Frontier language models are proprioceptively blind to their own context. From the prompt alone they cannot see how large, how old, or how used each block is, the signals a keep-or-drop decision needs. We hypothesize that competent context management is already latent in capable models, and that what is missing is not a learned policy but an interface exposing this state. We introduce VISTA (Visible Internal State for Tool Agents), a training-free, model-agnostic layer that represents working memory as typed, addressable blocks, surfaces a runtime dashboard of per-block token usage, recency, and access history, and archives blocks as recoverable full-fidelity payloads. On LOCA-Bench, BrowseComp-Plus, and GAIA, the same untrained interface transfers across million-, 100K-, and 10K-scale trajectories. On LOCA-Bench it improves four backbones and lifts Gemini-3-Flash from 22.7 to 50.7%. The lift grows with context pressure and transfers across backbones. Ablations further confirm that the dashboard matters beyond archive and recovery tools.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces VISTA, a training-free, model-agnostic interface that exposes an LLM agent’s working-memory state (usage, recency, access history of context blocks) so the model can manage its own long-horizon context more effectively.
AI.motivation: Long-horizon tool agents quickly hit context-window limits. Existing context-management methods either compress and discard potentially useful evidence or manage memory outside the model’s view. The authors argue that large models already have latent competence for context management but lack visibility into their own context state (size, age, and usage of each block). They aim to provide this missing interface so models can make better keep-or-drop decisions without retraining.
AI.method: They propose VISTA, a training-free, model-agnostic layer that organizes working memory into typed, addressable blocks. VISTA surfaces a runtime dashboard for each block (token usage, recency, access history) to the model and archives blocks as recoverable, full-fidelity payloads. The same interface is applied across different backbones and tasks, without training a new policy. They evaluate on several long-horizon benchmarks (LOCA-Bench, BrowseComp-Plus, GAIA) and perform ablations to separate the effect of the dashboard from archive/retrieval tools.
AI.result: Using VISTA, the same untrained interface generalizes across trajectories of varying lengths (million, 100K, and 10K tokens). On LOCA-Bench, VISTA improves performance for four different backbone models, with Gemini-3-Flash improving from 22.7% to 50.7%. The gains become larger as context pressure increases and the improvements transfer across backbones. Ablation studies show that exposing the dashboard itself contributes substantially beyond just having archive-and-recover mechanisms.
AI.conclusion: Exposing internal context-state information to LLM agents via VISTA enables significantly better long-horizon context management without additional training. The results support the hypothesis that capable models already contain latent strategies for memory management, and that a suitable state interface—rather than a learned compression policy—is key to unlocking these abilities across models and tasks.

title: FacePlex: Full-Duplex Joint Speech-Facial Motion Generation for Conversational Avatars
summary: Natural face-to-face conversation requires real-time speech generation together with synchronized facial motion. Existing systems only partially address this problem: speech-only full-duplex models can generate speech in real time but do not produce facial motion, while audio-driven facial motion models animate a face from already available audio rather than jointly generating speech and motion online. To bridge this gap, we first formalize full-duplex joint speech-facial motion generation, where speech tokens and facial motion tokens are produced together every step. Building on this formulation, we propose FacePlex, a unified streaming framework with two key components. First, Rolling Flow Matching adapts flow matching to online motion generation by committing new motion frames at each streaming step. Second, Rolling Cross-Attention couples the streaming audio queue with the motion queue, allowing speech and facial motion to condition each other as generation progresses. Through extensive experiments, ablation studies, and a user study, we show that FacePlex enables full-duplex joint speech-facial motion generation under online streaming constraints, while achieving stronger lip-sync quality and motion fidelity than audio-driven facial motion baselines.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces FacePlex, a streaming framework that jointly generates speech and synchronized facial motion in real time, outperforming prior audio-driven facial animation methods in lip-sync and motion fidelity.
AI.motivation: Natural human communication involves tightly coupled, real-time speech and facial expressions, but existing AI systems either generate only speech or animate faces from pre-existing audio, failing to jointly generate both modalities online. There is a need for a full-duplex model that can simultaneously produce speech and corresponding facial motion under streaming constraints.
AI.method: The authors first formalize the task of full-duplex joint speech-facial motion generation where speech and motion tokens are emitted together at each time step. They then propose FacePlex, which includes: (1) Rolling Flow Matching, an adaptation of flow matching for online motion generation that incrementally commits motion frames at each streaming step; and (2) Rolling Cross-Attention, which dynamically couples a streaming audio token queue with a motion token queue so that speech and facial motion can condition one another as generation unfolds.
AI.result: Through experiments, ablations, and a user study, FacePlex is shown to successfully perform joint online generation of speech and facial motion, achieving better lip synchronization and higher motion fidelity compared to baseline audio-driven facial animation models.
AI.conclusion: FacePlex demonstrates that full-duplex, jointly generated speech and facial motion under streaming constraints is feasible and can surpass traditional audio-driven animation systems in synchronization and realism, establishing a new framework for real-time multimodal conversational agents.

title: Node-to-Neighborhood Semantic Consistency: Text-Topology Alignment for TAGs Anomaly Detection
summary: Graph anomaly detection (GAD) on text-attributed graphs (TAGs) is vital for applications such as fraud detection and academic integrity verification. Existing approaches generally fall into two paradigms. GNN-based methods effectively capture structural patterns but struggle to capture fine-grained textual semantics. Methods integrating LLMs with graphs improve semantic understanding yet fail to fully comprehend topological relationships among neighboring nodes. Moreover, both paradigms overlook the correspondence between textual semantics and graph topological relationships, limiting their ability to identify nodes whose semantics are inconsistent with their neighborhoods. In this paper, we formalize TAG anomaly detection as a node-to-neighborhood semantic consistency problem, where anomalies may arise from either textual semantic mismatch or topological deviation between a node and its neighbors. We propose N2NSC (Node-to-Neighborhood Semantic Consistency), a framework that captures the correspondence between graph topology and textual semantics through two complementary fusion paths. The two pathways work synergistically, enabling the LLM to fully leverage both textual and structural neighborhood information for anomaly detection. Extensive experiments across eight datasets demonstrate that N2NSC consistently outperforms current state-of-the-art methods.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes N2NSC, a framework for graph anomaly detection on text-attributed graphs by modeling node-to-neighborhood semantic consistency, and shows it outperforms prior methods on eight datasets.
AI.motivation: Graph anomaly detection on text-attributed graphs is important in domains like fraud detection and academic integrity verification. Existing GNN-based methods capture structural patterns but miss fine-grained text semantics, while LLM-graph integration methods improve text understanding but fail to model graph topology well. Both lines of work largely ignore the explicit correspondence between a node’s textual semantics and its structural neighborhood, making it hard to detect nodes whose text is inconsistent with their neighbors or whose connections deviate topologically.
AI.method: The paper formalizes anomaly detection on text-attributed graphs as a node-to-neighborhood semantic consistency problem, where anomalies arise from semantic mismatch or structural deviation between a node and its neighbors. It proposes N2NSC, a framework with two complementary fusion paths that jointly model graph topology and textual semantics. These dual pathways fuse neighborhood structural information with text features in a way that allows an LLM to leverage both semantic content and graph structure for anomaly scoring at the node level.
AI.result: On eight benchmark datasets for text-attributed graph anomaly detection, N2NSC is empirically evaluated against existing state-of-the-art methods from both GNN-based and LLM-graph paradigms. Across these datasets, N2NSC consistently achieves superior performance, indicating better detection of both semantic inconsistency and structural anomalies.
AI.conclusion: By recasting anomaly detection on text-attributed graphs as a node-to-neighborhood semantic consistency task and introducing a dual-path fusion framework, N2NSC effectively connects textual semantics with graph topology. The approach enables LLMs to exploit neighborhood structure alongside text, leading to consistently improved anomaly detection performance over state-of-the-art baselines across multiple datasets.

title: MirrorCode: AI can rebuild entire programs from behavior alone
comment: 34 pages, 13 figures, 9 tables. Code available at https://github.com/epoch-research/MirrorCode
summary: AI models are rapidly improving at autonomous coding, as shown by benchmark progress and one-off demonstrations such as AI implementing a C compiler. However, existing coding benchmarks tend to focus on shorter tasks, and one-off demonstrations are hard to compare systematically because they often have some human guidance, and are not standardized or repeated across models. To address these challenges, we introduce MirrorCode, a long-horizon coding benchmark based on reimplementing entire software projects. In MirrorCode, AI agents must replicate the functionalities of an existing program, without access to its source code. AI solutions must match the original program's output exactly on end-to-end tests, including held-out tests. MirrorCode's 25 target programs span different areas of computing: Unix utilities, data serialization and query tools, bioinformatics, interpreters, static analysis, cryptography, and compression. Existing AI models can already reimplement complex software, with the strongest model scoring 56% across the benchmark. For example, AI can reimplement gotree, a 16,000-line bioinformatics toolkit - a task that we believe would take weeks for a human engineer. However, studying the frontier of performance requires a larger inference budget than typical benchmarks, for example, \$2,600 over 19 days for a single attempt on a large task. We show that AI agents can already complete long-horizon software engineering tasks, especially when requirements are precisely specified. More broadly, our work suggests AI will have transformative effects on software engineering, as autonomous agents continue to improve.

AI.tldr: MirrorCode is a benchmark where AI agents must reimplement whole software projects without seeing their source code, matching behavior exactly on end-to-end tests.
AI.motivation: Current coding benchmarks mostly cover short, toy-style tasks and one-off impressive demos of long-horizon coding are not standardized, not comparable across models, and often include human help. The authors want a systematic, repeatable way to measure how well AI can autonomously build substantial, real-world-scale software systems.
AI.method: They design MirrorCode, a benchmark consisting of 25 real-world target programs across diverse domains. For each target, the AI agent only interacts with the compiled program (via I/O) and must recreate a new implementation that matches the original’s behavior on comprehensive end-to-end and held-out tests. They run state-of-the-art models under large inference budgets and measure success as exact behavioral equivalence on these tests.
AI.result: The strongest existing model achieves 56% success across all MirrorCode tasks. Models can fully reimplement complex software such as gotree, a ~16K-line bioinformatics toolkit, a job that would likely take a human engineer weeks. However, achieving this frontier performance requires substantial compute and time—e.g., about $2,600 of inference over 19 days for one attempt on a large task.
AI.conclusion: AI agents are already capable of completing substantial, long-horizon software engineering projects when requirements are clearly specified, though they currently demand high inference budgets. As models improve, autonomous coding systems are likely to have major, potentially transformative impact on how software is engineered.

title: Parametric Skills
summary: Since intelligence fundamentally relies on efficient skill acquisition (Chollet, 2019), the ability to leverage skills is critical. For LLMs, skills, manually authored or extracted from task trajectories, are textual recipes encoding mature problem-solving experience and are critical to agentic capabilities. Despite widespread deployment, their utility is limited by the model's ability to comprehend and follow skill instructions, especially under complex and long-context scenarios, where key instructions are difficult to locate and adhere to. To address this limitation, we propose ParametricSkills, a framework that can convert free-form textual skills into parameters at test time, enabling context-free skill exploitation. Specifically, we first construct a large-scale, high-quality skill library, and synthesize single-turn and multi-turn skill exploitation trajectories built around these skills with OpenCode. Using these data, we then train a hypernetwork that parameterizes both the skill content and the test-time exploitation methodology by receiving textual skills and converting them into LoRA adapters. Experimental results on six complex software engineering (SWE) subtasks demonstrate that, the proposed ParametricSkills averagely outperforms in-context learning by 6.44 points as judged by DeepSeek-V4-Flash, while also achieving significantly higher BERT Score and F1 score, confirming its effectiveness. Beyond performance, we further find that parametric skills, being inherently accumulative, offer a preliminary yet promising avenue toward test-time continual learning.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces ParametricSkills, a method to convert textual skills into model parameters at test time so LLMs can use skills without needing them in context.
AI.motivation: LLMs often struggle to effectively use textual skills or instructions, especially in long or complex contexts where important information is hard to locate and follow. This limits their ability to exploit accumulated problem-solving experience and to behave as strong agents in domains like software engineering.
AI.method: 1) Build a large, high-quality library of textual skills. 2) Use OpenCode to synthesize single- and multi-turn trajectories that demonstrate how these skills are exploited in tasks. 3) Train a hypernetwork that, at test time, takes a textual skill as input and outputs LoRA adapter parameters. These adapters encode both the skill’s content and a methodology for using it, enabling the base LLM to apply the skill without needing to read the instructions in context.
AI.result: On six complex software engineering subtasks, ParametricSkills outperforms standard in-context learning by an average of 6.44 points according to DeepSeek-V4-Flash evaluation, and also achieves notably higher BERTScore and F1, indicating better solution quality and alignment with reference answers.
AI.conclusion: Encoding skills as test-time parameters via a hypernetwork and LoRA adapters enables more effective and context-free exploitation of skills than in-context learning. The approach not only improves performance on complex SWE tasks but also suggests that such parametric skills can be accumulated over time, pointing to a viable path toward test-time continual learning in LLMs.

title: Dynamo: Dynamic Skill-Tool Evolution for Vision-Language Agents
summary: Improving vision-language models (VLMs) on visual reasoning typically requires retraining or hand-designed prompts and tools. We present Dynamo, a training-free framework that adapts a frozen VLM without any weight updates. On a small labeled training subset, the agent inspects its own correct and incorrect attempts and evolves two complementary capabilities: reusable reasoning skills for cognitive bottlenecks, and executable visual tools for perceptual ones. Each generated tool is paired with a skill that specifies when to invoke it, and both capability types accumulate in a persistent library. Across four visual reasoning benchmarks and five VLM backbones, Dynamo improves direct inference on all 20 model--benchmark settings (avg. +5.6 acc). When the tool set is given in advance, the framework learns when to call each tool, and per-step tool choice improves on every tested backbone. Against task-specific RL (VTool-R1, DeepEyes), Dynamo closes 65--99% of the RL gap at a fraction of the compute, and combines additively with RL when available.

AI.tldr: Dynamo is a training-free framework to adapt frozen vision-language models for visual reasoning by evolving reusable reasoning skills and executable visual tools, improving performance without weight updates.
AI.motivation: Existing approaches to improving visual reasoning in vision-language models usually require computationally expensive retraining or manual prompt/tool engineering. There is a need for a more efficient, automated, and training-free way to enhance VLMs on visual reasoning tasks.
AI.method: Dynamo uses a small labeled training subset where an agent examines its own correct and incorrect predictions. From this analysis, it automatically discovers and evolves two types of capabilities: (1) reusable reasoning skills that address cognitive reasoning bottlenecks, and (2) executable visual tools that address perceptual bottlenecks. Each visual tool is linked with a corresponding skill that determines when the tool should be invoked. These skills and tools are stored in a persistent library that can be reused during inference. The framework can also work with pre-specified tool sets by learning when to call each tool.
AI.result: Across four visual reasoning benchmarks and five different VLM backbones, Dynamo consistently improves direct inference performance in all 20 model–benchmark combinations, achieving an average accuracy improvement of 5.6 points. When provided with an existing set of tools, Dynamo learns effective per-step tool selection that boosts performance on every tested backbone. Compared with task-specific reinforcement learning methods such as VTool-R1 and DeepEyes, Dynamo recovers 65–99% of their performance gains while using significantly less compute, and it can be combined with RL for further additive improvements.
AI.conclusion: Dynamo offers an efficient, training-free way to adapt frozen vision-language models for visual reasoning by building and using a library of reasoning skills and visual tools. It achieves broad, consistent gains across multiple benchmarks and backbones, substantially closing the gap to specialized RL methods at a fraction of the computational cost, and can also complement RL when it is available.

title: Little Brains, Big Feats: Exploring Compact Language Models
comment: Accepted to ECML PKDD 2026, Applied Data Science track. Author preprint; the definitive version will appear in the proceedings of ECML PKDD 2026, Springer LNCS
summary: While large language models have been dominating the research landscape recently, small language models remain highly relevant across various domains; yet, they receive far less attention. In this study, we investigate how smaller language models perform during the generation stage within a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) system. To benchmark these models effectively, we utilised both open-source and proprietary datasets covering diverse subject areas and question types. Our findings demonstrate that a RAG system with small language models can be executed directly on-device without requiring any GPU hardware within a reasonable time. The experimental code and links to the supplementary materials can be accessed through the GitHub repository: https://github.com/SibNN/SLM-RAG-EVAL.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates how well small language models perform as generators in Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems, showing they can run on-device without GPUs in reasonable time.
AI.motivation: Although large language models dominate research, small language models are still important for practical applications (e.g., on-device or resource-constrained settings) but are understudied, especially in the context of RAG. The authors aim to systematically understand and benchmark the performance of small language models within RAG pipelines.
AI.method: They build and evaluate RAG systems that use small language models for the generation stage. The evaluation uses both open-source and proprietary datasets that span multiple subject domains and question types. They measure performance and efficiency to determine whether such systems can run on local devices without GPUs within acceptable latency.
AI.result: The experiments show that RAG systems powered by small language models can indeed be executed directly on end-user devices, without any GPU hardware, while still answering queries in a reasonable time. Empirical results across diverse datasets support this claim.
AI.conclusion: Small language models, when combined with retrieval in a RAG setting, can be practical and efficient for on-device use, reducing dependency on large, GPU-intensive models. The released code and supplementary materials allow others to reproduce and extend the evaluation of small language models for RAG-based applications.

title: From Detecting Agency to Doing Work: Self-Caused Credit Builds a Durable Behavioral Self in a Minimal Spiking Agent
comment: 22 pages, 6 figures. Includes supplementary information in the same PDF
summary: How does an agent that can tell self from world come to be durably shaped by that distinction? Recent work shows that a predictive system can detect its own agency (Ye, 2026), but detecting agency does not explain durable, self-shaped behavior. We show that agency-gated slow credit -- a conjunctive term Own*Agency*Salience driving a slow parameter update -- produces post-unload behavioral residue: on a spiking substrate (Nengo LIF/PES), a learned self-preserving choice survives episodic buffer removal (retained fraction 0.96, N=50) and collapses when the slow decoders are reset or the agency gate is removed. Reproducing the agency comparator and toggling only the slow-credit channel, we find a clean dissociation: at matched agency gain, durable behavior develops only when self-credit performs slow work (post-unload self-preservation 1.00 vs 0.00). The same dissociation holds in 24-dimensional partially-observed control (0.74 vs 0.00), and a plastic-work analysis shows that basin deformation equals net self-credit work. Across eight sequentially-learned tasks under exogenous interference, the multiplicative veto also prevents forgetting: it retains old tasks (final post-unload accuracy 0.88, forgetting 0.13) where additive pooling collapses to chance-level recall, the no-agency ablation falls below chance, and episodic/replay baselines stay near chance after unload -- all with no replay buffer and no task-boundary-dependent protection mechanism (N=50). We formalize the durable residue as an operational behavioral self and argue that self-caused credit doing slow work is a necessary building block for agents that develop a self. No claim of consciousness is made.

AI.tldr: The paper shows how a predictive agent that can distinguish self from world can develop durable, self-shaped behavior via an 'agency-gated slow credit' mechanism that leaves long-lasting behavioral residues even after episodic memory is removed.
AI.motivation: While previous work demonstrated that predictive systems can detect their own agency, this does not explain how such systems become durably shaped by that distinction, i.e., how they acquire stable self-preserving behaviors rather than just transient agency detection. The paper is motivated by the need to understand mechanisms that turn momentary self–world distinctions into lasting changes in behavior, without relying on explicit replay buffers or task-boundary heuristics.
AI.method: They implement an 'agency-gated slow credit' mechanism on a spiking neural substrate (Nengo LIF/PES). This mechanism uses a multiplicative term Own*Agency*Salience to drive slow parameter updates (slow decoders) only when events are self-caused and salient. They reproduce an agency comparator from prior work, selectively toggle the slow-credit channel, and run experiments on simple self-preserving choices and 24-dimensional partially observed control tasks. They also analyze plastic work, relating basin deformation in parameter space to net self-credit work. Finally, they run eight sequential tasks with exogenous interference to test resistance to forgetting, comparing multiplicative veto vs additive pooling, no-agency ablations, and replay/episodic baselines.
AI.result: With agency-gated slow credit, a learned self-preserving choice persists even after removing the episodic buffer (retained fraction 0.96, N=50), but collapses if slow decoders are reset or the agency gate is removed. When holding agency gain constant and only toggling slow credit, durable behavior appears only when self-credit does slow work (post-unload self-preservation 1.00 vs 0.00). This pattern repeats in a 24D partially observed control task (0.74 vs 0.00). Plastic-work analysis shows that deformation of the behavioral basin matches net self-credit work. Across eight sequential tasks under interference, the multiplicative veto retains past tasks well (final post-unload accuracy 0.88, forgetting 0.13), whereas additive pooling, no-agency ablation, and replay/episodic baselines fall to chance or below after unload, despite the absence of replay buffers or task-boundary protections.
AI.conclusion: The authors define the long-lasting, post-unload behavioral residue created by agency-gated slow credit as an operational 'behavioral self.' They argue that self-caused credit performing slow work is a necessary building block for agents that can develop a self, in the sense of stable, self-preserving behavioral dispositions. They emphasize that they are not making any claim about consciousness, only about a mechanistic foundation for durable, self-shaped behavior.

title: Not-quite-human tastes: the stylized omnivorousness of LLM survey surrogates
summary: Large-language models have proven to be remarkable if inconsistent parrots of public attitudes and opinions. The extent to which LLMs are able to produce reasonable approximations of cultural taste remains an open empirical question that becomes more urgent by the day, with market research companies already offering provisional `synthetic' survey panels and the contamination of standard survey data from LLM-generated responses. In this study, we build on past work on silicon sampling by extending considerations of its algorithmic fidelity and alignment to the domain of cultural consumption. We use large-language models from OpenAI, Anthropic, and DeepSeek to each produce 277,470 (30x9249) silicon surrogates of survey respondents from the Survey of Public Participation in the Arts (SPPA). We find these silicon surrogates' tastes to be highly stylized facsimiles of human tastes. (1) Silicon samples have a systematic postive-bias for liking, resulting in inflated ecological estimates of tastes. The individual-level bias of silicon samples are not well-explained by the WEIRD-bias often discussed in the literature. (2) The complex relationality in real taste structures is completely lost among silicon samples. (3) Finally, very little of the known cultural alignment between tastes and social space are preserved. Silicon samples attenuate age-taste associations, resurrect anachronistic class-taste associations, caricaturize gender- and race-taste associations.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates how well large language models (LLMs) can mimic human cultural tastes using synthetic survey respondents, finding that LLM-generated “silicon surrogates” produce distorted, overly positive, and socially misaligned taste patterns compared to real survey data.
AI.motivation: LLMs are already being used or proposed for `synthetic' survey panels in market research, and they may also contaminate real survey data. However, it is unknown whether LLMs can accurately reproduce human cultural tastes and their social structuring. The authors aim to empirically test the reliability and social fidelity of LLM-based silicon sampling in the domain of cultural consumption.
AI.method: The authors generate 277,470 synthetic respondents (`silicon surrogates') using multiple LLMs (from OpenAI, Anthropic, and DeepSeek), each intended to mimic real respondents in the Survey of Public Participation in the Arts (SPPA). They then compare the LLM-generated cultural taste data with actual survey data, examining ecological estimates of liking, individual-level biases, the relational structure of tastes, and how taste aligns with social attributes such as age, class, gender, and race.
AI.result: 1) LLM silicon samples systematically overstate liking for cultural items, producing inflated estimates of tastes; this bias is not adequately explained by the usual WEIRD-bias argument. 2) The nuanced relational structure found in real human taste patterns is absent in LLM outputs. 3) The known associations between cultural tastes and social structures (age, class, gender, race) are poorly preserved: age-taste links are weakened, outdated class-taste patterns reappear, and gender/race associations are exaggerated into caricatures.
AI.conclusion: LLM-based silicon sampling performs poorly as a stand-in for real human cultural taste data. While LLMs can generate superficially plausible responses, they fail to capture actual levels of liking, the complex structure of tastes, and their alignment with social position. As a result, using LLMs as synthetic survey respondents for cultural consumption research or market research is likely to produce systematically biased and socially misleading findings.

title: Domain Adaptation with Adaptive Imagination for Visual Reinforcement Learning under Limited Target Data
summary: Sim-to-real transfer remains a major obstacle for reinforcement learning (RL), especially for vision-based control where image observations exacerbate the state-distribution shift between simulation and the real world. Domain adaptation (DA) is a promising remedy for this challenge. Prior sim-to-real DA works have demonstrated encouraging results, yet these approaches typically assume substantially more target data, which is not available in practice. Indeed, their performance degrades significantly when the target data budget is reduced. To address this challenge, we propose AIDA (Adaptive Imagination for Domain Adaptation), a domain adaptation framework for visual reinforcement learning that addresses sim-to-real transfer under scarce target data without requiring additional interaction with the target environment. Our key idea is adaptive imagination: generating reliable and semantic imagination rollouts to augment limited target data. Specifically, AIDA employs a distribution-shift-aware discriminator that truncates rollouts when imagined transitions drift into low-confidence regions, so that only reliable transitions contribute to the augmentation. On these reliable transitions, AIDA introduces a self-consistency loss that cycles through state -> image observation -> state, penalizing discrepancies between the original and reconstructed states. This provides additional adaptation signals beyond the scarce target data. Our experiments demonstrate that adaptive imagination effectively truncates unreliable rollouts. By enforcing a self-consistency loss on the resulting reliable transitions, AIDA learns semantically meaningful state representations and outperforms baselines across five MuJoCo tasks and two Gymnasium-Robotics tasks.

AI.tldr: AIDA is a domain adaptation framework for vision-based reinforcement learning that improves sim-to-real transfer under very limited real-world data by generating and filtering imagined rollouts and enforcing a self-consistency constraint on reliable ones.
