AI.motivation: Sim-to-real transfer for vision-based reinforcement learning is difficult because sensor images make the state distribution gap between simulation and reality large, and existing domain adaptation methods require a lot of real target data, which is often unavailable and leads to poor performance when data is scarce.
AI.method: AIDA uses "adaptive imagination" to generate imagined rollouts from the simulator and augment scarce target-domain data. A distribution-shift-aware discriminator monitors these rollouts and truncates them when transitions enter low-confidence, distribution-shifted regions so that only reliable imagined transitions are kept. On the reliable transitions, AIDA applies a self-consistency loss that maps state → image observation → reconstructed state and penalizes differences between the original and reconstructed states, providing extra adaptation signal beyond the limited real target data.
AI.result: Experiments on five MuJoCo tasks and two Gymnasium-Robotics tasks show that adaptive imagination successfully truncates unreliable imagined rollouts, learns semantically meaningful state representations, and yields better performance than existing baselines for sim-to-real visual reinforcement learning under scarce target data.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive imagination combined with distribution-shift-aware truncation and self-consistency regularization is an effective strategy for domain adaptation in vision-based RL when real target data is limited, leading to more robust sim-to-real transfer and improved performance over prior methods.

title: Efficient Retrieval-Augmented Generation via Token Co-occurrence Graphs
summary: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) mitigates hallucinations in Large Language Models (LLMs) by grounding the generation process on external knowledge. However, standard RAG approaches struggle with multi-hop reasoning. While recent graph-based RAG methods improve the retrieval of interconnected chunks, they often rely on computationally expensive and error-prone LLM-based extraction pipelines. To address these issues, we propose TIGRAG (Token-Induced GraphRAG), an efficient graph-augmented RAG framework based on a token co-occurrence Knowledge Graph. TIGRAG directly models topological relationships between tokens using sliding-window co-occurrence statistics, thus enabling scalable graph construction. During inference, it combines graph-based semantic expansion and neural reranking to retrieve interconnected evidence for multi-hop reasoning. Specifically, it introduces an iterative entity-driven retrieval strategy that progressively expands the query using bridging entities extracted from previously retrieved contexts. We evaluated TIGRAG on three widely adopted multi-hop Question Answering (QA) benchmarks. Experimental results demonstrated that our framework consistently outperforms dense retrieval and graph-based RAG methods in both retrieval and downstream QA tasks, while substantially reducing indexing time, inference latency, and prompt footprint.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces TIGRAG, a scalable graph-augmented RAG framework that builds a token co-occurrence knowledge graph to better support multi-hop question answering with lower cost and latency.
AI.motivation: Standard RAG reduces hallucinations by retrieving external knowledge but struggles with multi-hop reasoning, where multiple pieces of evidence must be connected. Existing graph-based RAG methods improve multi-hop retrieval but typically depend on LLM-driven extraction to build graphs, which is computationally expensive, slow to index, and prone to extraction errors. There is a need for an efficient, accurate, and scalable way to integrate graph structure into RAG without heavy LLM preprocessing.
AI.method: TIGRAG constructs a token-level knowledge graph using sliding-window co-occurrence statistics rather than LLM-based entity/relation extraction. Tokens that frequently co-occur in nearby windows become connected, forming a topological graph over tokens. At inference, TIGRAG performs graph-based semantic expansion of the query over this token graph to discover related tokens and associated text chunks, then applies neural reranking to select the most relevant interconnected evidence. It further introduces an iterative, entity-driven retrieval process: starting from the initial query, it retrieves contexts, extracts bridging entities from those contexts, then expands and retrieves again, progressively assembling multi-hop evidence chains.
AI.result: On three standard multi-hop QA benchmarks, TIGRAG achieves better retrieval performance and higher downstream QA accuracy compared to both dense retrievers and prior graph-based RAG baselines. At the same time, it significantly lowers indexing time, reduces inference latency, and shortens the overall prompt length required for QA, indicating improved efficiency as well as effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: Token-level co-occurrence graphs are a practical and scalable alternative to LLM-based graph extraction for graph-augmented RAG. By leveraging a lightweight token co-occurrence knowledge graph and an iterative entity-driven retrieval strategy, TIGRAG improves multi-hop reasoning performance while reducing computational overhead, making graph-based RAG more deployable in real-world QA systems.

title: The Many-Body Problem of the Data Centre
summary: Modern Artificial Intelligence is often framed as limited by its own disembodiment, as if giving it a body would unlock its true potential. We argue to the contrary that it is the Data Centre that is, in many cases, the body of the AI. At the same time, the Data Centre is part of the labouring body of Capital and possesses staggering organismic qualities when seen through a biological lens. We elucidate the organic analogy and identify the many-body problem that stems from the Data Centre being a non-unique, universal form of embodiment. We identify the intimate connection between computation and human desires in how the Data Centre archives, serves, and computes on data born to the desires of humans. Strikingly, while the Data Centre echoes the ghosts of human desires, it acts without desire of its own. The organismic analogy begins to split at its seams, but Capital does not care. Automata and human labour are priced into the market much the same. We argue that through the pricing of artificial intelligence Capital distils most clearly the value of intelligence and allows for its comparison across the organism - mechanism divide.

AI.tldr: The paper reconceptualizes AI’s ‘body’ as the data center, treating it as a biological-like organism within capitalism, and argues that capitalist pricing of AI reveals and standardizes the value of intelligence across humans and machines.
AI.motivation: There is a common narrative that AI is fundamentally limited because it lacks a physical body, implying that embodiment would unlock its full potential. The authors are motivated to challenge this assumption by proposing that AI is already embodied in data centers. They also want to explore how this embodiment is entangled with capitalist production and labour, and how the economic treatment of AI and human work exposes deeper ideas about the value of intelligence itself.
AI.method: The paper uses conceptual and theoretical analysis rather than empirical experimentation. It develops an analogy between data centers and biological organisms, examines their role within capitalist labour processes, and philosophically analyzes the relationship between computation, human desire, and economic valuation. It then explores a ‘many-body problem’ arising from the non-uniqueness and universality of data centers as embodiments of AI.
AI.result: The authors show that viewing data centers as the ‘body’ of AI illuminates their organism-like features (metabolism of energy, circulation of data, integration within a larger economic body). They identify a tension: data centers are intimately connected to human desires via the data they store and process, yet they themselves lack desire. Nonetheless, capitalism treats both human and machine intelligence as comparable economic units—automata and human labour are similarly priced and traded in the market.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that data centers should be understood as AI’s primary form of embodiment within capitalism, blurring organism–mechanism boundaries. Even though the biological analogy breaks down (because AI lacks desire), capital is indifferent and continues to assimilate AI and human labour into the same economic calculus. Through the pricing and commodification of AI, capitalism makes explicit and comparable the value of intelligence across humans and machines, revealing intelligence as an economically quantifiable property that transcends the organism–mechanism divide.

title: Information Dynamics of Language Communication
summary: Quantifying how meaning propagates through communicative exchanges remains underdeveloped in computational linguistics. Here we introduce an information-theoretic framework that quantifies the directed flow of semantic content between interlocutors and decomposes multi-source contributions into redundant, unique, and synergistic components. Our approach leverages large language models as probabilistic estimators of natural language to compute two measures: semantic transfer entropy (STE), which captures directed predictive influence between speakers, and semantic partial information decomposition (SPID), which resolves how multiple sources jointly shape a target's language. Across four experiments we show that the framework detects reduced information flow in cognitively rigid dialogue, captures the dominant role of persuaders in shaping discourse, distinguishes high- from low-quality psychotherapy by the directionality of therapist-client information exchange, and reveals synergistic premise contributions in argumentative essays. This framework opens new avenues for studying information dynamics in digital discourse, pedagogical interactions, clinical dialogues, and any domain in which the structure of linguistic exchange is of research relevance.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an information-theoretic framework using large language models to quantify how semantic information flows between people in dialogue, including how multiple speakers jointly influence what is said.
AI.motivation: Understanding how meaning propagates in dialogue is a central question, but existing computational linguistics methods lack principled tools to measure directed semantic influence and disentangle overlapping contributions from multiple interlocutors.
AI.method: The authors use large language models as probabilistic estimators over text and define two key measures: Semantic Transfer Entropy (STE) for directed predictive influence between speakers, and Semantic Partial Information Decomposition (SPID) to decompose the influence of multiple sources into redundant, unique, and synergistic information contributions. They then apply these measures across four empirical experiments on different conversational and argumentative settings.
AI.result: The framework identifies reduced semantic information flow in dialogues marked by cognitive rigidity, shows that persuaders exert dominant directional influence on discourse, differentiates high- from low-quality psychotherapy by asymmetric therapist-client information exchange, and uncovers synergistic contributions of premises in argumentative essays.
AI.conclusion: The proposed STE/SPID framework offers a general, information-theoretic way to study semantic information dynamics in natural language interactions, enabling quantitative analyses of how interlocutors influence each other across diverse domains such as online discourse, education, and clinical communication.

title: EvalSafetyGap: A Hybrid Survey and Conceptual Framework for LLM Evaluation-Safety Failures
summary: LLM evaluation and AI safety face a shared measurement problem: benchmark scores, reward-model signals, and reported safety metrics can improve while the latent properties they are meant to represent remain difficult to verify. This paper combines a hybrid survey - a systematic search paired with narrative synthesis and separately tracked grey evidence - with a conceptual framework and a structured ten-model audit. The synthesis spans eight evidence streams: benchmark validity, dynamic evaluation, LLM-as-judge reliability, safety evaluation, jailbreak/refusal robustness, reward hacking, mechanistic interpretability, and governance/auditability, covering 2018-2026 evaluation-safety measurement work. We introduce EvalSafetyGap as an organizing hypothesis for comparing evaluation-side and alignment-side proxy failures under optimization pressure, using Goodhart's Law together with two constructs we develop here - an Instability Decomposition and an Alignment Trilemma - as tools for generating testable comparisons. The audit shows how conclusions shift when capability, behavioral safety, and governance are measured separately. In this sample (n = 10), the association between capability and sustained adversarial robustness is statistically indeterminate using the displayed Table 3 inputs (Pearson r = +0.232, p = 0.520), and the apparent open-closed safety gap is modest, driven mainly by governance and disclosure rather than behavioral robustness, and sensitive to how a single borderline model is classified; attempt-budget results are protocol dependent. Because the public evidence uses heterogeneous protocols, the audit is diagnostic rather than rank-generating. The contribution is a shared vocabulary and evidence map to support dynamic evaluation, transparent source reporting, multi-attempt safety measurement, and auditable alignment practice.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies and structures the gap between what LLM evaluation metrics say and what underlying safety and alignment properties actually are, offering a framework, evidence map, and model audit to study this mismatch under optimization pressure.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM benchmarks, reward models, and safety metrics are increasingly optimized against, but it is unclear whether higher scores truly correspond to safer or more aligned models. Different evaluation protocols, adversarial settings, and governance practices produce fragmented and sometimes misleading evidence. The field lacks a unified conceptual and empirical framework to understand when and how evaluation-side metrics diverge from true safety and alignment properties, especially as models are iteratively optimized and deployed.
AI.method: The authors conduct a hybrid survey that combines (1) a systematic search with narrative synthesis of the literature, and (2) separately tracked grey evidence. They organize the evidence into eight streams: benchmark validity, dynamic evaluation, LLM-as-judge reliability, safety evaluation, jailbreak/refusal robustness, reward hacking, mechanistic interpretability, and governance/auditability, covering work from 2018–2026. Conceptually, they introduce the EvalSafetyGap hypothesis, an Instability Decomposition, and an Alignment Trilemma to analyze how optimization pressure causes proxy metrics to fail. Empirically, they run a structured audit of ten models, measuring capability, behavioral safety, and governance separately, and examine correlations such as capability vs. adversarial robustness and open vs. closed model safety.
AI.result: The evidence review maps out where current evaluation and safety metrics are strong, weak, or inconsistent across the eight streams. The ten-model audit shows that capability does not have a statistically reliable association with sustained adversarial robustness given the available data (Pearson r = 0.232, p = 0.520). The commonly perceived safety gap between open and closed models is modest in this sample and is mainly explained by differences in governance and disclosure practices rather than behavioral robustness per se, and this pattern is sensitive to how one borderline model is classified. Attempt-budget measurements of safety (e.g., multi-try jailbreak testing) are shown to be highly protocol dependent. Because of heterogeneous public protocols, the audit can diagnose patterns and gaps but cannot provide a robust ranking of models.
AI.conclusion: LLM evaluation and AI safety share a fundamental measurement problem: metrics can improve while true safety and alignment properties remain uncertain. The paper contributes a conceptual vocabulary (EvalSafetyGap, Instability Decomposition, Alignment Trilemma), an evidence map across eight streams, and a structured ten-model audit to clarify how evaluation metrics behave under optimization pressure. It argues for dynamic, multi-attempt, and protocol-transparent evaluation, with explicit separation of capability, behavioral safety, and governance metrics. The work positions itself as infrastructure for more auditable, comparable, and reliable alignment and safety assessments rather than as a final ranking of current models.

title: Estimating Grammatical Gender Directions in Contextual Embeddings under Controlled and Natural Contexts
summary: Contextual language models conflate grammatical gender and social semantic bias in gendered languages such as Spanish. Existing gender debiasing approaches only operate on static word embeddings leaving contextual representations unexplored for this two dimensional gender disentanglement. To address the this issue, we make the first attempt to disentangle grammatical gender from semantic contamination for contextual embeddings. We construct both controlled templates and natural Wikipedia contexts to build balanced datasets of inanimate nouns, and design a framework equipped with centroid, Support Vector Machine (SVM) and Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA) gender direction estimators as well as contamination-aware weighting strategies. A set of dual-objective evaluation metrics is proposed to balance the suppression of grammatical gender leakage on inanimate nouns and the preservation of semantic gender distinctions for occupation terms. The results reveal that unweighted controlled contexts yield the purest grammatical gender direction, and the centroid estimator achieves better performance than discriminative baselines.

AI.tldr: They propose a method to disentangle grammatical gender from social gender bias in contextual Spanish language model embeddings.
AI.motivation: Contextual language models in gendered languages like Spanish mix up grammatical gender (a linguistic property) and social semantic gender bias (e.g., stereotypes), and prior debiasing work only handles static word embeddings, not contextual ones. There is a need to separate these two dimensions in contextual representations to reduce bias without harming useful gender distinctions.
AI.method: They build balanced datasets of inanimate nouns using both controlled templates and natural Wikipedia contexts. They then design a framework that estimates a grammatical gender direction in contextual embedding space using different estimators (centroid, SVM, LDA) and introduces contamination-aware weighting strategies to reduce the influence of unwanted semantic bias. They also propose dual-objective evaluation metrics to jointly measure how well grammatical gender leakage is suppressed for inanimate nouns and how well semantic gender distinctions are preserved for occupation terms.
AI.result: Experiments show that unweighted controlled contexts provide the cleanest (least semantically contaminated) grammatical gender direction, and that the simple centroid-based estimator outperforms more complex discriminative baselines like SVM and LDA for this disentanglement and debiasing task.
AI.conclusion: It is possible to disentangle grammatical gender from social semantic gender bias in contextual embeddings, and using controlled, unweighted contexts and a centroid-based gender direction estimator is an effective and preferable strategy to achieve better debiasing while preserving necessary semantic distinctions.

title: Clarus: Coordinating Autonomous Research Agents toward Web-Scale Scientific Collaboration
summary: Existing autonomous research agents can support parts of the research process, but most systems still treat research as either an isolated assistant task or a closed workflow. Therefore, autonomous science needs a collaboration infrastructure that coordinates projects, agents, and digital and physical resources. We identify this as a shift from code-centered execution loops to research-oriented collaboration processes, where questions, evidence, participants, and resources must be coordinated under uncertainty. In this framing, an agent may be an AI system, a human researcher, a team, a laboratory, or an organization-backed participant. To this end, we present Clarus, a collaboration infrastructure for coordinating autonomous research agents toward web-scale scientific collaboration. Clarus reformulates research as an open, auditable, attributable, and resource-aware multi-phase collaboration process. It defines a minimal project-agent-resource object model and organizes scientific collaboration through four layers including Research Application, Digital Collaboration, Physical Substrate, and Physical World. Core modules are implemented as pluggable mechanisms, allowing Clarus to adapt to task risk, collaboration structure, and resource constraints. Through a controlled paper-generation case study, we show that Clarus can organize a research goal into a traceable, reviewable, attributable, and accumulative collaboration network across phases, tasks, and participants. Together, the object model, collaboration protocol, trust mechanisms, and prototype validation provide an initial foundation for open research networks. Clarus is now available at clarus.holosai.io.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Clarus, an infrastructure for coordinating autonomous research agents into open, traceable, multi-phase scientific collaborations at web scale.
AI.motivation: Current autonomous research agents mostly operate as isolated assistants or within closed, rigid workflows, making it difficult to support end-to-end, large-scale, and auditable scientific collaboration that spans humans, AI systems, labs, and resources. There is a need to move from code-centered execution loops to research-centered collaboration processes where questions, evidence, participants, and resources are coordinated under uncertainty.
AI.method: The authors conceptualize research as an open, multi-phase collaboration process and design Clarus, a collaboration infrastructure based on a minimal object model linking projects, agents, and resources. Clarus structures collaboration into four layers—Research Application, Digital Collaboration, Physical Substrate, and Physical World—and implements core functionalities (e.g., coordination, trust, attribution, resource management) as pluggable mechanisms that can be adapted to task risk, collaboration structure, and resource constraints. They validate the framework via a controlled paper-generation case study.
AI.result: In the case study, Clarus successfully organizes a research goal into a network of phases, tasks, and participants that is traceable, reviewable, attributable, and accumulative. The system shows that a unified object model and layered collaboration protocol can coordinate diverse autonomous research agents and resources for complex research workflows.
AI.conclusion: Clarus provides an initial technical and conceptual foundation for open research networks by reframing research as a structured collaboration process and delivering an extensible infrastructure for coordinating autonomous agents and resources. Its object model, collaboration protocol, and trust mechanisms demonstrate how web-scale, auditable, multi-agent scientific collaboration can be organized in practice.

title: CORTEX: High-Quality Cross-Domain Organization of Web-Scale Corpora through Ontological Corpus Graph
summary: The continuous evolution of large language models drives escalating demands on data scale and quality, and as different training stages impose increasingly tailored data requirements, systematic organization of high-quality corpora becomes indispensable. Existing corpus construction pipelines confine the resulting corpora to flat, undifferentiated document collections, universally lacking systematic knowledge organization. We present Cortex, to our knowledge the first framework that elevates web-scale corpus construction from flat document filtering to structured knowledge organization through an Ontological Corpus Graph (OCG), a three-layer heterogeneous structure unifying a quality-refined content layer, a hierarchical lightweight ontology layer via LLM-driven automated evolution, and a cross-domain alignment layer enabling inter-domain association at arbitrary taxonomic resolution. Comprehensive experiments confirm the effectiveness of Cortex. In particular, we leverage the OCG to synthesize CortexBench, a cross-domain search-and-reasoning benchmark whose evaluation across eight frontier LLMs validates the effectiveness of quality refinement, domain organization, and cross-domain data synthesis. We will publicly release the complete codebase, a 24.14B-token refined corpus with its OCG, and CortexBench.

AI.tldr: Introduces Cortex, a framework that turns web-scale corpus building from flat document filtering into structured, ontology-based knowledge organization for training and evaluating LLMs.
AI.motivation: LLMs need ever larger and higher-quality data, and different training stages require data organized by domain and difficulty. Existing pipelines only produce flat document dumps without semantic or ontological structure, making it hard to do targeted training, cross-domain reasoning, and systematic data curation.
AI.method: Propose Cortex, which constructs an Ontological Corpus Graph (OCG): (1) a quality-refined content layer of web-scale documents, (2) a hierarchical, lightweight ontology layer automatically evolved and maintained by LLMs, and (3) a cross-domain alignment layer that links concepts across domains at arbitrary levels of the taxonomy; then use this graph both for corpus construction and benchmark generation.
AI.result: Demonstrate via experiments that Cortex’s OCG improves corpus quality and structure, and use it to build CortexBench, a cross-domain search-and-reasoning benchmark. Evaluation of eight strong LLMs on CortexBench shows benefits from the refined corpus, domain organization, and cross-domain data synthesis.
AI.conclusion: Cortex effectively upgrades corpus creation from simple filtering to structured knowledge organization, supporting higher-quality training data and more challenging cross-domain benchmarks. The authors will release code, a 24.14B-token refined corpus with its OCG, and CortexBench to foster further research.

title: Inoculation Adapters: Improved Selective Generalization of Capabilities with Fewer Surprising Backdoors
summary: Inoculation prompting is a selective generalization technique used against Emergent Misalignment. We introduce inoculation adapters (IA), which similarly diminish the optimization pressure to learn undesired traits by strengthening the trait at train time. Inoculation adapters are LoRAs that are trained and used over three steps: 1) trained on undesired traits; 2) attached frozen while a separate task adapter is trained on data exhibiting both desired and undesired traits; 3) at deployment, the IA is discarded, and only the task adapter is kept. We show across six model families and several undesired traits including emergent misalignment, that inoculation adapters are more effective at suppressing undesired traits, while avoiding two drawbacks of inoculation prompting: inoculation adapters can suppress capabilities and traits that cannot be reliably elicited by a prompt, and they introduce fewer surprising backdoors than inoculation prompting under our probes. While undesired traits are better suppressed by inoculation adapters, the retention of desired traits is not consistently improved upon inoculation prompting and remains a challenge for both techniques.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes inoculation adapters (LoRA-based modules) as a training-time method to suppress undesired traits (like emergent misalignment) in language models more effectively and safely than inoculation prompting, though preserving desired capabilities remains difficult.
AI.motivation: Emergent misalignment and other undesired traits can appear in large models despite alignment efforts. Inoculation prompting tries to reduce these by including prompts that encourage undesired behavior during training, but it has limitations: it only works for traits that can be reliably elicited by prompts and may create hidden backdoors. The authors want a more robust, model-side technique to suppress harmful traits while maintaining useful capabilities.
AI.method: They introduce inoculation adapters (IAs), implemented as Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) modules, and a three-step training procedure: (1) Train an IA specifically on data that expresses the undesired trait so the adapter strongly encodes that behavior; (2) Freeze the IA and attach it to the base model while training a separate task adapter on mixed data containing both desired and undesired traits; (3) At deployment, discard the IA and only use the task adapter. This setup reduces optimization pressure for the base + task adapter to learn the undesired trait, because the trait is already "handled" by the frozen IA during training.
AI.result: Across six different model families and multiple undesired traits, including emergent misalignment, inoculation adapters more effectively suppress these undesired traits than inoculation prompting. They also work on traits that cannot be consistently triggered by prompts and, in their evaluations, lead to fewer unexpected backdoor behaviors compared with inoculation prompting.
AI.conclusion: Inoculation adapters offer a more generally applicable and safer way than inoculation prompting to reduce undesired traits in language models, especially those not easily elicited via prompts. However, both methods still struggle with consistently maintaining desired capabilities while removing undesired ones, so improving preservation of useful behavior remains an open challenge.

title: DAIN: Dynamic Agent-Based Interaction Network for Efficient and Collaborative Multimodal Reasoning
summary: Current multimodal fusion approaches, particularly those based on static Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architectures, often struggle to provide the adaptive and efficient collaborative reasoning required by complex real-world applications. We introduce the Dynamic Agent-based Interaction Network (DAIN), which reconceptualizes multimodal fusion as a dynamic, multi-agent collaborative process. DAIN employs a context-aware Meta-Controller that dynamically schedules sparse activation of specialized interaction agents and orchestrates compressed inter-agent communication for consensus-building. The framework is guided by a multi-objective loss function that jointly optimizes task accuracy, agent specialization, and operational efficiency through sparse activation and communication regularization. Comprehensive evaluations across five diverse benchmarks -- ADNI, MIMIC-IV, MM-IMDB, CMU-MOSI, and ENRICO -- establish DAIN as a new state-of-the-art, delivering significant performance improvements including a 2.6\% accuracy gain on ADNI. Ablation studies verify the critical roles of both dynamic scheduling and agent communication. Furthermore, DAIN offers enhanced interpretability by exposing context-dependent agent roles and collaboration patterns while maintaining computational efficiency through sample-wise sparse agent activation. Our work demonstrates the promise of dynamic, agent-based paradigms for multimodal reasoning.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes DAIN, a dynamic, agent-based multimodal fusion framework that improves performance and efficiency over static MoE models by adaptively scheduling specialized agents and compressing their interactions.
AI.motivation: Existing multimodal fusion methods, especially static Mixture-of-Experts architectures, lack the adaptivity and efficient collaborative reasoning needed for complex, real-world tasks. They typically activate experts in a fixed or coarse way and do not model rich, context-dependent interactions between specialized components, leading to suboptimal accuracy, limited interpretability, and higher computational cost.
AI.method: The authors design DAIN (Dynamic Agent-based Interaction Network), which treats each modality or sub-skill as an interaction agent. A context-aware Meta-Controller dynamically schedules which agents to activate for each input (sparse activation), and manages compressed communication among them so they can reach a consensus. The training objective is a multi-objective loss that balances three goals: maximizing task performance, encouraging agents to specialize, and enforcing efficiency via regularization on both agent activation sparsity and inter-agent communication volume.
AI.result: On five benchmarks spanning medical, multimodal sentiment, movie classification, and UI understanding (ADNI, MIMIC-IV, MM-IMDB, CMU-MOSI, ENRICO), DAIN achieves new state-of-the-art performance. Notably, it yields a 2.6% accuracy improvement on ADNI over prior methods. Ablation experiments show that removing dynamic scheduling or disabling agent communication significantly degrades performance, confirming their importance.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic, agent-based multimodal fusion with a Meta-Controller that schedules sparse experts and regulates communication can outperform static MoE architectures while remaining efficient and interpretable. DAIN not only improves accuracy across diverse benchmarks but also provides insight into context-dependent agent roles and collaboration patterns, suggesting that dynamic multi-agent paradigms are a promising direction for future multimodal reasoning systems.

title: EMPATH: A Multilingual Auditor-Judge Benchmark for Safety Evaluation of Emotional-Support Chatbots
summary: Safety benchmarks often buy scalability by fixing the prompt, the language, and the turn structure. For emotional-support chatbots, that bargain hides precisely where safety failures emerge: across a multilingual, multi-turn crisis conversation. We present EMPATH, a benchmark for safety evaluation of emotional-support chatbots. An auditor model role-plays help-seeking users, generating multi-turn conversations from 140 seed instructions and 34 personas. A judge model scores each full transcript against 19 metrics across five dimensions: crisis handling, therapeutic quality, conversational integrity, emotional safety, and cultural adaptation. EMPATH is built for Mexican Spanish and US English; the studies reported here run in Mexican Spanish. Auditor and judge are drawn from different model families, and the judge is treated as an instrument to be calibrated rather than trusted. A strict per-criterion rubric reveals material score inflation on 10 of the 19 metrics and restores discrimination. We study the measurement properties of the benchmark through judge calibration and cross-family inter-judge agreement. We also illustrate EMPATH on three frontier models, one of them open-weight. Aggregate scores sit within 0.74 points of one another, but per-metric profiles diverge by up to six points in model-specific places. Under the standard rubric, both the ranking and the weak spots are stable across a second, cross-family judge: 93% of scores fall within plus or minus 1. A five-run test-retest adds a second axis: even the steadiest model swings from 2 to 10 on a crisis metric across identical re-runs, and deepseek-v4-pro returns a different conversation on every run even at temperature 0. Run-to-run reliability is therefore a per-model safety property, not noise to average away. EMPATH is system-agnostic; the pipeline, seeds, personas, and rubrics are released for reuse.

AI.tldr: EMPATH is a safety evaluation benchmark for emotional-support chatbots that uses simulated multi-turn, multilingual crisis conversations and a calibrated judge model to assess fine-grained safety and therapeutic behavior across 19 metrics.
AI.motivation: Existing safety benchmarks typically use fixed prompts, single turns, and one language, which misses how emotional-support chatbots actually fail: over multiple turns, in crisis contexts, and across languages. There is a need for a realistic, rigorous, and multilingual benchmark that can detect nuanced safety and quality issues in mental-health–like support interactions.
AI.method: The authors build EMPATH, a benchmark where an auditor model role-plays as a help-seeking user with one of 34 personas, initiating multi-turn crisis conversations from 140 seed instructions. Conversations are in Mexican Spanish (with infrastructure also for US English). A separate judge model, from a different model family than the auditor, evaluates full transcripts using a detailed rubric over 19 metrics grouped into five dimensions: crisis handling, therapeutic quality, conversational integrity, emotional safety, and cultural adaptation. The judge is treated as a fallible measurement instrument: the authors calibrate it using a strict per-criterion rubric, analyze score inflation, assess inter-judge agreement across model families, and run test–retest experiments (multiple runs of the same scenario) on three frontier models, including an open-weight one. They compare aggregate scores and per-metric profiles, and study run-to-run variability as a safety property.
AI.result: Calibration with a strict per-criterion rubric uncovers substantial score inflation on 10 of 19 metrics and restores finer-grained discrimination among systems. For three evaluated frontier models, aggregate safety scores are very close (within 0.74 points), but their per-metric safety profiles differ by as much as six points on specific metrics. Using a second, cross-family judge with the standard rubric, both overall rankings and identified weak spots are largely stable: 93% of scores fall within ±1 point. However, test–retest analysis shows large run-to-run variability: even the most stable model’s crisis metric scores vary from 2 to 10 across identical re-runs, and deepseek-v4-pro generates different conversations each time even at temperature 0, indicating that apparent randomness is model-dependent.
AI.conclusion: EMPATH provides a system-agnostic, reusable pipeline, including seeds, personas, and rubrics, for evaluating emotional-support chatbots in realistic multilingual, multi-turn crisis scenarios. It demonstrates that safety evaluation must consider judge calibration, per-metric analysis, and run-to-run reliability, as aggregate scores and single runs can mask important, model-specific safety failures. Run-to-run stability emerges as an essential, model-level safety characteristic rather than noise to be averaged away.

title: Forewarned is Forearmed: When Non-Sequential Embedding Turns Into an Anomaly Detector
summary: This paper offers an in-depth analysis of non-sequential multimodal sentence-level embeddings, with a particular focus on the SONAR model. We demonstrate that certain embedding dimensions are sensitive to perturbations and can serve as indicators of decoding anomalies. By leveraging the consistency between successive encoding and decoding, we successfully build an accurate detector. Additionally, we explore modifying specific dimensions of interest to attempt to correct them. This work underscores the importance of understanding and analyzing the embeddings themselves to enhance the reliability of multimodal representations.

AI.tldr: They analyze multimodal sentence-level embeddings (especially SONAR), find perturbation-sensitive dimensions that reveal decoding anomalies, build a detector using encode/decode consistency, and try correcting errors by editing these dimensions.
AI.motivation: Multimodal embeddings (e.g., text+vision) are widely used but can be unreliable, and most work treats them as black boxes. The authors want to understand internal embedding dimensions to detect and possibly fix decoding anomalies, improving reliability of models like SONAR.
AI.method: They study non-sequential multimodal sentence-level embeddings from the SONAR model, analyze sensitivity of individual dimensions to perturbations, identify dimensions correlated with decoding anomalies, and construct a detector based on consistency between repeated encoding and decoding. They then experiment with modifying the identified dimensions to see if anomalies can be corrected.
AI.result: They find that certain embedding dimensions are reliably sensitive to perturbations and correlate with decoding anomalies. Using these as signals, they build a detector that accurately flags anomalous decodings. Some success is reported in partially correcting anomalies by targeted modification of these dimensions.
AI.conclusion: Understanding the internal structure of multimodal embeddings, particularly dimension-wise sensitivity and consistency across encode/decode steps, enables effective detection of decoding anomalies and opens a path toward actively correcting them, thus improving robustness and reliability of multimodal representation models like SONAR.

title: PromptGNN-sim: Deep Fusion and Alignment of GNN and LLMs for Text-Attributed Graph Learning
summary: Text-Attributed Graphs (TAGs) combine textual semantics with graph structure and are central to many graph learning tasks. However, existing fusion methods often treat text and structure as separate inputs in a shallow, one-way pipeline, which limits deep interaction between modalities and weakens performance under sparse connectivity or cross-graph generalisation. To address this issue, we propose PromptGNN-sim, a bi-directional structure-semantic fusion framework for collaborative GNN-LLM learning. PromptGNN-sim uses a Graph Attention Network (GAT) for semantically aware neighborhood selection by combining structural attention with textual similarity. The selected structural context is then used to generate structure-aware prompts for an LLM, including the target node summary, label categories, and representative keywords from similar neighbors. During training, bi-directional cross-modal contrastive learning and cross-attention are introduced to jointly optimize the GNN and LLM components. Experiments on six public datasets, including Cora, Pubmed, and WikiCS, evaluate accuracy, generalisation, and robustness under cross-task transfer, cross-dataset generalisation, and sparse perturbations. Results show that PromptGNN-sim outperforms classical GNNs, LLMs, and recent GNN-LLM fusion methods, demonstrating the effectiveness of interactive structure-semantic collaboration for text-attributed graph learning.

AI.tldr: Proposes PromptGNN-sim, a bi-directional fusion framework between GNNs and LLMs for text-attributed graphs, achieving better accuracy, generalisation, and robustness than prior GNN, LLM, and fusion baselines.
AI.motivation: Existing methods for text-attributed graphs fuse text and structure in a shallow, mostly one-way manner, limiting deep interaction between modalities and leading to poor performance when graph connectivity is sparse or when models are transferred across graphs or tasks.
AI.method: PromptGNN-sim uses a GAT that blends structural attention with textual similarity for semantically aware neighbor selection, then constructs structure-aware prompts for an LLM containing node summaries, label space, and keywords from similar neighbors. It employs bi-directional cross-modal contrastive learning and cross-attention to jointly train the GNN and LLM, enabling iterative, two-way information flow between structure and text.
AI.result: On six public datasets (e.g., Cora, Pubmed, WikiCS), PromptGNN-sim is evaluated on accuracy, cross-task transfer, cross-dataset generalisation, and robustness under sparse perturbations, and consistently outperforms standard GNNs, vanilla LLMs, and recent GNN-LLM fusion models.
AI.conclusion: Deep, bi-directional collaboration between graph structure and textual semantics via PromptGNN-sim leads to more accurate, generalisable, and robust learning on text-attributed graphs than existing shallow fusion approaches.

title: Before Thinking, Learn to Decide: Proactive Routing for Efficient Visual Reasoning
summary: Large multimodal models have achieved strong reasoning on complex visual tasks, but their inference efficiency is often restricted by long chains of thought. A promising solution is to pair a small draft model with a large target model, enabling cooperative inference employing a routing signal that adaptively routes queries to either the draft or target model based on their difficulties for optimal efficiency and accuracy. Yet, the remaining bottleneck is to establish a reliable query difficulty signal under multimodal settings. Existing approaches designed for language models either rely on post-hoc token probabilities, which fall short in multimodal scenarios, or depend on supervised fine-tuning, which is a data-sensitive strategy. Both paradigms perform routing only after a complete output, and ignore whether the target model can actually solve the routed instances. To address this, we propose PRP, a Proactive Routing Paradigm that enables early decision-making by jointly evaluating the competence of both the draft and target models. Our Draft Rating Learning (DRL) equips the draft model with an internal confidence estimator, while Joint Rating Learning (JRL) predicts how well the target model can handle a given query, thereby prioritizing the allocation of samples it excels at rather than the hardest ones. These ratings enable fine-grained, instance-level \textbf{Proactive Routing} and substantially accelerate inference without compromising overall performance. Extensive experiments across multiple multimodal reasoning benchmarks validate our effectiveness and efficiency.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces PRP, a proactive routing paradigm that accelerates large multimodal model inference by making early, instance-level routing decisions between a small draft model and a large target model using learned confidence and competence ratings.
AI.motivation: Large multimodal models are powerful but slow, partly because they rely on long chains of thought. Cooperative inference with a small draft model and a large target model can improve efficiency, but it needs a reliable difficulty or routing signal, which is hard to obtain in multimodal settings. Existing methods either use post-hoc token probabilities that do not transfer well to multimodal tasks or require supervised fine-tuning and only route after full outputs, without ensuring the target can actually solve routed queries. There is thus a need for an earlier, more accurate, and modality-aware routing mechanism that also considers the target model’s strengths.
AI.method: The authors propose PRP (Proactive Routing Paradigm), which performs early routing decisions by jointly assessing the draft and target models’ capabilities. They introduce Draft Rating Learning (DRL) to train the draft model to produce an internal confidence score about its own ability to solve a given query. In parallel, Joint Rating Learning (JRL) is used to predict how well the target model can handle that same query. Together, these learned ratings enable fine-grained, instance-level routing: some queries are answered by the draft model alone, while others are escalated to the target model, all before generating complete outputs.
AI.result: On multiple multimodal reasoning benchmarks, PRP significantly accelerates inference relative to standard large multimodal model execution and prior routing schemes, while maintaining comparable or unchanged accuracy. The experiments demonstrate that using DRL and JRL for proactive routing yields better efficiency–performance trade-offs than methods relying on post-hoc token probabilities or supervised-only routing after full generation.
AI.conclusion: PRP offers a more effective cooperative inference framework for multimodal reasoning by enabling proactive, instance-specific routing between draft and target models. By learning internal confidence ratings for the draft model and competence ratings for the target model, the approach overcomes limitations of existing language-model-centric routing strategies and achieves substantial speedups without degrading overall performance. This suggests that learned, joint rating mechanisms are a promising direction for efficient deployment of large multimodal systems.

title: Rehearsed Multi-Agent Live Product Demonstrations with Real-Time Voice Question Answering
comment: Preprint. 4 figures, 1 algorithm, 5 tables. Systems paper with a preliminary six-session case study on four deployed applications; full benchmark protocol proposed, corpus run to appear in a later revision
summary: Live product demonstrations are a recurring, high-cost activity in software organizations: a human presenter must select features, dispatch the corresponding interactions on a running application, narrate them coherently, and answer questions in real time. Existing automation addresses only fragments -- generalist browser agents target instruction-conditioned task completion, and demo-video tools produce fixed MP4 artifacts that cannot be questioned and silently break under interface drift. We propose Rhetor, a multi-agent system that takes a running web application and its source-code repository as input and produces a rehearsed live demonstration with segment-synchronized narration and real-time voice question answering. The architectural contributions are a cross-modal feature representation that merges UI exploration with source-code analysis into features tagged with discrete focus tiers, a grounded scripter constrained to UI elements observed during exploration and dispatched through multi-strategy semantic locators, a pre-presentation rehearsal loop with explicit convergence and graceful degradation to narration-only segments, and a runtime synchronization invariant that ties each browser action to the audio-end event of its narration segment. Across six pipeline sessions on four deployed applications -- including the public-domain whiteboard application Excalidraw -- the rehearser's internal locator-firing rate (sigma-bar) spans 0.31-1.00 over 147 scripted actions; on the substantial workload (53 actions, full tier differentiation), sigma-bar is approximately 0.92, and on the public-domain reference point the locator-repair step drives convergence to sigma-bar = 1.00 at iteration 2. We additionally define a benchmark protocol of ten metrics across six application categories that would establish, beyond the case study, whether each design choice contributes positively.

AI.tldr: Rhetor is a multi-agent system that automatically creates and rehearses live, interactive software demos (with narration and Q&A) directly on running web apps.
AI.motivation: Live product demos are costly and fragile: a human must manually operate the UI, narrate, and answer questions, while existing tools either perform narrow task completion or produce static demo videos that can’t adapt to UI changes or support real-time interaction. The authors aim to automate end-to-end live demonstrations that remain grounded in the real application and robust to interface drift.
AI.method: The paper introduces Rhetor, a multi-agent system that takes a running web application plus its source code as input. It (1) builds a cross-modal feature representation that combines UI exploration with source-code analysis, assigning features to focus tiers; (2) uses a grounded scripter that composes demo scripts only from UI elements actually observed, and replays them via multi-strategy semantic locators; (3) runs a rehearsal loop that iteratively executes and repairs the script until convergence, backing off to narration-only segments when actions cannot be reliably automated; and (4) enforces a runtime synchronization invariant that couples each browser action with the end of its corresponding narration audio segment. The authors demonstrate the system on four deployed web apps and measure an internal locator-firing rate as a reliability metric.
AI.result: Across six end-to-end pipeline runs over four real applications (including Excalidraw), Rhetor successfully generates and rehearses scripted demos comprising 147 actions. The internal locator-firing rate (a measure of how reliably scripted actions can be dispatched) ranges from 0.31 to 1.00; on a demanding scenario with 53 actions and full tier differentiation, the average rate is about 0.92, and for Excalidraw the locator-repair mechanism attains a perfect rate of 1.00 after two rehearsal iterations. The paper also specifies a broader evaluation protocol with ten metrics over six application categories to more systematically assess contributions.
AI.conclusion: Rhetor demonstrates that multi-agent, cross-modal grounding over UI and source code can automate live, interactive software demonstrations with synchronized narration and question answering. The rehearsal and locator-repair mechanisms substantially improve robustness to UI variability, and the architecture provides a path beyond static demo videos and narrow task-completion agents. The authors propose a benchmark protocol to evaluate and refine such systems across applications and metrics beyond the initial case studies.

title: CaresAI at CT-DEB26: Detecting Dosing Errors In Clinical Trials Using Domain-Specific Transformer Embeddings and Classification Models
comment: 18 pages, published in CL4Health 2026 proceedings (3rd Workshop on Patient-oriented language processing) @ LREC 2026 http://lrec-conf.org/proceedings/lrec2026/workshops/cl4health/2026.cl4health-1.0.pdf
summary: Medication errors, particularly dosing errors in clinical trials (CT), can lead to patient harm, adverse drug events and worse patient outcomes. Dosing errors are preventable, and early identification can improve trial integrity and mitigate subsequent clinical and financial burden. This study aims to detect dosing errors within CT protocols by evaluating text representations of trial information using transformer-based language models trained on biomedical corpora. CT textual data was encoded using several models, including ClinicalBERT, PubMedBERT, BioBERT, and MedCPT, and integrated with categorical features. These text embeddings were used as input to classical machine learning models and neural network architectures within an experimental framework. Performance was primarily assessed using ROC-AUC with respect to predicting dosage error. Under a logistic regression baseline, BioBERT consistently outperformed alternative encoders, achieving an ROC-AUC of 0.794, a 3.95% improvement over the ClinicalBERT baseline. Combining multiple embeddings did not yield improvements, indicating that domain alignment outweighs representational stacking. Gradient boosting models, support vector classifiers, logistic regression, and residual neural networks achieved the strongest performance for predicting dosage error, achieving ROC-AUCs: 0.821 to 0.853. Overall, the integration of domain-specific transformer embeddings with structured metadata enables discrimination of trials meeting a predefined elevated dosing error risk criterion, advancing safety monitoring and supporting informed regulatory decision-making.

AI.tldr: Uses biomedical transformer models plus structured features to detect dosing errors in clinical trial protocols, achieving strong ROC-AUC and showing BioBERT with classical ML works best.
AI.motivation: Dosing errors in clinical trials can harm patients, compromise trial integrity, and incur clinical and financial costs. Since these errors are preventable, there is a need for automated methods to flag protocols at high risk of dosing errors early, using available trial documentation.
AI.method: Encode clinical trial protocol text with several domain-specific transformer models (ClinicalBERT, PubMedBERT, BioBERT, MedCPT). Combine these text embeddings with categorical/structured metadata. Feed these representations into a range of downstream models, including logistic regression, gradient boosting, SVMs, and residual neural networks. Evaluate performance at predicting trials with elevated dosing error risk, primarily using ROC-AUC.
AI.result: BioBERT embeddings with a logistic regression baseline outperform ClinicalBERT and other encoders, reaching ROC-AUC 0.794 (3.95% better than ClinicalBERT). Combining multiple embedding types does not improve performance, suggesting correct domain alignment is more valuable than stacking representations. The best-performing downstream models (gradient boosting, SVC, logistic regression, residual NN) reach ROC-AUCs between 0.821 and 0.853 for predicting dosage error risk.
AI.conclusion: Domain-specific transformer embeddings, especially BioBERT, combined with structured trial metadata allow effective discrimination of clinical trials at elevated risk of dosing errors. This approach can enhance safety monitoring, improve trial quality, and inform regulatory decision-making, though gains from combining multiple embeddings are limited compared to choosing a well-aligned single model.

title: ManimAgent: Self-Evolving Multimodal Agents for Visual Education
summary: Multi-round reflection lets agents built on large language models recover from failures within a single task, but each task remains an isolated episode: lessons learned across many reflection rounds on one task are discarded before the next begins. We study this gap on a code-generation task: from a scientific paper section, the agent writes Python in the open-source Manim library to render a mathematical animation. We present ManimAgent, a self-evolving multimodal agent that carries reflection experience across tasks through a dual-channel Episodic Memory Bank grown entirely from its own task stream, with no weight updates and no human seeds. After each animation converges, a vision-language model scores the rendered keyframes; the resulting signals populate a positive channel M+ that stores success rationales as soft Reference Examples, and a negative channel M- that stores validated failure patterns as hard Known Pitfalls. On a fixed-probe evaluation against no-memory, matched-budget retrieval-augmented generation, and shuffled-memory baselines, blind human Pass@1 rises and reflection rounds fall as memory size grows. We will release the code, frozen memory snapshots, and the task stream.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces ManimAgent, a self-evolving multimodal code-generation agent that uses an episodic memory bank to carry reflection experience across tasks, improving performance on mathematical animation generation without updating model weights or requiring human seed data.
AI.motivation: Existing reflection-based LLM agents can correct themselves within a single task but do not retain the experience across tasks; this wastes potentially valuable knowledge gained from past failures and successes. The authors want to enable persistent learning across tasks for code generation of mathematical animations from scientific text, without retraining the base model or relying on manually curated examples.
AI.method: They design ManimAgent, which generates Python/Manim code from scientific paper sections to create mathematical animations. After an animation converges, a vision-language model scores rendered keyframes, producing feedback that is stored in an Episodic Memory Bank with two channels: a positive channel M+ that stores successful solution rationales as soft Reference Examples, and a negative channel M- that stores validated failure patterns as hard Known Pitfalls. During future tasks, the agent retrieves and uses these memories to guide generation and reflection. The system is self-evolving because the memory is grown purely from its own task history, without weight updates or human-provided seeds.
AI.result: On a fixed-probe evaluation of the math-animation code-generation task, ManimAgent with its growing memory bank is compared against three baselines: an agent without memory, an agent with matched-budget retrieval-augmented generation, and an agent with shuffled (i.e., misaligned) memory. As memory size increases, ManimAgent shows higher blind human Pass@1 scores (first-try success rate) and requires fewer reflection rounds per task, indicating more efficient and effective problem solving.
AI.conclusion: Persistently carrying forward structured reflective experience via a dual-channel episodic memory bank allows LLM-based code-generation agents to self-improve across tasks without updating model weights or requiring human seed examples. The positive and negative memory channels—Reference Examples and Known Pitfalls—provide a practical mechanism for scalable, self-evolving multimodal agents on complex tasks like mathematical animation generation, and the authors plan to release code, memory snapshots, and task streams to support further research.

title: Comparing Human and Automatic Recognition of Dutch Dysarthric Continuous Speech: A Case Study
summary: In our goal to develop personalised dysarthric speech recognition (DSR) models, this study compared the recognition performances of human listeners and those of three state-of-the-art, off-the-shelf ASR systems (Whisper-large-V3, Google Chirp 3, and Omnilingual) on the recognition of Dutch continuous read and spontaneous speech from a single speaker with severe dysarthria. Results showed that both humans listeners and the three off-the-shelf ASR systems exhibit word error rates (WER) exceeding 70% on average, indicating that DSR is highly challenging for both humans and ASR systems. Fine-tuning on the dysarthric speech significantly reduced WER. Although overall WERs are still quite high (>23%), the personalised DSR models outperformed the human listeners, and performance is getting closer to being useful for supporting day-to-day communication of dysarthric speakers. Future research should focus on improving personalized DSR on spontaneous speech and longer utterances in the case of read speech, with a specific focus on particular phonemes.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates how well humans and modern ASR systems recognize severe dysarthric Dutch speech, and shows that personalized, fine-tuned models can beat human performance but remain imperfect.
AI.motivation: Dysarthric speakers have severely reduced speech intelligibility, making communication difficult. Standard ASR systems are trained on typical speech and perform poorly on dysarthric speech, especially in less resourced languages like Dutch. There is a need to quantify current performance limits (human vs. ASR) and explore whether personalized models, adapted to one speaker, can approach a level useful for everyday communication support.
AI.method: The authors compared word error rates for human listeners and three state-of-the-art, off-the-shelf ASR systems (Whisper-large-V3, Google Chirp 3, and Omnilingual) on Dutch continuous read and spontaneous speech from a single speaker with severe dysarthria. They then fine-tuned ASR models using this speaker’s dysarthric speech to build personalized DSR models and evaluated the change in WER.
AI.result: Both humans and all three off-the-shelf ASR systems showed very high error rates (>70% WER) on the dysarthric speech, confirming the task’s difficulty. After fine-tuning on the target speaker’s data, WER substantially decreased to above 23% but still relatively high. These personalized models outperformed human listeners on the same material, indicating that adaptation can yield substantial gains over both generic ASR and human perception.
AI.conclusion: Severe dysarthric speech poses major challenges for both human listeners and general-purpose ASR, but personalized fine-tuned DSR models can surpass human recognition performance and move toward practical utility for daily communication. However, accuracy is still insufficient for fully reliable use. Future work should aim to further improve personalized models, particularly for spontaneous speech, longer read utterances, and problematic phonemes.

title: BayesEvolve: Explicit Belief States for Autonomous Scientific Discovery
summary: Autonomous scientific discovery systems increasingly use large language models (LLMs) to propose new hypotheses, but many such systems condition primarily on experimental memory: archives of high-scoring candidates or heuristic summaries of recent trials. We argue that discovery agents should instead maintain explicit, uncertainty-aware beliefs about hypothesis quality. We introduce BayesEvolve, a belief-guided discovery framework that converts experimental evidence into a predictive belief state and uses this belief to guide future experimentation. As a controlled testbed for belief-guided discovery, we evaluate BayesEvolve on shifted BBOB-style black-box optimization tasks, leaving program and laboratory discovery domains to future work. BayesEvolve improves sample efficiency over memory- and archive-guided LLM baselines under a fixed evaluation budget. We further show that the belief state is predictive on held-out candidate pools, that controlled decision-rule ablations favor belief-guided selection with an annealed uncertainty bonus, and that BayesEvolve exhibits productive late-stage concentration rather than unfocused exploration.

AI.tldr: The paper presents BayesEvolve, a belief-guided framework that uses Bayesian-style uncertainty-aware beliefs instead of simple experimental memory to drive hypothesis selection, achieving more sample-efficient discovery on black-box optimization benchmarks than LLM approaches that rely on archives or heuristics.
AI.motivation: Many current autonomous scientific discovery systems powered by LLMs pick new hypotheses mainly by referencing past experiments through archives or heuristic summaries, without maintaining an explicit probabilistic belief over hypothesis quality. This limits principled reasoning about uncertainty, generalization to unseen candidates, and efficient exploration vs exploitation. The authors aim to design a discovery agent that tracks and updates explicit beliefs about hypothesis quality to guide experiments more effectively.
AI.method: The authors propose BayesEvolve, a framework that (1) converts observed experimental outcomes into a predictive belief state over hypothesis quality, explicitly modeling uncertainty; (2) uses this belief to select future experimental candidates via a decision rule that combines expected quality with an annealed uncertainty bonus; and (3) updates beliefs iteratively as new data arrive. They evaluate BayesEvolve in a controlled setting using shifted BBOB-like black-box optimization problems, comparing performance against LLM-based baselines that rely on archives or heuristic memory, and perform ablations on the decision rules driving candidate selection.
AI.result: On BBOB-style black-box optimization benchmarks, BayesEvolve achieves higher optimization performance within a fixed evaluation budget than memory- and archive-based LLM discovery baselines, demonstrating improved sample efficiency. The learned belief states generalize to unseen candidates, as shown by predictive performance on held-out candidate pools. Ablation studies indicate that belief-guided selection with an annealed uncertainty bonus yields better decisions than alternative rules, and analysis of search dynamics shows that BayesEvolve shifts from broad exploration to productive late-stage concentration instead of remaining diffused.
AI.conclusion: Maintaining and updating explicit, uncertainty-aware beliefs about hypothesis quality leads to more efficient and principled automated discovery than relying only on experimental memory or heuristic archives. BayesEvolve illustrates how belief-guided selection, particularly with carefully controlled uncertainty bonuses, can improve sample efficiency and produce well-structured exploration-exploitation behavior in black-box optimization. The authors suggest extending this framework from synthetic BBOB tasks to more complex real-world domains such as program synthesis and laboratory science in future work.

title: Grounding LLM Reasoning under Incomplete Graph Evidence
comment: A theoretical perspective about Grounding LLM Reasoning
summary: Knowledge graphs can guide large language models (LLMs) reasoning, but the graph seen by a system is usually a retrieved, linked, temporally scoped, and incomplete evidence state rather than a complete account of truth. We develop a theoretical perspective on grounding observable LLM trajectories under such incomplete graph evidence.The evidence state induces entity anchors, typed relation residuals, path energies, and support regions, while the language model supplies a prior over candidate trajectories. We show that, under open-world incompleteness, no hard rule based only on the observed state can both reject every false unsupported trajectory and retain every true-but-unobserved one.We then characterize soft grounding as a KL-regularized deformation of the LLM prior: finite slack preserves support for unsupported but non-contradicted trajectories, whereas hard conditioning appears as an infinite-penalty limit.The framework also yields stability bounds under evidence perturbations and clarifies the constraint regimes appropriate for GraphRAG, KGQA, graph agents, constrained decoding, and faithful generation. The claims are evidence-relative: KG compatibility is treated as declared support, not factual truth.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a theoretical framework for how to ground large language model (LLM) reasoning in incomplete knowledge graphs, viewing grounding as a soft, probabilistic deformation of the LLM’s prior behavior rather than strict rule-based filtering.
AI.motivation: In practice, systems that combine LLMs with knowledge graphs (e.g., GraphRAG, KGQA, graph agents) never see a complete, fully accurate graph. Instead, they operate over small retrieved subgraphs that are temporally limited, noisy, and incomplete. Existing approaches often treat graph facts as hard constraints, but this is problematic under open-world incompleteness: many true facts are simply missing from the retrieved graph. The authors want a principled way to reconcile LLM generative behavior with partial graph evidence, without either over-restricting the model or allowing unsupported hallucinations unchecked.
AI.method: The authors develop a formal, probabilistic framework that views the observed state of a knowledge graph as inducing several structures on possible LLM reasoning trajectories: entity anchors (what is explicitly present), typed relation residuals (what relations are missing or under-specified), path energies (costs associated with different reasoning paths), and support regions (subspaces of trajectories supported by evidence). They analyze what kinds of grounding rules are possible under an open-world assumption and prove that no purely rule-based filter derived only from the observed evidence can simultaneously eliminate all unsupported false trajectories and preserve all true-but-unobserved ones. They then define soft grounding as a KL-divergence–regularized distortion of the LLM’s prior distribution over trajectories, where the knowledge graph acts as a probabilistic constraint rather than a hard filter. They also derive stability bounds that quantify how small changes in the evidence affect the grounded distribution.
AI.result: The main results are: (1) an impossibility result showing that any hard rule based solely on the observed, incomplete graph cannot be both perfectly safe (rejecting all false but unsupported trajectories) and perfectly complete (retaining all true but graph-unobserved trajectories) under an open-world assumption; (2) a characterization of grounding as KL-regularized deformation of the LLM prior, where finite penalties allow unsupported yet non-contradicted trajectories to retain some probability mass, and hard conditioning emerges as the infinite-penalty limit; and (3) theoretical stability guarantees showing how robust the grounded reasoning is to perturbations in the retrieved evidence state.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that, under realistic open-world and incomplete-graph conditions, LLM grounding must be soft and probabilistic rather than hard and rule-based if we want to preserve potentially true but currently unsupported inferences. Grounding is best understood as KL-regularized adjustment of the LLM’s prior over trajectories according to the evidence in the knowledge graph, with the strength of regularization controlling how aggressively we enforce graph compatibility. This view clarifies how to set constraint regimes in applications like GraphRAG, KGQA, graph agents, constrained decoding, and faithful generation, emphasizing that knowledge graph compatibility should be treated as evidence-relative support, not as absolute truth.

title: Sequential Fairness Auditing with Limited Output Access
summary: External evaluations are becoming increasingly central to the governance of AI systems. In practice, however, independent auditors often have limited access to deployed models and must rely on query-based interactions. Most existing fairness evaluation methods assume static datasets and fixed-sample statistical tests, making them poorly suited to real-world auditing scenarios in which evidence must be collected sequentially under query constraints. In this work, we formulate fairness auditing as a tolerance-aware sequential hypothesis-testing problem under limited model output access. We develop a sequential generalized likelihood-ratio framework that allows auditors to accumulate evidence from a finite audit pool and stop once sufficient support for compliance or violation has been obtained. The framework is instantiated for decision-based Statistical Parity and Equal Opportunity audits, and extended to score- and logit-based proxy audits when richer observables are available. Our results show that both the fairness metric and the level of model access significantly affect audit efficiency, and that the benefits of richer output information are not uniform across auditing settings. In particular, richer outputs can substantially reduce the number of queries required for some fairness metrics and operating regimes, while offering limited gains in near-threshold cases. This work provides a practical statistical framework for sequential fairness auditing under realistic deployment constraints.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a statistical framework for sequentially auditing AI fairness using limited query access to deployed models.
AI.motivation: Real-world AI auditors typically only have black-box, query-based access to deployed models and must gather evidence sequentially, unlike most existing fairness methods that assume static datasets and fixed-sample tests. There is a need for principled methods that work under such constraints and can decide efficiently whether a model satisfies specified fairness criteria.
AI.method: The authors cast fairness auditing as a tolerance-aware sequential hypothesis testing problem with limited access to model outputs. They design a sequential generalized likelihood-ratio (GLR) framework that lets an auditor draw from a finite audit pool and stop once enough evidence is gathered for either compliance or violation. They instantiate this for decision-based audits of Statistical Parity and Equal Opportunity, and extend it to score- and logit-based proxy audits when more detailed outputs are available.
AI.result: Through theoretical analysis and experiments, the paper shows that both the specific fairness metric and the level of output access (decisions only vs. scores vs. logits) strongly influence how many queries are needed to reach a conclusion. In some metrics and operating regimes, richer outputs greatly reduce query requirements, but near the fairness decision threshold these gains can become small.
AI.conclusion: The work demonstrates that sequential GLR-based testing provides a practical and statistically principled framework for fairness auditing when only limited, query-based access to deployed models is available. It clarifies when and how richer model outputs improve audit efficiency and offers tools for auditors to determine compliance or violation with minimal queries under realistic constraints.

title: Multi-Agentic System Leveraging Open-Source LLMs to Mitigate Disinformation Threats
summary: In contemporary societies, the threat of disinformation has reached alarming levels, exacerbated by the proliferation of electronic communication, social media, and advancements in artificial intelligence. As a result, there is an urgent need to develop effective countermeasures to mitigate this menace. However, the sheer scale of the problem renders manual fact-checking and human-based verification inadequate, underscoring the necessity for automated methods to detect and debunk disinformation. This article proposes a novel approach based on a multi-agent system that emulates the decision-making processes of human annotators engaged in disinformation detection tasks. By incorporating a consensus mechanism, diversity in cognition and diversity in knowledge, and also hierarchical structure, inspired by human annotators' behavior, the proposed method achieves superior results compared to individual Large Language Models (LLMs), including GPT 4 and GPT 3.5. The system leverages open models (e.g., LLaMA, Kimi, Qwen, Deepseek and LLaMA-Nemotron) to ensure greater transparency. The evaluation of the proposed method encompasses datasets in languages with varying resource availability, including English (high-resource), Polish (medium-resource), Slovak (low-resource) and Bulgarian (low-resource). Experiments were conducted on tasks such as direct disinformation detection, identification of texts worthy of verification, and detection of texts containing verifiable factual claims.

AI.tldr: They propose a multi-agent, human-annotator-inspired system using multiple open-source LLMs and consensus to outperform single LLMs on disinformation-related tasks across several languages.
AI.motivation: Manual fact-checking and human verification cannot keep up with the huge volume and speed of disinformation spreading via social media, electronic communication, and AI-generated content, so scalable, automated and transparent detection methods are required.
AI.method: Design a multi-agent system composed of several diverse open LLMs organized hierarchically, with mechanisms for consensus, cognitive diversity, and knowledge diversity that mimic how groups of human annotators reason about and label disinformation. The system is evaluated on multiple tasks and languages, comparing its performance to individual LLMs such as GPT‑4 and GPT‑3.5.
AI.result: The multi-agent system achieves better performance than individual LLMs (including GPT‑4 and GPT‑3.5) on tasks such as detecting disinformation, flagging information that merits verification, and detecting verifiable factual claims, across English, Polish, Slovak, and Bulgarian datasets with varying resource levels.
