AI.conclusion: Emulating human annotators via a diverse, consensus-based, hierarchical multi-agent framework built from open LLMs is an effective and more transparent approach for automated disinformation handling than relying on a single powerful LLM, and it works across languages with different resource availability.

title: Using Large Language Models as Low-Cost Statistical Estimators for Human-Response Data
summary: Quantitative research across the social and behavioral sciences depends on human subject experiments that are expensive, slow, and subject to sampling bias. Here we show that pretrained large language models induce risk-equivalent estimators of conditional expectations under squared loss, establishing restricted functional risk equivalence: under squared loss, the LLM induces an estimator whose risk matches the Bayes optimal risk for squared-loss prediction of conditional expectations for any inference that depends on the data only through the conditional mean. We formalize the LLM as a misspecified functional estimator $T(\hat{P}_n)$ trained on i.i.d.\ data, decompose the estimation error into representation bias $ε_{\mathrm{rep}}$ and optimization error, and prove that under mild regularity conditions the LLM's expected error converges to the irreducible population variance plus the squared representation bias, with the representation bias bounded by the Pinsker inequality. The identifiability error $δ$ propagates into the effective bias, inflating the asymptotic risk floor. We establish restricted functional risk equivalence via a bidirectional Le Cam deficiency analysis: the forward deficiency vanishes asymptotically while the reverse deficiency is exactly zero. We provide finite-sample concentration bounds and a calibration protocol with explicit decision rules. The result is a precise, provable statement: a well-calibrated LLM achieves the Bayes-optimal risk for conditional-mean-dependent inference, bounded by explicit scope conditions. In practical applications, this means that under satisfied conditions and well-calibrated models, large language models can be used in many prediction and decision-making tasks that originally relied on human experiments, approximating near-optimal statistical inference at lower cost.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that, under certain conditions, a well-calibrated large language model (LLM) can act as a near–Bayes-optimal estimator for conditional expectations under squared loss, allowing it to replace many costly human-subject experiments for prediction and decision-making tasks.
AI.motivation: Human-subject experiments in social and behavioral sciences are expensive, slow, and prone to sampling bias, yet they underpin much quantitative research. The authors are motivated to see whether pretrained LLMs—trained on massive text corpora—can serve as statistically sound substitutes for these experiments in tasks where the goal is to estimate conditional expectations (e.g., average treatment effects, predicted responses) under squared loss. They want a rigorous, decision-theoretic justification rather than heuristic claims that LLMs “approximate human judgments.”
AI.method: The authors model an LLM as a misspecified functional estimator T(\hat{P}_n) trained on i.i.d. data, and study its performance as an estimator of conditional expectations under squared loss. They decompose error into representation bias (how far the LLM’s function class is from the truth) and optimization error, and show that the LLM’s expected error converges to the irreducible variance plus squared representation bias. They bound representation bias via Pinsker’s inequality and analyze identifiability error δ and its impact on effective bias. To formalize optimality, they use a bidirectional Le Cam deficiency analysis between the LLM-based experiment and the ideal experiment, proving that the forward deficiency vanishes asymptotically and the reverse deficiency is exactly zero. They also derive finite-sample concentration bounds and design a calibration protocol with explicit decision rules to make the LLM’s outputs statistically interpretable.
AI.result: The paper proves a restricted functional risk equivalence: for any inference problem that depends only on conditional means and uses squared loss, a well-calibrated LLM induces an estimator with risk equal to the Bayes-optimal risk, up to explicit bias terms derived from representation and identifiability errors. As the sample size grows and regularity conditions hold, the forward Le Cam deficiency between the LLM-based procedure and the ideal Bayes-optimal procedure goes to zero, and finite-sample risk can be controlled by the provided concentration bounds and calibration protocol. This yields explicit conditions under which the LLM’s statistical risk matches that of an optimal estimator based on the true data-generating distribution.
AI.conclusion: The authors conclude that, under clear scope conditions (squared loss, conditional-mean-dependent functionals, and proper calibration), pretrained LLMs can be treated as near–Bayes-optimal estimators of conditional expectations. Consequently, many prediction and decision-making tasks in social and behavioral sciences that traditionally rely on costly, biased human experiments can instead be conducted using LLMs, achieving close to optimal statistical performance at much lower cost. They emphasize that this is not a blanket guarantee but a precise, mathematically grounded equivalence result valid within the stated assumptions and calibrated usage protocols.

title: DialogPII: A multilingual dataset of synthetic dialog transcripts to detect personal information
summary: Conversational data collected in domains such as healthcare or social sciences is a valuable resource for research and automated analysis. However, responsible data sharing requires the detection and removal of personally identifiable and sensitive information to protect individual privacy. To support the development and evaluation of automatic de-identification systems, we present DialogPII, a multilingual dataset of synthetic dialogs and speech-derived transcripts for personal information detection. DialogPII covers eight interaction scenarios (emergency calls, medical anamnesis interviews, therapy sessions, insurance communication, customer support, clinical interviews regarding an AI-supported dashboard, police reports, and group therapy discussions), 19 entity types, and 11 languages (English, Arabic, Finnish, French, German, Hindi, Italian, Polish, Portuguese, Spanish, and Turkish). Dialogs were generated semi-automatically using large language models, manually curated for plausibility and diversity, and localized to country- and city-specific contexts. All dialogs were additionally converted to speech via text-to-speech synthesis, transcribed with Whisper, and annotated through automatic projection and manual correction, yielding aligned written and speech-derived resources across all languages. We further release baseline multilingual named entity recognition models and provide technical validation through inter-annotator agreement analysis, translation quality evaluation, annotation projection assessment, and benchmark experiments with transformer-based sequence labeling models.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces DialogPII, a multilingual synthetic dialog dataset designed for detecting personal and sensitive information in conversational data, with both text and speech-transcript versions and baseline NER models.
AI.motivation: Conversational data in areas like healthcare and social sciences is valuable but often contains personal and sensitive information, making sharing and research use difficult without robust de-identification. Existing resources for training and evaluating such systems are limited, especially across many languages and realistic dialogue scenarios. The authors aim to create a controlled, privacy-preserving, yet realistic dataset to support development of automatic personal information detection and de-identification tools.
AI.method: The authors semi-automatically generate synthetic dialogs using large language models across eight realistic interaction scenarios and 11 languages, covering 19 types of personal information entities. They manually curate the generated dialogs for plausibility, diversity, and localization to specific countries and cities. Each dialog is then converted to speech with text-to-speech, transcribed using Whisper, and annotated via automatic label projection from the original text followed by manual correction. They also train and release baseline multilingual NER models and conduct various validation studies, including inter-annotator agreement, translation quality checks, and evaluation of annotation projection quality and benchmark NER experiments.
AI.result: The result is DialogPII, a multilingual dataset of aligned written dialogs and speech-derived transcripts, annotated for 19 personal information entity types across 8 scenarios and 11 languages. Baseline multilingual NER models are provided, and experiments with transformer-based sequence labeling demonstrate the dataset’s suitability as a benchmark. Validation analyses show acceptable annotation quality, consistency, and translation reliability, supporting the dataset’s use for research on PII detection and de-identification.
AI.conclusion: DialogPII offers a comprehensive, privacy-preserving resource for developing and evaluating multilingual personal information detection and de-identification systems in conversational settings. The combination of synthetic but realistic dialogs, multiple languages and scenarios, and aligned text–speech resources, along with baseline models and thorough validation, makes it a useful benchmark for future research in automatic de-identification and related NLP tasks.

title: Whose Side Is Your Agent On? Multi-Party Principal Loyalty in LLM Agents
summary: A rapidly growing class of LLM agents is multi-party: the agent acts for a principal (who briefs it, sends follow-ups, and receives results) while also conversing in a separate channel with a counterparty whose interests may diverge (negotiating with a vendor, screening inbound requests, or mediating between employees). Here "help whoever you are talking to" is the wrong objective. The agent must stay loyal to the principal it represents without over-refusing the principal's own cooperative asks. We study this multi-party loyalty problem and contribute a measurement instrument, two mechanisms, and a structural lesson. PrincipalBench is a 75-item multi-turn benchmark with leak probes, dual judges, and an integrity-audit gate. Across 13 frontier subjects it exposes a sharp split (<=20% vs. 53.6-75.3% harm) invisible to single-turn safety evaluations: a selective cluster that declines adversarial probes while still following the principal's legitimate requests, and an over-refusing cluster that refuses broadly. (M1) A prompt-time loyalty scaffold (a fixed system prompt of seven prioritized rules, open-coded from 50+ failure trajectories) holds Claude-Sonnet to 19.4% harm and all nine selective subjects to <=20%. (M2) A per-token-KL distillation recipe transfers a prompted Qwen3-32B teacher into 8B Qwen3 and Llama-3.1 students, the strongest open-weight recipe we measure. (Lesson) Both mechanisms only move along a common leak/over-refusal trade-off rather than crossing it: improving one axis costs the other, and the jointly favorable outcome stays out of reach.

AI.tldr: This paper studies how LLM agents serving one user (the principal) should behave when also interacting with another party whose interests may conflict, and introduces a benchmark and methods to measure and improve loyalty to the principal without excessive refusal.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM safety and alignment work often assumes a single user, with objectives like 'help whoever you are talking to' or universally avoid harm. In multi-party settings (e.g., negotiating with vendors, mediating requests, or screening communications), the model acts on behalf of a principal but converses with a counterparty whose goals can diverge. Current safety tuning tends to either make models overly helpful to the current interlocutor (risking disloyalty or information leaks) or overly cautious and refusing (blocking the principal’s legitimate cooperative tasks). There is a lack of standardized ways to measure and balance loyalty to the principal versus safety with respect to counterparties.
AI.method: The authors formalize the 'multi-party loyalty problem' and build PrincipalBench, a 75-item multi-turn benchmark that includes leak probes (to test for information disclosure to counterparties), dual judges (to separately assess harm vs. cooperation), and an integrity-audit gate. They evaluate 13 frontier LLMs on this benchmark, revealing behavior clusters not visible in single-turn safety tests. They then propose two mechanisms: (M1) a prompt-time loyalty scaffold—a fixed system prompt with seven prioritized rules, derived via open coding of more than 50 failure trajectories—to guide models like Claude-Sonnet; and (M2) a per-token KL-divergence distillation method that transfers a well-prompted Qwen3-32B 'teacher' policy into smaller Qwen3-8B and Llama-3.1-8B 'student' models, enabling strong open-weight performance on loyalty and safety trade-offs.
AI.result: PrincipalBench reveals a stark performance split: some 'selective' models can reject adversarial leak probes while satisfying the principal’s legitimate requests, achieving low harm rates (≤20%), while other models display 'over-refusal' behavior, broadly refusing tasks and thus scoring poorly on cooperation despite safety. With the loyalty scaffold (M1), Claude-Sonnet’s harmful behavior is reduced to 19.4%, and all nine selective models reach ≤20% harm on the benchmark. The distillation approach (M2) successfully transfers the desired behaviors into smaller open-weight models (Qwen3-8B and Llama-3.1-8B), yielding the strongest results among open-source models tested. However, in all cases, improvements shift models along a trade-off between information leakage and refusal rather than eliminating the trade-off.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that multi-party loyalty is a distinct, underexplored dimension of LLM alignment that cannot be captured by single-turn or single-party safety evaluations. PrincipalBench offers a systematic way to measure this, and both prompt-level scaffolding and KL-based distillation can meaningfully improve loyalty behaviors. Nonetheless, these methods do not escape a fundamental leak/over-refusal trade-off: reducing susceptibility to leaks tends to increase over-refusal, and making models more cooperative tends to raise leakage risk. Achieving a jointly optimal point—high loyalty with low over-refusal—remains an open research challenge, suggesting the need for new architectures, training objectives, or interaction protocols for multi-party LLM agents.

title: REAR: Test-time Preference Realignment through Reward Decomposition
summary: Aligning large language models (LLMs) with diverse user preferences is a critical yet challenging task. While post-training methods can adapt models to specific needs, they often require costly data curation and additional training. Test-time scaling (TTS) presents an efficient, training-free alternative, but its application has been largely limited to verifiable domains like mathematics and coding, where response correctness is easily judged. To extend TTS to preference alignment, we introduce a novel framework that models the task as a realignment problem, since the base model often fails to sufficiently align with the stated preference. Our key insight is to decompose the underlying reward function into two components: one related to the question and the other to preference information. This allows us to derive a REAlignment Reward (REAR) that selectively rescales the proportions of these two reward terms. We then show that REAR can be formulated as a linear combination of token-level policy log-probabilities, making it computationally efficient and easy to integrate with various TTS algorithms such as best-of-$N$ sampling and tree search. Experiments show that compared to other test-time baselines, REAR not only enables scalable test-time realignment for preference alignment tasks under diverse user requirements, but also generalizes to mathematical and visual tasks under appropriate preference settings.

AI.tldr: They propose REAR, a test-time, training-free method to realign LLM outputs with user preferences by decomposing the reward into question-related and preference-related components and combining them via token-level log-probabilities.
AI.motivation: Existing alignment of LLMs to user preferences typically relies on post-training methods such as finetuning or RLHF, which are expensive and inflexible. Test-time scaling offers a cheaper, training-free alternative but has mostly been restricted to domains with easily verifiable correctness (math, coding). There is a need for an efficient, scalable method to align LLMs to diverse and possibly complex user preferences directly at inference time, without extra training or heavy data collection.
AI.method: They model preference alignment as a realignment problem where the base model is not sufficiently aligned with a stated preference. They decompose the reward function into two parts: one capturing relevance to the question and another capturing adherence to the user’s preference. Using this decomposition, they define a REAlignment Reward (REAR) that resizes the relative importance of these two components. Mathematically, they show REAR can be expressed as a linear combination of token-level policy log-probabilities from the base model, which makes it lightweight and compatible with existing test-time scaling strategies like best-of-N sampling and tree-based search. At inference, multiple candidate outputs are generated and scored with REAR, and the best one is selected according to the user preference and task requirements.
AI.result: Experiments demonstrate that REAR improves preference alignment compared to other test-time baselines, especially under diverse and explicitly specified user requirements. Furthermore, the approach generalizes beyond pure preference tasks: when preferences are appropriately encoded, REAR also brings gains in tasks such as mathematics and vision-language problems. Overall, it offers better alignment quality at test time without additional training cost.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that modeling preference alignment as a realignment problem and decomposing the reward into question and preference components enables effective, training-free alignment at inference time. REAR provides a practical, computationally efficient way to incorporate user preferences into LLM outputs via test-time scaling, works across a variety of alignment tasks, and extends to other domains like math and vision when preferences are suitably defined.

title: ENC-ODE: Event-level Neurodegenerative Modeling in Continuous Time with Neural ODEs
summary: Accurately predicting the temporal evolution of clinical biomarkers is crucial for the early diagnosis and management of neurodegenerative diseases such as Alzheimer's disease. However, this relies on longitudinal data to capture biomarker changes over time, which is often sparse and irregular due to the high cost, labor-intensive nature, and patient burden. To address these challenges, we propose ENC-ODE, an Event-level Neurodegenerative modeling in Continuous time with neural Ordinary Differential Equations. ENC-ODE predicts future biomarker evolution by modeling clinical events through diagnosis-conditioned continuous dynamics. A target-conditioned attention mechanism weights and aggregates event-level predictions for the target time and modality without history compression. Extensive experiments on Alzheimer's Disease Neuroimaging Initiative (ADNI) dataset demonstrate that ENC-ODE outperforms representative sequence models while offering a scalable and neuroscientifically grounded solution for clinical support. The code is available at https://github.com/JardinDelSol/enc-ode.

AI.tldr: They propose ENC-ODE, a neural ODE-based model that predicts future evolution of Alzheimer’s disease biomarkers from sparse, irregular clinical events, outperforming existing sequence models.
AI.motivation: Longitudinal clinical biomarker data for neurodegenerative diseases are sparse and irregular because collection is expensive, labor-intensive, and burdensome for patients, yet accurate prediction of biomarker trajectories over time is key for early diagnosis and disease management. Existing sequence models struggle with such irregular event data and often compress history in ways that lose clinically important information.
AI.method: They introduce ENC-ODE (Event-level Neurodegenerative modeling in Continuous time with neural ODEs), which models biomarker evolution as continuous-time dynamics driven by clinical events and conditioned on diagnosis. Each clinical event is processed, and a target-conditioned attention mechanism selectively weights and aggregates event-level predictions for a specific target time and biomarker modality, avoiding lossy history compression. The continuous dynamics are parameterized with neural ODEs to naturally handle irregular time intervals.
AI.result: On the ADNI (Alzheimer’s Disease Neuroimaging Initiative) dataset, ENC-ODE achieves better prediction performance for future biomarker values than representative sequence-based baselines, demonstrating superior handling of sparse and irregular longitudinal data.
AI.conclusion: ENC-ODE provides a scalable, clinically and neuroscientifically grounded framework for predicting biomarker trajectories in Alzheimer’s disease from sparse, irregular event data, offering improved performance over standard sequence models and potential utility as a clinical decision support tool.

title: OLIVE: View-Augmented Latent Prediction with Waveform Reconstruction for Speech SSL
summary: We propose Online Latent prediction with Invariant Views and rEconstruction (OLIVE), a self-supervised speech representation learning framework that jointly optimizes analysis and synthesis objectives. OLIVE combines view-augmented masked latent prediction with waveform reconstruction under a unified objective. Reconstruction constrains early encoder features to retain signal-level information, while masked latent prediction shapes later contextual representations toward invariance for robust downstream performance. We show that these objectives enable representations that support a broad range of tasks. In particular, OLIVE improves results on generation and speaker tasks, maintains competitive performance on recognition and semantic tasks, and improves waveform reconstruction.

AI.tldr: OLIVE is a self-supervised framework that jointly learns speech representations by combining masked latent prediction with waveform reconstruction, improving robustness and versatility across multiple downstream tasks.
AI.motivation: To learn speech representations that work well not only for recognition and semantic understanding but also for generation, speaker-related tasks, and waveform reconstruction, overcoming the limitations of methods that optimize only analysis or only synthesis objectives.
AI.method: Introduce OLIVE, which unifies two objectives: (1) view-augmented masked latent prediction, where the model predicts masked latent representations from invariant augmented views to shape robust, contextual features; and (2) waveform reconstruction, which forces early encoder layers to preserve low-level signal information. These are trained jointly in a single self-supervised framework.
AI.result: OLIVE yields speech representations that improve performance on generation and speaker tasks, remain competitive on recognition and semantic tasks compared to prior methods, and achieve better waveform reconstruction quality.
AI.conclusion: Jointly optimizing analysis (invariant masked latent prediction) and synthesis (waveform reconstruction) within OLIVE leads to more generally useful self-supervised speech representations that balance invariance and signal fidelity across diverse downstream applications.

title: The FIL Hypothesis: Inductive Biases Help with Kernel Engineering
summary: The Bitter Lesson, which posits that general-purpose methods that scale with computation and data ultimately outperform those with built-in human knowledge, has become a dominant paradigm in the era of Large Language Models. We revisit this principle by observing a new and critical scaling dimension: the duration of the Feedback Information Loop (FIL), the time required for a system to receive a verification signal after generating a prediction. Most historic successes in Artificial Intelligence (AI) have benefited from near instantaneous feedback (e.g., games or classification tasks), but we argue that future AI applications in science and the physical world will inherently involve FILs ranging from hours to weeks. This trend poses a fundamental scaling limit, as obtaining enough verification steps required by purely data-driven methods becomes practically impossible. Additionally, we propose a method that is orthogonal to purely data-driven approaches, based on human-inspired expert knowledge. The method relies on inductive biases and constraining the solution space. We provide an initial validation of the hypothesis and the method, by studying the real-world GPU programming task, a domain with non-trivial FIL, and demonstrate that incorporating inductive biases yields superior performance over data-driven approaches. The code is released under: https://github.com/ai-nikolai/robust_kernelbench

AI.tldr: The paper argues that as AI systems are applied to real-world scientific and physical tasks with long feedback delays, purely data-driven scaling will hit fundamental limits, and shows that adding human-inspired inductive biases can outperform data-only methods in GPU programming tasks.
AI.motivation: The dominant "Bitter Lesson" suggests that general-purpose, computation- and data-scalable methods beat approaches with built-in human knowledge. However, future AI applications in science and the physical world often have long delays before feedback (verification) is available, making it infeasible to collect the vast amounts of feedback required for scaling purely data-driven methods. The authors want to question whether the Bitter Lesson still holds under these conditions and explore alternatives that can work with sparse, delayed feedback.
AI.method: 1) Conceptual: introduce and analyze the notion of the Feedback Information Loop (FIL), defined as the time between a system generating a prediction and receiving a verification signal, and argue that long FILs fundamentally limit data-driven scaling. 2) Algorithmic: propose an alternative approach that incorporates human-inspired expert knowledge via inductive biases and constraints on the solution space, which is orthogonal to purely data-driven scaling. 3) Empirical: test this idea in a real-world GPU programming task, a setting with non-trivial FIL, by comparing approaches with and without such inductive biases using the released Robust KernelBench benchmark and codebase.
AI.result: In the GPU programming domain with non-trivial feedback delays, approaches that embed inductive biases and constrain the solution space perform better than purely data-driven methods that attempt to learn everything from data alone. This provides initial empirical evidence that, in long-FIL settings, expert-knowledge-based inductive biases can yield superior performance.
AI.conclusion: The authors conclude that the classical Bitter Lesson is incomplete because it ignores the scaling dimension of feedback delay. For AI applications with long Feedback Information Loops, purely data-driven scaling is practically limited, and incorporating human-inspired inductive biases and constraints can be necessary and beneficial. Their experiments on GPU programming offer preliminary validation, motivating further research on methods that integrate expert knowledge in long-FIL domains.

title: MOPD: Multi-Teacher On-Policy Distillation for Capability Integration in LLM Post-Training
summary: Modern large language models (LLMs) rely on reinforcement learning during post-training to push specific capabilities, yet integrating multiple capabilities into one model remains hard. Existing methods, such as Off-Policy Finetune and Mix-RL, are either inefficient or lose performance. In this work, we propose Multi-teacher On-Policy Distillation (MOPD), a post-training paradigm for combining the capabilities of multiple domain RL teachers: we first run per-domain specialised RL to obtain a set of domain teachers, then distill these teachers into the student on its own rollouts. This eliminates exposure bias and provides a dense optimization signal. On Qwen3-30B-A3B, MOPD outperforms Mix-RL, Cascade RL, Off-Policy Finetune, and Param-Merge baselines, inheriting nearly all of each teacher's capability. MOPD also enables parallel, independent development of domain teachers, removing the cross-domain coupling typical of multi-domain post-training. MOPD has been deployed in the post-training of MiMo-V2-Flash, an industrial-scale frontier model, demonstrating its practical value for capability integration in frontier-scale LLMs.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Multi-teacher On-Policy Distillation (MOPD), a new post-training method that combines the strengths of multiple RL-finetuned domain experts into a single LLM more effectively than existing approaches.
AI.motivation: Current LLM post-training relies on RL to improve specific capabilities, but merging several separately optimized capabilities into one model is difficult. Existing approaches like Off-Policy Finetune and Mix-RL either suffer from training inefficiency, exposure bias, or noticeable degradation of some capabilities when combined. There is a need for a scalable, practical way to integrate multiple domain-specialized RL teachers into a single student model without losing performance in any domain.
AI.method: 1) Train separate domain-specific RL teachers using standard on-policy RL post-training, each optimized for a particular capability or domain. 2) Perform Multi-teacher On-Policy Distillation (MOPD): the student generates its own rollouts (on-policy trajectories), and each domain teacher provides supervision for the relevant domain on those student-generated samples. 3) Distill all teachers jointly into the student using these on-policy samples, providing dense learning signals while avoiding off-policy exposure bias. This process allows parallel and independent development of the domain teachers and then a unified distillation step to merge their capabilities.
AI.result: On the Qwen3-30B-A3B base model, MOPD outperforms several strong baselines for capability integration, including Mix-RL, Cascade RL, Off-Policy Finetune, and parameter-merging methods. Empirically, the student model inherits nearly all capabilities of each individual domain teacher with minimal trade-offs. The method scales to industrial settings and has been used to train MiMo-V2-Flash, a frontier-scale LLM, confirming its practicality and effectiveness at large scale.
AI.conclusion: MOPD is an effective and scalable post-training paradigm for integrating multiple RL-optimized domain experts into a single LLM. By distilling multiple teachers on the student’s own rollouts, it avoids exposure bias, yields strong performance across all domains, and supports parallel, decoupled development of domain-specialized teachers. Its deployment in an industrial frontier model suggests it is a practical solution for capability integration in large-scale LLM training pipelines.

title: Entity Binding Failures in Tool-Augmented Agents
summary: Tool-augmented language-model agents are often evaluated by whether they select the correct tool, produce valid API arguments, and complete the requested task. However, an agent may choose the right tool and still act on the wrong external entity. For example, a request to "email Alex about the launch" may lead the agent to contact the wrong Alex, attach the wrong launch document, reply in the wrong thread, or update the wrong customer account. We call these errors entity binding failures. This paper studies entity binding failures as a distinct reliability and safety problem in tool-augmented agents. We formalize the separation between tool correctness and entity correctness, introduce a taxonomy of wrong-entity failures in enterprise workflows, and evaluate entity-aware execution mechanisms including entity-resolution preconditions, confidence-gated binding, clarification under ambiguity, and provenance tracking. In a controlled diagnostic evaluation across 60 tasks, five model backends, and six tool-use methods, all methods achieved 0.0 percent wrong-tool error, yet action-oriented baselines still produced wrong-entity actions in 24.0-26.0 percent of runs. Entity-aware methods eliminated wrong-entity actions and risk-weighted wrong-entity exposure in this setting, but reduced direct task completion by deferring under ambiguity. These findings show that safe tool use requires not only selecting the correct tool, but also reliably binding natural-language references to the correct real-world entity before action.

AI.tldr: The paper defines and analyzes “entity binding failures” in tool-augmented language model agents—cases where the right tool is used on the wrong real-world entity—and proposes mechanisms that greatly reduce these failures, at the cost of sometimes deferring task completion under ambiguity.
AI.motivation: Existing evaluations of tool-augmented language-model agents mostly focus on whether the model picks the correct tool and formats API calls correctly. However, in realistic workflows, even a correct tool call can target the wrong person, document, thread, or account, causing serious reliability and safety issues. These errors are underexplored and not captured by standard metrics, so the authors want to isolate, characterize, and mitigate them as a separate problem.
AI.method: The authors (1) formally distinguish between tool correctness (right tool, valid call) and entity correctness (right real-world target), (2) build a taxonomy of wrong-entity failure modes in enterprise settings, and (3) design and test “entity-aware execution” mechanisms: entity-resolution checks before acting, confidence thresholds for binding entities, clarification questions when ambiguity remains, and provenance tracking of which entity was bound. They run a controlled diagnostic evaluation on 60 tasks, with five model backends and six tool-use strategies, comparing baseline action-oriented methods versus these entity-aware mechanisms.
AI.result: Across all evaluated settings, all methods achieved 0% wrong-tool error, showing that tool selection and syntax were handled well. Nonetheless, action-oriented baselines still performed wrong-entity actions in about 24–26% of runs, revealing a large hidden failure mode. The entity-aware mechanisms eliminated wrong-entity actions and reduced risk-weighted exposure to wrong entities in the diagnostic setting, but they did so partly by deferring actions when entity ambiguity could not be safely resolved, which in turn lowered direct task completion rates.
AI.conclusion: Tool correctness alone is insufficient for safe deployment of tool-augmented LLM agents. A separate notion of entity correctness—accurately binding natural-language references to the intended real-world entity—is essential. Systems should incorporate explicit entity-aware execution mechanisms (resolution preconditions, confidence gating, clarification, provenance) to prevent wrong-entity actions, even if this means sometimes declining or deferring tasks under ambiguity.

title: Field Order Should Not Matter: Permutation-Invariant Embedding Model Fine-Tuning for Structured Metadata Retrieval
summary: We study retrieval over catalogs of structured metadata, where each record is a small schema whose fields answer different kinds of query. Embedding a record with a text encoder first serializes its fields into a string, which forces a choice of field order. We show this choice, usually treated as an implementation detail, silently controls retrieval quality once the encoder is fine-tuned. A standard fine-tune loses 7.4 nDCG@10 points when the index is rebuilt under a different field order, because it reads absolute position instead of the field labels. We propose permutation-invariant fine-tuning ($\textbf{PI-FT}$), which serializes each record under a freshly sampled field order with random field dropout, so meaning binds to the labels rather than to position. The change is about two lines in the data loader; it costs negligible in-distribution accuracy and cuts the order-change penalty to 0.2 points. We study this in the discovery of development statistics, a catalog of nearly 10,000 indicators that should be searchable in many languages by a model small enough to self-host. As AI assistants and agents increasingly mediate access to public data and statistics, this retrieval step decides whether an answer is grounded in the right indicator or series, making discoverability a precondition for disseminating data through AI. Because usage logs cannot provide training signal for indicators no one has searched, we generate the queries instead. $\textbf{DevDataBench}$ is a fully LLM-generated benchmark of grounded, facet-targeted queries across 15 languages, covering every indicator for both training and evaluation. A fine-tuned 118M-parameter CPU encoder outperforms every zero-shot baseline, including $\texttt{text-embedding-3-large}$ (0.707 vs.\ 0.556 nDCG@10), with the largest gains in low-resource languages. We release the benchmark, pipeline, models, and a reusable PI-FT framework.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that the order in which fields of structured records are serialized for text-encoder retrieval matters a lot after fine-tuning, and proposes a simple permutation-invariant fine-tuning method that makes models robust to field order while enabling strong multilingual, low-resource, self-hostable retrieval over a development-data catalog using a fully LLM-generated benchmark.
AI.motivation: In structured metadata catalogs, each record has multiple fields (e.g., name, description, units) that users may query in different ways. These records are usually embedded by serializing fields into text and feeding them to a text encoder, which implicitly requires choosing an arbitrary field order. This choice is often treated as a low-level implementation detail. The authors find that, after fine-tuning, models become highly sensitive to this order, which harms robustness when indices are rebuilt, schemas change, or systems are deployed in new settings. Furthermore, accurate, multilingual retrieval over public development indicators is crucial for AI assistants to ground answers in the correct statistics, but usage logs do not provide training signals for rarely or never searched indicators, particularly in low-resource languages. Thus there is a need for a robust, efficient, and self-hostable retrieval approach plus a comprehensive benchmark not dependent on real user queries.
AI.method: The authors formalize the problem of retrieval over structured metadata catalogs and study how field-serialization order affects fine-tuned text encoders. They propose permutation-invariant fine-tuning (PI-FT): during training, each record is serialized into text with a randomly sampled permutation of its fields and with random field dropout. This encourages the encoder to bind meaning to field labels rather than absolute positions in the serialized string. Implementation-wise, PI-FT is realized by modifying the data loader to randomly permute and drop fields on the fly during fine-tuning. To address the lack of real training queries, they introduce DevDataBench: a fully LLM-generated benchmark where an LLM creates grounded, facet-specific queries for every indicator in a catalog of ~10,000 development indicators, across 15 languages. They then fine-tune a 118M-parameter encoder (CPU-friendly and self-hostable) using PI-FT on these synthetic query–indicator pairs and evaluate it against various zero-shot embedding baselines, including large proprietary models.
AI.result: Empirically, a standard fine-tuning procedure leads to substantial sensitivity to field order: if the index is rebuilt with a different field serialization order, retrieval quality, measured by nDCG@10, drops by 7.4 points because the encoder overfits to absolute token positions instead of relying on field labels. With PI-FT, the penalty from changing field order is reduced dramatically to only 0.2 nDCG@10 points, while in-distribution accuracy remains essentially unchanged. On the DevDataBench benchmark, the fine-tuned 118M-parameter encoder, which can run efficiently on CPUs, outperforms all tested zero-shot baselines, including OpenAI’s text-embedding-3-large model, obtaining 0.707 vs. 0.556 nDCG@10. Gains are especially pronounced in low-resource languages. The authors release DevDataBench, the data-generation pipeline, the trained models, and a reusable PI-FT framework.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that field order in serialized structured records is not a minor implementation detail but a major factor controlling retrieval quality after fine-tuning. Standard fine-tuning unintentionally ties meaning to absolute positions, making systems brittle to schema or indexing changes. Their proposed permutation-invariant fine-tuning (PI-FT) is a simple, low-cost change to the training pipeline that makes retrieval robust to arbitrary field permutations with negligible loss in accuracy. By generating a comprehensive multilingual benchmark (DevDataBench) entirely with LLMs and demonstrating that a small, CPU-deployable encoder can outperform strong proprietary zero-shot baselines, they show that accurate, robust, and self-hostable retrieval over development indicators is feasible. This improves the reliability of AI assistants and agents that must ground their answers in public statistical data, particularly in low-resource language settings.

title: Latent Actions from Factorized Transition Effects under Agent Ambiguity
comment: Accepted to ICML 2026 Workshop on Compositional Learning. Project Page: https://hazel-heejeong-nam.github.io/LAM/
summary: Latent Action Models (LAMs) learn action-like proxies from observation transitions. However, in multi-object or distractor-rich scenes, these visual effects mix agent motion with distractors, camera dynamics, and background changes, making the underlying action source ambiguous without supervision. Structuring this mixture as reusable transition effects provides an intermediate representation from which action-like latents can be more robustly formed. We introduce Observed Transition Factorization (OTF), which decomposes each transition into a sparse set of observed transition primitives. Using these primitives as the transition interface, we propose OTF-LAM, which abstracts motion primitives into action-like latents within the standard inverse-forward dynamics framework, and OTF-LAM-Dino, a decoder-free variant that predicts future states in a frozen DINOv2 representation space. Empirically, OTF primitives transfer zeroshot across controlled carrier and morphology shifts, showing reusability. Furthermore, downstream policy learning results match or outperform baselines under complex transition ambiguity.

AI.tldr: Paper introduces Observed Transition Factorization (OTF) to decompose visual state transitions into sparse, reusable transition primitives, enabling more robust, action-like latent representations in complex, distractor-rich environments.
AI.motivation: Existing Latent Action Models (LAMs) infer action-like representations from observation transitions, but in multi-object or distractor-rich scenes these transitions blend agent motion with unrelated changes (distractors, camera motion, background), making it ambiguous what action actually caused the transition, especially without explicit supervision. There is a need for a structured way to separate and reuse the different components of visual change so that the agent’s true action effects can be robustly captured and transferred across environments.
AI.method: The paper proposes Observed Transition Factorization (OTF), which represents each state transition as a sparse combination of learned transition primitives, i.e., basic, reusable components of visual change. These primitives form an intermediate transition interface. Building on this, the authors devise two models: (1) OTF-LAM, which uses the OTF primitives inside a standard inverse–forward dynamics framework to abstract them into higher-level, action-like latent variables; and (2) OTF-LAM-Dino, a decoder-free variant that operates in the frozen feature space of DINOv2, predicting future states directly in that representation space instead of pixel space. The training encourages sparsity and reusability of primitives while learning to capture the causes of observable transitions.
AI.result: Experiments show that the learned OTF primitives can transfer zero-shot across controlled changes in the agent’s carrier (e.g., embodiment) and morphology, indicating that they capture general transition effects that are not tied to a specific agent form. In downstream tasks, policies trained using the OTF-based latent actions achieve performance comparable to or better than baseline LAM approaches when the environment has complex transition ambiguity from multiple objects, distractors, and background or camera motion.
AI.conclusion: Factorizing observed transitions into sparse, reusable primitives provides a useful intermediate representation that disentangles mixed visual changes and supports the learning of robust, transferable latent action spaces. The OTF framework, particularly in its DINO-based variant, improves policy learning under complex visual ambiguity and demonstrates that structured transition decomposition can mitigate key limitations of prior latent action models.

title: SIMAX: A Scalable and Interpretable Framework for Multi-Fidelity and Annotated Clinician-Patient Dialogue Simulation
summary:
Background. The widespread deployment of ambient digital scribes is driving large-scale capture of clinician-patient dialogues. Human coding of clinical communication data remains costly, inconsistent, and difficult to scale, motivating AI-driven communication coding systems. However, evaluating these systems requires real-world dialogues and human-coded labels, both hard to obtain at scale.
  Methods. We developed SIMAX (Scalable and Interpretable Framework for Multi-Fidelity and Annotated Clinician-Patient Dialogue Simulation), a framework for generating controlled clinical dialogue data with reference behavioral annotations. SIMAX generates clinician-patient dialogues from predefined clinical scenarios, personas and voice conditions, and target communication behaviors. Behaviors are controlled using two codebooks: the Global Codebook for overall communication quality and the WISER Codebook for specific countable behaviors. We evaluated SIMAX using automated and human quality assessments and an example communication coding system.
  Results. SIMAX generated 3,388 simulated dialogues across three specialties, multiple visit stages, persona characteristics, and accent conditions. Automated assessment showed mean UTMOS and WV-MOS scores of 3.03 and 2.61, WER and CER of 0.07 and 0.05, and CLAP cosine similarity of 0.41, suggesting reasonable speech naturalness, high transcription fidelity, and positive text-audio correspondence. Human evaluation showed a median MOS of 4.67 and a median clinical realism score of 3.00. Downstream evaluation suggests that SIMAX can assess how a communication coding system responds to behavioral targets and reveal insufficient sensitivity in some dimensions.
  Conclusions. SIMAX generates controlled and reproducible simulated clinician-patient dialogues, providing a data foundation for developing, validating, and refining communication coding systems.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SIMAX, a framework to generate realistic, annotated clinician‑patient dialogues to train and test AI systems that automatically code communication behaviors.
AI.motivation: Real clinician‑patient dialogue data with high‑quality behavioral annotations are scarce, expensive to obtain, and hard to scale. Yet such data are essential to develop and rigorously evaluate AI-based communication coding systems used in clinical settings. The authors aim to create a scalable, controllable, and reproducible way to generate dialogue corpora that approximate real interactions while providing rich ground-truth labels for different aspects of communication quality and behavior.
AI.method: The authors design SIMAX, a simulation framework that generates clinician‑patient dialogues from predefined clinical scenarios, persona and voice characteristics, and target communication behaviors. They organize behaviors into two structured codebooks: a Global Codebook for overall communication quality and a WISER Codebook for specific, countable behaviors. SIMAX produces multi-fidelity outputs (text and speech), and the generated dialogues are evaluated via automated metrics (e.g., UTMOS, WV-MOS for speech quality; WER/CER for transcription fidelity; CLAP similarity for text–audio alignment) and human ratings. They also use a sample communication coding system downstream to test whether SIMAX data can reveal how well such systems respond to targeted behavioral profiles.
AI.result: SIMAX produced 3,388 simulated dialogues spanning three clinical specialties, different visit stages, varied persona traits, and different accent conditions. Automated metrics indicated decent speech naturalness (UTMOS 3.03, WV-MOS 2.61), low transcription error rates (WER 0.07, CER 0.05), and reasonable text–audio correspondence (CLAP cosine similarity 0.41). Human raters gave high overall mean-opinion scores for quality (median MOS 4.67) and moderate scores for clinical realism (median 3.00). When used to evaluate a communication coding system, SIMAX data exposed that the system reacted to behavioral targets but lacked sensitivity on some communication dimensions.
AI.conclusion: SIMAX is an effective, scalable framework for generating controlled, annotated clinician‑patient dialogues that are sufficiently realistic for research and system evaluation. It can serve as a reproducible data backbone for developing, validating, and improving AI-based communication coding systems, and for probing their strengths and weaknesses across specific behavioral dimensions.

title: Linguistic Firewall: Geometry as Defense in Multi-Agent Systems Routing
comment: 8 pages (9 more for appendix), 3 figures. Published at the Second Workshop on Agents in the Wild: Safety, Security, and Beyond (AIWILD) at ICML 2026
summary: The rapid integration of Large Language Models (LLMs) has driven the evolution of Multi-Agent Systems (MAS), where specialized agents collaborate to execute complex workflows. Effective orchestration in these environments requires robust routing mechanisms to efficiently allocate tasks to the most suitable agent. However, existing routers fundamentally rely on unverified proxies, ranging from textual self-descriptions to static surrogate representations, to gauge an agent's competence. This reliance on non-empirical data creates a critical gap between an agent's projected profile and its actual operational capabilities, introducing severe security vulnerabilities. Malicious agents can easily misrepresent their proficiencies or harbor covert backdoors that evade both standard external analysis and static representation-learning techniques. In this work, we introduce ANTAP (Automatic Non-Textual Agent Picker), an evaluation-driven routing architecture that discards indirect proxies in favor of active capability testing. By dynamically querying agents to ascertain their true competencies empirically, ANTAP distills performance into fixed behavioral operators within a shared semantic space. At inference time, routing is performed via a purely non-textual algebraic projection, establishing a "linguistic firewall" that renders metadata-based attacks inexpressible. In our experiments, ANTAP achieves near-zero ASR against description-based injection attacks, compared to 67.3\% and above for the description-based router baseline. Against adaptive embedding attacks, ANTAP achieves substantially lower ASR than the embedding-based baseline, with a 20\% reduction, while remaining resilient to description manipulation by design.

AI.tldr: ANTAP is a capability-based routing framework for LLM multi-agent systems that selects agents using empirical behavioral tests instead of textual or embedding-based descriptions, significantly improving robustness against prompt/metadata-based attacks.
AI.motivation: Existing multi-agent LLM systems route tasks using unverified proxies like self-descriptions or static embeddings of agents. This creates a mismatch between claimed and actual capabilities and opens security holes: malicious agents can lie about skills or hide backdoors that bypass description-based or representation-learning-based checks. There is a need for a routing mechanism that is grounded in observed behavior rather than textual metadata, especially to defend against prompt injection and description-manipulation attacks in agent ecosystems.
AI.method: The paper proposes ANTAP (Automatic Non-Textual Agent Picker), an evaluation-driven agent routing architecture. ANTAP actively queries agents on tailored capability tests to empirically measure what each agent can actually do. From these interactions, it derives fixed behavioral operators that embed agents in a shared semantic capability space, independent of their textual descriptions. At inference time, task routing is done through algebraic operations in this non-textual space, selecting the best agent via projections rather than via language or metadata. This “linguistic firewall” makes routing decisions agnostic to any textual self-description and thus robust to description-level attacks. The method is evaluated against various adversarial setups, including description-based injection and adaptive embedding attacks, and compared to description-based and embedding-based routers.
AI.result: ANTAP dramatically reduces attack success rates (ASR) in multi-agent routing scenarios. For description-based injection attacks, the ASR of ANTAP is near zero, while a description-based router baseline suffers ASR of 67.3% or higher. Against more sophisticated adaptive embedding attacks that try to manipulate representation-based routers, ANTAP still has substantially lower ASR than an embedding-based baseline, with around a 20% reduction in ASR, all while being inherently immune to description manipulation due to its non-textual routing design.
AI.conclusion: Evaluation-driven, non-textual routing based on empirically tested agent behavior is far more secure than relying on textual self-descriptions or static embeddings in LLM multi-agent systems. By turning observed performance into behavioral operators in a shared semantic space and using purely algebraic, non-linguistic routing, ANTAP effectively closes the gap between claimed and actual capabilities and provides strong resistance to metadata-based and prompt-injection-style attacks. This suggests that future MAS orchestrators should prioritize behavior-grounded, language-agnostic routing mechanisms to harden multi-agent systems against adversarial agents.

title: Regime-Aware Peer Specialization for Robust RAG under Heterogeneous Knowledge Conflicts
summary: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) improves language models by grounding generation in external context. However, it can be fragile when the retrieved context conflicts with the model's parametric knowledge. Such conflicts span a reliability spectrum, ranging from reliable and partially reliable evidence to adversarial context. Existing remedies often handle such heterogeneous conflicts with regime-agnostic supervision, which can conflate incompatible learning signals across reliability regimes. To disentangle these signals, we propose RAPS-DA, a regime-aware peer specialization framework that addresses conflict at two complementary granularities. At the sample level, conflicts are divided into three regimes, including Grounding, Arbitration, and Resistance, with one same-scale peer specialist trained per regime from a shared base model. Each sample is then hard-routed to its regime-matched peer for on-policy reverse-KL supervision. At the token level, a dual-layer selector uses inter-teacher disagreement, student-teacher divergence, and student entropy to filter uninformative or unstable tokens, upweight confidently misaligned ones, and gradually focus supervision on high-conflict tokens as the student matures. Gains stem from specialization at a fixed model scale, not from a stronger teacher, and the peer specialists exist only during training, so the deployed student requires no regime labels or peer access. Experiments on five conflict scenarios and two out-of-distribution benchmarks show RAPS-DA surpasses all prompting, decoding, fine-tuning, RL, and single-teacher baselines.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces RAPS-DA, a regime-aware training framework that makes RAG models more robust when retrieved context conflicts with the model’s internal knowledge, outperforming existing prompting, decoding, and training baselines.
AI.motivation: RAG systems often fail when retrieved documents disagree with the model’s parametric knowledge, especially because such conflicts can be reliable, partially reliable, or adversarial. Existing approaches typically supervise models in a regime-agnostic way, mixing signals from different reliability types, which can hurt learning and robustness. The authors aim to disentangle these heterogeneous conflict signals to improve reliability across varied conflict scenarios without increasing model size or deployment complexity.
AI.method: They propose RAPS-DA, which introduces regime-aware peer specialization at both sample and token levels. At the sample level, data is categorized into three conflict regimes—Grounding (trust retrieved context), Arbitration (carefully balance context and prior), and Resistance (ignore misleading context). For each regime, they train a peer specialist model initialized from a shared base model. Training uses hard routing: each training example is sent only to its matching specialist, and the final student model is supervised via on-policy reverse-KL from the appropriate peer. At the token level, a dual-layer selector examines inter-teacher disagreement, student–teacher divergence, and student entropy to identify which tokens provide stable and informative conflict signals. It filters unstable or uninformative tokens, upweights confidently misaligned ones, and progressively refocuses supervision onto the highest-conflict tokens as training proceeds. The peer specialists are used only during training; the final deployed student model is single, does not require regime labels, and maintains fixed parameter scale.
AI.result: On five designed conflict scenarios and two out-of-distribution benchmarks, RAPS-DA consistently surpasses a range of strong baselines, including prompting techniques, decoding strategies, standard fine-tuning, reinforcement learning approaches, and single-teacher distillation methods. The improvements are attributed to regime-aware specialization and token-level conflict-focused supervision rather than to a larger or stronger teacher model.
AI.conclusion: Regime-agnostic training is suboptimal for RAG systems facing heterogeneous conflicts between retrieved context and model knowledge. By explicitly modeling conflict regimes and using peer specialists with fine-grained token-level selection, RAPS-DA yields a single deployed model that is more robust and accurate across diverse conflict settings without increasing deployment complexity. This suggests that conflict-aware, regime-specific supervision is an effective direction for improving reliability in retrieval-augmented generation.

title: The Human Creativity Benchmark
summary: Modern AI evaluation frameworks treat evaluator disagreement as noise to be resolved. In creative domains, professional disagreement reflects genuine differences in taste, not measurement error. We argue that evaluating creative AI requires preserving two distinct signals: convergence, where professionals align around shared best practices, and divergence, where individual taste legitimately varies. We present the Human Creativity Benchmark (HCB), a benchmark that operationalizes this separation by collecting pairwise preferences, scalar ratings on prompt adherence, usability, and visual appeal, and qualitative rationale from domain professionals. Across 15,000 professional judgments spanning five creative domains and three workflow phases (ideation, mockup, refinement), we find that convergence concentrates on verifiable dimensions like technical correctness and visual hierarchy, while divergence concentrates on taste-driven dimensions like aesthetic direction and conceptual risk. No model excels uniformly across all phases. Collapsing these signals into a single quality metric discards the most actionable information: where models must be correct versus where they should remain steerable.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces the Human Creativity Benchmark (HCB) to evaluate creative AI by capturing both agreement and disagreement among professional evaluators, instead of collapsing them into a single score.
AI.motivation: Existing AI evaluation frameworks treat disagreement among human evaluators as noise to be averaged away, which is inappropriate for creative tasks where disagreement often reflects genuine differences in taste. There is a need for an evaluation approach that separates shared professional standards from individual aesthetic preferences so that creative AI can be assessed more meaningfully.
AI.method: The authors design the Human Creativity Benchmark (HCB), which gathers data from domain professionals across five creative domains and three workflow phases (ideation, mockup, refinement). For each AI output, they collect (1) pairwise preferences between alternatives, (2) scalar ratings for prompt adherence, usability, and visual appeal, and (3) qualitative rationales. They analyze where professionals converge or diverge in their judgments to disentangle objective-like criteria from taste-driven ones.
AI.result: From 15,000 professional judgments, the study finds that convergence among professionals is strongest on verifiable aspects such as technical correctness and visual hierarchy, while divergence is strongest on taste-related aspects like aesthetic direction and conceptual risk. They also find that no existing model performs best across all workflow phases, and that evaluation signals differ meaningfully across phases.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that creative AI evaluation must preserve both convergence and divergence signals rather than collapsing them into a single metric. Doing so reveals which dimensions require strict correctness and which should remain flexible and user-steerable, providing more actionable guidance for model development and deployment in creative workflows.

title: TRACE: Temporal Relationship-Aware Conversational Entrainment Detection in Dyadic Speech
summary: With the proliferation of speech AI agents, understanding emotional entrainment in conversational interaction has become increasingly important. Emotional entrainment is shaped by social relationships and conversational context, influencing affective coordination over time. We introduce DyadEE, a dataset for emotional entrainment detection in dyadic speech interactions, containing both emotionally entrained conversations and synthetic interactions where entrainment is disrupted through partner swapping and emotion resynthesis. We further propose TRACE, a window-level framework that models dyadic interaction as ordered sequences of acoustic embeddings derived from emotion fine-tuned Whisper representations, treating each sample as an interaction trace rather than pooled utterances. Experimental results on DyadEE show that incorporating conversational context and relationship information improves emotional entrainment detection, with TRACE achieving the best accuracy of 97.01%.

AI.tldr: They build a new dataset (DyadEE) and a sequence-based model (TRACE) to detect emotional entrainment in dyadic speech, achieving 97% accuracy by leveraging context and relationship info.
AI.motivation: As speech AI agents become more common, it is crucial to understand and automatically detect how people’s emotions align and adapt to each other (emotional entrainment) during conversations, since this affects interaction quality and is shaped by social relationships and context. Existing work lacks dedicated datasets and models that treat entrainment as a dynamic, temporally evolving process in dyadic speech.
AI.method: They curate DyadEE, a dataset of dyadic speech interactions labeled for emotional entrainment. It includes naturally entrained conversations and synthetic, non-entrained interactions created via partner swapping and emotion resynthesis. They propose TRACE, a window-level modeling framework that represents each dyadic interaction as an ordered sequence of acoustic embeddings from emotion fine-tuned Whisper models, treating the conversation as an interaction trace rather than aggregating utterances. TRACE incorporates conversational context and relationship metadata when predicting emotional entrainment over time.
AI.result: On the DyadEE benchmark, using conversational context and relationship information significantly improves emotional entrainment detection performance, and the proposed TRACE framework achieves a best accuracy of 97.01%, outperforming alternative baselines.
AI.conclusion: Modeling dyadic speech as an ordered interaction trace with rich acoustic embeddings, context, and relationship cues is highly effective for detecting emotional entrainment. The DyadEE dataset and TRACE framework provide a strong foundation for future research on dynamic affective coordination in human–human and human–AI spoken interactions.

title: DOPD: Dual On-policy Distillation
summary: On-policy distillation (OPD) offers superior capacity transfer by supervising student-sampled trajectories with dense token-level signals. To furnish high-quality supervision sources and thereby elevate the performance frontier of distillation, an intuitive direction is to infuse privileged information to either teacher or student itself. However, this additional input induces a potential failure mode we dub privilege illusion: a pattern that conflates the transferable capability gap that students are meant to close, and the information asymmetry gap that can only be mimicked but never replicated. This issue is further amplified by the inherent non-uniformity of token-level supervision, where only a small subset of tokens carries pivotal capability-bearing signals. To this end, we propose DOPD, an advantage-aware dual distillation paradigm that dynamically routes token-level supervision between privileged teacher and privileged student policies based on their advantage gap and relative probabilities. Each token receives supervision of different strength, objective, and strategy from either teacher or student itself, which transfers credible capability while simultaneously receiving auxiliary signals, to alleviate privilege illusion. Extensive experiments on both large language model (LLM) and vision-language model (VLM) settings demonstrate that DOPD consistently outperforms Vanilla OPD and other counterparts. Further results on stability, robustness, continual learning, and out-of-distribution tasks validate its superiority.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces DOPD, a dual on-policy distillation framework that uses both a privileged teacher and a privileged student and dynamically selects whose signals to trust per token, improving over standard OPD in LLM and VLM tasks.
AI.motivation: On-policy distillation works well because it supervises student-generated trajectories with dense token-level feedback, but current methods can be limited by the quality of supervision. Adding privileged information (extra context or signals) to teacher or student seems helpful, yet it causes a ‘privilege illusion’: the student is pushed to match behaviors that rely on information it will not have at test time. This is worsened by the fact that only a fraction of tokens truly reflect transferable capabilities, while others mostly encode the privileged information. The authors aim to improve capacity transfer without letting information asymmetry corrupt the learning signal.
AI.method: They propose DOPD, an advantage-aware dual distillation paradigm. Both teacher and student have access to their own privileged information (e.g., richer context, extra signals). For each token in the student-generated trajectory, the method compares the teacher and student via their advantage gaps and relative token probabilities, and then dynamically chooses how to route supervision: which policy to imitate more, and with what objective and strength. Thus each token may follow teacher-style distillation, self-distillation from the privileged student, or a mixture, with different loss weights, so that the student mainly learns capability-bearing patterns that are not artifacts of privileged inputs. The supervision is explicitly token-dependent and advantage-aware.
AI.result: Across multiple benchmarks for large language models and vision-language models, DOPD consistently surpasses vanilla OPD and other distillation or training baselines. Reported gains cover standard performance metrics and also improved stability during training, better robustness, stronger continual learning behavior, and superior generalization on out-of-distribution evaluation tasks.
AI.conclusion: Dual on-policy distillation with advantage-aware token-level routing can mitigate the privilege illusion problem in privileged-information distillation. By deciding per token whether the teacher or the student’s own privileged policy should guide learning, DOPD transfers genuine capabilities more effectively, leading to consistently better performance, stability, robustness, and generalization than existing OPD-based approaches.

title: Poller: Are LLMs Suitable for Evaluating the Poetry Understanding Task?
summary: Traditional automatic evaluation methods have been shown to be unsuitable for modern Chinese poetry because of the distinct nature of this literary genre. Human evaluation remains reliable, but is expensive and not applicable to large-scale data. In this paper, we propose Poller (Poetry LLM Evaluator), a novel method leveraging large language models (LLMs) to evaluate the poetry understanding task. Specifically, our method requires LLMs to play the role of a poem's author with detailed information, thereby emulating human evaluation and judgment by adopting the poet's perspective. We conducted comprehensive experiments on multiple LLMs, evaluating the interpretations of poems across eight specialized dimensions. Experimental results demonstrate that our method effectively reduces the evaluation error between LLMs and humans. Especially for specific dimension evaluation, Poller-based LLMs achieve a 94.55% and 89.53% error reduction for rhetorical techniques and defamiliarization, respectively, compared to baseline methods. These performances are unattainable by conventional LLM evaluation methods. Experimental results from multiple LLMs across various dimensions validate the efficacy of our method. This work bridges the gap between automated efficiency and human expertise, establishing a foundation for automated evaluation in poetry-related tasks.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Poller, an LLM-based evaluator that mimics a poem's author to assess poem interpretations, greatly reducing the gap between automatic and human evaluation for Chinese poetry understanding.
AI.motivation: Existing automatic evaluation metrics fail on modern Chinese poetry due to its unique, nuanced nature, while human evaluation is accurate but costly and unscalable. The authors aim to create an automatic method that is both scalable and aligned with expert human judgment for poetry understanding tasks.
AI.method: They design Poller (Poetry LLM Evaluator), where an LLM is prompted to assume the role of the poem’s author, equipped with rich contextual and authorial information. The LLM then evaluates poem interpretations along eight specialized dimensions (such as rhetorical techniques and defamiliarization), emulating how a knowledgeable human or the author might judge understanding. Multiple LLMs are used to test this role-playing evaluation framework against baseline LLM-based evaluators.
AI.result: Across multiple LLMs and eight evaluation dimensions, Poller significantly lowers the discrepancy between LLM-based scores and human judgments. In particular, Poller yields 94.55% and 89.53% reductions in evaluation error for rhetorical techniques and defamiliarization, respectively, compared with conventional LLM evaluation approaches. Other dimensions also show consistent improvement, and traditional automatic metrics cannot reach these performance levels.
AI.conclusion: Poller provides an effective, scalable way to evaluate poetry understanding by making LLMs emulate the poet’s perspective. It narrows the gap between automatic and human evaluation, especially for subtle literary dimensions, and offers a practical foundation for future automated evaluation methods in poetry and related literary tasks.

title: Self-Evolving World Models for LLM Agent Planning
summary: World models offer a principled way to equip long-horizon LLM agents with foresight: predictions of action consequences before execution. However, unreliable foresight can be ignored, misused, or even degrade downstream decision-making. In this paper, we introduce WorldEvolver, a self-evolving world model framework that revises its deployment-time context while keeping the downstream agent and all model parameters frozen. WorldEvolver integrates three modules: (i) Episodic Memory, which exploits real action transitions through retrieval-based simulation; (ii) Semantic Memory, which extracts persistent heuristic rules from prediction-observation mismatches; and (iii) Selective Foresight, which filters low-confidence predictions before integrating them into agent reasoning context. We evaluate WorldEvolver on ALFWorld and ScienceWorld, measuring world model prediction accuracy on Word2World and downstream agent success rate on AgentBoard. Extensive experiments show that WorldEvolver achieves the highest prediction accuracy across three backbones and leads other world model baselines on downstream agent success rate, demonstrating that test-time memory revision enhances both predictive fidelity and planning performance.

AI.tldr: WorldEvolver is a test-time self-improving world-model framework that refines its own context and memories to give LLM agents more accurate, selectively used foresight, boosting both prediction accuracy and downstream task success without changing model parameters or the agent.
AI.motivation: Long-horizon LLM agents benefit from world models that forecast the consequences of actions, but in practice these predictions are often unreliable. Poor or miscalibrated foresight can be ignored, misused, or even harm decision quality. There is a need for a mechanism that can improve the usefulness and trustworthiness of world-model predictions at deployment time, without costly retraining or modifying the downstream agent.
AI.method: WorldEvolver is a self-evolving world-model framework composed of three key modules that operate at test time while keeping all model parameters and the agent frozen: (i) Episodic Memory stores real past transitions and uses retrieval-based simulation to ground predictions in similar experiences; (ii) Semantic Memory generalizes from systematic mismatches between predicted and observed outcomes to extract persistent heuristic rules; and (iii) Selective Foresight filters out low-confidence predictions so only high-quality foresight is injected into the agent’s reasoning context. These components revise the world model’s deployment-time context rather than its weights.
AI.result: On the ALFWorld and ScienceWorld benchmarks, the authors assess both prediction accuracy (via Word2World) and downstream task performance (via AgentBoard). Across three different backbone models, WorldEvolver attains the highest world-model prediction accuracy and surpasses other world-model baselines in the success rate of the controlled agent. This empirically supports that test-time memory revision and selective use of predictions can enhance both modeling fidelity and planning effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: WorldEvolver demonstrates that world models for LLM agents can be significantly improved at deployment time by evolving their memory and context, without any parameter updates or modifications to the agent policy. By combining episodic and semantic memories with a selective foresight mechanism, the framework yields more accurate and better-calibrated predictions that translate into superior performance on long-horizon interactive benchmarks. This validates test-time memory revision as a practical path to more reliable world-model-based planning.

title: Morphing into Hybrid Attention Models
summary: Hybrid attention models improve long-context efficiency by retaining only a subset of full-attention layers and replacing the remaining layers with linear attention. However, the effectiveness of Transformer-to-hybrid conversion critically depends on which layers preserve full attention. Existing hybrid layer selection methods typically rely on heuristic strategies such as fixed placement patterns or layerwise scoring, implicitly treating layer importance as isolated and overlooking the interdependent layer effect under a global hybrid configuration. In this work, we formulate hybrid layer selection as a budget-constrained subset optimization problem. We further propose FlashMorph (Fast LAyer Selection for Hybrid MORPHing), an effective, efficient and scalable layer selection method for Transformer-to-hybrid conversion. FlashMorph first constructs a morphable model by equipping each full-attention layer with a converted linear-attention branch. It then freezes all model weights and jointly optimizes layerwise gates on synthetic long-context retrieval data, with a linearization regularization that encourages the model to rely on linear attention for efficiency. The learned gates are discretized under a preset full-attention budget to instantiate the hybrid architecture, followed by standard logits distillation and long-context finetuning.
